Marketing Audit & Growth Plan

Situational analysis and 2026 commercial accelerator for Able Care and Able Assess

April 2026 US & UK Prepared for Able Care leadership
Data sources: Falls Prevention Commercialization Strategy 2026, Able Care AI & Website Plan, Website Opportunity Analysis, Sitemap Audit, full SEO and CRO audits (March 2026), live walk-through of able-care.co, industry market research (Grand View, Fortune Business Insights, McKnight's Senior Living), and public company data.

Executive Summary

Able Care enters 2026 with a rare combination that most early-stage health-tech companies spend years assembling. It owns an unusually credentialed proof base, with Harvard, Johns Hopkins, Oxford, Mount Sinai, MD Anderson, and Mass General affiliations already on the page, and regulatory posture (FDA, CE, ISO 27001, ISO 13485, IEC 60601, HIPAA, GDPR) that sits above the waterline for corporate healthcare buyers. The clinical story, anchored to CDC STEADI and the 2022 World Guidelines for falls prevention, is defensible. The product, Able Assess, is the only platform currently bundling the four most-cited functional measurements (grip strength, timed up and go, thirty-second chair to stand, and four-metre gait speed) into a single five-minute screening any staff member can run. The market tailwind is real: one in four US seniors falls each year, 61% of senior care facilities plan to implement smart systems by 2026, and projected annual medical expenditure on falls is on course to pass $100 billion by 2030.

The 2026 commercialization strategy correctly identifies the two priority targets where this proof base converts fastest into repeatable revenue: PE-backed and franchised non-clinical home care, and corporate senior living (CCRCs, rental retirement, IL, AL, for-profit SNF). Both are consolidated enough that a single corporate win unlocks a franchise network, and both face P&L and liability pressures that a five-minute objective screening directly addresses. The strategy also correctly sequences the motion: bottom-up BDR and franchise-level outreach paired with top-down corporate introductions, with an urgent "two segment one-pagers ASAP" marketing need. This audit agrees with that frame and extends it.

The gap is execution surface. The website, which is the landing layer for every motion in the plan, is in a credible-but-under-converting state. Prior audits scored it 4/10 on SEO and 6/10 on CRO and both diagnoses hold up. The site has no buyer-segment pages at all, frames every value prop for the clinician rather than the operator buyer, gives champions nothing to forward internally, and is bleeding organic discovery through missing schema, missing H1s on the highest-value commercial pages, 60+ unlabelled images on the homepage, and an apparent indexation gap where ~36 sitemapped URLs yield only ~7 indexed pages. These are not redesign problems. They are hygiene problems that compound daily until they are fixed.

The competitive landscape is in Able Care's favour at the layer that matters. At the consumer pendant layer, the category is crowded (Lifeline, ADT, Medical Guardian). At the digital-screening-for-operators layer where Able Care actually sells, the set is small and none of the serious players own the "non-clinical-staff usable, five-minute, four-measurement, CDC-aligned" position. SafelyYou has the strongest senior-living brand but competes on reactive AI video rather than proactive screening. Linus Health is well-funded but leads with cognition. VirtuSense and Zibrio address slices of the same measurement stack. None of them hold the top-right quadrant of staff-wide-usable and proactive. That quadrant is Able Care's to claim, and the plan to claim it is a function of messaging and website architecture, not product development.

The single largest unlock this audit identifies is the pairing of (a) segment landing pages mapped one-to-one to the strategy's buyer tiers, with (b) an AI-accelerated content factory that produces the segment one-pagers, email sequences, and social assets at 5–10× current velocity. The 2026 plan needs seven segment one-pagers, a growing cadence of blog and thought-leadership content, per-account outbound briefs, and conversation intelligence, all with a team that is small by design. AI is the only plausible way to hit that surface area. This report therefore treats the content factory, claims guardrail library, and brand-voice assistant as Tier 1 commercial infrastructure, not optional experiments.

The central question for Able Care leadership is not whether the brand has the raw materials to win this category. It does. The question is whether the team will sequence the next ninety days tightly enough to convert a credible proof base into booked demos from PE operators and corporate senior living COOs before a better-funded competitor rewrites the positioning conversation. This audit recommends a ninety-day sprint covering website hygiene, segment page rollout, the AI content factory, and an evidence layer the sales team can point champions at from day one.

Brand Overview

What Able Care Does

Able Care sells Able Assess, a digital falls-risk screening platform built to be delivered by clinical or non-clinical staff in under five minutes. The platform captures four functional measurements (single maximum grip strength, timed up and go, thirty-second chair to stand, and four-metre gait speed) and stratifies residents into low, intermediate, or high risk in line with the 2022 World Guidelines for falls prevention and management for older adults. The brand promise, taken from the guidelines document, is "Able Care is enabling intelligent health."

Credibility Anchors

The brand's proof base is stronger than its current traffic levels suggest. Institutional affiliations and research partnerships with Harvard, Johns Hopkins, Oxford, Mount Sinai, MD Anderson, and Mass General give it academic authority most peers cannot match. Regulatory posture is comprehensive: FDA, CE, ISO 27001, ISO 13485, IEC 60601, HIPAA, and GDPR. These are assets that should anchor every segment page and every champion-forwarding asset, not sit in a footer strip or rotate through a homepage carousel.

Brand System

The visual system is defined in the Able Care brand guidelines v1: a confident, modern sans-serif, grey-dominant backgrounds, with Blue (#1432FF) and Aqua (#00FFD2) as strategic accents, and the stylised "A" mark as a hero graphic device. Title case "Able Care" in body copy; the mark is hero, the wordmark supports. The overall brand temperature is clinical but human, tech-literate but trustworthy. This audit follows those guidelines verbatim.

!
Key observation: The credentials and regulatory posture Able Care has already earned would cost a competitor two to three years and low-seven-figures of research investment to replicate. The single biggest brand error the company can make in 2026 is failing to put those credentials above the fold on every commercial page.

Positioning Recommendation

The current website positions Able Assess as a clinical tool for clinicians. The 2026 strategy sells it as a P&L lever for operators. Those are two different products commercially even though the underlying software is identical. The recommended one-line positioning for the operator buyer is:

The falls-prevention platform built for non-clinical teams in home care and senior living. Easy to roll out, proven to reduce falls, and designed to protect NOI, residents, and reputation alike.

This positioning is then carried through three pillars, which should appear on the homepage and every segment page: Protect residents (CDC-aligned screening in minutes, delivered by any staff member), Protect the P&L (fewer falls, lower turnover, reduced liability, stronger census), Protect your growth (a plug-and-play tech layer that scales across franchises, campuses, and portfolios).

Market & Category Context

Market Sizing

Market estimates vary by definition. The most useful framing for Able Care's commercial planning is TAM/SAM/SOM across the digital falls-prevention layer, not the broader category of grab bars and bathroom sensors that inflates industry reports.

LevelEstimateBasis
TAM (Global fall prevention products, 2026)$3.48B, mid-single-digit CAGRGrand View Research, Fortune Business Insights, DataHorizzon Research
TAM (Fall detection systems only, 2026)$526MMarket Growth Reports, Future Market Insights
SAM (US digital screening for home care & senior living)$250–400MEstimated from SAM slice of US operator spend on clinical-workflow software
SAM (Non-clinical home care alone)~$60MFalls Prevention Commercialization Strategy 2026
SOM (Reachable by Able Care, 2026–2027)$10–25M ARRGated by sales capacity and segment sequencing, not demand

Category Tailwinds

PE consolidation of home care and senior living

The 2026 strategy names twelve PE-backed home care operators including Help at Home, Elara Caring, Care Advantage, Senior Helpers, Altocare, Aveanna, BrightStar Care, Interim HealthCare, Home Instead, Comfort Keepers, and Right at Home. Each one represents a single buying centre that, if won, propagates across dozens of locations. Senior living is on the same trajectory. This is the critical structural feature of the category: a small number of corporates now control a disproportionate share of the addressable endpoints.

Regulatory pressure

OASIS Section GG in home health, the SAFE Act in multiple states, and Medicaid MLTSS quality ratings are shifting falls screening from nice-to-have toward compliance-adjacent. Twenty-four states now route HCBS contracts through private MCOs, which opens a preferred-vendor-list opportunity Able Care should pursue with a dedicated response engine.

Non-clinical workforce reality

Home care staff are largely 1099 contractors, non-clinical, and in a permanent high-turnover loop. Any screening tool that requires onboarding friction loses. Any tool a new hire can run on day one wins. This is the single biggest product-market-fit lever for Able Assess and it should be the lead message on the home care segment page.

Generative AI and the shift in how buyers research

A non-trivial share of 2026 B2B healthcare buyers now begin research in ChatGPT, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews, and Claude. Current Able Care content is not structured for AI citation. This is addressed in detail in the SEO & GEO section.

PESTLE Summary

FactorImplication for Able Care
PoliticalSAFE Act passage in multiple US states and CMS quality-rating pressure are elevating falls prevention in operator scorecards. Federal focus on aging-in-place policy is a structural tailwind.
EconomicStaff turnover in home care is a $10,000-per-hire cost centre. Tools that reduce onboarding time have a direct ROI story. PE-backed operators are operating under NOI pressure, which makes liability reduction a top-tier buying criterion.
Social72% of seniors prefer aging in place. Family members are increasingly the information-gatherers for care decisions, which opens a secondary consumer-facing content opportunity Able Care does not currently address.
Technological61% of senior care facilities plan to implement smart systems by 2026. The operator buyer is primed for tech but deeply sceptical of complexity. Integration, ease of rollout, and security posture are the decisive technology features, not raw capability.
LegalFall-related lawsuits are among the largest liability exposures in senior living. HIPAA, Section GG, and state-level MLTSS requirements are both a sales lever (compliance pain) and a risk (claims must be verifiable). The claims library is not optional.
EnvironmentalLower-priority factor for this category, but the broader ESG push in PE reporting is giving resident-wellbeing metrics unexpected visibility at board level.

Audience & Segments

The strategy document names seven priority sub-segments across non-clinical home care and corporate senior living. The audit treats the segments as the commercial spine of the website and the content engine. Every piece of marketing collateral should pick a segment, pick a persona, and speak that person's language.

Priority Segments

SegmentCommercial priorityBuyer languagePrimary motion
PE-backed / franchised home careTier 1NOI, turnover cost, franchise economics, staff retention, censusTop-down to corporate HQ + bottom-up franchise owners
Independent multi-site home careTier 1Operational efficiency, staff retention, differentiatorBottom-up BDR / LinkedIn
CCRCs (corporate)Tier 1Resident retention, liability, census velocity, quality ratingTop-down to corporate + ED-level champion
Rental retirement (IL/AL corporate)Tier 1Census, turnover, ancillary revenue, liabilityTop-down corporate
IL-only communities (corporate)Tier 2Resident retention, aging-in-place extensionCorporate
AL-only communities (corporate)Tier 2Clinical outcomes, liability, Section GG defensibilityCorporate
For-profit SNFs (corporate)Tier 2Quality rating, reimbursement, Section GG codingCorporate

Personas Across the Buying Committee

Corporate and PE decision-makers (Operating Partners, Portfolio Managers, CEO/COO, VP Ops) are the primary buyers; they care about NOI, turnover, liability, standardisation, and a scalable tech stack. Clinical and operational leaders (Head of Clinical, VP Clinical Services, Director of Nursing, Wellness Director) are the primary champions; they care about resident outcomes, reduced staff burden, standardisation, and CDC alignment. Innovation and IT leaders (Head of Innovation, IT Director) are the enablers; they care about integrations, ease of rollout, and security. Franchise owners and community EDs are the bottom-up path; they care about census, staff retention, P&L, and ease of adoption. Care managers and nurses are the daily users; they care about workflow speed, objective data, and defensible recommendations.

!
The single most important buyer distinction: the person using the product is almost never the person buying it. Every current page on able-care.co speaks to the user. Every new commercial page this audit recommends should speak to the buyer.

Competitive Landscape

The falls-prevention market has three distinct layers and conflating them is the most common strategic error made by buyers, analysts, and even some of the players themselves. Able Care's entire competitive narrative depends on separating the layers cleanly.

Three-Layer Map

LayerWhat it doesRepresentative playersRelationship to Able Care
1. Consumer PERS / pendantsReactive fall detection, emergency callLifeline, ADT Health, Medical Guardian, Bay Alarm, MobileHelp, LivelyNot competitors. Buyers may conflate. Messaging must separate "detect" from "prevent."
2. Digital screening & risk platformsProactive functional screening and risk stratificationAble Care (Able Assess), SafelyYou, Linus Health, VirtuSense, Zibrio, Nymbl Science, BioSensicsThe real competitive set.
3. Legacy clinical protocolsPaper-based STEADI, TUG, Berg, Tinetti POMA"Do nothing new" defaultPositioned as the layer Able Assess digitises, not competes with.

Layer 2 Competitor Comparison

BrandCore approachPrimary segmentPosition vs Able Care
Able Care5-minute functional screening (grip + TUG + CTS + gait), usable by non-clinical staffHome care, senior livingOnly player bundling all four measurements with operator-facing positioning
SafelyYouAI video monitoring for fall detection and reviewSenior living, memory careStrongest senior-living brand but reactive, not proactive; does not screen
Linus HealthDigital cognitive + functional assessmentPrimary care, senior livingWell funded, Harvard-origin, leads with cognition rather than falls
VirtuSenseSensor + AI gait and balance analysis (hardware-led)Senior living, rehabCapital-intensive install; narrower measurement stack
ZibrioSmart balance scale producing a stability scoreClinics, senior living, consumerSingle-metric approach; complementary rather than competitive at platform level
Nymbl ScienceMobile balance-training app, payer distributionHealth plans, older adult consumersB2B2C training play, not an assessment tool for operators
BioSensicsWearables and digital biomarkers for researchClinical trials, researchResearch-focused, not a commercial operator product

Perceptual Map

The two axes that actually matter in this category are who can run the assessment (clinician-only through to any staff member) and what the product does (reactive detection through to proactive screening). The top-right quadrant is staff-wide usable and proactive. It is currently unoccupied. Able Care should own it.

Perceptual Positioning — Digital Falls Layer

Points of Parity and Points of Difference

Points of parity (what every credible player must offer): HIPAA-compliant data handling, a clinically defensible measurement basis, mobile-friendly delivery, integration with common operator tech stacks, and a meaningful customer list. Able Care meets all of these.

Points of difference (what genuinely sets Able Care apart): the four-measurement bundle in a single workflow, the non-clinical staff usability, the five-minute run time, alignment to the 2022 World Guidelines, the academic credential stack, and the longevity-adjacent grip-strength thought-leadership angle. None of these differentiators are currently foregrounded on the website.

Product & Value Framing

Product Surface

Able Assess captures four functional measurements in a single workflow: single maximum grip strength, timed up and go, thirty-second chair to stand, and four-metre gait speed. The output is a stratified risk category (low, intermediate, high) with an accompanying record suitable for care planning, reporting, and reimbursement documentation. The platform is mobile-delivered and designed for a non-clinical operator to complete unsupervised.

Value Framing by Buyer Pillar

The 2026 strategy identifies five repeatable value props across all segments. The website should lead with different ones for different segments, but the underlying five are stable:

PillarWhat the buyer hearsStrongest for
Customer acquisitionTech differentiator, reduced resident acquisition time, census liftPE operators, corporate CCRC, rental retirement
Staff efficiency and retentionNon-clinical friendly, reduces documentation burden, reduces turnoverHome care, franchise owners, EDs
Customer / resident retentionFewer falls, fewer hospitalisations, higher LTV and quality scoresCCRC, IL, AL
Liability reductionFall-related lawsuits are a primary cost centre; objective screening is the defenceAL, SNF, corporate senior living
CDC-aligned standardisationDelivered by non-clinical workforces across states and sitesMulti-site corporate, franchise, PE portfolios

BCG Growth-Share View

Able Care's product portfolio is concentrated. Able Assess is the single commercial product, with grip strength thought-leadership and clinical reference content as supporting brand assets. Viewed at the use-case rather than SKU level, the portfolio maps as follows:

Stars (high growth, strong share opportunity)

Able Assess in non-clinical home care. The market is consolidating fast under PE, there is no incumbent at the "staff-wide usable, four-measurement" position, and the ROI story is clean. This is where capital should concentrate.

Question marks (high growth, share to be won)

Able Assess in corporate senior living (CCRC, rental retirement, corporate AL). The category is growing and the value framing is strong, but the buying cycle is longer and the competitive set is more aware. Winnable with focused segment pages and named case studies.

Cash cows (stable share, moderate growth)

Grip strength content and the longevity-adjacent narrative. Generates credibility and inbound signal. Low cost to maintain, should not be over-invested in at the expense of the commercial segment engine.

Dogs (low growth, low share)

Consumer-direct fall screening. Not a commercial priority for 2026. Mentioned here only to flag that the site should not split its narrative across consumer and operator stories.

Pricing Posture

Pricing is not currently published. In B2B healthcare, silence on price reads as "too expensive to show." The audit recommendation is to publish a starting-from figure on the relevant segment pages ("starting from $X per site per month, contact for a detailed quote") rather than leave the buyer to infer. This is addressed in the website section.

Website Audit & Rebuild Case

The website is the single most leveraged surface in the 2026 plan. Every motion the strategy document describes — bottom-up BDR, top-down Paul/Dani corporate outreach, LinkedIn, paid, earned, content — ultimately lands the prospect on a page. That page is, today, working against the sale.

The Headline Diagnosis

A full SEO and CRO audit (March 2026, included in this document) scored able-care.co 4/10 on SEO and 6/10 on CRO. This audit confirms those scores and sharpens the read: the site is optimised to educate curious clinicians at a moment when the 2026 commercial reality is that the buyers are PE operating partners, corporate COOs, EDs, and franchise owners. The gap is not cosmetic. It is architectural.

Five Structural Gaps

1. No buyer-segment pages at all

The site has two product pages and zero segment pages. There is no /solutions/home-care/, no /solutions/ccrc/, no /solutions/assisted-living/. A BDR sending a PE CFO to the homepage is asking that CFO to translate "grip strength sensor" into "NOI lever" on their own. They will not.

2. Clinician-first messaging on an operator-first buyer

Every value prop on the site is framed for the nurse or PT. The buyer speaks NOI, turnover, liability, census velocity, Section GG. None of that language appears on the current homepage.

3. No evidence layer for champions

Champions (an ED, a Head of Clinical) are the forward-this-to-my-CFO layer of B2B healthcare. The site gives them nothing to forward: no /evidence/ page, no per-segment PDF, no procurement packet, no security one-pager.

4. A single high-commitment CTA

"Book a Demo" is the only path. Any visitor not meeting-ready leaves with no downstream hook. A secondary 1-pager download and a 60–90 second product tour video would widen top-of-funnel from ~1% to a realistic 8–15%.

5. Technical hygiene bleed

Pages return no-store, no-cache, killing caching and crawl efficiency. Zero schema markup site-wide. Missing H1s on About and both Solution pages. 60+ images on the homepage with empty alt text, including every trust logo. 36 sitemapped URLs vs ~7 indexed. The resources.able-care.co subdomain is splitting authority.

6. No instrumentation or accessibility story

No confirmed GA4 event setup, no baseline conversion rate, no accessibility statement. The audience is people designing services for older adults; WCAG 2.2 AA is a procurement checkbox.

What's Working (Do Not Break)

The elite trust logos, the regulatory posture, the problem framing ($80B, 50%, 36%, 3x statistics), the depth of the 25-post content library, and the clean URL structure, HTTPS, sitemap, and mobile viewport. These carry forward.

The Four-Tier Rebuild Plan

TierPhaseWorkExpected result
Tier 1 FoundationsPhase 1Turn on caching. Verify GA4 and Search Console. Investigate indexation gap. Add site-wide schema (Organization, Article, Product, FAQ, BreadcrumbList). Resolve subdomain split. Run accessibility baseline.Crawl efficiency restored. Measurement backbone in place. Indexation unlock.
Tier 2 Hygiene winsPhase 2Add missing H1s. Rewrite title tags with buyer-benefit framing. Add alt text to every image. Rewrite homepage hero around operator outcomes. Add secondary CTA and product tour video. Qualify form fields.SEO score 4→6. CRO score 6→8. Hero conversion rate lift.
Tier 3 Strategic rebuildPhase 3Five segment landing pages (PE home care, CCRC, AL, IL+rental retirement, for-profit SNF). Dedicated /evidence/ page. Homepage rebuilt around operator buyer. Two content hubs (grip strength biomarker, falls risk screening). Four commercial-term pages. Seven downloadable segment 1-pagers. ROI calculator.Segment pages convert at 3× the current homepage rate. Seven new commercial keywords owned.
Tier 4 CompoundingOngoingMonthly CRO test cadence. One meaningful blog post every two weeks. Quarterly AI-search citation audit. Quarterly accessibility review. One new named case study per quarter.Compounding organic growth, AI-search share of voice, champion enablement.

The Segment Page Template

Every segment page follows the same structure so production is fast and consistent: segment-specific outcome headline, "work to be done" framed in the buyer's language, "how Able Care helps" mapped to the five value pillars, proof (stats, named logo, segment-specific quote), ease of rollout and integrations, primary CTA ("Book a demo for [segment]") and secondary CTA ("Download the [segment] 1-pager").

!
The single highest-leverage change: five segment landing pages, built to one template, with one named customer outcome and one segment-specific CTA each. This single piece of work converts the sales motion from "send them to the homepage and hope" into "send them to a page written for them."

SEO Audit

This section reproduces the full March 2026 SEO audit of able-care.co. The site scores 4/10. The technical foundation is not broken, it is poorly configured, and the commercial pages that matter most to the 2026 strategy are effectively invisible to search.

Headline Findings

!
Overall score: 4/10. Six structural issues are suppressing organic performance today. None are expensive to fix. Most are mechanical. Together they are the single largest unlock on the site before any new content is written.

1. Zero Server-Side Caching

Every page on able-care.co currently returns no-store, no-cache headers. This means the browser and any intermediate CDN must re-fetch the page from origin on every request. The practical effects are three-fold: Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, TTFB) all suffer because nothing is served from the edge; Google's crawl budget is wasted refetching pages it has already seen, which compounds indexation problems; and the site feels slow to human visitors, which feeds both bounce rate and ranking. The fix is a LiteSpeed or Hostinger cache configuration change. Static assets should be cached for months, HTML for at least 24 hours. This is the lowest-effort highest-payoff fix on the site.

2. Zero Schema Markup Anywhere on the Site

There is no Organization, Product, Article, FAQ, BreadcrumbList, or WebSite schema anywhere on able-care.co. This eliminates any chance of rich snippets in SERPs, removes Able Care from Google's knowledge graph entirely, and (critically in 2026) makes the site almost invisible to LLM-based search. ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude, and Google AI Overviews preferentially cite pages with clear structured data because structured data is how they verify entity relationships. A basic site-wide schema rollout (Organization on the homepage, Article on every blog post, Product on the Solution pages, FAQ where applicable) is a one-day job with disproportionate payoff across both traditional and AI search.

3. Missing H1 Tags on High-Value Commercial Pages

The About page, the Solutions → Grip Strength page, and the Solutions → Able Assess page all ship without an H1. The Resources page has a truncated H1 that is effectively unusable. These are the four most commercially important pages on the site. Google uses H1 as a primary signal of what a page is about, and its absence on the Solution pages is the single biggest reason those pages do not rank for any commercial falls-screening keywords. The fix is adding one tag per page. It is a trivial change with a material impact.

4. 60+ Images With Empty Alt Text on the Homepage Alone

Every trust logo (Harvard, Johns Hopkins, Oxford, Mt Sinai, MD Anderson, Mass General), every certification badge (FDA, CE, ISO 27001, ISO 13485, HIPAA, GDPR), every product screenshot, and every hero image ships with an empty alt attribute. These are free ranking signals being thrown away, and the loss is disproportionately felt on exactly the images that would most support Able Care's credibility narrative. The fix is a two-hour copywriting pass. The SEO and accessibility payoff is permanent.

5. Severe Indexation Gap (~36 Sitemapped URLs vs ~7 Indexed)

The Yoast sitemap lists approximately 36 URLs. A site:able-care.co query in Google returns approximately 7. The gap is large enough that something structural is wrong. Likely causes include noindex tags left over from staging, canonical conflicts between the main site and the resources.able-care.co subdomain, thin or duplicate content being filtered by Google's quality algorithms, crawl blocks from the cache-header issue above, or an orphan pages problem where pages are not internally linked. The single most urgent SEO action on this site is to open Search Console, identify which pages are excluded and why, and fix the underlying cause. This is potentially the largest single unlock on the site — possibly 4× the current indexed footprint recovered with no new content.

6. No Pillar or Hub Structure for Topical Authority

The blog contains eight "Grip Strength and [condition] — The New Essential Biomarker" posts (diabetes, cardiovascular disease, cancer, sarcopenia, and so on) plus a hand-dynamometer guide. Each is a standalone post. None are linked into a central pillar page. This diffuses the topical authority that Google rewards for cluster-based content. Two pillar pages would consolidate the existing authority: a "Grip Strength as a Health Biomarker" hub linking to all eight condition posts, and a "Falls Risk Screening: The Complete Guide" hub linking to the TUG, gait speed, chair stand, and Able Assess content. Both can be structured for AI-search citation at the same time (clear definitions, sourced statistics, FAQ schema, named clinical authors).

Secondary Findings

The following issues are smaller individually but collectively shape the site's SEO posture and should be addressed as part of the hygiene pass:

  • Missing meta descriptions on four pages, with no canonical tags on the same four. Each is a two-minute fix.
  • Title tags describe the product, not the query. "Grip Strength – Able Care" should be "Grip Strength Assessment for Clinical Teams | Able Care" — category + benefit, not just product name.
  • TUG test cannibalisation. Two URLs target the same query (/timed-up-and-go-test/ and /blog/timed-up-and-go-test-tug-test/). Consolidate or differentiate with explicit internal linking.
  • Eight near-identical "Grip Strength and [condition]" titles. Same content pattern but query-aligned titles per condition.
  • The resources.able-care.co subdomain is leaking authority. It appears in search results showing an error page. A 301 redirect to the main site recaptures the link equity and removes a bad user experience from SERPs.
  • No hreflang implementation despite the site carrying both US and UK content. Confuses Google about which audience to serve and dilutes both.
  • No internal search for a site with 25+ blog posts, which hurts engagement and dwell time.
  • No XML sitemap for images or video, which matters for the image-rich clinical content.

Unclaimed Commercial Keywords

Three commercial-intent phrases have almost no competition from commercial landing pages. The competition for these queries is currently dominated by CDC, MedlinePlus, and clinical journals, none of which are selling anything. Able Care could own this category with a modest content effort:

  • "digital falls screening" — zero commercial landing pages. A single 1,500-word page could rank.
  • "fall prevention technology" — category term, thin commercial competition.
  • "STEADI falls screening" — named after the CDC framework, high buyer intent, zero commercial pages.
  • "SAFE Act falls screening" — newly relevant due to federal legislation, zero competition, buying season starting.
  • Segment long-tails — "falls prevention for home care", "falls screening for assisted living", "falls risk assessment home health". High commercial intent, almost no competition in the landing-page category.

SEO Score Components

ComponentScoreReasoning
Technical foundation3/10No caching, no schema, indexation gap
On-page optimisation3/10Missing H1s, empty alt text, weak title tags
Content depth7/1025 blog posts, clinical rigour, real topical seeds
Topical authority4/10Good raw material, no pillar structure
Commercial targeting2/10Zero commercial landing pages; category is unclaimed
AI-search readiness2/10No schema, no FAQ structure, not citation-ready
Overall4/10Honest diagnosis, fully recoverable with focused hygiene work
!
The single highest-leverage SEO move: fix the indexation gap. Recovering the ~29 sitemapped URLs that are not currently indexed is a 4× multiplier on the site's organic footprint with no new content. Everything else in this section is additive once that is done.

CRO Audit

This section reproduces the full March 2026 CRO audit of able-care.co. The site scores 6/10. The brand credibility is genuinely strong, but the site is optimised to educate a curious clinician rather than convert an operator buyer, and the conversion architecture offers a single high-commitment path ("Book a Demo") with no intermediate steps for the 99% of visitors who are not meeting-ready.

Headline Findings

!
Overall score: 6/10. The site does not fail any single test catastrophically. It fails a series of small-to-medium tests that collectively suppress conversion below what the credibility warrants. The fixes are cheap. The payoff is a site that converts at a rate consistent with how strong its trust signals actually are.

1. The Hero Describes the Product, Not the Outcome

The current hero reads "Standardized and Scalable Falls Risk Screening." It is accurate. It is also a description of the product, not the reason any buyer would care. A PE operator reading that headline has to translate "standardised and scalable" into "this might protect our NOI," and they will not do that translation on their own. The outcome-led alternative is something like "Cut preventable falls and the costs that come with them with a 5-minute screening any staff member can run." The difference is not cosmetic. The first version sells to a clinician. The second sells to the person with the budget. The 2026 strategy is selling to the second person.

2. Single High-Commitment Conversion Path

"Book a Demo" is the only way to engage with Able Care from the website. There is no secondary, lower-commitment path. Visitors who are not meeting-ready (which is 95%+ of site traffic on any B2B SaaS site) simply leave with no downstream hook. A secondary CTA — "Watch the 2-minute product tour," "Download the Home Care 1-pager," "See a sample report" — widens top-of-funnel capture from the current ~1% (demo-ready) to a realistic 8–15% (content-ready). That is an order-of-magnitude improvement in lead volume with no change to the sales motion.

3. No Pricing Signal At All

The site says nothing about price. In B2B healthcare, silence on price is not neutral — it is read as "too expensive to show." Corporate procurement teams disqualify vendors on exactly this signal before a demo is ever booked. The fix is not to publish a detailed price list; it is to publish a directional signal. "Starting from $X per site per month, contact us for a detailed quote" converts better than silence because it tells the buyer Able Care is in their range. Pricing pages in general are one of the highest-traffic pages on any B2B SaaS site and their absence is a known conversion drag.

4. No Product Demo Video

There is no video of the product in action anywhere on the site. For a sensor-plus-software product, video is the single highest engagement lever on the page. A 60–90 second clip showing a non-clinical staff member running the four measurements (grip, TUG, chair stand, gait) in five minutes would do more to communicate "easy to roll out" than any amount of copy. Video also lifts time-on-page, which feeds ranking, which compounds. The current absence is a large unforced error.

5. Outcome Proof Is Missing

No customer case studies. No named operator outcomes. No "reduced falls by X% in Y months" figures. No ROI numbers. For a product whose entire positioning rests on being data-driven, the absence of outcome data on the marketing site is a credibility hole. This is the single biggest CRO lever on the site. Even one named case study with one hard number would materially lift demo conversion rate. The commercial work behind this (interviewing a happy customer, getting approval for a quote and a number) is urgent regardless of any website work.

6. Trust Content Buried in Carousels

The trust, validation, regulatory, and security content — the genuinely elite material that should be front and centre — is currently distributed across four carousel slides that most visitors never click through. Carousel engagement rates are consistently under 10%. This means the Harvard, Johns Hopkins, and Oxford logos, and the FDA, ISO, and HIPAA badges, are being seen by a minority of visitors. Un-stacking the carousel into a scannable grid puts the credibility layer on screen for everyone.

Secondary Findings

  • Contact form has no qualifying fields. Organization type, role, number of sites or residents are not captured. Every demo request arrives un-qualified, wasting Paul and Dani's time and making the BDR handoff noisier than it needs to be.
  • Form submission uses a generic thank-you page with no onward journey (no calendar link, no PDF download, no "while you wait, here is our most-read blog post"). Every form submission is a missed nurture opportunity.
  • No exit-intent capture. Visitors who are about to leave are not offered anything (one-pager download, newsletter signup, "we'll email you the ROI calculator").
  • No live chat or scheduling widget. Calendly-style instant booking converts at 2–3× the rate of form-then-wait.
  • Navigation is product-first, not buyer-first. The main nav shows "Grip Strength" and "Able Assess" — product names. A buyer-first nav would lead with "Home Care," "Senior Living," "CCRC," "Evidence," "Pricing."
  • No social proof near the CTA. The "Book a Demo" button has no testimonial, logo row, or stat next to it. Social proof within the conversion element itself is one of the most reliably replicated CRO findings in the literature.
  • Mobile experience is functional but not optimised. The hero stacks awkwardly, the carousels do not work well on touch, and the primary CTA falls below the fold on most phones.
  • Blog CTAs are generic. Every blog post ends with the same "Book a Demo" button. Contextual CTAs matched to the post topic (a TUG-test post should link to the TUG segment page, a CCRC post should link to the CCRC landing page) convert materially better.

CRO Score Components

ComponentScoreReasoning
Brand and trust signals9/10Elite logos, real regulatory posture, credible thought leader
Value proposition clarity5/10Clinician-first framing, no operator business case
Conversion architecture4/10Single high-commitment CTA, no nurture layer
Outcome proof and evidence3/10Zero named case studies, no hard numbers
Pricing signal2/10Complete silence is read as "too expensive"
Form and nurture UX5/10Forms work but capture no qualifying data
Mobile experience6/10Functional, not optimised
Overall6/10Strong raw material, thin conversion engineering

Test Backlog

Recommended test cadence once the foundation fixes are in: one A/B test per month, ranked by expected impact.

TestHypothesisExpected impact
Outcome-led hero vs product-led heroOutcome framing lifts PE and operator engagementHigh
Dual CTA vs single "Book a Demo"Low-commitment secondary captures 5–10× more visitors into the funnelVery high
Video hero vs static heroVideo lifts engagement and time-on-pageHigh
Calendly instant booking vs form-then-emailInstant booking converts at 2–3× form rateHigh
Pricing signal vs silenceDirectional price range lifts qualified demosMedium
Un-stacked trust grid vs carouselVisible credibility lifts early-funnel trustMedium
Qualifying form fields vs minimal formBetter lead quality outweighs slight volume dropMedium
Contextual blog CTAs vs generic CTATopic-matched CTAs lift blog-to-demo conversionMedium
!
The single highest-leverage CRO move: add one named customer outcome with a hard number to the homepage. Everything else in this section is a percentage lift. That single addition changes the buyer's mental category from "maybe" to "proven." The commercial work to source it is urgent and is the bottleneck for most of the other recommendations here.

The Conversion Funnel

The 2026 strategy is sales-led, which means the team has a working Stage 4 (Convert) but is under-supplied at every other stage of the funnel. The point of this section is to name the whole funnel, show which plan activities feed which stage, and then model what happens to revenue when conversion at each stage is lifted even modestly. The underlying insight is that small improvements at multiple stages compound multiplicatively, not additively. That compounding is the single strongest argument for investing in a full-funnel marketing function rather than adding more BDRs.

The Five Stages

Activities That Feed Each Stage

StageJobActivities from the planKPI
1. AttractFill the top of the funnel with the right buyers15 Inbound content engine · 16 GEO / AI-search · 17 Market intel briefing · 13 LinkedIn for Paul and Dani · 14 Partner and connector marketing · 18 Events engineImpressions, ranked keywords, AI-search citations
2. EngageEarn the click and hold attention4 Segment 1-pager factory · 5 Segment landing pages · 6 Segment KB notes · 8 Case study production line · 3 Persona cardsTime on page, scroll depth, soft-conversion rate
3. QualifySeparate buyers from browsers and warm the lead9 BDR enablement pack · 10 Account-specific outbound briefs · 7 Testimonial pipeline agentMarketing qualified leads, BDR accept rate
4. ConvertClose the deal and handoff to onboarding11 RFP and procurement agent · 12 Sales conversation intelligence loop · 19 Onboarding content libraryDemo-to-opportunity rate, close rate, time-to-first-use
5. Retain and expandGrow the account and generate advocacy8 Case study production line · 17 Market intel briefing · 20 Strategy support and reporting packNet revenue retention, referred opportunities, NPS
FoundationFeeds every stage with the same source of truth1 Living knowledge base · 2 Claims libraryCoverage of segments, currency of claims

The Compounding Model: Why Small Lifts Matter So Much

The funnel is multiplicative, not additive. A 25% improvement at one stage is good. A 25% improvement at every stage is transformational, because each improvement operates on the output of the previous one. This is the mathematical argument for investing in a full-funnel marketing function rather than adding more headcount at the Convert stage, where the maths already runs against you.

The table below uses illustrative numbers to make the point. Assume Able Care drives 10,000 monthly visitors to the site (a realistic target after the content and GEO programmes), and assume an average annual contract value of $25,000 per closed deal. The baseline column uses conversion rates consistent with the current state of the site. The "10% lift per stage" column applies a modest 10% improvement to each stage. The "25% lift per stage" column applies a 25% improvement. The final row shows the annual revenue impact.

StageBaseline rateBaseline volume+10% per stage+25% per stage
Monthly visitors (Attract)—10,00011,00012,500
Engaged visitors5%500605 (5.5%)781 (6.25%)
Marketing qualified leads10%5067 (11%)98 (12.5%)
Sales qualified opportunities40%2029 (44%)49 (50%)
Closed deals20%4.06.4 (22%)12.2 (25%)
Deals per month—4.06.412.2
Annual revenue @ $25k ACV—$1.20M$1.92M (+60%)$3.66M (+205%)
!
The compounding headline: a 10% improvement at each stage delivers a 60% revenue lift. A 25% improvement at each stage more than triples revenue. Neither requires a breakthrough at any single point, only steady pressure at every stage. This is only possible with a full-funnel marketing function. Adding BDRs lifts Stage 3 and Stage 4 alone and misses the compounding.

Where Each Activity Bites Hardest

Not every plan activity moves every stage equally. The table below shows which stage each of the twenty activities has the biggest effect on, and why it matters. This is how the investment case is made to Paul and Dani when they ask "which of these twenty do we do first?". The answer depends on which stage is most broken today. On the current state of the Able Care site, Stages 1 and 2 are the biggest constraints, which is why the Segment Content Engine and the Inbound workflow appear so often below.

ActivityPrimary stageSecondary stageModelled impact
1 Living knowledge baseFoundationAllEnabler. No direct lift on its own; unlocks everything else.
2 Claims libraryFoundationQualify, ConvertEnabler. Lifts close rate by removing procurement friction.
3 Persona cardsEngageQualifyLifts time on page and BDR accept rate.
4 Segment 1-pagersEngageQualifySoft-conversion lift of 3–5× where deployed.
5 Segment landing pagesEngageAttractHighest single lift on Stages 1 and 2 combined.
6 Segment KB notesFoundationEngageEnabler for 4 and 5.
7 Testimonial pipelineQualifyConvertLifts close rate 10–25% per named case study on the page.
8 Case study productionEngageConvert, RetainLifts both demo conversion and expansion pipeline.
9 BDR enablement packQualifyConvertLifts BDR accept rate and meeting book rate.
10 Account briefsQualifyConvertLifts top-down outreach response rate 3–5×.
11 RFP agentConvert—Lifts close rate on enterprise deals materially.
12 Sales conversation intelConvertFoundationCloses the loop that keeps every other activity sharp.
13 LinkedIn content engineAttractEngageWarms accounts before outbound; lifts Stage 1 and 3 both.
14 Partner marketingAttractQualifyCheapest way to reach otherwise-unreachable accounts.
15 Inbound content engineAttractEngageThe long-game compounder for Stage 1.
16 GEO / AI-searchAttractEngageThe next-frontier Stage 1 channel.
17 Market intel briefingAttractRetainFeeds Stage 1 content and Stage 5 expansion signals.
18 Events engineAttractQualifyHigh-quality Stage 1 source with Stage 3 velocity.
19 Onboarding contentConvertRetainShortens time-to-value and lifts NRR.
20 Reporting packAll—Meta. Tells leadership what is working and where to invest next.
!
The strategic read: today, every point of failure on the Able Care funnel is upstream of the salesperson. The team has invested in the Convert stage (Paul, Dani, BDRs, a working product) but has no Attract layer, almost no Engage layer, and a thin Qualify layer. This is why the strategy asks marketing for 1-pagers and inbound plans in the first place — the team can feel the shape of the gap even if the funnel has not been named explicitly. Naming it, as this section does, makes the investment case unambiguous.

AI Initiatives — Twelve Plays

The 2026 strategy is ambitious relative to the size of the team. Seven segment 1-pagers, a content cadence, per-account outbound briefs, conversation intelligence, an RFP engine, and an onboarding assistant are not realistic at current manual velocity. AI is the only way to hit that surface area without hiring a content team. This section lays out twelve initiatives, each tied to a commercial lever from the strategy document rather than to "AI for AI's sake."

Marketing Acceleration

1. Segment content factory

High impactLow effort

A Claude workflow that takes the master value framing and generates tailored 1-pagers, email sequences, LinkedIn posts, and landing page copy per segment and persona. Directly solves the "two 1-pagers ASAP" ask and scales to the remaining five segments within weeks.

2. Claims guardrail library

High impactLow effort

Every AI draft passes through a structured fact-check prompt that cross-references an approved claims library. Stops hallucinated statistics from reaching regulated buyers. Non-negotiable for a health-tech brand.

3. Brand-voice assistant

Medium impactLow effort

A custom GPT trained on the Able Care brand guidelines that every team member uses for drafting. Eliminates voice drift as output volume grows 5×.

4. Case study production pipeline

High impactLow effort

AI-assisted transcription and drafting on champion interviews. Turns a 30-minute customer conversation into a brand-voiced testimonial, quote card, and long-form case study within a day.

Sales Acceleration

5. Account intelligence briefs

High impactMedium effort

Before every Paul or Dani top-down outreach, an AI agent produces a one-page brief: corporate structure, PE sponsor thesis, recent M&A, likely KPIs, relevant value-framing lines, recommended opening hook. Turns persona tables into live per-account ammunition.

6. Conversation intelligence

High impactMedium effort

Discovery calls through a recording plus summarisation pipeline to auto-populate the CRM, surface objections, and feed a library of real-world talk tracks per segment. Becomes institutional memory over time.

7. MCO vendor-questionnaire engine

Medium impactMedium effort

A living knowledge base that drafts MLTSS preferred-vendor-list responses in hours rather than weeks. Real moat for a small team trying to win 24-state HCBS contracts.

8. Pipeline scoring

Medium impactLow effort

Lightweight LLM layer over the CRM that ranks deals by fit against the PE-backed, multi-site corporate ICP. Focuses Paul and Dani's scarce top-down bandwidth.

Customer & Product-Adjacent

9. In-product onboarding assistant

Medium impactHigh effort

A Claude-powered assistant inside the Able Assess portal answering "how do I run a screening?", "how do I interpret this score?", "what does Section GG require?". Reduces the onboarding burden the strategy explicitly calls out.

10. Smart care-plan narratives

High impactHigh effort

Combines an Able Assess score with AI-generated, CDC-aligned narrative recommendations a non-clinical staff member can hand to families or care managers. Differentiation at the product layer, not the marketing layer.

11. Family engagement summaries

Medium impactMedium effort

Plain-language monthly progress notes ("Here's how Mom's mobility has changed") generated from screening history. Reinforces the "maintain independence" value prop and drives resident retention.

12. Portfolio falls-risk dashboard

High impactHigh effort

An AI rollup of individual screenings into portfolio-level insights for PE operators and corporate COOs. Turns Able Assess from a per-resident tool into an enterprise analytics product. Natural ACV expansion path.

Phased Sequencing

PhaseInitiativesDeliverable
Phase 11, 2, 3Brand-voice assistant live, claims guardrail library operational, two urgent 1-pagers shipped, draft of remaining five segment 1-pagers.
Phase 24, 5, 8Account briefs for top 50 PE home care and top 25 corporate senior living targets. Personalised outbound sequences live. First three case studies in production.
Phase 36, 7, 11Conversation intelligence rollout. MCO questionnaire knowledge base seeded. Family engagement summary prototype in pilot with one customer.
!
Risk and guardrail: healthcare-adjacent positioning means hallucinated clinical claims are the single biggest AI risk. Every AI output touching efficacy, reimbursement, or regulatory language passes through the claims library before publication. PHI never enters a third-party LLM without a BAA in place. The brand-voice assistant and the review checklist address brand drift as a secondary risk.

SEO & GEO

Strategic SEO

Two opportunities dominate the SEO picture for Able Care. The first is an indexation recovery; the second is a commercial-keyword land grab.

Indexation recovery

The SEO audit found ~36 URLs in the sitemap but only ~7 in Google's index. That is a red flag severe enough to be the single largest organic unlock on the site before any new content is written. Week one should include a Search Console review of the excluded pages and the underlying cause (noindex tags, canonical conflicts, thin content, crawl blocks, or cache headers). Once the gap is understood, the fix is usually mechanical.

Commercial-keyword land grab

Several high-commercial-intent terms are currently unclaimed at the commercial-landing-page level. Competition on these terms is dominated by CDC pages, MedlinePlus, and clinical journal summaries — not commercial vendors. A focused set of four pages claims ground no-one else is fighting for:

Target keyword / phraseIntentRecommended page
digital falls screeningCommercial/digital-falls-screening/
fall prevention technologyCommercial / research/fall-prevention-technology/
STEADI falls screeningClinical / commercial bridge/steadi-falls-screening/
SAFE Act falls screeningRegulatory / commercial/safe-act-falls-screening/
falls prevention for home careSegment + commercial/solutions/home-care/
falls screening for assisted livingSegment + commercial/solutions/assisted-living/
falls risk assessment home healthSegment + commercial/solutions/home-health/

Content hub consolidation

The existing 25-post content library is strong but diffuse. Eight "Grip Strength and [condition]" posts share nearly identical titles and cannibalise each other. Two hub pages resolve this: one on grip strength as a health biomarker (consolidating the eight condition posts and the hand dynamometer guide), and one on falls risk screening as a complete guide (consolidating TUG, gait speed, chair stand, and Able Assess content). Both hubs should be structured for AI citation (clear definitions near the top, FAQ section with FAQ schema, named author with clinical credentials).

Technical SEO

Technical findings (collapsible detail)

Caching. Every page currently returns no-store, no-cache. This is suppressing Core Web Vitals and crawl efficiency. Configure LiteSpeed / Hostinger so static assets are cached for months and HTML for at least 24 hours.

Schema markup. Zero schema anywhere on the site. Even a basic rollout (Organization on homepage, Article on every blog post, Product on Solution pages, FAQ where applicable, BreadcrumbList site-wide) meaningfully improves rich-snippet eligibility and AI-search citation probability.

H1 hygiene. About, Solutions → Grip Strength, and Solutions → Able Assess are missing H1s. Resources has a truncated H1. These are the most commercially important pages on the site and they are structurally invisible to search.

Image alt text. 60+ images on the homepage alone have empty alt attributes, including every elite trust logo. This is two hours of work with disproportionate SEO and accessibility upside.

Title tags. Every title should include a buyer-relevant benefit or category term, not just the product name. "Grip Strength – Able Care" should become "Grip Strength Assessment for Clinical Teams | Able Care."

Meta descriptions and canonicals. Four pages lack meta descriptions; the same four lack canonicals. Both are one-hour fixes.

Cannibalisation. /timed-up-and-go-test/ and /blog/timed-up-and-go-test-tug-test/ should be consolidated or differentiated with explicit canonicalisation and cross-linking.

Subdomain split. resources.able-care.co is splitting domain authority. 301-redirect to the main site and canonicalise the content.

GEO (Generative Engine Optimisation)

A non-trivial share of 2026 B2B healthcare research now starts in ChatGPT, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews, and Claude. The current site is not structured to be cited by LLMs: no schema, thin Q&A structure, no "who / what / for whom / how much / what outcomes" at the top of pages. Able Care can punch above its weight in AI search with relatively little effort because the authoritative signals (Harvard, Johns Hopkins, CDC alignment, regulatory posture) are already in place and just need to be surfaced in structured form.

The recommended programme: test 15 buyer-language queries monthly across ChatGPT, Claude, and Perplexity ("best falls risk assessment for assisted living", "how do PE-backed home care operators reduce liability", "what is SAFE Act falls screening"), record which brands are cited, and publish one "definitive answer" page per quarter aimed specifically at closing the biggest gap. This is a direct extension of AI initiative #11 in the previous section.

SWOT Summary

Strengths

  • Academic credential stack (Harvard, Johns Hopkins, Oxford, Mount Sinai, MD Anderson, Mass General) that a competitor would need years to replicate
  • Comprehensive regulatory posture: FDA, CE, ISO 27001, ISO 13485, IEC 60601, HIPAA, GDPR
  • Only player bundling grip + TUG + CTS + gait in a single five-minute workflow
  • Non-clinical usability — the single biggest PMF lever for home care
  • 2022 World Guidelines alignment and CDC STEADI anchoring
  • A credible longevity-adjacent thought-leadership angle
  • A deep 25-post content library with real clinical references

Weaknesses

  • Zero buyer-segment landing pages on the website
  • Clinician-first messaging on an operator-first buyer
  • Single high-commitment "Book a Demo" CTA with no downstream nurture hook
  • No evidence layer for champions — nothing to forward internally
  • Severe technical hygiene issues (no caching, no schema, missing H1s, missing alt text, indexation gap)
  • No published customer outcome numbers despite a regulated-buyer audience that asks for them
  • No pricing posture — silence reads as "expensive"
  • No accessibility story for an audience designing services for older adults

Opportunities

  • PE consolidation of home care concentrates the buyer universe — one corporate win propagates across dozens of franchises
  • Four unclaimed commercial keywords with no vendor competition at the landing-page layer
  • The top-right quadrant of the perceptual map (staff-wide usable + proactive) is vacant
  • State-level Medicaid MLTSS vendor lists in 24 states — a dedicated questionnaire engine opens a moat
  • AI-search visibility (ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity) is an open frontier where academic credentials convert well
  • SAFE Act and Section GG regulatory pressure are moving falls screening toward compliance-adjacent
  • A credible ROI calculator doubles as a sales tool and a lead magnet

Threats

  • SafelyYou and Linus Health are better funded and can outspend Able Care on brand and events
  • Consumer PERS brands (Lifeline, ADT, Medical Guardian) may confuse operator buyers who conflate detection with screening
  • Hallucinated clinical claims in AI-generated content are a real reputational and regulatory risk
  • High staff turnover in home care means any friction in onboarding is amplified across every site
  • PE ownership changes at target accounts reset sales cycles
  • AI-search ecosystem is still volatile — citation patterns can shift without warning

Strategic Implications

The audit points to a single high-confidence conclusion: Able Care's biggest bottleneck in 2026 is not product, credentials, or the quality of the strategy. It is the execution surface between the strategy and the buyer. Every recommendation in this report is a different way of widening that surface. The summary below translates those recommendations into three phases, three leadership decisions, and a horizon view.

The Phased Sprint

Phase 1

Unblock Discovery

  • Turn on caching and resolve the indexation gap
  • Verify Search Console and GA4
  • Ship site-wide schema and fix missing H1s and alt text
  • Resolve the resources.able-care.co subdomain split
  • Launch the brand-voice assistant and claims library
  • Ship the two urgent segment 1-pagers

Outcome: crawl efficiency restored, measurement backbone in place, first segment assets shipped.

Phase 2

Rebuild and Launch

  • Rewrite the homepage around the operator buyer
  • Launch three segment pages (PE home care, CCRC, corporate AL)
  • Stand up the AI account-intelligence brief pipeline
  • Ship the first three case studies from the production line
  • Add the secondary CTA and embed the product tour video

Outcome: SEO score 4→6, CRO score 6→8, qualified pipeline lift.

Phase 3

Compound

  • Launch the remaining segment pages (rental IL/AL, for-profit SNF)
  • Ship the /evidence/ page and two content hubs
  • Publish the four commercial-keyword landing pages
  • Roll out conversation intelligence to discovery calls
  • Seed the MCO vendor-questionnaire knowledge base
  • Start the monthly GEO citation audit

Outcome: compounding organic growth, expansion pipeline, AI-search share of voice.

Three Decisions Leadership Needs to Make

$

Pricing Posture

Publish a starting-from figure, publish a range, or stay silent. This affects the entire funnel and should be decided before the homepage rewrite, not after.

◎

US vs UK Positioning

The 2026 strategy is US-first but the current site is hreflang-ambiguous. Commit to a /us/ and /uk/ split or pick one and redirect the other.

★

First Named Customer

The evidence layer cannot ship without one named customer willing to be quoted with a number. Treat this as the single most urgent customer-success task of the quarter.

The Horizon

If the phased sprint lands, Able Care shifts from being a credible niche brand into being the default answer when a PE operator asks "what is our falls-prevention stack?" The four outcomes below are the specific shape of that shift.

7–10 Commercial keywords owned that competitors are not fighting for
3–5× Lift in Paul and Dani's top-down outreach response rate
1 / 2 wks Content factory producing a meaningful piece on autopilot
ACV ↑ Expansion path from per-resident to per-portfolio pricing
!
Bottom line: Able Care has already done the hard parts. The credentials are real, the product is real, the market is real, the strategy is sound. The remaining work is execution surface, and it is buildable in three phases. This audit recommends sprinting.

Marketing Services Plan

This section pulls together the twenty activities we believe Able Care needs from an AI marketing specialist, and organises them into logical workflows rather than a flat to-do list. It is written to sit alongside the Falls Prevention Commercialization Strategy 2026 and support it directly. Every activity is tied to a specific objective from that document. No dates are attached. Sequencing is described in terms of dependencies, so the work can slot into whatever working pattern is agreed.

How to read this plan

The twenty activities cluster naturally into seven workflows. Each workflow is a small system: one activity produces an asset, which feeds the next activity, which feeds the next. A single piece of research (for example, a CCRC buyer interview) can cascade through a workflow and produce a segment page, a one-pager, a LinkedIn post, a BDR email template, and an entry in the knowledge base without ever being re-researched. That compounding leverage is the reason these activities belong in workflows rather than as isolated deliverables.

Workflow 1: Foundation — The Corporate Brain

Strategic objective: the entire 2026 plan. The strategy describes fifteen to twenty market segments, seven priority sub-segments, and multiple buyer personas. None of the marketing motions it asks for can be produced consistently without a shared source of truth.

Logical flow: build the knowledge base, populate it with claims and personas, and then treat it as the single input for every downstream activity. The knowledge base is best thought of as a corporate brain — every segment, competitor, persona, proof point, objection, and customer quote as an interlinked note. Each note is queryable by AI. Each is reachable from every other. The graph below is a real snapshot of the Able Care knowledge base already under construction: the dark central node is the Map of Content, and every lighter node is a note about a segment, competitor, framework, or piece of evidence that will feed the downstream workflows.

1. Living knowledge base (Obsidian vault)

Every competitor, segment, persona, objection, proof point and customer quote as interlinked notes, queryable by AI, growing with every interview and sales call.

Tie: supports the Executive Summary's "this document will be live."

2. Claims library with evidence links

Every statistic, outcome and clinical claim with its source, strength of evidence and approved usage. Doubles as procurement response fuel and AI-search structured content.

Tie: underwrites the value framing tables for every segment in the strategy.

3. Persona cards for the six core buyers

ED, DON, PE operating partner, franchise owner, corporate VP Clinical, CCRC CEO. Pains, buying criteria, objections, proof they respond to. Checked against by every downstream asset.

Tie: the strategy's segment value framing assumes a clear buyer picture; these cards make it explicit.

Workflow 2: Segment Content Engine

Strategic objective: "Marketing: work with Dani to create 1-2 pagers for each market segment with messaging on value props," plus the seven priority sub-segments in the Executive Summary.

Logical flow: design one reusable segment content model, then spin each segment through it. The same facts produce a 1-pager, a landing page, and outbound snippets. Update the model once, everything regenerates.

4. Segment 1-pager factory

Seven branded PDFs (PE home care, independent multi-site, CCRC, IL, AL, SNF, HHA) generated from a shared content model and the claims library.

Tie: direct answer to the Marketing work-to-be-done column.

5. Segment landing pages

A /solutions/ path with a page for each Tier 1 segment. Fixes the gap where BDRs send PE CFOs to a homepage that requires them to translate "grip strength" into "NOI lever" on their own.

Tie: every outbound motion in the strategy lands on a page that does not yet exist per segment.

6. Segment notes in the knowledge base

A matching KB note for every segment chapter in the strategy. Mirrors the strategy's value framing and adds the evidence, personas and messaging that generate the 1-pagers and pages.

Tie: gives the strategy a marketing-side counterpart so no line item is unsupported.

Workflow 3: Proof Engine

Strategic objective: "Connect with key customer champions to develop testimonials."

Logical flow: interview happy customers once, then atomise each interview into every asset type the downstream workflows need.

7. Testimonial pipeline agent

Identifies champions in the CRM, drafts segment-specific interview questions, summarises transcripts, produces quote candidates ready for approval.

Tie: operationalises the testimonial ask in the strategy.

8. Case study production line

Three-tier output per interview: hero case study PDF, segment-specific one-pager update, LinkedIn carousel. One interview, three assets, one source.

Tie: named case studies are the single most requested asset by enterprise senior living and PE-backed buyers in the strategy.

Workflow 4: Sales Enablement

Strategic objective: "BDRs cold call targets bottom-up, Dani to review scripts," "Dani and Paul seek introductions to corporate HQs," "Opp to Close managed by Paul and Dani."

Logical flow: package upstream assets into formats the sales team can use live in a deal, then feed what happens in those deals back into the knowledge base.

9. BDR enablement pack

Cold-call scripts, email sequences, objection cards, and an "if they say X send Y" matrix per segment, grounded in the persona cards.

Tie: directly supports "Dani to review scripts."

10. Account-specific outbound briefs

An agent takes a named target from the BD list and produces a two-page brief: recent news, likely pains, relevant proof, recommended opener. Used before every Paul or Dani top-down outreach.

Tie: supports "work with Jack on targeting, LinkedIn research and reach out."

11. RFP and procurement response agent

Ingests an RFP or security questionnaire and drafts responses from the KB and claims library. Every enterprise deal hits one.

Tie: unblocks the "Opp to Close" column owned by Paul and Dani.

12. Sales conversation intelligence loop

Sales calls summarised, tagged for objections and buying signals, patterns fed back into the KB, claims library and persona cards. Without this loop the KB becomes stale within a quarter.

Tie: operationalises "this document will be live."

Workflow 5: Outbound and Social

Strategic objective: "Utilize LinkedIn for connections," "seek introductions via partners and connectors," "identify key connectors to work with to develop relationships in target segments."

Logical flow: make Paul and Dani visible to their buyers before outbound lands, so cold touches arrive warmer. Build the partner and connector layer on top of that visibility.

13. LinkedIn content engine for Paul and Dani

Weekly ghostwritten posts grounded in the KB, tuned to each founder's voice, sequenced to surface in front of BD-list target accounts. Reuses Segment and Proof Engine material.

Tie: makes "utilize LinkedIn for connections" compounding rather than one-off.

14. Partner and connector marketing

Co-branded content with PE operating partners, tech integrations, franchise HQs and trade associations. Each partner gets a co-created asset they are proud to share.

Tie: supports the connector workstream named in the strategy.

Workflow 6: Inbound and Discovery

Strategic objective: "Marketing: create plan for inbound lead generation."

Logical flow: the KB already contains answers to the questions buyers are typing into Google and ChatGPT. This workflow publishes those answers in the formats that modern search rewards and monitors how they perform.

15. Inbound content engine

Editorial calendar and steady production of SEO and GEO-optimised articles answering the questions operators actually ask. Grounded in the KB and claims library.

Tie: direct answer to the inbound lead generation ask.

16. GEO / AI-search visibility programme

Structured content, schema, citation-worthy claims, recurring audit of how Able Care appears in ChatGPT, Perplexity and Google AI Overviews.

Tie: protects the inbound motion as search behaviour shifts.

17. Monthly market intel briefing

Curated by an agent monitoring M&A, PE activity, regulatory changes, clinical updates and competitor news. Published internally for Paul and Dani.

Tie: supports the strategy's implicit need to stay ahead of a fast-consolidating market.

Workflow 7: Operations and Reporting

Strategic objective: "Identify target events for 2026," "explore potential to streamline onboarding and customer support processes," and the need to prove marketing is supporting the plan.

18. Events engine

Target event list, pre-event outreach, booth content, on-site lead capture, post-event nurture. Grounded in the same persona cards and segment content, so nothing is bespoke.

Tie: supports Dani's events workstream.

19. Onboarding and staff-facing content library

Short videos, quick-start guides and a staff-facing microsite so a new 1099 carer can run Able Assess on day one. Doubles as a sales proof point.

Tie: supports "streamline onboarding and customer support processes with improved resources."

20. Strategy support and reporting pack

Recurring report on inbound pipeline contribution, content performance, AI-search share of voice, case studies shipped, BD target coverage, and what the KB learned. The scorecard Paul and Dani can take to the board.

Tie: closes the loop back to the strategy's Executive Summary.

The website sits underneath all seven workflows

The website rebuild is not a twenty-first activity. It is the surface every one of the seven workflows lands on. The Segment Content Engine publishes to it, the Proof Engine publishes case studies to it, the Inbound workflow drives traffic to it, the Onboarding library lives on it, and the reporting pack measures what happens on it. Treating the website as the nerve centre of the programme is what allows one knowledge base to power seven workflows. Treating it as a separate project would break that leverage.

Sequencing, without dates

No time commitments are attached, because the working pattern has not been agreed. The sequencing logic is about dependencies rather than deadlines. Workflow 1 (Foundation) unlocks everything, has to start first, and keeps going forever. Workflows 2 (Segment Content) and 3 (Proof) can start as soon as Foundation has enough in it to generate the first segment's assets, and run in parallel after that. Workflow 4 (Sales Enablement) starts as soon as the first segment's assets exist and is the fastest ROI workflow because it plugs directly into deals already in motion. Workflow 5 (Outbound and Social) runs in parallel with 4 and strengthens as the Proof Engine produces more case studies. Workflow 6 (Inbound and Discovery) is the slowest to compound but the most valuable long-term, and should start early even when its outputs feel small, because the content backlog is what AI search rewards. Workflow 7 (Operations and Reporting) is continuous once any of the above is live.

The most important thing about the sequencing is that it is a system, not a list. Work done in one workflow feeds the next. The knowledge base grows every time a customer is interviewed, a sales call is recorded, a market report is read, or a piece of content is published. That compounding is what makes an AI-powered marketing function cheaper and faster than a traditional content team, and it is what lets a small founder-led commercial org support the ambitions of the 2026 plan.

Outbound Marketing Plan 2026

Account-based outbound programme for Able Assess, targeting US home care and senior living.
Version 0.2 (Pass 2 complete) Owner: Matt K. Sign-off: Dani Last updated: 21 April 2026
Source inputs: Falls Prevention Commercialization Strategy 2026, Market Sizing workbook, BD Target List, CDC STEADI, CMS LTSS/HCBS guidance, ZoomInfo firmographic taxonomy, Able Care Salesforce schema.

Executive Summary

This document lays out a repeatable, account-based outbound programme for Able Assess through 2026. It translates the Falls Prevention Commercialization Strategy 2026 into a working system of target lists, personalised content, and sequenced outreach, powered by ZoomInfo for data and Salesforce for orchestration. The goal is straightforward: generate qualified pipeline in the two segments with the best mix of deal size, close velocity, and repeatability, without burning domain reputation or rep bandwidth.

!
The timing window is open now. CMS scrutiny of fall-related rehospitalisation, the build-out of state Medicaid HCBS programmes, and the accelerating PE consolidation of home care and senior living have created a rare moment where clinical differentiation and operational efficiency are both being bought at the same time. Able Assess sits exactly on that intersection. We move fast or we watch competitors define the category.

Three core bets

01

Segment concentration

Two segments, seven sub-segments, roughly 40 named Tier 1 accounts. We do not chase everything. We build a motion that wins repeatedly in a small number of buyers we understand deeply.

02

Value-first engagement

Every cold touch leads with a useful asset (guide, video, interactive tool). We give the buyer something they would pay for, then earn the meeting. This is how personal-inbox outbound stays deliverable and effective.

03

Signal-triggered selling

Nobody gets called without a reason. Asset downloads, landing-page fills, return visits, and ZoomInfo intent surges all trigger SLAs into Salesforce. Reps work warm signals, not cold spreadsheets.

What success looks like by end of Q2 2026

Dimension Baseline (today) End of Q2 2026 target Leading indicator
Named Tier 1 accounts in active motion < 10 40+ Accounts with multi-thread sequence running
Lead magnet library ~3 pieces 35–45 published Asset-by-segment coverage matrix (see § Lead Magnets)
Qualified meetings per month Inconsistent 25–40 Engagement-triggered calls completed
Active sales opportunities sourced by outbound Ad hoc 60+ net-new SAL created from engaged accounts
Domain deliverability health Unmeasured >95% inbox placement Weekly seed-list deliverability test
+
Read this document in this order: the Strategy Overview explains how the system works as a whole. Segments & ICP defines who we sell to and how we find them in ZoomInfo. Messaging, Lead Magnets, and Sequences are the content and motion layer. Salesforce + ZoomInfo Ops is the system that runs it. KPIs & Reporting and the 90-Day Roadmap are how we know it is working and what we ship next.

How this plan will be built out

This is version 0.1. It is being delivered in three passes so we can lock shape early and iterate on substance. Each pass ends with a walkthrough and sign-off from Dani.

Pass 1 · now

Shape and segments

Nav, structure, Strategy Overview, and fully written Segments & ICP for both primary targets. What you are reading.

Pass 2

Substance

Messaging architecture by segment, full Lead Magnet library, Salesforce + ZoomInfo operations playbook, measurement framework.

Pass 3

Execution-ready

Every email drafted, sequences per persona, 90-day roadmap, cross-links and drill-down working end-to-end. Charts populated with baseline data.

Strategy Overview

Outbound works when it is a closed-loop system, not a stack of activities. Target definition feeds content. Content feeds sequences. Sequences feed signals. Signals feed calls. Calls feed the CRM. The CRM feeds the target definition. Every part of this plan belongs to one of those loops, and every rule below exists to protect it.

The system at a glance

01
Segment & tier
Strategy + ZoomInfo
›
02
Account & contact list
ZoomInfo » SF
›
03
Lead magnet
Guide / PDF / Video / App
›
04
Personal-inbox sequence
SF Sales Engagement
›
05
Engagement signal
Form fill / visit / intent
›
06
Warm call (24h SLA)
ZoomInfo phone + SF task
›
07
Opp in Salesforce
Pipeline stage

Eight operating principles

1

Tier accounts before you message them.

Three tiers per segment: Tier 1 is the 20–40 named accounts worth bespoke sequences and hand-built research. Tier 2 is the 100–300 accounts that get segment-level personalisation. Tier 3 is the volume layer where a good segment sequence does the work. Tier assignment is driven by deal size, strategic fit, and buying-signal strength, never by rep enthusiasm. Every account in the CRM carries a tier tag from day one.

2

Multi-thread every account.

Never run outbound into a single persona. For a PE-backed franchisor, we target the CEO, the Head of Clinical or Quality, and the Head of Innovation or IT in parallel. For senior living operators we add the COO and the regional VP of operations. Each persona gets a sequence pitched to what they care about. Single-threaded deals die the first time your one contact changes jobs.

3

Give-to-get is the stance. Always.

The best outbound from personal inboxes looks nothing like marketing. It looks like a well-informed peer sharing something useful. The cold email never asks for a meeting. It offers a guide, a tool, a short video that genuinely helps the recipient do their job. The meeting ask lives on the follow-up call, which is triggered by engagement, not by hope. This is also the cheapest insurance policy we have against regulators, unsubscribes, and spam complaints.

4

Engagement is the trigger, not opens.

Open rates are noise. Apple Mail Privacy Protection inflates them by roughly 30% globally, and filters fire them without a human ever seeing the email. Our triggers are: lead magnet downloads, landing-page form fills, repeat site visits in 72 hours, and ZoomInfo intent surges on relevant topics.
