Any of those creates a Salesforce task with a 24-hour SLA for a human call. Everything else is ignored at the rep level.

5

Deliverability is a sales problem, not an IT problem.

One careless week burns the domain for a quarter. Non-negotiable setup: SPF, DKIM, and DMARC configured correctly on every sending domain; secondary sending domains (for example firstname-ablecare.com) set up so we never risk the primary; 2–3 week warm-up before scale; hard cap of 40–50 cold sends per mailbox per day; plain text only, no marketing images or footers, no tracking pixels; list scrubbing through ZoomInfo and an email verifier on every send; real signatures; spam-word hygiene. Target reply rate above 3%, bounce rate below 2%, spam complaints under 0.1%.

6

A sequence is a story, not a series.

Seven touches across roughly three weeks, each building on the last. Touch 1: hook rooted in a problem we see in their world. Touch 2: credibility, why we are worth listening to. Touch 3: proof, a customer very like them. Touch 4: value drop, a guide or tool they can use today. Touch 5: social proof from a different angle. Touch 6: light challenge, what most teams get wrong. Touch 7: polite bow-out with a door left open. Each email is standalone. Any one of them could be the one that works.

7

Salesforce is the source of truth, ZoomInfo is the fuel.

ZoomInfo enriches accounts and contacts on create, feeds intent signals in as Salesforce tasks, and drives the reverse-IP layer (ZoomInfo WebSights) into a "warm accounts visiting the site" dashboard. Salesforce runs the engagement cadence (Sales Engagement), holds the campaign membership, tracks every disposition, and triggers the call workflow. Reps do not keep prospects in their inbox. Every touch is logged. Every engagement becomes a task. Every task is dispositioned.

8

Content is a portfolio, not a checklist.

Three asset depths per primary segment. Quick-skim: infographic, checklist, 1-pager. Medium-invest: 10–15 minute guide or a 3-minute explainer video. Deep-invest: whitepaper or an interactive tool such as a scorecard app. Each asset maps to a named persona, funnel stage, segment, and Salesforce campaign. No orphan content. No content we produced because it felt like good marketing hygiene. If we cannot name the buyer and the trigger, we do not build it.

How to read this document

Each section is designed to be readable on its own, with cross-links back to the overview. The Segments & ICP section contains a drill-down page per sub-segment; in each drill-down the order is the same so you always know where to look: market thesis, tier breakdown, named Tier 1 accounts, personas, ZoomInfo filter recipes, disqualifiers, and the "why now" regulatory or market trigger. Where a section is still being built in Pass 2 or Pass 3, a preview panel explains exactly what will land there.

⚠
Compliance posture, up front. Able Assess is sold into regulated care settings. Every claim we make in a lead magnet or email must be defensible: grounded in CDC STEADI, peer-reviewed literature, or specific customer evidence. We never imply clinical diagnosis. We never make outcome claims (“reduces falls by X%”) without a citable source. This is not just a legal cover. It is a positioning advantage in a market tired of vendor noise.

Segments & ICP

We are targeting two categories, broken into seven sub-segments, prioritised by deal repeatability, close velocity, and strategic ceiling. Home Care is category 1, with PE-backed and franchised operators as the primary target. Senior Living is category 2, with five corporate-owned sub-segments in scope. Every account in our outbound universe carries one of these tags, plus a tier. Nothing ships without both.

1.1 PE-Backed Home Care 1.2 Independent Multi-Site Home Care 2.1 Corporate CCRCs 2.2 Rental Retirement IL+AL 2.3 IL-Only Corporate 2.4 AL-Only Corporate 2.5 For-Profit SNF Corporate

How tiers work

Tier Account profile Outbound motion Account count (steady state)
Tier 1 Named strategic accounts with the scale and fit to anchor the quarter. Corporate HQs of PE-backed franchisors, top-15 senior living operators, CCRC portfolios. Hand-built multi-thread. Custom messaging. Personalised assets. Direct involvement from Paul and Dani. ~40 across both categories
Tier 2 Franchisee networks, mid-size corporate operators, multi-site independents with 5+ locations. Segment-specific sequences. Heavy use of lead magnets. BDR-led with franchise-level personalisation tokens. ~300 across both categories
Tier 3 Smaller franchise locations, single-site operators meeting size threshold, long-tail accounts. Programmatic sequences, segment-level only. Calls triggered purely by engagement signal. 1,500–2,500 across both categories

Segment size at a glance

i
Every segment drill-down below follows the same structure: market thesis, tier breakdown, named Tier 1 accounts, personas with hiring titles and LinkedIn search strings, ZoomInfo filter recipes you can paste directly into Advanced Search, disqualifiers, and the “why now” that justifies acting this quarter.

1.1 PE-Backed & Franchised Home Care

Category 1Primary targetQuick close potentialScalable via franchise model

Market thesis

Non-clinical home care has consolidated rapidly over the last decade under private equity ownership and franchise models. These operators share three properties that make them ideal for Able Assess: corporate management focused on operational efficiency and defensible differentiation; a non-clinical workforce that needs tools it can actually use; and a franchise-to-franchise expansion path that turns a single win into a network. The primary budget holder sits at the corporate level, but individual franchises can also buy, which gives us two viable entry motions (top-down and bottom-up) running in parallel.

A sale into a corporate franchisor unlocks rollout across 100–600+ locations. A sale into a single franchise is a useful foothold that can be repeated across the network, and the corporate team will often take the call once three or four franchises are using us. Either way, the centre of gravity is the corporate HQ.

♥
Why this segment first. Corporate-level decision making plus a franchise multiplier gives us the shortest path from first meeting to multi-site ARR. The operating model already values standardised, low-training tools, and Able Assess is built for exactly that.

Tier breakdown

TierProfileEstimated account countTypical ACV range
Tier 1Corporate HQ of PE-backed or franchised home care operators. Decision for portfolio-wide rollout.~15 corporate HQs$150k–$800k pilot, $1m+ network
Tier 2Multi-location franchisees (5+ locations) inside top operators. Regional master franchisees.~120 multi-site franchisees$25k–$120k per franchisee
Tier 3Single-location franchisees in target networks, meeting a minimum revenue threshold.~2,000 franchise locations$6k–$20k per location

Named Tier 1 accounts

These are the 15 corporate HQs we will run dedicated plays against. Three are already in the pipeline and take priority for Paul and Dani's direct involvement. The rest form the outbound development backlog. Every Tier 1 account gets a dedicated research brief, a multi-thread persona map, and a sequence customised with the operator’s specific business context before any cold touch goes out.

AccountOwnershipApprox. locationsStatusEntry persona
Right at HomeInvestors Management Corp~700PipelineCEO, Head of Clinical
Senior HelpersWaud Capital Partners~375PipelineVP Clinical Services (D. Chandler)
Griswold Home CareGraycliff Partners~170PipelineCEO (M. Slupeki), Head of Innovation (T. Swan)
Help at HomeCenterbridge / Vistria~190Outbound Q1Chief Operating Officer
Elara CaringBlue Wolf / Kelso & Co.~210Outbound Q1Chief Clinical Officer
Home InsteadHonor Technology~1,200Outbound Q1VP Product / Chief Innovation Officer
Comfort KeepersThe Halifax Group~700Outbound Q1Chief Operating Officer
BrightStar CarePeak Rock Capital~400Outbound Q1Chief Nursing Officer
Interim HealthCareLevine Leichtman / Wellspring~600Outbound Q2Chief Clinical Officer
Care AdvantageSearchlight Capital Partners~55Outbound Q2CEO, VP Operations
AltocareWaud Capital PartnersTBDOutbound Q2CEO, Head of Clinical
Aveanna HealthcareBain Capital (public)~340BacklogChief Medical Officer (requires deeper clinical fit)
Touching HeartsPrivate investment~70BacklogCEO, Franchise Operations
Visiting AngelsPrivately held (non-PE)~600BacklogPresident (relationship-led)
Synergy HomeCareNexPhase Capital~440BacklogCOO

Location counts are 2025 estimates and will be refreshed on each quarterly account-planning cycle. Ownership structures shift; verify through ZoomInfo Intelligence before any Tier 1 outreach.

Personas

Five personas inside every Tier 1 account. The first three are corporate-level and sit at the franchisor HQ. Personas 4 and 5 are franchise-level, relevant for Tier 2 and Tier 3 motions. Each is given a concise outbound angle, a hiring-title pattern for ZoomInfo search, and the LinkedIn string we use for sanity checking.

Persona 1 — CEO / President (corporate franchisor)

Example: Michael Slupeki, Griswold. Budget holder for strategic platform investments. Reports to the PE board.
Angle
Standardised falls-risk screening as a differentiator they can sell into franchisees and to payers. Drives franchise growth, operational density, and retention of high-margin customers.
Hooks
PE operating playbook whitepaper, peer CEO case study, benchmark on network-wide rehospitalisation cost.
Title patterns
CEO, President, Chief Executive Officer
LinkedIn string
("CEO" OR "President") AND ("home care" OR "home health") AND ("franchise" OR "private equity")

Persona 2 — Chief Clinical / Quality / Outcomes Officer

Example: David Chandler, VP Clinical Services, Senior Helpers. Owns care quality across the franchise network. Accountable for clinical outcomes and survey readiness.
Angle
Objective, CDC-grounded falls-risk screening that non-clinical staff can carry out reliably, giving corporate a defensible clinical standard.
Hooks
CDC STEADI implementation guide, clinical-governance 1-pager, validated scoring methodology explainer video.
Title patterns
Chief Clinical Officer, VP Clinical Services, Chief Nursing Officer, Head of Quality, Head of Outcomes
LinkedIn string
("Chief Clinical" OR "Clinical Services" OR "Chief Nursing" OR "VP Quality") AND ("home care" OR "home health")

Persona 3 — Head of Innovation / IT / Digital

Example: Tim Swan, Griswold. Owns the franchise tech stack. Evaluates integration, rollout, and training cost.
Angle
Plug-and-play deployment that doesn’t break the existing EHR / scheduling / billing stack. Low training overhead, measurable adoption reporting.
Hooks
Integration architecture one-pager, rollout-in-a-box toolkit, API/integration explainer video.
Title patterns
Chief Innovation Officer, Head of Digital, VP Technology, Head of Product, CIO, CTO
LinkedIn string
("Innovation" OR "Digital" OR "CIO" OR "CTO" OR "VP Technology") AND ("home care" OR "home health")

Persona 4 — Franchise Owner / Operator

Example: Jeff Welsh, Right at Home Tri-County Metro. Small-business owner responsible for local P&L. Price-sensitive, time-poor.
Angle
Grow the book (more clients, better conversion on consults) and protect margin (lower turnover, fewer hospitalisations that end episodes of care). Straight commercial language.
Hooks
Franchise owner's 1-pager, "first 90 days" operational playbook, client-conversion template, payor conversation script.
Title patterns
Owner, Franchise Owner, Franchisee, Operator, Managing Director (at franchise level)
LinkedIn string
("Franchise Owner" OR "Owner" OR "Franchisee") AND ("Right at Home" OR "Home Instead" OR "Senior Helpers" OR "Griswold") (rotate by target brand)

Persona 5 — Care Manager / Director of Nursing

Example: Kristen Sheston, Cypress Home Care. Owns the day-to-day clinical operations. Strong influencer, rarely the sole decision-maker.
Angle
Defensible, objective data when setting a care plan. A tool non-clinical staff can use without retraining. Support when justifying care level to a family or payer.
Hooks
Care-planning template pack, family conversation script, "what the CDC recommends" infographic.
Title patterns
Care Manager, Director of Nursing, DON, Care Coordinator, Clinical Lead
LinkedIn string
("Care Manager" OR "Director of Nursing" OR "DON" OR "Care Coordinator") AND ("home care" OR "home health")

ZoomInfo filter recipes

These are the baseline filter recipes we paste into ZoomInfo Advanced Search. Each recipe returns a working list you can then export into Salesforce as a campaign target. Save them as ZoomInfo saved searches so they refresh automatically and net new movers land in the queue.

Recipe A — Corporate HQ, PE-backed / franchised home care
  • Industry (primary)Home Health Care Services; Home Care / Personal Care Services
  • Industry (secondary)Consumer Services > Home & Personal Services
  • Company employee size500+
  • Company revenue$50M+
  • OwnershipPrivate Equity; Privately Held (IsPE=true)
  • Keyword (company)"franchise" OR "franchisee" OR "franchisor"
  • HQ locationUnited States
  • Contact titlesCEO, President, COO, Chief Clinical Officer, Chief Nursing Officer, Chief Innovation Officer, VP Quality, VP Operations, VP Clinical
  • Contact seniorityC-Level; VP; Director
  • ExclusionsVertically integrated payer subsidiaries: UnitedHealth, Humana, CVS, Aetna, Optum
Recipe B — Franchise owners (Tier 2 / Tier 3)
  • Parent company[Rotate: Right at Home, Senior Helpers, Home Instead, Griswold, Comfort Keepers, BrightStar, Visiting Angels, Synergy]
  • Company employee size10–200
  • Company location count1–10
  • Contact titlesOwner, Franchisee, Franchise Owner, President, Managing Partner
  • HQ locationUnited States (tier by state)
  • Contact verified phoneTRUE (required for engagement-triggered call)
Recipe C — Care Managers / DONs across target networks
  • Parent company[Top 12 Tier 1 brands]
  • Contact titlesCare Manager, Director of Nursing, DON, Care Coordinator, Clinical Lead, RN Care Manager
  • SeniorityManager; Director
  • DepartmentNursing & Patient Care; Operations
  • Contact verified emailTRUE

Disqualifiers

  • Vertically integrated payer-owned home care (Optum / LHC / Amedisys, Humana Centerwell, CVS / Aetna Signify). Sales cycles are multi-year and opaque; revisit only via partnership route.
  • Single-location independent agencies under $2M revenue. Volume too low, margin too thin, tech adoption friction too high for programmatic outbound.
  • Pure Medicare-reimbursed HHAs: they belong in a different motion (see Home Health sub-plan, not in Pass 1).
  • Any account flagged by Legal or CS as a contested compliance territory.

Why now

Three converging pressures make 2026 the right moment. First, CMS and state Medicaid agencies are tightening scrutiny of fall-related ED visits and rehospitalisations, and MLTSS contracts increasingly include outcomes-based quality requirements. Corporate operators need a defensible, standardised falls-risk protocol they can point to. Second, staff turnover remains structurally high across non-clinical care, which is pushing operators toward tools that reduce training burden and drive consistency across a distributed workforce. Third, PE sponsors are entering year 3–5 of their hold periods on several Tier 1 accounts, which sharpens focus on operational differentiation ahead of exit. Any platform that measurably tightens care quality and operating efficiency is getting unusual attention right now.

!
What this unlocks for the plan: every lead magnet in this segment should lean into one of those three pressures. Compliance-and-quality (Chief Clinical angle), workforce-and-consistency (CEO / COO angle), or operational-differentiation (PE-backed CEO angle). If a proposed asset doesn’t sit cleanly on one of those three, it probably shouldn’t be built.

1.2 Independent Multi-Site Home Care

Category 1Secondary targetSlower close, smaller ACV, still repeatable

Market thesis

Around 75% of the home care market is privately owned and independent. Inside that, the subset we care about is independent operators running 2–10 locations. They are large enough to value a standardised tool, small enough to decide quickly (no corporate procurement), and often actively looking for tech that helps them compete with franchise giants without paying franchise fees. They are the best Tier 2 volume engine we have while Tier 1 corporate sales cycles mature.

Tier breakdown

TierProfileEstimated countTypical ACV
Tier 1Top 30 independent multi-site operators (6+ locations) by revenue.~30 accounts$30k–$150k
Tier 2Independent operators with 3–5 locations, $5M+ revenue.~350 accounts$12k–$40k
Tier 3Two-location operators, $2M+ revenue.~1,200 accounts$6k–$15k

Named Tier 1 accounts (examples; list to be hardened in Pass 2)

Specific named accounts require a ZoomInfo + Home Care Pulse cross-reference that Paul and Dani will commission in week 1 of the programme. The placeholder list below is directional.

AccountRegionApprox. locationsWhy Tier 1
Cypress HomeCare SolutionsArizonaMultiple AZ locationsExisting relationship; known care-manager champion (K. Sheston).
Family Resource Home CarePacific NW~30 locationsLarge independent operator with regional scale.
HomeWell Care ServicesNational~50+ locationsFranchise-like model under independent ownership.
Always Best CareNational~200 locationsFranchise model, deserves consideration alongside Tier 1 franchisors.
ComForCareNational~270 locationsFranchise model; re-evaluate against PE-backed list.

Personas

Simpler than Tier 1 franchisors. Two primary personas per account: the Owner / Founder (often still the P&L lead) and the Director of Nursing / Care Manager.

Owner / Founder / President

Pragmatic, cost-aware, direct.
Angle
Grow bookings, reduce turnover, compete with franchise brands on care quality without their overhead.
Titles
Owner, Founder, President, Managing Partner

Director of Nursing / Care Manager

Clinical lead and operational champion.
Angle
Standardised assessment across locations; objective data for care-plan defensibility and family conversations.
Titles
DON, Director of Nursing, Care Manager, Clinical Director

ZoomInfo filter recipe

Recipe D — Independent multi-site home care
  • IndustryHome Health Care Services; Home Care / Personal Care Services
  • OwnershipPrivately Held (exclude PE-backed and public)
  • Company location count2–10
  • Company revenue$2M–$50M
  • HQ locationUnited States
  • Exclusions (company name)Franchise brands already in Tier 1 list
  • Contact titlesOwner, Founder, President, Director of Nursing, DON, Care Manager

Disqualifiers

  • Single-location operators under $2M revenue.
  • Medicare-reimbursed HHA-only operators (different motion).
  • Accounts based in states with unusual regulatory burdens that slow pilots (re-evaluate NY, NJ with Paul / Dani before sending).

Why now

Independent operators are feeling PE consolidation pressure from every direction. They see franchise competitors winning on perceived professionalism. They see private-pay clients asking harder questions. They want a clinical-grade tool that signals seriousness without requiring them to become a franchise. Able Assess is exactly that story.

2.1 Corporate CCRCs

Category 2 · Senior LivingPrimary targetHighest ACV ceiling

Market thesis

Continuing Care Retirement Communities (also called Life Plan Communities) offer IL, AL, and SNF on a single campus, with residents typically paying a large entrance fee in exchange for guaranteed access to higher levels of care. That structure gives the operator two things we can sell to at once: a clinical governance motive (higher acuity residents in AL/SNF need defensible screening) and a sales/marketing motive (falls risk objectivity is a powerful signal for prospective residents and their adult children). CCRCs also have the longest operational tenure and the most institutional buying behaviour in senior living, which favours a product like Able Assess that can show evidence and integrate.

IL units are over-represented inside CCRCs (roughly 45% of units) because they act as the feeder into higher-acuity care. That makes CCRCs a particularly high-value foothold: a sale lands inside IL and expands into AL and SNF naturally.

Tier breakdown

TierProfileCountTypical ACV
Tier 1Top 25 multi-campus CCRC operators (non-profit and corporate) by unit count.~25$80k–$400k
Tier 2Regional CCRC operators with 3–6 campuses.~90$35k–$120k
Tier 3Single-campus CCRCs, $40M+ revenue.~220$20k–$50k

Named Tier 1 accounts

AccountOwnership modelApprox. campusesEntry persona
Erickson Senior LivingCorporate for-profit~20Chief Clinical / COO
HumanGoodNon-profit~25Chief Operating / Head of Wellness
Acts Retirement-LifeNon-profit~26Chief Clinical / VP Health Services
Lifespace CommunitiesNon-profit~17Chief Operating Officer
LCS (Life Care Services)Management + ownership~135SVP Operations
Presbyterian Senior Living (PSL)Non-profit~30Chief Clinical Officer
Kendal CorporationNon-profit network~14System-level Quality Lead
MatherNon-profit4Chief Operating Officer
Masonic Homes of CaliforniaNon-profit~10Chief Clinical / Wellness
Presby's Inspired LifeNon-profit~6VP Health & Wellness
Covenant LivingNon-profit~16Chief Health Services Officer
Westminster Communities of FloridaNon-profit~10VP Health Services

Non-profit CCRCs behave differently from corporate operators: longer consideration cycles, more committee-led buying, and a stronger preference for evidence-led framing. Messaging should emphasise resident outcomes and mission alignment, not commercial differentiation.

Personas

Chief Operating Officer

Owns portfolio-wide operations and resident outcomes.
Angle
Standardised screening across IL, AL, SNF gives the operator a defensible operational signal and a marketable differentiator.

Chief Clinical / VP Health Services

Owns clinical governance and regulatory readiness.
Angle
Objective, CDC-grounded falls risk protocol that moves with the resident from IL into higher-acuity care. Clinical trust chain.

VP Wellness / Resident Experience

Owns the IL lifestyle and prospect-facing story.
Angle
Falls risk insight packaged as a resident-engagement and peace-of-mind story for prospects and adult children.

Executive Director (campus-level)

Community-level P&L owner, the “franchise owner” of senior living.
Angle
Operational simplicity, staff efficiency, occupancy protection, survey readiness.

ZoomInfo filter recipe

Recipe E — Corporate CCRC operators
  • IndustrySenior Living & Retirement Communities; Nursing Home & Continuing Care
  • Keyword (company)"CCRC" OR "Life Plan Community" OR "continuing care retirement"
  • Company employee size500+
  • Company location count3+
  • OwnershipNon-profit OR Private Equity OR Privately Held
  • HQ locationUnited States
  • Contact titlesCOO, CCO, Chief Clinical, Chief Health Services, VP Wellness, VP Health Services, SVP Operations, Executive Director

Disqualifiers

  • Single-campus CCRCs under $40M revenue (deal size too small to justify Tier 1 effort).
  • Non-profit networks with strict vendor-approval committee processes that preclude a pilot within two quarters.
  • Faith-affiliated operators with prior procurement blocks flagged by CS or Sales.

Why now

Post-pandemic occupancy recovery is not yet complete across the CCRC sector, and prospective residents are asking harder questions about clinical readiness and fall prevention. Adult children increasingly research CCRCs the way they research healthcare providers. A visible, CDC-grounded falls-risk protocol is becoming a marketable differentiator, not just a back-office operational tool. Operators that adopt early can own the story in their market.

2.2 Rental Retirement Communities (IL + AL, Corporate)

Category 2 · Senior LivingPrimary targetLargest IL unit pool

Market thesis

Rental retirement communities combine IL and AL on a leasehold model (no large entry fee). This segment represents roughly 20% of the total IL unit pool through corporate operators, which makes it one of the biggest addressable volumes we have in senior living. The buyer behaviour is more commercial than CCRC non-profits: occupancy, resident length-of-stay, and operational efficiency drive decisions. The AL side of the campus also gives us a natural up-sell story from IL screening into AL care planning.

Tier breakdown

TierProfileCountTypical ACV
Tier 1Top 20 corporate rental IL+AL operators by unit count.~20$100k–$500k
Tier 2Regional operators with 5–15 communities.~140$30k–$120k
Tier 3Smaller corporate operators (2–5 communities).~380$15k–$45k

Named Tier 1 accounts

AccountOwnershipApprox. communitiesEntry persona
Atria Senior LivingCorporate (incl. former Holiday)~380Chief Operating Officer
Brookdale Senior LivingPublic (NYSE)~650Chief Medical / Chief Clinical Officer
Sunrise Senior LivingCorporate~260Chief Operating Officer
Leisure CareCorporate~40Chief Operating Officer
Watermark Retirement CommunitiesCorporate~70Chief Clinical Officer
Senior LifestyleCorporate~130Chief Operating Officer
Merrill GardensCorporate~70Chief Operating Officer
Integral Senior LivingCorporate~80VP Clinical / Quality
Kisco Senior LivingCorporate~25Chief Operating Officer
Discovery Senior LivingCorporate~350 (across portfolio)Chief Clinical Officer
Frontier ManagementCorporate~120VP Clinical Services
Senior Resource GroupCorporate~35Chief Operating Officer

Personas

Identical persona spine as CCRC with one addition: VP Sales / Marketing is a live persona here because occupancy and lead conversion are central operational metrics. Falls risk objectivity can be a sales differentiator in the prospect tour, and a VP Sales will pay attention to that angle.

ZoomInfo filter recipe

Recipe F — Corporate rental IL+AL operators
  • IndustrySenior Living & Retirement Communities; Assisted Living Facilities
  • Keyword (company)"independent living" AND "assisted living" AND NOT "continuing care"
  • Company employee size500+
  • Company location count5+
  • HQ locationUnited States
  • Contact titlesCOO, CCO, Chief Clinical, VP Clinical Services, VP Sales, VP Operations, Executive Director

Disqualifiers & Why now

  • Pure IL-only or AL-only operators (see dedicated sub-segments below).
  • Operators outside US.
  • Single-community accounts.

Corporate operators are in the middle of a measured occupancy recovery. Falls-related hospitalisations are a leading cause of involuntary move-outs from IL, which directly hits length-of-stay metrics that drive NOI. Falls prevention is increasingly boardroom-visible for this reason.

2.3 IL-Only Corporate Communities

Category 2 · Senior LivingPrimary target

Market thesis

IL-only communities house active, self-sufficient seniors and focus on hospitality, social life, and wellness, not clinical care. For Able Assess, this looks like a stretch segment at first glance, but the buying thesis is different: falls prevention sits inside the wellness and resident-experience agenda, not the clinical agenda. The value proposition is about resident confidence, family peace-of-mind, early warning for downstream care, and a visible commitment to healthy ageing. That is a marketing and operations sale, not a clinical one.

Tier breakdown

TierProfileCountTypical ACV
Tier 1Top 15 corporate IL-only operators by unit count.~15$60k–$250k
Tier 2Regional IL-only operators with 3–8 communities.~80$25k–$80k
Tier 3Smaller corporate operators.~260$12k–$35k

Named Tier 1 accounts (directional, confirm in Pass 2)

Most of the largest senior-living operators combine IL with AL. True IL-only corporate portfolios are narrower. The work in Pass 2 is to confirm this list through NIC MAP data and recent ownership changes.

  • Holiday by Atria (IL-focused sub-brand inside Atria)
  • Eskaton (multi-type; IL is significant)
  • Cogir Senior Living
  • Milestone Retirement Communities
  • Greystar Senior Living (growing IL platform)
  • Priority Life Care (selected IL portfolios)
  • Resort Lifestyle Communities
  • Solera Senior Living

Personas

Two dominant personas in IL-only: VP Wellness / Resident Experience and VP Sales & Marketing. A Chief Clinical persona exists only at the largest operators and should not be the default entry. The Executive Director at the community level is the second best entry if corporate is hard to reach.

ZoomInfo filter recipe

Recipe G — Corporate IL-only operators
  • IndustrySenior Living & Retirement Communities
  • Keyword (company)"independent living" AND NOT ("assisted living" OR "skilled nursing")
  • Company location count3+
  • HQ locationUnited States
  • Contact titlesVP Wellness, VP Resident Experience, VP Sales, VP Marketing, Executive Director, Chief Experience Officer

Disqualifiers & Why now

Exclude single-community operators and affordable-housing-only portfolios (Section 202, LIHTC) which sit outside the commercial motion. The 2026 opportunity is clear: IL operators are competing harder on wellness programming as a differentiator, and a CDC-grounded falls protocol sold as a wellness service is an unusually credible talking point for a corporate sales and marketing team.

2.4 AL-Only Corporate Communities

Category 2 · Senior LivingPrimary targetClinical-first buyer

Market thesis

AL-only corporate communities sit at higher clinical acuity than IL, with a resident population that needs support for activities of daily living and often has memory-care needs. The buying motion is much closer to a clinical sale: Chief Nursing, Chief Clinical, and Regional Directors of Nursing lead the conversation, and survey readiness plus resident safety are centre stage. Falls are the single largest cause of preventable harm in AL, and operators know it.

Tier breakdown

TierProfileCountTypical ACV
Tier 1Top 20 corporate AL operators by unit count.~20$80k–$350k
Tier 2Regional AL operators with 5–15 communities.~160$30k–$100k
Tier 3Smaller corporate operators.~420$15k–$40k

Named Tier 1 accounts

  • Belmont Village Senior Living
  • Enlivant
  • Sonida Senior Living (formerly Capital Senior Living)
  • Charter Senior Living
  • Harbor Retirement Associates (HRA)
  • Grace Management
  • Oakmont Management
  • Pegasus Senior Living
  • Stellar Senior Living
  • Civitas Senior Living
  • 12 Oaks Senior Living
  • Legend Senior Living

Personas

Chief Nursing Officer / Chief Clinical Officer

Portfolio-wide clinical lead.
Angle
Survey readiness, consistent falls protocol across communities, defensible documentation. CDC STEADI as the reference framework.

Regional Director of Nursing / Clinical Ops

Operational clinical owner across 5–20 communities.
Angle
Standardising care-planning across variable staff skill levels. Early warning on residents trending toward higher care needs.

ZoomInfo filter recipe

Recipe H — Corporate AL operators
  • IndustryAssisted Living Facilities; Senior Living & Retirement Communities
  • Keyword (company)"assisted living" AND NOT "continuing care"
  • Company location count5+
  • HQ locationUnited States
  • Contact titlesChief Nursing Officer, Chief Clinical Officer, VP Clinical, Regional DON, Regional Clinical Director, Director of Health & Wellness

Disqualifiers & Why now

Exclude memory-care-only operators (different motion, needs dedicated framing). Exclude operators in states with unusually restrictive AL regulations until we validate with Legal. The timing driver is survey-cycle pressure: AL operators are increasingly cited for fall-related incident reporting inconsistencies, and a standardised screening protocol is treated as a credible mitigation.

2.5 For-Profit Skilled Nursing Facilities (Corporate)

Category 2 · Senior LivingPrimary targetHeavily regulated buyer

Market thesis

Corporate for-profit SNF operators run the highest acuity settings in our universe. Residents are medically complex and fall-related harm is the single most common adverse event tracked by CMS. Able Assess positions into the Quality Assurance and Performance Improvement (QAPI) programme as an objective, staff-administered screening that feeds the facility's fall-prevention workflow. The buying motion is more formal than elsewhere in senior living, with Clinical, Compliance, and Quality all involved. Sales cycles are longer but ACVs are higher and renewal behaviour is very sticky.

Tier breakdown

TierProfileCountTypical ACV
Tier 1Top 12 corporate for-profit SNF chains by bed count.~12$120k–$600k
Tier 2Regional SNF operators with 10–50 facilities.~70$40k–$150k
Tier 3Smaller for-profit operators (5–10 facilities).~180$20k–$60k

Named Tier 1 accounts

  • The Ensign Group (including Flagship and regional operators)
  • Genesis HealthCare
  • ProMedica Senior Care (formerly HCR ManorCare)
  • Consulate Health Care
  • Signature HealthCARE
  • Ciena Healthcare
  • Diversicare Healthcare Services
  • Life Care Centers of America
  • Trilogy Health Services
  • CommuniCare Health Services
  • SavaSeniorCare
  • National HealthCare Corporation (NHC)

Personas

Chief Nursing Officer / Chief Clinical Officer

Clinical governance across the chain.
Angle
Objective falls screening aligned to CMS Section GG and fall-event reporting. Defensible data for survey and re-admission pushback.

Chief Quality / Compliance Officer

Owns QAPI programme and survey outcomes.
Angle
Consistent screening protocol integrated into QAPI, reducing variance in fall-event documentation across facilities.

VP Clinical Informatics / Head of EHR

Owns PointClickCare / MatrixCare / similar.
Angle
Integration-ready screening that writes back to the EHR and doesn’t create new documentation burden.

Regional VP Operations / Administrator

Owns facility-level P&L and survey performance.
Angle
Protect 5-star rating, reduce re-admissions, reduce insurance claim exposure.

ZoomInfo filter recipe

Recipe I — Corporate for-profit SNF chains
  • IndustryNursing Homes & Long-Term Care; Skilled Nursing Facility
  • OwnershipFor-profit; exclude non-profit and government-owned
  • Company employee size500+
  • Company location count10+
  • HQ locationUnited States
  • Contact titlesCNO, CCO, Chief Quality, Chief Compliance, VP Clinical Informatics, VP Operations, Regional VP

Disqualifiers & Why now

  • Non-profit SNFs (different buying behaviour; revisit later).
  • Government-owned SNFs (procurement path too slow for outbound).
  • Hospital-owned SNFs (integrated into health-system buying).

CMS has been steadily tightening reporting and quality-measure scrutiny on fall-related harm. The industry-wide shift from the old MDS 3.0 fall-event coding to more granular fall-risk reporting has made standardised screening a defensible compliance posture. Operators that can evidence a consistent, CDC-grounded protocol across their facilities are insulated from the most common survey findings. That is what we are selling into.

Messaging Architecture

Messaging is the bridge between the strategy and every artefact the prospect sees. It ensures the cold email, the landing page, the lead magnet, and the sales deck all sound like one company talking in one voice, with messaging that is calibrated to the segment and persona on the receiving end. Every asset and every sequence must be traceable to a specific value proposition and pillar in this section.

Value proposition spine (by sub-segment)

Each spine is the one sentence every piece of content in that segment has to be consistent with. If copy drifts, the spine is how we bring it back.

Sub-segmentValue proposition spine
PE-backed / franchised home careAble Assess gives multi-site home care operators a CDC-grounded falls-risk screening that any staff member can run in under three minutes, delivering consistent, defensible care insights across every location without adding clinical headcount.
Independent multi-site home careAble Assess gives independent home care operators the clinical-grade falls-risk tool the big franchises use, without the overhead, so they can compete on quality and win more private-pay and payer business.
Corporate CCRCsAble Assess gives CCRC operators a single falls-risk protocol that moves with residents across IL, AL, and skilled nursing, strengthening clinical governance and giving families objective reassurance during move-in and every annual review.
Rental retirement IL+AL (corporate)Able Assess helps IL+AL operators protect length-of-stay and reduce involuntary move-outs by catching falls risk early, in a three-minute assessment any community associate can run.
IL-only corporateAble Assess turns your wellness programme into a proof point: a CDC-grounded falls-risk check that reassures prospects, engages residents, and gives your sales team something credible to talk about on tour.
AL-only corporateAble Assess gives AL operators a standardised, survey-ready falls-risk screening that every community runs the same way, protecting clinical governance and reducing the documentation burden on DONs.
For-profit SNF corporateAble Assess slots into QAPI as an objective, staff-administered falls-risk screening that standardises practice across facilities and produces the kind of evidence surveyors and insurers ask for.

Pillars — PE-backed / franchised home care (primary)

Pillar 1 · Standardisation at scale

One protocol, every location, same answer. Corporate gets a defensible screening standard they can point to across the franchise network. Franchisees get a tool they don’t have to invent for themselves.

Proof points
  • CDC STEADI alignment (national clinical reference).
  • <Named customer>: 35-franchise rollout completed in 11 weeks.
  • Audit trail exportable by franchise, region, or network.

Pillar 2 · Built for non-clinical workforces

Designed for the people who actually do the work. Three-minute administration, plain-language output, zero retraining. Fits the operating reality of a home care workforce without over-clinicising it.

Proof points
  • <Customer>: 94% caregiver adoption in week one.
  • Average time to administer: 2:40 (internal telemetry).
  • Output language tested with non-clinical users.

Pillar 3 · Operational lift, not operational cost

Reduces rehospitalisation, lengthens episodes of care, and gives franchisees a differentiator in consultation calls. The tool earns its keep, it doesn’t just sit on the stack.

Proof points
  • <Customer>: X% reduction in fall-related ED visits across 12 franchises.
  • Consultation-to-signup conversion improvement (franchisee testimonial).
  • Integration-ready: Axxess, WellSky, HHAeXchange, ClearCare.

Pillars — Corporate CCRCs (primary)

Pillar 1 · One protocol across the continuum

Falls risk insight follows the resident from IL into AL and SNF. No re-papering, no parallel systems. Clinical governance that actually spans the campus.

Proof points
  • Longitudinal scoring from first IL screening through acuity transitions.
  • EHR integration patterns for PointClickCare and MatrixCare.
  • CDC STEADI as the reference framework.

Pillar 2 · A story residents and families trust

Prospective residents and their adult children increasingly research CCRCs the way they research healthcare. A visible, CDC-grounded falls protocol is an answer, not a talking point.

Proof points
  • Family-facing output packaging.
  • Use at move-in and annual review cycles.
  • Quote: CCRC VP Wellness, <named customer>.

Pillar 3 · Quiet insurance for the operator

Reduces avoidable incidents, strengthens survey posture, and protects the metric that actually matters to NOI: length of stay.

Proof points
  • Fall-related involuntary move-out data (internal, customer-referenced).
  • Survey-citation mitigation example.
  • Operational simplicity: no new clinical headcount required.

Pillars for other sub-segments (condensed)

Sub-segmentPillar 1Pillar 2Pillar 3
Independent multi-site home careFranchise-grade clinical posture without the franchise feesCompete harder on private pay and payer contractsStand out in a crowded market with objective care data
Rental retirement IL+ALProtect length of stay and NOICross-acuity screening that scales across communitiesGive Sales & Marketing a credible tour-ready story
IL-only corporateReframe wellness as evidence-led programmingTour-ready differentiator for sales teamsEarly warning on residents trending toward higher care
AL-only corporatePortfolio-wide protocol consistencyReduced documentation burden for DONsDefensible survey posture
For-profit SNF corporateIntegrated into QAPI with minimal frictionConsistent fall-event practice across facilitiesEvidence surveyors and payers accept

Persona messaging library

Each persona gets a problem, a hook, and a disqualifier line. Writers work from these cards so every email and asset pitches to what this person actually cares about on a Tuesday morning.

CEO / President (corporate franchisor)

Problem
Differentiation across a franchise network is hard to engineer. Every technology bet has to pay back in operational lift and franchisee retention, not just look good in a deck.
Hook
"A CDC-grounded falls protocol that every one of your franchises can run the same way, on day one, without hiring a clinician. Franchise operators we work with see it as a differentiator in their consultation calls."
Don’t say
Anything that sounds like an EHR pitch or a product that needs a 12-week rollout.

Chief Clinical / VP Clinical Services

Problem
Clinical standard setting across dozens of sites run by non-clinical staff. No way to evidence consistency when the surveyor shows up.
Hook
"A falls-risk screening grounded in CDC STEADI, administered consistently by non-clinical staff, with an audit trail clinical governance can actually point to."
Don’t say
"Clinical decision support" or anything that implies the tool replaces clinical judgement.

Head of Innovation / CIO

Problem
Every rollout fights the EHR, the scheduling platform, the training backlog, and the patience of franchisees.
Hook
"We plug into Axxess, HHAeXchange, WellSky, PointClickCare, and MatrixCare. Training is a 20-minute video. Adoption data is in your Salesforce the day you go live."
Don’t say
"Enterprise-grade" or anything that hints at a 90-day technical integration.

Franchise Owner / Operator

Problem
Growing the book, keeping staff, winning consultations against competitors, surviving the month.
Hook
"A falls-risk screening you can run on a consultation call that turns a vague conversation into a concrete care plan. Operators using it tell us it helps them close more consultations and keep clients longer."
Don’t say
Anything corporate-shaped. This persona does not have time for strategic framing.

Director of Nursing / Care Manager

Problem
Defending care plans to families and payers. Training new caregivers. Maintaining consistency across a workforce that turns over constantly.
Hook
"Objective, CDC-grounded falls-risk data that backs up your care planning and makes family conversations easier. Any caregiver can run it. Results land in your system the same day."
Don’t say
"Transformative" or "revolutionary" clinical framing. DONs are suspicious of vendor noise.

Objection handling bank

ObjectionResponse spineSupporting proof
"We already have a falls protocol."Acknowledge and reframe. Most operators have a protocol on paper. What changes with Able Assess is that every staff member runs the same assessment the same way, and the data is usable across the network.Audit-trail demo; consistency-across-sites dashboard.
"Our caregivers are non-clinical, they can’t do this."That is exactly the design point. Able Assess is built for non-clinical administration and has been used by home care aides, resident associates, and community staff.94% week-one adoption stat; 2:40 admin time.
"We don’t have budget for another tool."Frame against the cost of a single fall. Average fall-related ED visit cost, rehospitalisation, lost episode of care, insurance exposure. Able Assess is priced to pay back against a handful of avoided events.ROI calculator (Pass 2 asset), customer TCO example.
"Our EHR already captures falls risk."EHR fields capture events, not risk. The screening is the missing half: it identifies risk before the incident, and writes back to the EHR so the record is complete.Integration 1-pager, sample data write-back.
"We’re about to change EHR / go through integration."Good news, we run independently and integrate on both sides of the change. Adoption doesn’t wait on your EHR timeline.Integration patterns; deployment-without-EHR path.
"We need to see clinical evidence."Grounded in CDC STEADI. Peer-reviewed literature supports the screening methodology. Happy to send the evidence pack.Evidence pack; STEADI citation brief.
"Our franchisees won’t buy this."Understood. That is why we offer a franchisee-level programme with its own commercial structure and franchisee-specific onboarding.Franchisee economics one-pager.
"We’re not looking at new vendors right now."Respect that. When it is useful, here is the one-pager and the benchmark report. No follow-up unless you open them.Benchmark report; clean unsubscribe path.
"This sounds like a liability risk, not a risk reducer."Valid concern. The screening is a risk identification tool, not a clinical diagnosis. The audit trail actually strengthens the operator’s legal posture, it does not weaken it.Legal-posture one-pager (Dani sign-off).
"We tried a falls tool before, didn’t stick."What broke? In most cases it was training load, not the tool. Able Assess is specifically designed against that failure mode: three minutes, no clinical training, visible adoption metrics from day one.Adoption playbook; customer rollback example.

Voice & tone guide

How we sound

  • Direct. Every sentence earns its place.
  • Evidence-led, never breathless.
  • Specific over generic. Numbers beat adjectives.
  • Warm but not chatty. We are a peer, not a friend.

Structure rules

  • Short sentences. Average length under 20 words.
  • One idea per paragraph.
  • Active voice.
  • First mention of a concept spells it out, second mention can shorthand.

Banned words

  • Revolutionary, transformative, game-changing.
  • Leverage (as a verb), synergy, solution (as a product noun).
  • Passionate, cutting-edge, best-in-class.
  • Any claim unsupported by citation or customer.

Clinical hygiene

  • Never imply diagnosis or treatment.
  • Never quantify outcomes without a citable source.
  • Always lead CDC STEADI references with the full name on first use.
  • Every clinical claim carries Dani’s sign-off flag.

On-brand vs off-brand

On-brand

"Able Assess is a three-minute falls-risk screening any staff member can run. It’s built on CDC STEADI. Home care operators using it see consistent fall-risk documentation across every location, and their franchisees use it as a differentiator on consultation calls."

Off-brand

"Able Care is the leading, best-in-class falls prevention solution that revolutionises how care organisations leverage objective data to transform resident safety outcomes at scale."

Claim register

Every claim we make anywhere (email, landing page, deck, asset) has to be traceable to a source. This register is the source of truth. It is maintained by Marketing, reviewed quarterly, and cleared by Dani on clinical items.

ClaimSource typeCitation / evidenceClinical sign-off
"Grounded in CDC STEADI."Reference frameworkCDC STEADI (Stopping Elderly Accidents, Deaths & Injuries) toolkit.Cleared
"Three-minute administration."Internal telemetryMean administration time 2:40 across production customers (telemetry pull, date-stamped).Cleared
"Non-clinical staff can administer."Design claim + customer evidenceAdoption profiles at <customers>.Cleared
"Integrates with <EHR>."Product capabilityIntegration documentation per platform. Marketing owns list refresh.Per-item
"Reduces rehospitalisation."Outcome claim — requires careCustomer-specific; use only with a named customer and citable metric.Case-by-case
"Improves length of stay."Outcome claim — requires careCustomer-specific. Must be tied to a specific cohort and timeframe.Case-by-case
"Full audit trail."Product capabilityProduct documentation. Never used without “by location, by assessor, by resident” specificity.Cleared
"Used by <number> of home care locations."Marketing metricRefresh monthly from Salesforce Account.Active=TRUE.Cleared

Competitive positioning

How we talk about ourselves relative to the visible players in falls risk and senior tech. The rule is simple: name them only when they name us. In outbound, our default is to position against the status quo, not the competition.

PlayerTheir shapeWhere they winWhere we win
SafelyYouAI-enabled fall detection via camera in memory care / ALExisting AL footprint, memory-care storyProactive risk screening (before the fall); broader settings; lower deployment friction; non-clinical administration
CarePredictWearable-based behavioural monitoring in senior livingContinuous monitoring narrativeLow-friction, no hardware, no wearable adoption dependency; integrated with existing workflows
Foresite HealthcareAmbient sensor monitoring in SL / SNFSensor-rich, strong clinical affiliationDeployable in a week, not a quarter; works anywhere including home care; staff-administered on demand
Home-grown paper protocolsClipboard / spreadsheet / one-off formsZero costConsistency across locations, audit trail, digital data that compounds, training burden absorbed by product
Generic risk tools inside an EHRForm field inside PointClickCare / MatrixCareSingle pane of glassPurpose-built scoring methodology; usable by non-clinical staff; not buried in the EHR
>
Positioning discipline: in the first five touches we never mention a competitor. We position against the status quo of inconsistent, paper-based, clinically fragile screening. Competitors only come up if the prospect raises them, at which point the table above is the script.

Lead Magnet Library

The lead magnet library is the engine of the outbound programme. Every cold email points at a piece of content. Every piece of content has a landing page that fires a Salesforce event on engagement. Every event triggers the human workflow. The library is organised as a portfolio: three consumption depths per segment, each matched to a persona and a funnel stage. Nothing ships that does not have a named buyer, a named trigger, and a named owner.

Library principles

Three depths, every segment

Quick-skim (1–3 min), medium-invest (10–15 min), deep-invest (20+ min or interactive). A prospect should be able to land on one piece and be drawn toward the next.

Gating with intent

Quick-skim content is ungated (low friction, shareable). Medium and deep assets are gated with a short form. Gating choice is deliberate, not reflexive.

Every asset names its buyer

If we cannot write the persona and the trigger in one sentence, we don’t build it. Orphan content drags marketing velocity and dilutes the brand.

Clinical sign-off is not optional

Every outbound-facing asset with a clinical claim passes Dani before publication. This is our compliance moat and our credibility moat.

Master asset matrix

Forty-one assets across segments and depths. Status is tracked in Notion and mirrored in Salesforce Campaigns. The matrix is the single view the team lives in.

#AssetSegmentPersonaDepthStageOwnerStatus

i
How to read the status column. Q1 = in production or shipped for Q1. Q2 = committed for Q2. Q3 = slated for Q3 pending Q1–Q2 learnings. A quarter-level slip against a single asset is acceptable; a full library behind schedule isn't.

Cornerstone asset drill-downs

Eight assets are cornerstone, meaning they carry disproportionate weight in the programme and therefore get full written briefs. The remaining assets use a condensed template maintained in Notion.

#10 — Franchise operator’s playbook for standardised falls risk

Hook
Every franchise leader has the same unsolved problem: a clinical standard that the corporate office believes in but individual franchises don’t run consistently. This guide is the operational path through it.
Persona
CEO and Chief Clinical Officer at PE-backed / franchised home care operators
Format
PDF, 16 pages, designed. Accompanying 4-page summary for franchisees.
Outline
1. Why standardisation breaks at the franchise level. 2. What “good” looks like (CDC STEADI baseline). 3. A 90-day rollout pattern. 4. How to measure adoption without policing it. 5. What corporate needs to monitor. 6. Case study. 7. Cheat sheet for franchisees.
Gating
Gated. Form: first name, last name, work email, company, role.
Landing page
/resources/franchise-falls-playbook · branded hero · social proof stripe · 3-paragraph preview · form · post-download redirect to thank-you with booking CTA
SF Campaign
HC-PE_Playbook_Download_2026
UTM
utm_source=email · utm_medium=outbound · utm_campaign=hc-pe-playbook · utm_content={{sequence_id}}
Trigger on fill
Salesforce task created for owner, 24-hour SLA. ZoomInfo enrichment on contact. LinkedIn view from rep.
Sign-off
Dani on clinical claims; Paul on commercial framing.

#02 — Falls Risk Scorecard (interactive app)

Hook
A prospect enters a simple resident / client profile (age, mobility, medications count, history of falls). Receives an illustrative score and a one-paragraph explanation rooted in CDC STEADI. Exit CTA: "See how this runs across your portfolio."
Persona
Any (high-intent signal regardless of role)
Format
Single-page React app embedded at /scorecard. Mobile-friendly.
Gating
Ungated entry. Gated exit on the full result (email + company).
Tracking
Event firing on every input, gated-reveal, and CTA click. High-intent score when user completes all fields + reveals result.
Trigger on reveal
Lead scored +25. Salesforce task created, 4-hour SLA for high-intent accounts.
Clinical posture
Output explicitly framed as illustrative only. Not a clinical assessment. Never used for real resident screening.

#15 — Whitepaper: operational differentiation in PE-backed home care

Hook
PE sponsors in year 3–5 of hold are looking for differentiation that outlasts the next quarter. Clinical tooling is the quiet lever most operators underuse.
Persona
CEO at PE-backed / franchised home care
Format
PDF, 22 pages, with data visualisations. Short-form summary in Substack format (for a CEO to skim on a phone).
Outline
1. Where home care operators actually compete. 2. Why clinical tooling is undervalued on the PE operating playbook. 3. The consolidation-era differentiation story. 4. Three case patterns. 5. Appendix: operator questions for their next board meeting.
Gating
Gated.
Owner
Marketing + Paul (PE-native framing)

#23 — VP Wellness’s playbook for family-facing falls data (CCRC)

Hook
Adult children are asking harder questions on tours. A VP Wellness who can turn a resident’s falls-risk profile into a clear, reassuring conversation wins the admission call.
Persona
VP Wellness / VP Resident Experience
Format
PDF, 14 pages; includes two conversation scripts and a family-facing one-pager template.
Outline
1. What adult children are actually asking. 2. Using falls data as reassurance, not alarm. 3. The tour conversation script. 4. The annual review conversation script. 5. Family-facing summary sheet template.

#38 — QAPI integration guide (SNF)

Hook
Surveyors cite SNFs for inconsistency in fall-event documentation more than almost any other category. A screening slotted into QAPI is the cheapest insurance a Chief Quality Officer can buy.
Persona
Chief Quality Officer and Chief Nursing Officer at for-profit SNF chains
Format
PDF, 18 pages. Includes a sample QAPI insertion document and a flowchart.
Sign-off
Dani (must); Legal advised on survey-citation framing.

#03 — Programme ROI Calculator

Inputs
Number of locations / communities, average acuity, current fall rate (self-reported or benchmark), average cost per fall-related ED visit or rehospitalisation.
Outputs
Projected rehospitalisations avoided, episode days retained, net-of-licence ROI range, payback window. All presented with visible assumptions.
Framing
Every number is labelled as an estimate with its assumption inline. No "guaranteed savings" framing anywhere.
Exit CTA
"Book a 20-minute read-out with our team where we walk through these numbers against your data."

#04 — Able Care Falls Prevention Benchmark 2026

Why this asset
Anchors the category. Published annually. Becomes the inbound link magnet and the reason Able Care is quoted in trade press.
Data source
Anonymised usage data from the customer base plus an industry survey (n ≥ 120 operators). Dani and Data team co-own the methodology.
Promotion
Gated PDF + ungated exec summary + three derivative blog posts + three LinkedIn carousels + one webinar.
Due
Q2 2026 for v1.0; annual refresh thereafter.

#26 — Whitepaper: evidence base for CDC STEADI in CCRC settings

Hook
CCRC Chief Clinical Officers ask for evidence before they ask for a demo. This asset is the evidence.
Format
Peer-review-adjacent whitepaper, 20–26 pages, with full reference list and methodology appendix.
Sign-off
Dani (mandatory). Optional review by an external clinical advisor.

Landing page pattern

Every gated asset uses the same landing page template, so Marketing can spin up a new page in under two hours and performance data stays comparable across assets.

ElementRule
URL pattern/resources/{asset-slug} on able-care.co (consolidated, no subdomain)
HeroAsset title (H1), one-sentence deck (who it is for, what they get), asset type pill (Guide / Whitepaper / Checklist)
Social proof stripeTwo customer logos or one named customer quote; never dead white space
PreviewThree paragraphs + one pull quote or stat; visible without scroll on desktop
FormFirst name, last name, work email, company, role. Nothing else. Hidden field: utm_content captured for attribution.
Post-submitThank-you page with asset auto-delivered + calendar booking CTA ("Talk to someone on the team")
TrackingSalesforce Web-to-Campaign integration; ZoomInfo FormComplete auto-enriches; UTM captured to Campaign Member
AccessibilityWCAG 2.1 AA — form labels, contrast, alt text

Content production backlog (first 90 days)

WeekShipsOwner(s)
W1–2Assets 01, 05, 09, 13, 25 (cornerstone 1-pagers and integration briefs)Marketing
W3–4Assets 06, 07, 08, 16, 18, 21, 22 (quick-skim portfolio)Marketing
W5–6Asset 10 (cornerstone playbook)Marketing + Dani
W7–8Assets 11, 38 (CFO guide, QAPI guide)Marketing + Dani
W9–10Assets 02, 03 (scorecard + ROI calculator alpha)Product + Marketing
W11–12Assets 12, 15, 23, 26, 28, 29, 30, 32, 35, 36, 37, 39 (mid-Q2 ship)Marketing, CS, Data
!
Production capacity reality check. This cadence assumes one FTE marketing writer plus Dani's clinical review time plus one external designer on retainer. If any of those three resourcing assumptions shifts, the ship dates shift with it; the answer is to re-sequence, not skip sign-off.

Outbound Sequences

Pass 3 deliverable

Sequences are where strategy, messaging, and assets meet the prospect. In Pass 3 every sequence is written end-to-end and ready to paste into Salesforce Sales Engagement. Sequences are segment-specific, persona-specific, and named so any rep can look up exactly which cadence to enrol a contact in.

Pass 3 · Sequence library coming

Sequence taxonomy

Naming convention: SEG_PERSONA_STAGE_VERSION. Example: HC-PE_CEO_COLD_v1, CCRC_CCO_ENGAGED_v1, IL-ONLY_VPWellness_REACTIVATE_v2.

Sequence types we will build

  • Cold sequences (7 touches across 3 weeks) per segment × persona. ~20 sequences total.
  • Engagement-triggered sequences (4 touches) fired when a prospect downloads a gated asset, submits a form, or hits a high-intent page twice in 72 hours.
  • Reactivation sequences (5 touches) for contacts that engaged 90+ days ago and have gone quiet.
  • Multi-channel plays: LinkedIn view + connect + InMail, woven into the email cadence.
  • Referral sequences for warm intros from Paul, Dani, Jack, or named customers.

What each email includes

  • Subject line (plus 2 alternates for A/B).
  • Preview text.
  • Body, written to sound like a human on a personal inbox: short sentences, one idea, no marketing voice, no tracking pixels, no footer graphics.
  • Personalisation token map (first name, company, signal).
  • Internal notes: why this email works, what objection it pre-empts, what step the CTA triggers.
  • Asset link, UTM, landing page, and the downstream Salesforce campaign.

Sample (shape only)

Sequence: HC-PE_CEO_COLD_v1
Day 1 · Email 1: Hook rooted in a franchise standardisation problem. Offers the CFO's guide to reducing fall-related hospitalisation churn. No meeting ask.
Day 3 · LinkedIn view.
Day 5 · Email 2: Proof. Customer like them, in their franchise tier. One metric, one quote.
Day 8 · LinkedIn connect with short note.
Day 10 · Email 3: Value drop. Links to the interactive ROI calculator, pre-populated.
Day 14 · Email 4: Challenge. "Most CEOs I speak with underestimate X. Here’s a quick read on it."
Day 17 · Phone call (ZoomInfo-verified direct dial).
Day 21 · Email 5: Polite bow-out with door left open.

Salesforce + ZoomInfo Operations

Pass 2 complete

The operations playbook is what turns the rest of this document into a working system. It is the section the next BDR, Marketing Ops hire, or RevOps analyst should be able to read on day one and be productive. Everything here is written to be operated, not admired: every rule has an owner, every signal has a destination, every SLA has a clock.

Stack overview Enrichment rules Intent routing WebSights Cadence architecture Engagement-to-call SLA Call scripts Lead scoring Campaigns & UTM Dispositions Data hygiene Deliverability

The stack, at a glance

Six systems, one directional flow. Data is captured at the edges (ZoomInfo, the website, sending mailboxes) and concentrated in Salesforce, which is the single record of truth. Anything that is not in Salesforce did not happen.

01
Source
ZoomInfo Advanced Search
→
02
Enrich
ZoomInfo Enrich
→
03
Orchestrate
Salesforce Sales Engagement
→
04
Send
Personal mailboxes
→
05
Capture
Landing page + FormComplete
→
06
Route
Salesforce (tasks + campaigns)
i
The design principle. Every external signal (ZoomInfo intent, WebSights visit, form fill, email reply) lands in Salesforce as either a task, a campaign member, or a lead-score event. A rep never goes looking. The system brings the next best action to them.

1. Enrichment rules

ZoomInfo Enrich runs in three places. Each one has a different ownership model to avoid the classic problem of ZoomInfo overwriting field edits that a human has just made.

Trigger Object ZoomInfo fields applied Ownership model
Lead create (inbound) Lead Company, HQ, revenue band, employee count, industry, NAICS, SIC, LinkedIn URL, direct dial, job title normalised, seniority, department ZoomInfo writes on create only; never overwrites
Account create (manual or ABM import) Account All firmographics + parent/subsidiary, ownership type (Public / PE / Franchise / Independent), website, social handles ZoomInfo overwrites only on empty fields; Salesforce-owned fields (e.g. Tier, Segment) never touched
Contact update (daily) Contact Title, seniority, department, email, direct dial, LinkedIn URL Append-only: if a contact’s title has changed, ZoomInfo writes a new title AND archives the old one to the contact’s Title History field
Re-verify stale contacts Contact Email validity + phone validity Runs weekly on any Contact with Last_Activity__c > 90 days. Invalid emails flagged, not deleted.

Salesforce-owned vs ZoomInfo-owned fields

This matters because most operators don’t draw the line clearly, and then get surprised when a rep’s note on a lead is overwritten by a nightly enrichment run.

Salesforce-owned (never overwrite)ZoomInfo-owned (authoritative source)Append-only (preserve history)
Segment, Sub-segment, Tier, Account Owner, Opportunity Stage, Lead Status, Rating, Next Step, Notes Revenue band, employee count, industry code, HQ address, ownership type, parent/subsidiary, technologies in use Title, direct dial, email; each change timestamped in a history field rather than overwritten in place

2. Intent signal routing

ZoomInfo Intent gives us buyer-side research signals: which companies are researching which topics, how aggressively, and over what time window. We subscribe to a small, focused topic basket so the signal stays meaningful instead of noisy.

ZoomInfo Intent topics subscribed
  • Clinical "fall prevention", "falls risk assessment", "resident safety", "CDC STEADI", "patient falls"
  • Operational "home care software", "senior living operations", "survey readiness", "QAPI"
  • System "PointClickCare integration", "MatrixCare", "Axxess", "WellSky", "HHAeXchange", "ClearCare"
  • Financial "rehospitalisation", "length of stay retention", "Medicare Advantage quality"
  • Competitive "AI falls detection", "senior care analytics"
Surge signal Threshold Auto-action in Salesforce Rep action
Single-topic surge Score ≥ 75 for 1 week Add Intent_Active__c = true; Hot Accounts report picks it up Review at weekly pipeline
Multi-topic surge Score ≥ 75 for 2+ topics concurrently Tier auto-escalates one step (T3 → T2, T2 → T1); task created for Account Owner, 48h SLA Enrol in segment-specific intent sequence within 48h
Sustained surge Score ≥ 75 for 2 consecutive weeks Account added to "BDR priority" list; task for Account Owner, 24h SLA Multi-thread: 2 personas minimum enrolled
Competitive surge Competitive topic score ≥ 80 Flag Competitive_Research__c = true; Paul notified by Slack Tailored outreach — competitive-specific messaging track

3. ZoomInfo WebSights (reverse-IP)

WebSights identifies the companies visiting able-care.co even when no form is filled. We use it sparingly — a single visit is noise, a pattern is a signal.

What triggers a rule

  • 2+ visits from the same company in 7 days
  • At least one visit to a "high-intent" page: /scorecard, /resources/*, /roi-calculator, /pricing
  • Company matches one of our seven sub-segments in ZoomInfo

What the system does

  • Creates or updates the Account in Salesforce
  • Adds it to the "Warm Accounts — WebSights" report
  • Raises a task for the segment-aligned BDR with a 48h SLA
  • Pulls the 3 most relevant contacts from ZoomInfo into a suggested-contacts list

What we don't do

  • Cold-call an account on a single anonymous visit
  • Email a named contact on a WebSights-only signal (we reach out on LinkedIn first)
  • Send a reverse-IP-triggered email referencing that we saw the visit. Ever.

4. Cadence architecture

We run on Salesforce Sales Engagement (Cadences). The architecture is simple: one cadence per segment-persona-stage, named by a strict convention, pinned to one Salesforce campaign. Reps do not improvise enrolments; enrolment is either automatic (signal-triggered) or from a locked Account Plan.

Cadence family Trigger Touches Length Example name
Cold outbound Rep-initiated from Account Plan 7 (email + LinkedIn + call) 21 days HC-PE_CEO_COLD_v1
Engagement-triggered Asset download / form fill / ROI calculator reveal 4 (2 email + 1 call + 1 LinkedIn) 10 days CCRC_CCO_ENGAGED_v1
Intent-triggered ZoomInfo multi-topic surge 5 (email-heavy) 14 days HC-PE_CEO_INTENT_v1
Reactivation 90-day quiet + prior engagement 5 (email + LinkedIn) 21 days SNF_CNO_REACTIVATE_v1
Event / moment CMS rule change, conference, acquisition announcement 3 (timely + short) 7 days IL-AL_COO_EVENT_v1

Auto-pause triggers

Any one of these pauses the cadence immediately and alerts the Account Owner. This is what prevents us from sending touch 4 on a sequence that has already booked a meeting (one of the most common outbound embarrassments).

  • Prospect replies to any email in the cadence (even a bounceback auto-reply; Owner reviews manually)
  • Prospect books a meeting via the calendar link
  • Prospect opts out / unsubscribes
  • Opportunity is created on the Account
  • Contact’s Lead Status changes to Disqualified, Customer, or Do Not Contact
  • Email bounces hard (single hard bounce = pause; two bounces = Contact flagged and reviewed)

5. Engagement-to-call SLA

Warm leads decay by the hour. A prospect who downloads a whitepaper at 10am Tuesday and hears from us on Thursday afternoon remembers nothing. The SLA is the single rule that protects conversion, and therefore is the single operational KPI that gets a daily report.

Engagement type Lead score add Task SLA Outreach method Escalation if missed
Scorecard reveal (#02) +25 4 hours during business hours Phone call (ZoomInfo direct dial) + same-day email Alerts BDR Manager + auto-reassignment after 8h
ROI calculator completion +20 4 hours Phone call + email offering a 20-min read-out Same as scorecard
Cornerstone asset download (#10, 15, 23, 26, 38) +15 24 hours LinkedIn view + personalised email Alerts BDR Manager after 36h
Standard asset download +10 48 hours Enrol in ENGAGED cadence (email-led) Manager weekly review
Second visit to pricing page +20 4 hours Phone + email Same as scorecard
Email reply with buying language +30 (auto-classified) 2 hours Reply from rep, propose a meeting Manager-reviewed same day
+
SLA scoreboard. The daily 8:30am report shows every engagement still inside or breaching its SLA. Anything amber (< 25% of SLA remaining) or red (breached) is a named accountability item. Nothing hides.

6. Call scripts per segment × persona

Scripts are not monologues. They are a shared opening, a discovery arc, and a disposition. Every rep personalises the middle; the shape stays consistent so reporting is comparable.

Shared structure (all segments)

  1. Opening (15 seconds). Name, company, why calling, the one-line reason specific to their signal. "I’m calling because you downloaded our CDC STEADI primer last week — quick question about what prompted that."
  2. Permission (10 seconds). "Have I caught you at a reasonable moment for 3 minutes, or would another time be better?" Acknowledge a no.
  3. Discovery (2–5 minutes). Three segment-specific questions. Never interrogate — the goal is to understand their situation, not to qualify them against a BANT grid.
  4. Hook (60 seconds). A specific observation relevant to what they just said, tied back to an Able Assess outcome.
  5. Proposal (30 seconds). A concrete next step: a 20-minute demo, a personalised read-out, a send-over of a segment-specific asset.
  6. Disposition. Log outcome in Salesforce immediately after the call.

Discovery question banks

Segment-personaDiscovery questions (choose 3)
HC-PE · CEO Where is clinical quality on your KPI tree this year? How standardised is the falls-risk assessment across your franchises today? What is the sponsor asking you about outcome data? What do you see your competitors doing on clinical tooling?
HC-PE · Chief Clinical Who owns adoption of a new clinical tool across your field staff? How do you measure whether an assessment is being used consistently? What’s your relationship with CDC STEADI today?
HC-PE · Franchise Owner If corporate gave you a new screening tool tomorrow, what would adoption actually look like? What’s the biggest barrier to a change like this — time, training, or clinician buy-in?
CCRC · VP Wellness What are families actually asking about falls on your tours? How do you turn a resident’s risk profile into a conversation? Where does clinical data show up in your sales motion?
CCRC · Chief Clinical How consistent is the falls protocol across your IL, AL, and SNF campuses today? What evidence does your clinical team need before adopting a new tool?
IL+AL · COO What’s your involuntary move-out rate today, and how much of that ties back to falls? Where is length-of-stay going in your portfolio?
SNF · Chief Quality What surveyor citations have you seen trend up in the last 12 months? Where does QAPI fit in your falls protocol today? If a screener slotted into your QAPI programme, who would need to sign off?

7. Lead scoring model

The model is deliberately small. Two axes (Fit and Engagement) with a third modifier (Intent) for ZoomInfo surge. Anyone above both thresholds is an MQL; above both thresholds for a Tier 1 or Tier 2 account is an SAL.

DimensionWhat it measuresScoringWeight
Fit (firmographic) Segment match, account size, ownership type, tier T1 = 30 pts · T2 = 20 pts · T3 = 10 pts · Out of segment = 0 Static; recalculates on account update
Persona fit Is the contact in a target role? Bullseye (CEO, Chief Clinical, VP Wellness, Franchise Owner, COO, Chief Quality, CNO, CIO) = 20 pts · Adjacent (Director, Manager) = 10 pts · Off = 0 Static
Engagement (behavioural) Asset downloads, calculator completions, multi-visit, email opens/replies See SLA table above (+10 to +30 per event) Decay: -5 pts / 30 days of inactivity
Intent (ZoomInfo) Topic surge +15 on single topic surge · +25 on multi-topic surge 30-day rolling
Negative signals Bounces, unsubscribes, DQ disposition Hard bounce = -30 · Unsubscribe = -100 (DNC) · DQ disposition = -50 Immediate

MQL threshold

Total score ≥ 60 AND at least one engagement event in the last 30 days. Triggers cadence enrolment + task for BDR.

SAL threshold

MQL + Tier 1 or Tier 2 + persona fit ≥ 20. Triggers phone outreach within SLA + direct routing to AE if one is named on the account.

Opp-creation threshold

SAL + confirmed discovery call completed + a signed-off ICP fit from the AE. No auto-creation — opps are a human decision.

8. Campaign taxonomy and UTM convention

Clean campaign hygiene is what makes attribution possible. If a prospect touches four campaigns across three quarters, we want to be able to see that. The taxonomy below is simple on purpose, because a complicated taxonomy is an unused taxonomy.

Salesforce Campaign naming

Pattern: SEG_ASSET_CHANNEL_YEAR[-QTR]. Every campaign has an owner, a start date, an end date, and a target ("Campaign Goal").

ExampleMeaning
HC-PE_Playbook_Download_2026PE-backed home care, cornerstone playbook (#10), download-based, 2026 annual
CCRC_Whitepaper_Nurture_2026-Q2CCRCs, STEADI whitepaper (#26), engagement-led, Q2
ALL_Scorecard_App_2026Cross-segment, interactive scorecard (#02), 2026 annual
HC-PE_Intent_Surge_2026-Q2PE-backed home care, intent-triggered, Q2

UTM convention

ParameterValue patternExample
utm_sourceThe mailbox or channel (lowercased)matt-email, linkedin, paul-email
utm_mediumThe motion typeoutbound, nurture, organic, paid
utm_campaignThe segment-asset codehc-pe-playbook, ccrc-steadi, all-scorecard
utm_contentThe sequence touch identifierHC-PE_CEO_COLD_v1-E2
utm_termOptional — persona for manual sendsfranchise-owner

Monthly audit

On the first business day of each month, Marketing Ops runs a five-minute audit:

  • Any Campaign with zero members after 30 days is flagged for closure
  • Any Campaign Member with a broken UTM (non-conforming) is reassigned manually
  • Any Campaign without a named owner gets an assignment task
  • Top-performing campaigns by opps-sourced are surfaced to the weekly team review

9. Disposition codes

Dispositions are how we make reporting honest. One list, used everywhere (email replies, call logs, LinkedIn conversations, meeting outcomes). Every rep trained on them. No free-text "Other."

CodeMeaningNext system action
POS_MEETING_BOOKEDProspect agreed to a meetingCadence pauses; meeting event created; SAL flag set
POS_INTERESTED_LATERInterested but wants a later follow-upCadence pauses; follow-up task created with specified date
POS_REFERNot the right contact but referred us onNew contact created; inherits engagement signal
NEUT_NO_RESPONSENo response after full cadenceCadence ends; contact sits in reactivation pool for 90 days
NEUT_WRONG_TIMENot now; specific reasonCadence pauses; manual reactivation after specified trigger
NEG_NOT_FITOut of ICPLead Status = DQ; Account flagged out-of-segment
NEG_HAS_COMPETITORUsing a competing tool todayFlag competitor name; reactivation sequence at 6–9 months
NEG_DO_NOT_CONTACTOpt-outLead Status = DNC; score set to -100; locked from all future campaigns
NEG_WRONG_CONTACTNo longer at companyContact flagged inactive; ZoomInfo re-verifies
NEG_BOUNCEHard bounce on emailEmail invalid flag; manual review

10. Data hygiene

The database rots if it is not worked. One 30-minute weekly audit + one 2-hour monthly review is enough to keep it clean, provided the rules are automated.

CadenceWhat we checkHow it’s automatedOwner
Daily Hard bounces, DNC requests, opt-outs Auto-flag in Salesforce; daily digest email to Marketing Ops Marketing Ops
Weekly Duplicate accounts / contacts; missing Account Owner; missing Segment or Tier Salesforce duplicate rule + saved report; weekly 30-min triage Marketing Ops + Account Owner
Monthly Stale contacts (no activity 90+ days); campaign membership orphans; cadence performance drift Scheduled ZoomInfo re-verification job; performance dashboard reviewed live Marketing Ops + Paul
Quarterly Full ICP re-scoring; tier reshuffles; out-of-segment accounts archived; asset performance audit Rescoring flow rerun; archival batch; asset matrix updated Paul + Dani + Matt

11. Deliverability stack

We are sending from personal inboxes because that is what works in this buyer segment. Personal-inbox sending is sustainable only with a proper stack underneath. Half-measures here end with Matt’s or Paul’s personal domain blacklisted, which would take weeks to recover from.

Authentication

  • SPF record published for able-care.co with every sending provider included
  • DKIM signed on every mailbox; keys rotated annually
  • DMARC policy set to p=quarantine with rua reports monitored weekly
  • BIMI optional — evaluate Q3 once DMARC is clean

Warm-up

  • Every new sending mailbox runs a 4–6 week warm-up via a dedicated warm-up service (Warmup Inbox, Mailreach, or equivalent)
  • Volume ramp: 5 / 10 / 20 / 30 / 40 sends per day across the first 5 weeks
  • Never cold-send from a new mailbox before inbox-placement score > 95%

Daily caps

  • 40 cold sends per mailbox per day (hard cap)
  • No more than 25% of daily sends to a single domain
  • Warm replies uncapped (they repair reputation)
  • No bulk-send outside of business hours

Content hygiene

  • No tracking pixels on cold sends (trades reporting for deliverability — correct trade)
  • Plain-text HTML only; no heavy images, no footer graphics, no unsubscribe template that looks like a marketing send
  • Links: 1 per email max on cold sends; never a shortener
  • Subject lines: under 60 characters, no ALL CAPS, no "!", no "Re:" fakes

Monitoring

  • Seed-list run weekly in GlockApps (or equivalent)
  • Inbox-placement score published to Slack each Monday
  • Google Postmaster monitored for domain reputation on Gmail
  • Microsoft SNDS monitored for Outlook reputation

Alarms

  • Reply rate < 2% over rolling 7 days → pause & review the sequence
  • Bounce rate > 3% → quarantine mailbox + re-verify list
  • Spam-complaint rate > 0.1% → pause all cold sends, full audit
  • Inbox placement < 90% → revert to warm-up for 14 days
!
Protect the founder domain. Matt’s and Paul’s personal inboxes are strategic assets. Cold sending scales from named secondary mailboxes on a separate sending domain (e.g. ablecare-team.com) with forwarding back to primary. Cold volume never runs through the primary domains. This is the single most important deliverability rule in this entire document.

The ops checklist, for a new BDR

  1. Day 1: Salesforce access, Cadences permissions, ZoomInfo seat, sending mailbox provisioned (still in warm-up)
  2. Day 2: Walk through the seven segments, read the segment drill-downs in this document
  3. Day 3: Review the lead magnet matrix and the three cornerstone playbooks
  4. Day 4: Shadow two live calls with Paul; observe discovery pattern
  5. Day 5: Run the call-script structure against a warm account, with Paul listening
