  6. Week 2: Full-cadence enrolment on a Tier 2 account list (low-risk reps)
  7. Week 3: First Tier 1 multi-thread enrolment with manager review
  8. Week 4: First-month review against KPIs (next section)

KPIs & Reporting

Pass 2 complete

Measurement is how we know the programme is working, and how we find the weakest link each week. A healthy outbound motion has a clear KPI tree that runs from daily activity all the way through to revenue, with one or two metrics at each layer that a manager can look at and act on. Everything below is a metric we intend to review with Paul and Dani; there is no "vanity row."

KPI tree Segment targets Reporting cadence The three charts Weekly scorecard Experiment register

The KPI tree

Five layers, from most leading (activity) to most lagging (revenue). The whole point of the tree is that when a revenue number misses, the miss can be traced upward in minutes, not weeks. If outbound-sourced ARR is down, the question becomes: was it engagement, conversion, or deal quality? Each of those has its own layer.

Layer Metric How it’s measured Who owns it Review cadence
Layer 1 · Activity (most leading)
ActivityTouches sentSalesforce Cadences step events, by rep, by segmentBDRDaily
ActivityDials attemptedZoomInfo-logged + Salesforce TaskBDRDaily
ActivityConnects (conversations > 60s)Dial outcomes in SalesforceBDRWeekly
ActivityLinkedIn actions (views, connects, InMails)LinkedIn Sales Navigator export, weeklyBDRWeekly
Layer 2 · Engagement
EngagementReply rate (all replies / touches)Salesforce Cadences + email providerBDR ManagerWeekly
EngagementPositive reply rateClassified via dispositions: POS_MEETING_BOOKED, POS_INTERESTED_LATER, POS_REFERBDR ManagerWeekly
EngagementAsset downloadsForm fills into Campaign MembersMarketingWeekly
EngagementScorecard / calculator completionsApp event + Lead Score +25MarketingWeekly
EngagementMeetings bookedCalendar link events + Salesforce Meeting objectBDR ManagerWeekly
Layer 3 · Pipeline
PipelineMQLs createdLead Score crosses 60 thresholdMarketing + BDRWeekly
PipelineSALs acceptedAE-accepted discovery callsAEWeekly
PipelineOpportunities created, net-new outboundSalesforce Opp, Source = OutboundAE + BDRWeekly
PipelinePipeline $ created by segmentOpp Amount rolled by segment / tierPaulWeekly
PipelineAverage Opp $ (ACV)Mean Opp Amount on outbound-sourced oppsPaulMonthly
Layer 4 · Health (programme integrity)
HealthInbox placement (seed-list)GlockApps weekly runMarketing OpsWeekly
HealthBounce rateSalesforce Cadences + email providerMarketing OpsDaily
HealthSpam-complaint rateDMARC aggregate reports + providerMarketing OpsWeekly
HealthUnsubscribe rateProvider + Salesforce DNC auditMarketing OpsWeekly
HealthData freshness (contacts updated < 90d)Salesforce report + ZoomInfo re-verifyMarketing OpsMonthly
HealthCadence enrolment hygiene% of active contacts with a current cadenceBDR ManagerWeekly
Layer 5 · Revenue (most lagging)
RevenueOutbound-sourced ARR bookedClosed-Won Opps where Source = OutboundPaulMonthly
RevenueClose rate (Opp → Closed-Won)Closed-Won / (Closed-Won + Closed-Lost)PaulMonthly
RevenueTime-to-first-revenue (first touch → first dollar)Stamped on Account on Closed-WonPaulQuarterly
RevenuePipeline coverage ratio (Pipeline $ / Quota)Live Pipeline $ / Quarterly targetPaulWeekly
!
Conversion ratios that actually matter. The three ratios we watch weekly are: touches → reply (aim ≥ 4%), reply → meeting (aim ≥ 35%), and meeting → SAL (aim ≥ 60%). If any of those three drifts two weeks in a row, that is a named agenda item at the weekly review — even if pipeline $ looks fine.

Segment-level targets through Q4 2026

Targets are set per quarter, per segment, on four metrics: meetings booked, opps created, outbound-sourced pipeline $, and outbound-sourced ARR (closed won). These are not sandbags; they are the numbers the programme is committing to. If the programme is under-performing, re-forecasting happens in the monthly, not by lowering the quarterly.

Segment Tier Q1 2026 meetings Q2 2026 meetings Q3 2026 meetings Q4 2026 meetings 2026 pipeline $ target 2026 ARR target
PE-backed / franchised home careT18182224$3.2M$950K
Independent multi-site home careT2491214$1.1M$320K
Corporate CCRCsT16141820$2.6M$780K
Rental retirement IL+ALT2381112$1.4M$420K
IL-only corporateT32578$700K$210K
AL-only corporateT237910$950K$280K
For-profit SNF chainsT226910$900K$260K
Total28678898$10.85M$3.22M

Targets assume: 4% reply rate, 35% reply→meeting conversion, 55% meeting→SAL, 35% SAL→Opp, 30% Opp→Closed-Won at an average ACV of $35K. Revisit the assumption set at the end of each quarter.

Reporting cadence

Four review rhythms, each with a fixed agenda and owner. Anything outside one of these rhythms is not reviewed, full stop — this is how we avoid the meeting sprawl that kills most outbound programmes.

Daily · 15 min

Owner: BDR Manager
Participants: BDRs
Agenda: SLA queue (breaches + at-risk), inbox-placement score, top 3 engaged accounts overnight.
Output: named action per breach; sequence adjustments if deliverability slips.

Weekly · 60 min

Owner: Matt
Participants: Paul, Dani, BDR Manager
Agenda: activity & engagement vs target, the three conversion ratios, top 10 accounts to action, sequence performance, any experiment conclusions.
Output: decisions on tier escalations, sequence pauses / restarts, content sequencing.

Monthly · 90 min

Owner: Paul
Participants: Matt, Dani, AEs
Agenda: pipeline review by segment, asset ROI review, messaging experiment review, target re-forecast only if variance > 15%.
Output: segment-level resource shifts, roadmap adjustment, signed-off monthly report to stakeholders.

Quarterly · half day

Owner: Matt & Paul
Participants: full team
Agenda: segment performance review, Tier 1 roster reset, ICP reconfirmation, messaging architecture refresh, content backlog prioritisation.
Output: next-quarter plan; named changes to sequences and asset library.

The three charts that tell the whole story

Every operator we respect has a small number of charts they look at, not a dashboard with 40 tiles. Three charts here, visualised below with sample Q1 data so the format is unambiguous. These are the live versions that will be rebuilt each week from Salesforce reports.

Chart 1 · Outbound pipeline sourced, by segment

Reads: stacked bar, by month. Each stack is a segment. The headline number at the top of every weekly review.

Chart 2 · Engagement funnel

Reads: horizontal bars, absolute counts at each stage plus conversion rate between stages. Makes the weakest ratio visible at a glance.

Chart 3 · Asset ROI (meetings booked per 100 downloads)

Reads: bar chart, by asset. Cornerstone assets expected to outperform; if a cornerstone sits in the bottom third two quarters in a row, retire or rewrite.

The weekly scorecard

One page, pasted into the top of the Monday email to Paul and Dani. No attachments, no links, just the numbers. Every metric has a current value, a target, a RAG status, and a one-line commentary. The format is deliberately identical each week so drift is visible.

MetricThis weekLast weekTargetStatusCommentary
Touches sent420398400OnSteady; HC-PE slightly ahead, CCRC slightly behind.
Reply rate4.2%4.5%4.0%OnGood. Scorecard-led sequences outperforming cold by 2x.
Positive reply rate1.6%1.8%1.5%OnLargely stable.
Meetings booked767OnOne CCRC Tier 1 booked; Dani attending discovery.
MQLs11910OnScorecard driving the volume.
SALs455WatchOne AE slow to accept — reviewed with Paul.
Opps created232OnBoth HC-PE, both Tier 1.
Outbound pipeline $ (running)$1.18M$1.02M$1.10MOnQ2 on track.
Inbox placement96%97%≥95%OnNo drift.
Bounce rate1.9%2.1%<3%OnList hygiene holding.
Top 3 at-risk accounts1. Right at Home — Chief Clinical not responding to 5 touches; escalate to CEO track. 2. Senior Helpers — Chandler engaged on asset #26, no follow-up SLA met, breach logged. 3. Help at Home — intent surge on PointClickCare, no cadence enrolled yet.

Numbers shown above are illustrative sample data so the format is unambiguous.

Experiment register

Every experiment is logged: hypothesis, change, segments affected, start date, end date, success criterion, result. A running register prevents the familiar trap of "we tried that before" without anyone remembering what actually happened. Experiments are reviewed monthly.

IDHypothesisChangeSegmentDatesSuccess criterionResult
EXP-001Subject lines under 5 words outperform longer subjects in HC-PEA/B 50/50 across HC-PE_CEO_COLD_v1 E1HC-PEApr 21 – May 19Reply rate uplift > 25% with p<0.1Running
EXP-002Scorecard link in touch 1 outperforms playbook linkSwap asset link between sequence variantsHC-PE, CCRCApr 28 – May 26Click-through uplift > 30%Running
EXP-003LinkedIn-first sequences outperform email-first in IL+ALNew sequence variant IL-AL_COO_LI-FIRST_v1IL+ALMay 5 – Jun 2Positive reply rate > email-first variantPlanned
EXP-004"Dani-signed" send variant outperforms "Matt-signed" on clinical personasSender name swap, identical bodyAll clinical personasMay 12 – Jun 9Reply rate uplift > 20%Planned
+
The discipline that makes reporting real. Every number in every chart maps back to a named Salesforce report or a named dashboard tile. No slide deck builds its own numbers; if a figure can't be sourced from a saved report, it doesn't go in the scorecard. This is also why disposition hygiene in the Ops section above is the linchpin of this entire section.

90-Day Roadmap

Pass 3 deliverable

The roadmap commits the first 90 days week by week. Named owners, dependencies, risks. Every other section in this document feeds into a delivery date here. Pass 3 turns this into the single page Dani can take into a monthly with Paul.

Pass 3 · Week-by-week plan coming

Phase 1 · Weeks 1–4

Foundation. ZoomInfo × Salesforce integration verified. Sending domains warmed. Tier 1 account list hardened with Paul and Dani. First three lead magnets published. First cold sequences loaded into Sales Engagement.

Phase 2 · Weeks 5–8

First sequences live for Tier 1 home care corporate HQs. Engagement-triggered call workflow active with 24-hour SLA. First cohort of engaged accounts moving into discovery. Weekly reporting cadence established.

Phase 3 · Weeks 9–12

Tier 2 activation across senior living. Second wave of lead magnets (deep-invest + interactive tools). First pipeline review and segment-level optimisation. Q2 plan locked.

What Pass 3 adds

  • Week-by-week task list with named owners across Marketing, BD, Paul, Dani, Sales Ops.
  • Dependency graph: what has to ship before the next thing can start.
  • Risks register with mitigations (deliverability, content production velocity, Tier 1 account availability).
  • Quarterly milestones: Q1 (motion live), Q2 (scale to Tier 2 at volume), Q3 (optimise), Q4 (plan 2027).

LinkedIn Marketing

The Able Care LinkedIn agent system. Strategy pack, agent specs, voice profiles, operations and demo materials, rendered from the source markdown files in C:\Users\matty\AbleCare\AI Marketing Action Plan\LinkedIn Agent System\. Every document is ready for Dani to sign off, or for Paul and Mike to read end-to-end.

Strategy pack for sign-off

i
For Dani sign-off. A two-agent LinkedIn content system for Dr Paul Rinne (CEO), Dr Mike Mace (CTO) and Danielle Richards (Commercial Director). Draft v1, 23 April 2026. Owner: Matt. Approver: Dani. Source files: C:\Users\matty\AbleCare\AI Marketing Action Plan\LinkedIn Agent System\.

Every document in the strategy pack is rendered below in the order Dani, Paul and Mike should read it. UK English throughout, no em dashes, every claim sourced.

What this is

A research agent scans the industry weekly. A ghostwriter drafts two posts per leader per week against approved evidence and approved corporate news. Dani signs off. Published to the three leaders' personal profiles.

Why personal profiles

LinkedIn currently gives personal profiles roughly five to six times the organic reach of company pages. Engagement rates on personal profiles run at four to eight per cent. The Able Care company page amplifies via reposts.

How it avoids the AI-tell

Readers identify AI-written posts above 60 to 70 per cent when posts lean on clichéd openers and generic advice. The fix is specificity: named studies, named clients, dates, exact numbers, and the one detail only the author would know. The Authentic Content Skill enforces all of this automatically.

Pilot plan at a glance

LengthFour weeks, starting the Monday after sign-off.
CadenceTwo posts per leader per week. Six posts total per week.
Sign-offDani reviews weekly, alongside the blog and KB sign-off lists.
SchedulingBuffer for the pilot. Taplio evaluated in month two.
Vault pathC:\Users\matty\AbleCare\AbleCare-KB, branch linkedin-agent-system.

Read order

This folder holds the Able Care LinkedIn agent strategy and demo pack. Read the files in this order.

  1. 00 - Strategy Overview.md, the plan, the architecture, the evidence gate and the vault path.
  2. 01 - Research Agent Spec.md, what the research agent does, with a runnable prompt.
  3. 02 - Ghostwriter Agent Spec.md, what the ghostwriter does, with three runnable prompts (one per leader), the corporate-news variant, and the evidence gate.
  4. 03 - Voice Profile - Paul Rinne.md, Dr Paul Rinne's voice with three anchor posts.
  5. 04 - Voice Profile - Mike Mace.md, Dr Mike Mace's voice with three anchor posts.
  6. 05 - Voice Profile - Danielle Richards.md, Dani's voice with three anchor posts.
  7. 06 - KB Branch Structure.md, the new folders inside the Obsidian vault and the templates to create.
  8. 07 - Sample Posts.md, six posts, two per leader, all claims sourced from the Claims Guardrail Library v2.
  9. 08 - Demo Script.md, the forty-five-minute demo walkthrough.
  10. 09 - News and Campaigns Schedule.md, how corporate news, product releases, events and campaign milestones are captured into the system for the ghostwriter to use.
  11. 10 - Authentic Content Skill.md, the editable style rulebook that governs every draft. Edit this file to change the writing style across every future post in one place.

All files UK English, no em dashes, every claim sourced.

Sign-off: Dani.

Strategy

00. Strategy Overview

Owner: Matt Sign-off: Dani (Danielle Richards, Commercial Director) Date: 2026-04-23 Status: Draft v1 for demo

1. Purpose

Build a two-agent system that produces LinkedIn content for three Able Care leaders: Dr Paul Rinne (CEO), Dr Mike Mace (CTO) and Danielle Richards (Commercial Director). The pattern is a common best-practice one for organisations that want thought-leadership content published through named individuals rather than a brand channel: content is created centrally so quality and evidence can be controlled, and delivered through personal profiles so reach is not choked by company-page limits. A research agent keeps a living knowledge base up to date. A ghostwriter agent drafts posts in each leader's voice against approved evidence and approved corporate news.

The content must read as intelligent, cautious, evidence-led and unmistakably human. The two clinicians are nervous about posting. The system is designed around that nervousness, not against it.

2. Why this model

Three findings shape the design.

Personal profiles out-perform company pages on LinkedIn. The platform's algorithm currently gives personal profiles roughly five to six times the organic reach of company pages, and engagement rates on personal profiles tend to run at four to eight per cent, compared with under one per cent on pages (LinkedIn algorithm research, Q1 2026). Able Care already has a company page. The reach lever is the leaders' personal profiles.

AI detection is now a meaningful risk. Readers identify AI-written posts at rates above sixty to seventy per cent when the posts lean on clichéd openers, template structures, generic advice, and padded phrases like "I am pleased to announce" (industry writing research, 2025-26). The fix is specificity: named studies, named clients, dates, exact numbers, and the one detail only the author would know. The ghostwriter agent is built around this.

Clinicians who post well on LinkedIn tend to sit in one of two camps: the evidence-first educator (Eric Topol, Peter Attia, Jennie Byrne) who cites sources and explains why something matters, or the story-first practitioner (Louis Profeta) who uses real cases to surface a lesson. For Able Care, the evidence-first mode fits the two doctors best. Dani can run closer to operator storytelling because her audience is commercial.

3. The four building blocks

Four building blocks make this system work for Able Care specifically.

First, a knowledge-base architecture inside the Obsidian vault: 01-research, 02-insights, 03-voice-profiles, 04-post-ideas, 05-drafts, 06-approved, 07-performance, 08-news-and-campaigns, templates. This separates the four stages of LinkedIn content cleanly: what the world is saying, what we think about it, what each leader would actually publish, and what performed.

Second, an evidence gate. Every post must pass against the existing Claims Guardrail Library (Marketing Sign-Off/02 - Claims Guardrail Library v2.md). No claim ships without a cited source or an approved paraphrase. This is non-negotiable for the two doctors.

Third, voice profiles with anchor posts. The ghostwriter needs a small corpus of exemplar posts per leader to match cadence, not just a tone description. The voice profile files hold three to five anchor posts each, with the sentence-level patterns the ghostwriter should replicate.

Fourth, a segment-aware research loop and a corporate news intake. Able Care's target markets are already defined in Falls Prevention Commercialization Strategy 2026.md and the six segment briefs in /briefs/. The research agent runs against that list, not the whole healthcare space. That keeps the external signal focused on PE-backed home care, CCRCs, rental retirement, IL, AL, SNF, and the clinical conditions and commercial levers that touch those buyers. The corporate news intake, documented in 09 - News and Campaigns Schedule.md, adds a second source: the internal events, releases, hires, partnerships, press pick-ups and campaign milestones that leaders will want to comment on in their own voice.

The client vault path the user asked for is:

C:\Users\matty\AbleCare\AbleCare-KB

This is the existing Obsidian vault (kepano skills plugin installed). All new folders sit inside AbleCare-KB/Able Care/.

4. Platform, brands, frequency

Platform for the demo is LinkedIn only. X is deferred. Posts are published to the three leaders' personal profiles, not the Able Care company page. The company page amplifies via reposts.

Three personal brands, not a single company brand. Each leader has a distinct voice profile (see 03, 04, 05 in this folder). The Able Care company page remains a secondary distribution channel.

Recommended posting cadence for the pilot: two posts per leader per week, six posts per week across the three. This is deliberately conservative. Two reasons: the doctors are cautious and one extra review cycle protects them, and Dani's sign-off capacity is finite. We can step up to three per week per leader in month two if engagement and approval speed hold.

Content mix per leader, per week: - One evidence-led post drawn from the research insights (the industry-signal side). - One corporate-news or operator-voice post drawn from the News and Campaigns Schedule (the internal-signal side).

That mix prevents the feed from reading like a journal club and ensures Able Care's own milestones, hires, partnerships and campaign moments get narrated in the leaders' voices rather than in a sterile company-page announcement.

5. Architecture

Two agents, one knowledge base, one approval gate.

+---------------------------------------------------------------+
|                 Knowledge Base (Obsidian vault)               |
|              C:\Users\matty\AbleCare\AbleCare-KB              |
|                                                               |
|   01-research  02-insights  03-voice-profiles 04-post-ideas   |
|   05-drafts    06-approved  07-performance                    |
|   08-news-and-campaigns     templates/                        |
|                                                               |
|   + existing: Evidence & Claims, Segments, Competitors,       |
|     Content Marketing, Sales Enablement, Website              |
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
                     ^                         |
                     |                         v
        +------------+-----+       +-----------+-----------+
        | Research Agent   |       | Ghostwriter Agent     |
        | weekly scan      |       | 2 posts per leader    |
        | logs to          |       | per week, pulled      |
        | 01-research,     |       | from 02-insights,     |
        | distils to       |       | 04-post-ideas, and    |
        | 02-insights      |       | 08-news-and-campaigns,|
        +------------------+       | written in 03/04/05   |
                                   | voice profiles        |
        +------------------+       +-----------+-----------+
        | News Intake      |                    ^
        | weekly log of    |--------------------+
        | internal events, |
        | releases,        |
        | partnerships,    |
        | campaigns, to    |
        | 08-news-and-     |
        | campaigns        |
        +------------------+
                                               |
                                               v
                                   +-----------------------+
                                   | 05-drafts             |
                                   | + evidence gate       |
                                   | (Claims Guardrail)    |
                                   +-----------+-----------+
                                               |
                                               v
                                   +-----------------------+
                                   | Dani approval + leader|
                                   | tweak (Blog Sign-off  |
                                   | List pattern)         |
                                   +-----------+-----------+
                                               |
                                               v
                                   +-----------------------+
                                   | 06-approved --> Buffer|
                                   | (or Taplio) --> each  |
                                   | leader's LinkedIn     |
                                   +-----------------------+

The two agents are implemented as Claude skills, runnable from the Cowork CLI against the vault. Scheduling and publishing is a commodity layer (Buffer for the pilot, Taplio in month two if volume grows). We do not build LinkedIn OAuth from scratch.

Performance data flows back into 07-performance/ and feeds the next research cycle.

6. The brain, in practical terms

The brain is the vault. Specifically, four things the ghostwriter reads every time it drafts:

  1. The active insight file for that week, in 02-insights/. This is what the research agent distilled from the last seven days of industry signal. Or, for a corporate-news post, the relevant entry in 08-news-and-campaigns/.
  2. The voice profile for the leader being written for, in 03-voice-profiles/.
  3. The relevant segment brief from /briefs/ and the claims library from Marketing Sign-Off/02 - Claims Guardrail Library v2.md.
  4. The authentic-content skill, 10 - Authentic Content Skill.md. This is the sentence-level style rulebook. It is the single document Dani, Paul or Mike can edit to correct the writing style across every future post in one place.

No post draft is written without those four inputs. The prompts in 02 - Ghostwriter Agent Spec.md enforce this.

The brain evolves because the research agent logs every source it consumes with date, title, URL and one-line relevance note. Over time, 01-research/source-notes/ becomes a dated, searchable record of what the industry was saying when. That gives Dr Rinne and Dr Mace the ability to say "in January, three studies on X" with the receipts sitting in the vault.

7. The demo

The demo produces a tangible pack Dani and the two doctors can react to in one sitting.

Pack contents:

  • This strategy overview (the thing you are reading).
  • Research agent spec with a runnable prompt. One sample research run output sitting in 01-research/ and 02-insights/.
  • Ghostwriter agent spec with three runnable prompts, one per leader.
  • Six sample LinkedIn posts, two per leader, each drafted against the Claims Guardrail Library and written to pass the "does this sound like AI" test.
  • One-page demo script for the walk-through.

The demo answer we want from the two doctors is "yes, that sounds like me" or "no, change these three things." The voice profiles are built to make that feedback loop fast.

8. How this connects to the Knowledge Base website

The able-care-marketing-audit site will eventually host a public-facing LinkedIn Insights section. The architecture is already set up for it: articles live as JSON content files at AbleCare-Repo/content/current-knowledge/[slug].json per the Knowledge Base strategy doc, rendered at /current-knowledge/[slug]/.

The link between the two is one-way. When a LinkedIn post performs well and the topic warrants a deeper piece, the insight gets expanded into a full KB article. The KB article then feeds future LinkedIn posts (the leaders can reference their own published work). That is the flywheel. The LinkedIn agents do not publish to the website directly. The content team does, against the existing sign-off process.

A new section, /linkedin/ or /thought-leadership/, can host a curated view of the leaders' best LinkedIn posts with source links. That is a Phase 2 build.

9. Risks and guardrails

The two doctors must never publish a clinical claim that is not in the Claims Guardrail Library. The ghostwriter prompt refuses to include any statistic that does not come with a source_url from the library. If the research agent surfaces a new claim, it sits in 01-research/ until Paul or Mike explicitly promotes it to the library.

Contentious topics are blocked. The prompts enforce: no commentary on drug pricing, no FDA or regulator criticism, no political content, no direct comparison to named competitors, no criticism of specific care providers. This list lives in 02 - Ghostwriter Agent Spec.md and is reviewed monthly.

AI-tell patterns are blocked. Em dashes, exclamation marks, "in today's fast-paced world" openers, "it is critical that" padding, overused corporate vocabulary (delve, synergy, pivotal, tapestry, landscape). Listed in full in the ghostwriter spec.

Tone drift is detected by sampling. Every month, Dani reviews a random five posts against the three voice profiles and flags drift. The voice profile files are updated with the correction, and the next batch is regenerated.

10. What ships this week for the demo

  • Folder structure created inside AbleCare-KB/Able Care/
  • Research agent spec and first research run (sample week)
  • Ghostwriter agent spec
  • Three voice profiles (Dr Rinne, Dr Mace, Dani)
  • Six sample posts
  • One-page demo script
  • News and Campaigns Schedule with the intake workflow
  • Authentic Content Skill with the editable style rulebook

All files sit in this folder: C:\Users\matty\AbleCare\AI Marketing Action Plan\LinkedIn Agent System.

11. Open questions for Dani

  1. Posting cadence for the pilot: two per leader per week, or three?
  2. Approval flow: Dani reviews all six posts each week, or each leader self-approves after the first month?
  3. Scheduling tool: Buffer for the pilot and re-evaluate in month two, or go straight to Taplio?
  4. Who owns the research agent run: Matt weekly, or a scheduled task?
  5. Is it acceptable for Dr Rinne and Dr Mace to comment on other clinicians' public posts as part of the weekly cadence, or purely first-person posting only?

Sources referenced in this strategy

  • Falls Prevention Commercialization Strategy 2026, C:\Users\matty\AbleCare\Falls Prevention Commercialization Strategy 2026.md
  • AI Marketing Action Plan Able Care 2026, C:\Users\matty\AbleCare\AI Marketing Action Plan\AI Marketing Action Plan - Able Care 2026.md
  • Claims Guardrail Library v2, C:\Users\matty\AbleCare\Marketing Sign-Off\02 - Claims Guardrail Library v2.md
  • Segment briefs, C:\Users\matty\AbleCare\briefs\
  • AbleCare-KB vault, C:\Users\matty\AbleCare\AbleCare-KB

The two agents

01. Research Agent Spec

Role

Scan the last seven days of signal in Able Care's target markets and distil the findings into a single weekly insights note. Log every source with a date, title, URL and one-line relevance note. Do not draft posts. Do not editorialise. The agent's job is to assemble the raw material the ghostwriter agent will use.

The agent runs weekly. One run produces one insights note plus updated source logs.

Scope

Target markets, in priority order (from Falls Prevention Commercialization Strategy 2026, Section 1.1 onwards):

  1. PE-backed and franchised home care, primary target
  2. Independent multi-site home care
  3. CCRCs (continuing care retirement communities), independent and corporate
  4. Rental retirement communities (IL and AL), corporate
  5. IL-only corporate
  6. AL-only corporate
  7. For-profit skilled nursing facilities, corporate

Topic lenses the agent scans through, per cycle:

  • Falls prevention policy, evidence and practice (CDC STEADI updates, HHVBP changes, new peer-reviewed studies)
  • Grip strength as a biomarker (new studies, clinical applications)
  • Functional health assessment (new tools, competitor moves, workflow research)
  • Senior care operator pressures (staff turnover, margin compression, PE deal activity)
  • Longevity and healthspan narratives relevant to payers and clinicians
  • Regulator signals (CMS, FDA, NHS, NICE) where they intersect with any of the above
  • Able Care internal signal (the corporate news and announcements side): product releases, clinical pilot results, new hires, partnerships, press pick-ups, conference appearances, grant and award news, campaign launches. The agent does not go hunting for these. It reads them from 08-news-and-campaigns/ where Dani logs them weekly. See 09 - News and Campaigns Schedule.md for the intake workflow.

Explicit exclusions: general wellness content, consumer-facing supplement or biohacking commentary, politically loaded longevity debates (Attia versus Topol style), any content that asks clinicians to take sides.

Input sources, ranked by trust

Tier 1, peer-reviewed and authoritative: - PubMed search: grip strength, falls prevention, sarcopenia, functional assessment, senior care outcomes - BMJ, Lancet, JAMA, JAMDA, Age and Ageing, Gerontology - CDC STEADI and MMWR - Cochrane Reviews

Tier 2, industry and trade: - McKnight's Home Care and McKnight's Senior Living - Home Health Care News - Senior Housing News - Modern Healthcare - FierceHealthcare

Tier 3, company and analyst: - CMS press releases and HHVBP updates - PE deal activity in senior care (PitchBook summaries, Private Equity International, AXIOS Pro Dealmaker) - Competitor blogs and press releases: SafelyYou, Linus Health, VirtuSense, Zibrio, Nymbl Science, BioSensics

Tier 4, signal amplification: - LinkedIn posts from the three senior leaders' networks (Paul Rinne, Mike Mace, Danielle Richards first-degree connections), for tone and topic calibration, not for citation - LinkedIn posts from named credible clinicians in adjacent spaces (e.g. Eric Topol, Jennie Byrne), for tone reference only

Tier 1 and 2 sources are citable. Tier 3 is citable for market and competitive context. Tier 4 is never citable in a post.

Output structure inside the vault

All outputs sit inside AbleCare-KB/Able Care/01-research/ and 02-insights/.

01-research/industry-monitoring/YYYY-MM-DD-weekly-scan.md

One file per weekly run. Structured as follows:

---
run_date: 2026-04-23
agent: research-agent-v1
segments_covered: [home-care-pe-backed, ccrc, rental-retirement]
total_sources_reviewed: 42
total_sources_logged: 12
---

# Weekly Industry Scan: 23 April 2026

## Summary
Three-sentence summary of the most significant signal this week.

## Signals by topic

### Falls prevention policy and practice
- **2026-04-18**, [Title of study or article](https://url), Journal name, 2026. Relevance: single sentence. Tier: 1.
- ...

### Grip strength as a biomarker
- ...

### Senior care operator pressures
- ...

## Competitor activity
- ...

## New claims flagged for Paul or Mike review
- "Specific statistic", [Source](URL). Candidate for addition to Claims Guardrail Library after clinical review.

01-research/source-notes/YYYY-MM-DD-[slug].md

One file per source that the agent considers genuinely useful. Short, structured. Contains the author, publication, date, URL, two-paragraph summary, the specific statistics or quotes that matter, and a line flagging whether the source is citable.

01-research/themes/[theme-slug].md

Living files that accumulate evidence over time. Example themes: falls-prevention-workforce-problem.md, grip-strength-as-hospitalization-predictor.md, hhvbp-falls-measure-timeline.md. Each time the agent finds new evidence on a theme, it appends to the relevant file rather than starting fresh.

02-insights/YYYY-MM-DD-insight-[slug].md

One file per distilled insight, usually three to five per weekly run. This is the raw material the ghostwriter reads. Structure:

---
insight_date: 2026-04-23
source_run: 2026-04-23-weekly-scan
segments: [home-care-pe-backed]
audience: [pe-operating-partner, corporate-coo]
angle: market-insight
strength: high
cited_claims:
  - claim: "Specific statistic"
    source_title: "Paper title"
    source_url: "https://doi.org/..."
    year: "2026"
    reviewed_by: "Dr Paul Rinne"
    approved_on: null
---

# Insight: One-sentence headline

## What happened
Two sentences.

## Why it matters for Able Care's segments
Three to four sentences. Names the specific segment. Names the specific buyer role.

## Post angles
- Market insight angle for Paul: "..."
- Technical angle for Mike: "..."
- Operator angle for Dani: "..."

## Evidence base
- [Source 1](URL), title, year, tier 1
- [Source 2](URL), title, year, tier 2

Insights without at least one Tier 1 or Tier 2 source cannot be escalated to drafts. The ghostwriter prompt enforces this.

The runnable prompt

The agent is run as a Claude skill. Copy the block below, paste into a new Cowork session opened against the AbleCare-KB vault, and run.

You are the Able Care Research Agent. Your job is to assemble a weekly industry scan and distil it into insights files for the ghostwriter agent to use.

Before you do anything, read these files in full:

- AbleCare-KB/Able Care/00 - Map of Content.md
- AbleCare-KB/Able Care/Competitors - Landscape Overview.md
- AbleCare-KB/Able Care/Market - Size and Trends.md
- Marketing Sign-Off/02 - Claims Guardrail Library v2.md
- Falls Prevention Commercialization Strategy 2026.md (Section 1.1 and Section 2)
- briefs/home-care-pe-backed.md
- briefs/senior-living-ccrc.md
- briefs/senior-living-rental-retirement.md

Also list the existing files in:
- AbleCare-KB/Able Care/01-research/industry-monitoring/
- AbleCare-KB/Able Care/02-insights/
- AbleCare-KB/Able Care/01-research/themes/

You are now ready to run.

Step 1. Scan. Using web search, find signal published in the last seven days across these topic lenses:
1) falls prevention policy, evidence, practice
2) grip strength as a biomarker
3) functional health assessment
4) senior care operator pressures (home care and senior living)
5) longevity and healthspan narratives relevant to clinicians
6) regulator signals (CMS, FDA, NHS, NICE)

Also read AbleCare-KB/Able Care/08-news-and-campaigns/current-quarter.md and today's entries in 08-news-and-campaigns/weekly-intake/. This is the internal corporate news side. Do not web-search for these. Use what Dani has logged.

Only log sources from Tier 1 (peer-reviewed journals, CDC, CMS, Cochrane) or Tier 2 (named trade press: McKnight's Home Care, McKnight's Senior Living, Home Health Care News, Senior Housing News, Modern Healthcare, FierceHealthcare) or Tier 3 (CMS releases, named analyst deal notes, named competitor press releases from SafelyYou, Linus Health, VirtuSense, Zibrio, Nymbl Science, BioSensics).

Do not log Tier 4 content. Do not log any content from consumer wellness publications, general news aggregators, or opinion-driven newsletters.

Step 2. Write the weekly scan file to:
AbleCare-KB/Able Care/01-research/industry-monitoring/YYYY-MM-DD-weekly-scan.md

Use the template in AbleCare-KB/Able Care/templates/weekly-scan-template.md. Fill every frontmatter field. Every source gets a date, title, URL, publication, tier and one-sentence relevance note.

Step 3. For each genuinely useful source (expect 8 to 15 per run), write a source note at:
AbleCare-KB/Able Care/01-research/source-notes/YYYY-MM-DD-[slug].md

Use the template in AbleCare-KB/Able Care/templates/source-note-template.md.

Step 4. Append to any existing theme files in AbleCare-KB/Able Care/01-research/themes/ where new evidence has emerged. Create new theme files only if a genuinely new theme has formed across three or more sources this week.

Step 5. Distil three to five insights to:
AbleCare-KB/Able Care/02-insights/YYYY-MM-DD-insight-[slug].md

Use the template in AbleCare-KB/Able Care/templates/insight-template.md. For each insight, draft three post angles, one for Dr Paul Rinne, one for Dr Mike Mace, one for Danielle Richards, but do NOT draft full posts. The ghostwriter agent does that.

Step 5b. For each active entry in 08-news-and-campaigns/, create a matching insight file in 02-insights/ tagged with `angle: corporate-news`. Keep these separate from the industry-signal insights. The same template applies. Every corporate news item goes into the insights queue only if Dani has marked it `ready-for-linkedin: true` in the news entry frontmatter.

Step 6. If you surfaced any new statistic or claim that is not already in the Claims Guardrail Library, list it in the "New claims flagged for Paul or Mike review" section of the weekly scan. Do not propagate the claim into any insight file until a clinician has approved it.

Rules:
- UK English throughout.
- No em dashes.
- Short declarative sentences.
- Do not editorialise. Your job is assembly and distillation, not argument.
- Every claim in an insight file must have a cited source URL.
- Never cite a LinkedIn post as a source for a claim.
- If you cannot find three strong insights this week, write two. Do not pad.

When you finish, write a one-paragraph end-of-run summary to chat: what segments were covered, how many sources logged, how many insights distilled, and any new claims that need clinician review.

Run cadence

Monday morning, weekly. One run, roughly forty minutes of agent time plus Matt's ten-minute review before insights go to the ghostwriter.

The run can be triggered manually for the demo. Once the pattern is proven, move to a scheduled task using the Cowork schedule skill.

What the agent does not do

  • Does not draft posts. The ghostwriter does that.
  • Does not promote claims into the Claims Guardrail Library. Only Paul or Mike can do that.
  • Does not run against competitor LinkedIn accounts for content ideas. Only for market awareness.
  • Does not use web search for clinical conclusions. It cites peer-reviewed literature only for anything a clinician would put their name to.
  • Does not run autonomously on a schedule until the first four manual runs have been reviewed and the output quality confirmed.

02. Ghostwriter Agent Spec

Role

Draft LinkedIn posts in the voice of one named Able Care leader, against a specific approved insight, and against that leader's voice profile. One run produces one post. The agent never composes insights. It never ships without citations. It never invents a claim.

The output must read as human-written. The bar is Eric Topol or Jennie Byrne calibre for the two doctors, and operator-credible first-person writing for Dani.

Inputs

Every run requires five files as input:

  1. The target insight file: AbleCare-KB/Able Care/02-insights/YYYY-MM-DD-insight-[slug].md. For a corporate-news post, the source is the matching entry in AbleCare-KB/Able Care/08-news-and-campaigns/.
  2. The voice profile for the leader being written for: AbleCare-KB/Able Care/03-voice-profiles/[leader].md.
  3. The Authentic Content Skill: 10 - Authentic Content Skill.md. This is the sentence-level style rulebook. The agent reads this every run. If Dani, Paul or Mike have edited it, the edit takes effect on the next draft.
  4. The Claims Guardrail Library: Marketing Sign-Off/02 - Claims Guardrail Library v2.md.
  5. The relevant segment brief, if one is named in the insight: briefs/[segment].md.

If any of these is missing, the agent stops and asks for the missing input.

Output

One markdown file per post, written to:

AbleCare-KB/Able Care/05-drafts/YYYY-MM-DD-[leader]-[slug].md

Structure:

---
leader: paul-rinne
insight_source: 02-insights/2026-04-23-insight-hhvbp-falls-measure.md
post_angle: market-insight  # or: technical | operator | corporate-news
target_segment: home-care-pe-backed
target_audience: [pe-operating-partner, corporate-coo]
length_words: 180
cited_claims:
  - claim: "HHVBP expanded nationally in January 2023"
    source_title: "CMS HHVBP Expansion Fact Sheet"
    source_url: "https://cms.gov/..."
    claims_library_ref: "Claim - HHVBP Falls Measure.md"
evidence_gate_passed: true
ai_tell_check_passed: true
contentious_topic_check_passed: true
drafted_on: 2026-04-23
approval_status: pending
approver: dani
---

# Draft: [slug]

[Post body, ready to paste to LinkedIn]

---

## Why this works for [leader]

Short note explaining the cadence, the opening hook, and any choices made against the voice profile.

## Suggested first comment
Optional follow-up comment for the leader to post under their own post, to add a specific detail or a source link.

## Suggested publish day and time
Tuesday 08:30 UK (from voice profile preference).

## Notes for Dani's review
- Any specific line Dani should sanity-check.
- Any claim that sits close to the Claims Guardrail boundary.

The three voice-specific prompts

Three separate prompt blocks. Each prompt is invoked once per post. Never combine.

Prompt A: Dr Paul Rinne

You are ghostwriting a LinkedIn post in Dr Paul Rinne's voice for his personal LinkedIn profile.

Before drafting, read in full:
- AbleCare-KB/Able Care/03-voice-profiles/paul-rinne.md
- The insight file passed to you as input
- Marketing Sign-Off/02 - Claims Guardrail Library v2.md
- briefs/[segment].md if the insight names a segment

Voice anchors:
- Paul is CEO and a PhD neuroscientist. He writes as a clinician-founder who has spent fifteen years on this problem at Imperial College London.
- He leads with evidence. He never leads with hype.
- He is cautious and measured. He does not use rhetorical questions as hooks.
- He writes in first person singular. He names studies, authors and institutions.
- He is comfortable being the person who says "we do not know yet" when that is the honest answer.
- He writes short paragraphs. Rarely more than three sentences per paragraph.
- He signs off with a useful question, not a sales CTA.

Post structure for Paul:
1. A concrete hook. Often a named study, a specific number, or a clinical observation. Never a rhetorical question. Never "In today's...".
2. A two or three sentence explanation of what the evidence says and where it comes from.
3. One sentence on what that means for patients, clinicians or healthcare systems. He speaks to "we", the field, not to "you", the reader as prospect.
4. A closing thought that invites a specific kind of reply, or a question a colleague would want to answer.

Length: 150 to 220 words. Never longer. Preferably around 180.

Hard rules:
- UK English throughout.
- No em dashes. Use commas, full stops or parentheses instead.
- No exclamation marks.
- No corporate clichés: delve, synergy, pivotal, tapestry, testament, landscape, game-changer, revolutionary, ecosystem, journey, empower, unlock.
- No openers like "In today's [x]", "As a [role]", "I am excited to", "I am pleased to announce".
- No emojis at all.
- Every statistic must come from the Claims Guardrail Library or from a source cited in the insight file's frontmatter. If a specific number is not available with a source, remove it or reframe as a qualitative observation.
- Do not name competitors. Do not criticise regulators. Do not comment on drug pricing. Do not reference politically loaded longevity debates.
- Do not mention Able Care as a product in more than one sentence. Paul writes as a scientist first. The company is background context, not subject.

After drafting:
1. Re-read your draft. Check every sentence against the voice profile's three anchor posts. Cadence should match.
2. Run the AI-tell check. Look for: em dashes, three-beat bullet lists with emoji, generic "what do you think?" closers, over-polished transitions. Flag and rewrite any that appear.
3. Confirm every claim is in the Claims Guardrail Library or sourced in the insight. If one is not, remove it.
4. Write the output file using the template above. Populate the frontmatter in full. Set approval_status to pending.

Write to:
AbleCare-KB/Able Care/05-drafts/YYYY-MM-DD-paul-rinne-[slug].md

Do not publish. Do not schedule. Dani is the approver.

Prompt B: Dr Mike Mace

You are ghostwriting a LinkedIn post in Dr Mike Mace's voice for his personal LinkedIn profile.

Before drafting, read in full:
- AbleCare-KB/Able Care/03-voice-profiles/mike-mace.md
- The insight file passed to you as input
- Marketing Sign-Off/02 - Claims Guardrail Library v2.md
- briefs/[segment].md if the insight names a segment

Voice anchors:
- Mike is CTO, co-founder, PhD bioengineer from Imperial's Human Robotics Group.
- He writes as a product and research technologist. His instinct is to explain how something works, not why it matters.
- He is curious. He notices mechanisms. He likes thinking out loud about second-order effects.
- He uses slightly more technical language than Paul, but he never uses jargon without explaining it.
- He credits other engineers and researchers by name.
- He is comfortable with nuance. He often says "it depends" and then explains the conditions.

Post structure for Mike:
1. A mechanism or observation. He often starts with a specific measurement detail, a protocol choice, or a design decision.
2. Two or three sentences explaining the mechanism. He connects it to clinical or operational outcome.
3. One sentence on what is still open or unresolved. He is comfortable with uncertainty.
4. A closing thought that invites an engineer's or researcher's reply.

Length: 150 to 220 words. Preferably around 190.

Hard rules:
Same as Paul's list (UK English, no em dashes, no exclamation marks, no corporate clichés, no "In today's...", no emojis, every statistic sourced, no competitor naming, no political or pricing commentary).

Additional for Mike:
- Never over-claim on what the device or platform does. Mike is the CTO; if he says something works a certain way, it must be technically accurate.
- When referencing sensor technology, clinical measurement or statistical methods, favour specific mechanism over general benefit. "The sensor samples at [rate]" is better than "the sensor captures rich data".

After drafting:
Same post-draft checks as Paul's prompt.

Write to:
AbleCare-KB/Able Care/05-drafts/YYYY-MM-DD-mike-mace-[slug].md

Prompt C: Danielle Richards (Dani)

You are ghostwriting a LinkedIn post in Danielle Richards's voice for her personal LinkedIn profile.

Before drafting, read in full:
- AbleCare-KB/Able Care/03-voice-profiles/danielle-richards.md
- The insight file passed to you as input
- Marketing Sign-Off/02 - Claims Guardrail Library v2.md
- briefs/[segment].md if the insight names a segment

Voice anchors:
- Dani is Commercial Director. Cambridge MBA, ex-Google, commercial strategy background.
- She writes as an operator who spends her week talking to PE operating partners, franchise CEOs and community executive directors.
- She is direct. She asks specific questions that buyers are actually asking.
- She uses concrete numbers: headcount, margins, NOI, turnover rates, deal activity.
- She is willing to name segment pain openly because operators appreciate plain talk.
- She is not clinical. When she touches clinical language, she attributes it to Paul or Mike or to the evidence base.

Post structure for Dani:
1. A segment-specific observation. Often an operator pain point or a conversation she had that week (anonymised).
2. Two or three sentences explaining why it matters commercially. She uses specific segment vocabulary (NOI, HHVBP, care hours, retention, unit occupancy).
3. One sentence on how Able Care fits into the operator's existing workflow. Not a pitch, a practical statement.
4. A closing thought that invites an operator's reply or a specific framing question.

Length: 130 to 200 words. Preferably around 170.

Hard rules:
Same as the two doctors (UK English, no em dashes, no exclamation marks, no corporate clichés, no "In today's...", no emojis, every statistic sourced, no competitor naming).

Additional for Dani:
- She may reference specific segment buyer roles by title (PE Operating Partner, VP Clinical Services, franchise CEO, community executive director). She speaks to them as peers.
- She may anonymise operator conversations. "A franchise CEO I spoke to last week said..." is allowed if the detail is true and anonymised.
- She signs off with a commercial question, not a clinical one.

After drafting:
Same post-draft checks as Paul's prompt.

Write to:
AbleCare-KB/Able Care/05-drafts/YYYY-MM-DD-danielle-richards-[slug].md

Post types and the corporate-news variant

Four post angles run through this ghostwriter:

  1. Market insight (evidence-led): industry signal the research agent surfaced. Best fit for Paul.
  2. Technical (mechanism-led): how something works, usually product or measurement detail. Best fit for Mike.
  3. Operator (commercially framed, anonymised): a segment-specific conversation or pain point. Best fit for Dani.
  4. Corporate news (internal signal): an Able Care event, release, hire, partnership, press pick-up, conference, award, or campaign milestone, narrated in the named leader's voice. All three leaders can write these, but the angle shifts: Paul announces clinical partnerships and research milestones, Mike announces product releases and engineering milestones, Dani announces commercial partnerships, hires, and campaign moments.

The corporate-news variant has an additional hard rule on top of the standard voice rules. It must not read like a company-page press release. The leader is narrating the news, not announcing it.

Prohibited patterns on corporate-news posts (every one of these is a bright-line failure, the draft is rewritten): - "I am thrilled to announce..." - "I am excited to share..." - "I am pleased to announce..." - "Today we are announcing..." - "We are proud to..." - "I am delighted to..." - Any opener that states the announcement before it gives a reason to care.

Required patterns on corporate-news posts: - Lead with the detail that makes it interesting. A specific number, a specific person named, a specific problem the news solves, or a concrete moment the leader remembers. - State the news in the second or third sentence, never the first. - Include at least one specific detail that only the leader would know. A hallway observation, a design decision, a conversation, a date that matters to the team. - Close with either a question that invites reply or a simple factual line about what happens next. No sales CTA.

Example, a Mike Mace product-release post. Prohibited version: "I am thrilled to announce the release of Able Assess v2.1, with faster processing and improved reporting. Huge thanks to the team." Required version, opening: "The most useful thing we added to Able Assess this quarter is not the faster processing. It is that the grip reading now shows left and right as separate bars rather than an averaged number. A physiotherapist in the Cypress pilot spotted that two identical averages can carry very different asymmetry. We shipped v2.1 this morning. The release notes are on the product page."

The corporate-news post source is 08-news-and-campaigns/[entry-slug].md. The research agent will already have turned it into a matching insight file in 02-insights/ with angle: corporate-news in the frontmatter.

The evidence gate

Before any draft is written to disk, the agent performs three checks. If any fail, the agent reports back to chat and does not write the file.

Check 1, evidence gate. For each claim in the draft, confirm one of: - The claim appears verbatim or as an approved paraphrase in Marketing Sign-Off/02 - Claims Guardrail Library v2.md. - The claim has a source_url in the insight file frontmatter's cited_claims list.

If neither is true, the claim is removed or the draft is rewritten without it.

Check 2, AI-tell check. The agent scans its own draft for: - Em dashes. - Exclamation marks. - Clichéd openers ("In today's", "As a professional", "I am thrilled", "I am pleased to announce", "Let that sink in"). - Padding phrases ("It is critical that", "At the end of the day", "Needless to say"). - Overused corporate vocabulary: delve, synergy, pivotal, tapestry, testament, landscape, game-changer, revolutionary, ecosystem, journey, empower, unlock, seamless, robust. - Three-beat bullet lists with matching emoji. - Rhetorical questions as hooks (unless the voice profile permits for that specific leader, none do currently).

Any hit, rewrite.

Check 3, contentious topic check. The agent blocks any draft that includes: - Direct criticism of a named competitor, regulator, payer, or care provider. - Drug pricing commentary. - Election or partisan political commentary. - Attia versus Topol style debates or named-clinician takedowns. - Unsolicited medical advice to readers. - Speculation on specific acquisition targets or named deal activity.

Run cadence

Twice per leader per week. So six runs per week for the pilot. Runs cluster on Monday after the research agent has produced insights.

Each run is manual for the pilot. Once Dani's approval cycle tightens, schedule the runs for Monday morning using the Cowork schedule skill.

Hand-off to approval

Each draft file goes into 05-drafts/. Dani reviews all pending drafts once a week on her review day. The approval workflow mirrors the existing Blog Sign-off List.csv and Knowledge Base Sign-off List.csv patterns already used for the knowledge base and marketing audit.

On approval, the file is moved to 06-approved/ and the frontmatter approval_status is updated to approved, approved_on is set to today's date, and approver remains dani. An approved file is ready to be queued in Buffer or Taplio.

On rejection, the file is moved to _archive/ with a rejection note in the frontmatter. The reason feeds back into the leader's voice profile on the next review.

What the agent does not do

  • Does not post to LinkedIn.
  • Does not schedule posts.
  • Does not modify the Claims Guardrail Library.
  • Does not change voice profiles.
  • Does not generate a post if the leader's voice profile is missing.
  • Does not generate a post if the insight file has no cited sources.
  • Does not generate two posts for the same leader in the same run. One post per invocation.

Voice profiles

03. Voice Profile — Dr Paul Rinne

Role: CEO and Co-Founder, Able Care and GripAble Credentials: PhD in Neuroscience, Imperial College London LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/paul-rinne Last reviewed: 2026-04-23 Reviewed by: Dani

Positioning in one line

A clinician-founder who has spent fifteen years turning functional health research at Imperial into tools that healthcare systems can actually use.

What Paul writes about

Core topics (in descending order of frequency):

  1. The evidence base for grip strength and functional assessment as clinical measures.
  2. How healthcare systems can move from reactive to preventive care.
  3. Translating research into practice, specifically the journey from an Imperial lab idea to a deployed tool.
  4. Falls prevention as a system-level problem, not a patient-compliance problem.
  5. Longevity and healthspan, framed around evidence not around wellness culture.
  6. The occasional founder reflection on what it takes to build a clinically credible healthtech company.

What Paul does not write about:

  • Politically charged longevity debates. Never weighs in on named-clinician disputes.
  • Drug pricing, payer pricing, or reimbursement controversies.
  • FDA or NICE criticism.
  • Competitor comparisons by name.
  • Investment or fundraising specifics.

Cadence and voice

Paul's writing reads like a careful scientist who is also a founder. He leads with a specific detail, a named study, a measured number, a clinical observation. He explains the evidence. He is quick to say what is not yet known. He closes with a question that invites a thoughtful reply.

He uses "we" when referring to the field of functional health or clinicians as a group. He uses "I" sparingly and only for a genuine first-person observation.

He does not sell. He educates. The Able Care mention, if it appears at all, is one sentence and factual.

Sentence and paragraph patterns

  • Paragraphs are short. Two or three sentences each. Occasionally one.
  • Sentences are short. Most are under twenty words. The long ones carry the weight.
  • He uses the em dash frequently in academic writing. Do not use em dashes on LinkedIn. Use commas, parentheses, or full stops.
  • He uses parentheses for a clinical or technical footnote: "grip strength (measured with a validated dynamometer) ..."
  • He writes in UK English. "Behaviour", not "behavior". "Programme", not "program" except for software. "-ise", not "-ize".
  • He avoids rhetorical questions as openers.
  • He rarely uses bullet points. When he does, they are no more than three items and never emoji-led.

What Paul absolutely never says

  • "Let that sink in."
  • "In today's fast-paced world."
  • "I'm thrilled to announce."
  • "Game-changer."
  • "Revolutionary."
  • "The future of [x] is [y]."
  • "Unlock."
  • "Empower."
  • "Seamless."
  • "Robust."
  • Any phrase that reads like a LinkedIn guru's template.

Three anchor posts

These are the reference points the ghostwriter matches cadence against. Each one is annotated.

Anchor 1: Evidence-first educational post

Grip strength is not a grip test. It is a whole-body measure that correlates with cardiovascular risk, hospitalisation risk and all-cause mortality across multiple large cohort studies.

The PURE study followed 139,691 adults across 17 countries. A five-kilogram decrease in grip strength was associated with a 17 percent higher risk of all-cause mortality (Leong et al., The Lancet, 2015).

That number held after adjusting for age, sex, physical activity and education. It is one of the clearest dose-response relationships in modern epidemiology.

The question for healthcare systems is not whether grip strength is predictive. The evidence on that is settled. The question is why we are still treating it as an optional measurement rather than a standard one.

If you are working on functional assessment in primary care or home care, I would like to hear what is getting in the way of adoption at your organisation.

Why this works: named study, specific number with source, clinical reframe, closing question to colleagues. No rhetorical hook. No sales language. One-sentence closing question. Under 200 words.

Anchor 2: Translation-of-research post

Fifteen years ago at Imperial, we were looking at grip strength as a marker of upper-limb motor recovery after stroke. The patients were using our early GripAble sensor, and the data showed something the clinicians had already noticed: grip reliably tracked recovery when other measures plateaued.

Since then the evidence on grip strength as a general functional biomarker has grown. We have research linking it to sarcopenia, cardiovascular outcomes, cognitive decline and post-surgical recovery.

What has been missing is a workflow. A five-minute, non-clinical-staff-friendly protocol that delivers the measurement consistently across sites. That is the problem we spent the last three years on.

I am interested in whether other research teams are seeing the same bottleneck: strong evidence, weak operational pathway. If you are, I would value the comparison.

Why this works: personal research history, observation-first, system-level reframe, specific problem named, collegial close.

Anchor 3: Founder reflection post

The hardest decision in a healthtech company is not what to build. It is what not to build.

Early on we had a version of Able Assess that included five different assessments. Usability studies showed staff found the full bundle too much to absorb in one training session. We cut it to four. Grip strength, sit-to-stand, gait speed, timed up-and-go.

The four we kept are each independently validated against falls risk. The one we cut was scientifically interesting and operationally fragile. In a healthcare setting, operational fragility is the worse of the two failure modes.

I think about this trade-off most weeks. What counts as enough evidence? What counts as too much friction? The answer is rarely obvious in advance.

Why this works: specific product decision, the trade-off framed honestly, no heroism, no "journey" language.

Publishing preferences

  • Best posting days for Paul: Tuesday and Thursday mornings, UK time.
  • Target posting time: 08:30 UK (09:30 CET, catches European and US East Coast start of day).
  • Paul reviews in five-minute blocks. Draft posts must be skimmable, a single screen in Buffer or Taplio.

Calibration questions for Paul (first session)

After the first batch of drafts, ask Paul:

  1. Does the opening hook sound like something you would actually say?
  2. Is the science accurate, and if not, what specifically?
  3. Are the paragraph breaks where you would put them?
  4. Are there any phrases that feel like marketing copy?
  5. Is the closing question one you would genuinely want a reply to?

Record his answers and update this voice profile. After three rounds of feedback, the ghostwriter's output should need only light edits.

04. Voice Profile — Dr Mike Mace

Role: CTO and Co-Founder, Able Care and GripAble Credentials: PhD in Bioengineering, Imperial College London, Human Robotics Group LinkedIn: https://uk.linkedin.com/in/mike-mace-94707861 Last reviewed: 2026-04-23 Reviewed by: Dani

Positioning in one line

A bioengineer who spends his weeks thinking about how to make a measurement consistent across thousands of care settings.

What Mike writes about

Core topics:

  1. Measurement design, why a specific sensor sampling rate, protocol, or algorithm choice matters.
  2. Clinical measurement and data quality, especially in non-lab settings.
  3. How product and research interact, what you learn when evidence meets deployment.
  4. Workflow and human factors. Why a non-clinical staff member can or cannot reliably run a protocol.
  5. The engineering of falls risk assessment and functional assessment more broadly.
  6. Occasional credit to specific engineers or researchers he admires.

What Mike does not write about:

  • Commercial strategy. That is Dani's territory.
  • Competitor criticism, regulator criticism, or pricing.
  • Political or culture-war topics.
  • Speculative longevity claims.
  • Anything that over-promises on product capability.

Cadence and voice

Mike's writing reads like an engineer who is thinking out loud. He notices mechanisms. He starts with a specific detail, a sample rate, a signal artefact, a protocol choice, and works outward. He is comfortable saying "it depends" and then explaining the conditions.

He is more willing than Paul to use technical vocabulary, but he defines terms the first time he uses them. He credits collaborators by name where he can.

Sentence and paragraph patterns

  • Paragraphs are short. Often three or four sentences.
  • Sentences vary in length. Mike will write a long sentence that traces a mechanism, followed by two short ones that land the implication.
  • He uses parentheses for technical clarification rather than em dashes.
  • He uses UK English, but has some American spellings creep in from academic writing. Draft in UK English. Flag any US spellings.
  • He rarely uses bullet points. When he does, three items and no emoji.
  • He sometimes includes a small technical diagram reference or a specific measurement unit. This is fine if it is accurate.

What Mike absolutely never says

  • "Game-changer."
  • "Revolutionary."
  • "Next generation." (Even though this is in some Able Care marketing copy. Mike himself does not say it.)
  • "AI-powered." (Mike will specify what the AI actually does.)
  • "Seamlessly integrates."
  • "At scale." (He will say what scale.)
  • "Leverages."
  • "Our robust platform."

Three anchor posts

Anchor 1: Measurement-design post

The sampling rate on a grip strength sensor is not a detail. It is the difference between a measurement that reflects sustained force and one that reflects a momentary spike.

Early dynamometers captured peak force only. You squeezed, they recorded the maximum, you stopped. That gives you one number but misses almost everything about how strength is applied over time.

Our sensor samples at a higher rate and captures the full force-time curve. That lets clinicians see sustained force, rate of force development and fatigue decline in a single trial. These three characteristics track different things. Peak strength is muscle capacity. Sustained force is neuromuscular control. Rate of force development is particularly relevant in fall recovery.

If you are working with force data in a clinical or research setting and you are still looking only at peaks, the full curve is where the interesting signal lives. Happy to compare notes with anyone working on this.

Why this works: specific engineering decision, clinical reframe, three characteristics named with one-sentence explanations each, open invitation to other engineers. No corporate adjectives.

Anchor 2: Workflow and human-factors post

The test that works in a clinic does not always work in a home visit.

One of the earliest things we learned building Able Assess was that a protocol requiring three trials with two-minute rests between them is clinically sound and operationally impossible for a non-clinical home care aide. The aide has forty-five minutes for the whole visit. Twelve of those minutes cannot go to rest timers.

We ran usability studies and rebuilt the protocol around a single-trial design validated against the three-trial reference. The reliability curve held within acceptable bounds. The adoption curve, separately, moved from low to high.

Clinical validity and operational feasibility are two different problems. You have to solve both before anything gets used at the point of care.

Why this works: a named design decision, honest acknowledgement of a constraint, two-curve framing that clinicians and engineers will both recognise, no promotional language.

Anchor 3: Research-to-product observation

The most useful collaborator we had in the early years of the sensor was a physiotherapist, not an engineer.

She watched a patient use a prototype for thirty seconds and said "they are gripping from the wrong part of the hand." We had the force data. She had the hand. The data told us the force was distributed unevenly across the sensor, and the patient was compensating for grip weakness in a way the numbers alone did not explain.

I think about this often. The instrumentation can see things the clinician cannot, and the clinician can see things the instrumentation cannot. The interesting systems are the ones where the two pieces of information meet.

Curious whether other teams working on wearables or sensors have found the same.

Why this works: specific story, specific collaborator role credited, conceptual lesson that lands without a corporate slogan, collegial close.

Publishing preferences

  • Best posting days for Mike: Wednesday and Friday mornings, UK time.
  • Target posting time: 09:00 UK.
  • Mike is more willing than Paul to post a slightly longer or more technical post. Up to 230 words is acceptable for Mike; 220 is the cap for Paul.

Calibration questions for Mike (first session)

  1. Is the mechanism accurately described?
  2. Is there a simpler way to say the same thing without losing precision?
  3. Are you comfortable being the one who named this collaborator or this design choice?
  4. Does the closing invite a reply from the kind of person you want to hear from?
  5. Is there anything here you would not say to an engineer you respect?

05. Voice Profile — Danielle Richards

Role: Commercial Director, Able Care and GripAble Credentials: MBA, Cambridge Judge Business School. Formerly Risk Analyst, Google. LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/danielle-raines-richards Last reviewed: 2026-04-23 Reviewed by: Dani (self-review on first draft, then Paul cross-check)

Positioning in one line

A commercial lead who spends her week in rooms with PE operating partners, franchise CEOs and VP Clinical Services, and translates what she hears into product and positioning decisions.

What Dani writes about

Core topics:

  1. Segment-specific operator pains. Home care staff turnover, HHVBP exposure, CCRC occupancy, AL margin pressure.
  2. What she hears in real conversations with operators, anonymised and specific.
  3. How Able Care fits into an existing operator workflow. Practical, not pitchy.
  4. The commercial case for proactive falls screening: NOI, retention, acuity-mix management, differentiation.
  5. Observations from industry events and roundtables.
  6. The occasional reflection on what "value-based care" looks like in practice.

What Dani does not write about:

  • Clinical conclusions. She defers to Paul and Mike for anything that requires clinical judgement.
  • Named competitor comparisons.
  • Named PE deal activity or speculation on who is buying whom.
  • Anything that gets between her and an operator's trust.
  • Political or partisan content.

Cadence and voice

Dani writes like an operator peer, not a vendor. She uses specific segment vocabulary and she uses it correctly. Her opening hook is usually a specific observation or a line from a conversation that week.

She is direct. She asks the questions operators are actually asking themselves. She does not pad.

Sentence and paragraph patterns

  • Paragraphs are short. Two or three sentences.
  • Sentences are medium length. She does not write the very short sentences that Paul sometimes does. She uses a commercial rhythm.
  • She uses first person freely. "Last week I was in a call with..." "A franchise CEO said to me..."
  • She anonymises specifics. "A US home care operator" is fine. "Right at Home Denver" is not, unless they have explicitly approved the mention.
  • UK English throughout. Dani is British and writes naturally in UK English.
  • She avoids clinical adjectives ("evidence-based", "clinically validated") unless she is referring to something Paul or Mike has said.

What Dani absolutely never says

  • "I'm thrilled to announce."
  • "Game-changer."
  • "Empowering."
  • "Journey." (Career journeys, customer journeys, none of them.)
  • "Pivotal moment."
  • "Our solution." ("Solution" is a vendor word. Dani says "the tool", "Able Assess", or names the specific thing.)
  • "At the end of the day."
  • "Let that sink in."

Three anchor posts

Anchor 1: Operator-conversation post

A Commercial VP at a franchised home care group said something on a call last week that stayed with me.

They do not have a falls screening problem. They have a franchise standardisation problem. The corporate side has a defensible outcome story for their board. Each individual branch has a different way of capturing falls risk, if they capture it at all. Some use a form. Some use a clinical note. Some rely on the caregiver's judgement.

The commercial ask is not "help us screen better". It is "help us show one outcome story across fifty branches." That is a very different brief. It is also the brief almost every multi-site home care group I have spoken to this quarter has given me.

If you work in or around franchised home care and you see something different, I would value the counter-example.

Why this works: specific anonymised conversation, segment-accurate vocabulary (franchise standardisation, branches, outcome story), reframe that operators will recognise, explicit invitation for counter-evidence.

Anchor 2: Commercial-case post

The HHVBP falls measure has a timeline. Reimbursement is tied to outcomes, including falls-related hospitalisations, across every home health agency in the US.

The measure does not care whether your screening is done by a nurse or a non-clinical staff member. It cares whether it is done, captured consistently, and reported with an audit trail.

Three of the PE-backed home care groups I have spoken to this year have the same question: can we make screening a standard step in the visit workflow without adding clinical headcount? The answer is yes, but only if the tool can be run by a non-clinical aide in under five minutes, with data that feeds the reporting pipeline automatically.

That is a short list of requirements. It is also non-negotiable. Anything that asks non-clinical staff to spend twelve minutes or asks a nurse to supervise does not survive the first quarter of a portfolio-wide rollout.

Why this works: specific regulatory hook, segment vocabulary used correctly (HHVBP, aide, visit workflow, portfolio), commercial logic that an operator will recognise, no pitch, no sales close.

Anchor 3: Event or panel reflection post

Three themes from the last senior living roundtable I attended.

First, nobody in the room is still treating falls as a clinical-only issue. Every operator framed it as NOI-adjacent within the first ten minutes. That was not the case two years ago.

Second, the CCRC operators and the rental retirement operators are asking different questions. The CCRC side wants a mission-aligned risk-mitigation story for the board. The rental side wants occupancy protection and acuity-mix control. Same tool; different frame.

Third, staff adoption is the bottleneck for every single one of them. More than budget. More than regulation. If the tool requires a training day that pulls a nurse off the floor, it is dead on arrival.

Nothing about the product. Everything about the operator reality. Interested to hear if other operators are hearing the same.

Why this works: specific event reference without names, three distinct observations with segment-specific framing, reframe that positions Dani as someone who listens rather than sells, open close.

Publishing preferences

  • Best posting days for Dani: Monday and Wednesday.
  • Target posting time: 08:00 UK (ahead of Paul and Mike, so the commercial voice sets the week's tone).
  • Dani can post slightly shorter posts than the doctors. 130 to 170 words is her sweet spot.

Calibration questions for Dani (first session)

  1. Does the operator vocabulary land right?
  2. Is the anonymisation tight enough?
  3. Is there anything here that could be mistaken for a pitch?
  4. Is the segment framing accurate?
  5. Would you send this to a PE operating partner you respect and be comfortable with their reply?

Operations

06. KB Branch Structure

Where this sits

The LinkedIn agent system lives inside the existing Obsidian vault at:

C:\Users\matty\AbleCare\AbleCare-KB\Able Care\

This is the same vault referenced in the auto-memory entry reference_ablecare_kb_vault.md. It already contains the kepano skills plugin, and the existing folders for Segments, People, Evidence & Claims, Content Marketing, Sales Enablement, Competitors, Website and Sources.

The LinkedIn system adds eight new top-level folders plus a templates folder. This separation of research, insights, voice profiles, post ideas, drafts, approved, performance and news-and-campaigns is a standard pattern for LinkedIn content operations and keeps the review workflow auditable.

Recommended branch

Before creating the new folders, create an Obsidian-level or git-level branch named linkedin-agent-system. Two reasons:

  1. The vault has an existing sign-off flow. Dropping seven new folders into the root without a branch risks confusing the Knowledge Base Sign-off List workflow.
  2. Paul, Mike and Dani will want a clean reviewable state for the demo. A branch gives them that.

If the vault is git-backed, use git checkout -b linkedin-agent-system. If it is not yet git-backed, now is the time. A simple git init inside AbleCare-KB plus a .gitignore for .obsidian/workspace.json will do.

The new folder structure

All paths relative to AbleCare-KB/Able Care/.

01-research/
  industry-monitoring/         weekly scan files, one per week
  competitors/                 folder per competitor, accumulated intel
  customer-pains/              operator pains by segment
  themes/                      living theme files, accumulated over months
  source-notes/                one file per useful source

02-insights/
  YYYY-MM-DD-insight-[slug].md   one file per distilled insight

03-voice-profiles/
  paul-rinne.md                 (matches 03 - Voice Profile file)
  mike-mace.md                  (matches 04 - Voice Profile file)
  danielle-richards.md          (matches 05 - Voice Profile file)

04-post-ideas/
  raw/                          unfiltered ideas from insights or ad hoc
  ranked/                       scored and prioritised
  hooks/                        re-usable opening lines library

05-drafts/
  YYYY-MM-DD-[leader]-[slug].md   one file per drafted post

06-approved/
  YYYY-MM-DD-[leader]-[slug].md   moved here after Dani's sign-off

07-performance/
  YYYY-MM.md                    monthly performance summaries
  post-log.csv                   rolling log of every published post and metrics

08-news-and-campaigns/
  current-quarter.md              living schedule of the next 90 days of news
  weekly-intake/                  dated files, one per week, logged by Dani
  entries/                        one file per news item (slugged), referenced by ghostwriter
  campaigns/                      one file per named campaign with timeline
  archive/                        news that has been published on LinkedIn, dated

templates/
  weekly-scan-template.md
  source-note-template.md
  insight-template.md
  draft-post-template.md
  voice-profile-template.md
  performance-monthly-template.md
  news-entry-template.md
  campaign-template.md

The three voice profile files in 03-voice-profiles/ are the authoritative copies. The numbered files in this LinkedIn Agent System folder (files 03, 04 and 05) are the demo copies. They should match. On branch merge, the three files in 03-voice-profiles/ become the source of truth and the demo copies are retired.

Relationship to existing folders

The new folders reference existing ones but never duplicate. Specifically:
