  • Claims in post drafts must reference AbleCare-KB/Able Care/Evidence & Claims/ and Marketing Sign-Off/02 - Claims Guardrail Library v2.md.
  • Competitor observations go into 01-research/competitors/[competitor-slug].md and reference AbleCare-KB/Able Care/Competitors/ where a full competitor profile already exists.
  • Segment context for ghostwriting comes from briefs/ at the AbleCare workspace root, not from a duplicate inside the vault.
  • Sign-off tracking uses the existing pattern of Blog Sign-off List.csv and Knowledge Base Sign-off List.csv, extended with a new LinkedIn Sign-off List.csv at the workspace root.

Templates to create

Six templates sit in templates/. Their content is reproduced below so the user can copy them into the vault directly.

templates/weekly-scan-template.md

---
run_date: YYYY-MM-DD
agent: research-agent-v1
segments_covered: []
total_sources_reviewed: 0
total_sources_logged: 0
---

# Weekly Industry Scan: [Date]

## Summary
[Three sentences.]

## Signals by topic

### Falls prevention policy and practice
- **YYYY-MM-DD**, [Title](URL), Publication, year. Relevance: [one sentence]. Tier: [1/2/3].

### Grip strength as a biomarker
- [...]

### Functional health assessment
- [...]

### Senior care operator pressures
- [...]

### Longevity and healthspan
- [...]

### Regulator signals
- [...]

## Competitor activity
- [competitor name], [observation], [Source](URL)

## New claims flagged for Paul or Mike review
- "[Claim]", [Source](URL). Candidate for addition to Claims Guardrail Library after clinical review.

templates/source-note-template.md

---
source_date: YYYY-MM-DD
source_url: https://...
source_title: "..."
publication: "..."
authors: ["..."]
tier: 1
citable: true
relevance_segments: []
---

# [Source Title]

## Summary
[Two paragraphs.]

## Key figures or claims
- [Specific number or claim with exact phrasing from source]

## Quotes worth remembering
> "[Direct quote]", [Author, context]

## Citation
[Full formatted citation.]

templates/insight-template.md

---
insight_date: YYYY-MM-DD
source_run: YYYY-MM-DD-weekly-scan
segments: []
audience: []
angle: market-insight
strength: high
cited_claims:
  - claim: ""
    source_title: ""
    source_url: ""
    year: ""
    reviewed_by: ""
    approved_on: null
---

# Insight: [One-sentence headline]

## What happened
[Two sentences.]

## Why it matters for Able Care's segments
[Three to four sentences. Name the specific segment. Name the specific buyer role.]

## Post angles
- Market insight angle for Paul: "..."
- Technical angle for Mike: "..."
- Operator angle for Dani: "..."

## Evidence base
- [Source 1](URL), title, year, tier
- [Source 2](URL), title, year, tier

templates/draft-post-template.md

Matches the output structure described in 02 - Ghostwriter Agent Spec.md. Frontmatter:

---
leader: [paul-rinne|mike-mace|danielle-richards]
insight_source: 02-insights/...
post_angle: [market-insight|technical|operator]
target_segment: ""
target_audience: []
length_words: 0
cited_claims: []
evidence_gate_passed: false
ai_tell_check_passed: false
contentious_topic_check_passed: false
drafted_on: YYYY-MM-DD
approval_status: pending
approver: dani
---

# Draft: [slug]

[Post body]

---

## Why this works for [leader]
...

## Suggested first comment
...

## Suggested publish day and time
...

## Notes for Dani's review
...

templates/voice-profile-template.md

Use the structure from 03 - Voice Profile - Paul Rinne.md as the template, with placeholders.

templates/performance-monthly-template.md

---
month: YYYY-MM
posts_published: 0
total_impressions: 0
total_engagements: 0
new_followers_paul: 0
new_followers_mike: 0
new_followers_dani: 0
---

# LinkedIn Performance: [Month YYYY]

## Headline metrics
[Three sentences.]

## Top three posts
1. [Leader], [slug], [metric].
2. [Leader], [slug], [metric].
3. [Leader], [slug], [metric].

## What worked
[Two sentences.]

## What did not
[Two sentences.]

## Feedback on voice profiles
- Paul: [updates to make].
- Mike: [updates to make].
- Dani: [updates to make].

## Actions for next month
- [Action 1]
- [Action 2]

templates/news-entry-template.md

---
news_date: YYYY-MM-DD            # when it happens (or happened)
news_type: [product-release|hire|partnership|press|award|conference|grant|campaign-milestone|pilot-result|clinical-update|other]
category: [commercial|clinical|technical|corporate]
owner: [paul-rinne|mike-mace|danielle-richards]   # who will post about it
suggested_leader_voice: [paul-rinne|mike-mace|danielle-richards]
confidentiality: [public|embargoed-until-YYYY-MM-DD|internal-only]
ready_for_linkedin: false
linkedin_embargo: YYYY-MM-DD     # earliest date we can post
related_campaign: ""              # if part of a campaign, slug of campaign file
logged_by: dani
logged_on: YYYY-MM-DD
---

# [Short, plain title: what happened]

## What happened, in one paragraph
[Two to four sentences. Plain English. What, when, who, where.]

## The specific detail a leader would notice
[The one thing the leader would actually remember. A number, a conversation, a design choice, a moment. This is the raw material that keeps the post out of press-release territory.]

## Why it matters (to Able Care, to the audience, or both)
[Two sentences. No marketing voice.]

## What must not be said publicly
- [Any embargoed detail]
- [Names not yet public]
- [Commercial terms not disclosed]

## Suggested post angle by leader
- Paul: [...]
- Mike: [...]
- Dani: [...]

## Supporting evidence
- [Link to internal doc, press release, study, partner letter, etc.]

## Claims library check
- [List of any claims this post would need that are not yet in Claims Guardrail Library v2]

templates/campaign-template.md

---
campaign_slug: [slug]
campaign_name: "[name]"
start_date: YYYY-MM-DD
end_date: YYYY-MM-DD
owner: dani
segments: []
goals:
  awareness: "[one sentence]"
  pipeline: "[one sentence]"
  thought-leadership: "[one sentence]"
linkedin_content_count: 0
status: [planned|active|closed]
---

# Campaign: [Name]

## Summary
[Three sentences.]

## Milestones and LinkedIn moments
| Date | Milestone | Suggested leader | Post angle |
|------|-----------|------------------|------------|
| YYYY-MM-DD | [milestone] | paul-rinne | [angle] |

## Assets produced
- [Link to asset in KB]

## Post-campaign notes
[Filled in after end_date.]

Kepano skills plugin considerations

The vault already has the kepano skills plugin installed (from the auto-memory reference). Four skills in the plugin map well onto this system:

  • A research-scan skill wrapping the research agent prompt in 01 - Research Agent Spec.md.
  • A ghostwrite-post skill wrapping each of the three prompts in 02 - Ghostwriter Agent Spec.md.
  • A voice-profile-update skill that captures Paul, Mike or Dani's feedback after a review session and applies it to the relevant profile file.
  • An authentic-content skill wrapping 10 - Authentic Content Skill.md so the style rulebook can be applied as a standalone check on any draft, regardless of which leader is writing.

These can be registered as kepano skills in a subsequent iteration. For the demo, the prompts are runnable directly from the Cowork CLI without being wrapped as skills.

Git hygiene

If the vault is now git-tracked, the suggested .gitignore at the vault root:

.obsidian/workspace.json
.obsidian/cache
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db

Commit pattern: one commit per completed folder, with messages like Add 01-research structure and weekly scan template. This gives Dani a clean history to review on the branch.

09. News and Campaigns Schedule

Owner: Dani Supported by: Matt Last reviewed: 2026-04-23 Status: Draft v1 for demo

1. Purpose

The research agent catches what the outside world is publishing. This document covers the other side: what Able Care itself is doing. Product releases, new hires, partnerships, pilot results, press pick-ups, conference appearances, awards, grant wins, campaign launches, and any other internal signal the leaders should be narrating on LinkedIn in their own voice.

Without this intake, the ghostwriter can draft credible industry posts but will never draft the post that announces a partnership, a product release, or a conference keynote. Those posts are the ones that drive most of the practical commercial value. They are also the posts most likely to slip into press-release voice if the system does not have a clear workflow. This document prevents both failures.

2. Where it lives

All content for this workflow sits in:

C:\Users\matty\AbleCare\AbleCare-KB\Able Care\08-news-and-campaigns\

Inside that folder:

  • current-quarter.md, a living schedule of the next ninety days of Able Care news, events, campaigns and embargoed items. Updated weekly by Dani.
  • weekly-intake/YYYY-MM-DD-intake.md, one file per week. Dani logs new news items here on her regular review day.
  • entries/[slug].md, one file per news item, using templates/news-entry-template.md. This is the file the ghostwriter reads.
  • campaigns/[slug].md, one file per named campaign, using templates/campaign-template.md.
  • archive/, where entries move once they have been published on LinkedIn. Archive filename prefix is the publication date.

3. What counts as a news item

The intake is deliberately wide. Ten categories in scope:

  1. Product release. Any version release of Able Assess, any new feature, any clinical-workflow change.
  2. Clinical pilot update. A new pilot starting, a pilot reading out, a pilot closing.
  3. Partnership. Commercial, clinical, academic or integration.
  4. Hire. Any senior hire. Any hire in a notable role (scientific advisory board, medical director, head of clinical, VP commercial).
  5. Press pick-up. Coverage in a trade publication or mainstream outlet.
  6. Conference or speaking appearance. Paul, Mike or Dani on a stage or panel. Webinar appearances count.
  7. Award or grant. Recognition, awards shortlists, grant wins, Innovate UK or NIHR awards, US equivalent.
  8. Publication. A peer-reviewed paper published by the Able Care team or with a named collaborator. A white paper or report published on the site.
  9. Campaign milestone. The kickoff, midpoint or close of a named marketing campaign. Usually three to five LinkedIn moments per campaign.
  10. Other notable. Anniversaries, team milestones, moments the leaders themselves want to mark.

Out of scope, explicitly:

  • Internal operational milestones with no external interest (e.g. a new HR policy, a CRM migration).
  • Day-to-day product updates that are not worth a dedicated post (bug fixes, minor UI tweaks).
  • Anything still under NDA or embargoed without an embargo-lift date that Dani has confirmed.

4. The weekly intake workflow

Dani owns this. Thirty minutes per week, on her existing review day.

Step 1. Open the weekly intake file

Create 08-news-and-campaigns/weekly-intake/YYYY-MM-DD-intake.md (date is the Monday of that week). Use this starter template:

---
intake_week: 2026-04-20
logged_by: dani
logged_on: 2026-04-23
entries_added: 0
entries_promoted: 0
---

# News Intake: Week of 20 April 2026

## New items this week
- [ ] ...

## Updates to existing items
- ...

## Items ready to promote to entries/
- ...

## Items to move to archive (published)
- ...

## Open questions for Matt
- ...

Step 2. Gather input from five sources

Dani reviews, in order:

  1. Internal Slack or Teams #announcements channel (or equivalent).
  2. The AbleCare-KB Obsidian vault, specifically Content Marketing/ and Sales Enablement/ for any campaign activity.
  3. The product release log, wherever Mike keeps it. For the demo, assume this is a pinned message in product Slack; post-pilot, formalise it as AbleCare-KB/Able Care/product/release-log.md.
  4. Dani's own commercial calendar, for partnerships, new customer signings, or conference bookings.
  5. Google Alerts or equivalent press monitoring, for any pick-up of the Able Care name.

Step 3. Log each new item as a draft entry

For each new item, create a file at 08-news-and-campaigns/entries/[slug].md using templates/news-entry-template.md. Fill the frontmatter:

  • news_date, the date of the event or the intended news day.
  • news_type, one of the ten categories.
  • owner, the leader whose voice this should be in.
  • ready_for_linkedin, keep as false until Dani has confirmed the claim gate and the embargo.
  • linkedin_embargo, the earliest date we can post.
  • related_campaign, if applicable.

Dani fills in the "What happened", "The specific detail a leader would notice", "Why it matters" and "What must not be said publicly" sections in plain English.

Step 4. Gate it

Before Dani flips ready_for_linkedin to true, two checks:

  • Any claim the post will rely on must be in the Claims Guardrail Library v2, or flagged for Paul or Mike to approve before the post is drafted.
  • Any named third party (partner, customer, journalist, conference organiser) must have either given explicit permission to be named, or the naming must be clearly public-domain (a published press release, a publicly advertised conference).

If both pass, flip the flag.

Step 5. Update the current-quarter schedule

Add a line to current-quarter.md with the date, slug and leader. See schedule template in section 6 below.

Step 6. Handoff

On the same day, mention in Matt's channel that new entries are ready for the ghostwriter to pick up on the next run. No more than two minutes of hand-off. The entries are the source of truth.

5. How the research agent and ghostwriter consume it

The research agent, on its weekly run:

  • Reads every entry in 08-news-and-campaigns/entries/ where ready_for_linkedin: true and linkedin_embargo has passed (or is today).
  • For each matching entry, generates a companion insight file in 02-insights/ with angle: corporate-news in the frontmatter and a news_source field pointing back to the entry.
  • Does not web-search for corporate news. The entry is the single source of truth.

The ghostwriter, on its run:

  • If the ghostwriter is drafting a corporate-news post, reads both the insight file and the linked entry file. The entry contains the specific detail a leader would notice; the ghostwriter lifts that detail into the opening.
  • Applies the corporate-news rule set (see 02 - Ghostwriter Agent Spec.md, Post types section). No "I am thrilled" openers. News stated in the second or third sentence. Specific detail lifted from the entry's "Specific detail a leader would notice" field.

6. The current-quarter schedule

08-news-and-campaigns/current-quarter.md is the single-screen view of the next ninety days. Updated weekly. Template:

---
quarter: 2026-Q2
last_updated: 2026-04-23
updated_by: dani
---

# Able Care News and Campaigns: Next 90 Days

## This week
| Date | Type | Leader | Slug | Status |
|------|------|--------|------|--------|
| 2026-04-24 | press | paul-rinne | mcknights-grip-strength-feature | ready |

## Next 30 days
| Date | Type | Leader | Slug | Status |
|------|------|--------|------|--------|
| 2026-05-06 | product-release | mike-mace | able-assess-v2-1 | embargoed-until-2026-05-06 |
| 2026-05-14 | conference | paul-rinne | hhvbp-summit-keynote | ready |
| 2026-05-20 | partnership | dani | cypress-home-care-expansion | ready |

## 30-60 days
...

## 60-90 days
...

## Active campaigns
- [hhvbp-measure-ready](campaigns/hhvbp-measure-ready.md), runs 2026-05-01 to 2026-06-30, owner: Dani, five LinkedIn moments planned.
- [pe-operator-playbook](campaigns/pe-operator-playbook.md), runs 2026-06-01 to 2026-07-31, owner: Dani, four LinkedIn moments planned.

## Embargoed, not yet in schedule
- [slug], [embargo lift date], [one line on why it cannot be scheduled yet].

## Newly logged this week (pending promotion)
- [slug], [status], [what is needed to promote].

Purpose of the schedule: any of the three leaders can open this single file and see the next ninety days of their own corporate news posting load.

7. Handling campaigns

A campaign is a named push with a start date, an end date, a commercial goal, and between three and five LinkedIn moments that fall inside it. Examples: the HHVBP Measure-Ready campaign, the PE Operator Playbook campaign, the STEADI Awareness Week push.

Each campaign gets its own file at campaigns/[slug].md, using templates/campaign-template.md.

Inside the file, the Milestones and LinkedIn moments table lists each planned LinkedIn moment with date, milestone, suggested leader and post angle. Each row that is within the current ninety-day window is also listed in current-quarter.md. Each row has a matching entry file at entries/[slug].md once it is ready for the ghostwriter.

Rule of thumb: a campaign has at least one post per leader. If a campaign only has Dani's voice on it, reconsider whether it is a campaign or just a commercial moment.

8. Handling embargoes

Embargoes are common. Partner announcements, hires not yet public, product releases with a fixed launch date, press pick-ups the publisher is holding. The workflow handles them cleanly:

  • The entry file is created with confidentiality: embargoed-until-YYYY-MM-DD.
  • ready_for_linkedin: false until the date passes.
  • The entry sits in 08-news-and-campaigns/entries/ but the research agent ignores it until the date passes.
  • Dani can still draft the post before the embargo lifts by running the ghostwriter manually against the entry. The resulting draft sits in 05-drafts/ marked approval_status: embargoed, ready to schedule on the day the embargo lifts.

If an embargo is internal only (a decision not yet made public but that Dani wants to prepare copy for), the workflow is the same. The entry has confidentiality: internal-only and ready_for_linkedin: false. No ghostwriter runs automatically. Dani triggers the draft manually when the timing is right.

9. Who can log an entry

For the pilot, only Dani. She is the single approver. This is intentional: it keeps the claim gate tight and the embargo discipline clean.

From month two, Paul and Mike can raise entries directly for anything originating in their own function. Paul for clinical partnerships and research; Mike for product releases and engineering milestones. Dani still confirms ready_for_linkedin: true in every case.

Matt supports the process but does not raise entries. He helps with prompt runs, schedule updates, and any edge cases.

10. Review cadence

  • Weekly. Dani's thirty-minute intake. Updates current-quarter.md. Logs new entries.
  • Monthly. Dani and Matt review the schedule together. Strip entries that never published. Note which category of news drives the highest engagement (see 07-performance/).
  • Quarterly. Archive the completed quarter's file (current-quarter.md becomes archive/YYYY-Q[n].md). Open a fresh quarter file. Review which campaigns delivered against their LinkedIn goals.

11. What this prevents

Three failure modes the intake specifically prevents:

  1. Press-release voice on LinkedIn. Every corporate news post goes through the ghostwriter against the authentic-content skill. The rule set blocks "I am thrilled" openers and mandates a specific detail in the lead. Without this intake, corporate news bypasses the voice filter and ends up in company-page voice on a personal profile.
  2. Missed moments. An unlogged milestone never gets a LinkedIn post. The weekly intake is the single guarantee that product releases, partnerships and press pick-ups reach the posting queue.
  3. Embargo mistakes. A structured linkedin_embargo field enforced by the research agent means a leader cannot accidentally publish an embargoed item early.

12. Related files

  • 02 - Ghostwriter Agent Spec.md, Post types section. Specifies the corporate-news post rule set.
  • 06 - KB Branch Structure.md, section on 08-news-and-campaigns/ and the news-entry and campaign templates.
  • 10 - Authentic Content Skill.md, the style rulebook every corporate-news post must pass.
  • Marketing Sign-Off/02 - Claims Guardrail Library v2.md, the claim gate that still applies to every news post.

10. Authentic Content Skill

Owner: Dani (editable by Paul, Mike, Dani at any time) Version: v1 Last reviewed: 2026-04-23

What this document is

This is the sentence-level rulebook that every Able Care LinkedIn post must pass before it ships. Voice profiles (files 03, 04, 05) define how each leader sounds. This file defines the universal floor: what any post, by any leader, on any topic, must and must not do.

Edit this file to change the writing style across every future post in one place. A single change here propagates to every draft the ghostwriter produces on the next run.

Three audiences read this document:

  • The ghostwriter agent reads it every draft run.
  • Dani reads it before sign-off, as the reference for any rewrite.
  • Paul, Mike and Dani read it when they want to change how the system writes.

The file has eight sections. Sections 1 to 5 are rules the ghostwriter applies automatically. Sections 6 to 8 are reference material and failure examples.

1. What "authentic" means in this context

A post reads as authentic when a thoughtful reader in the target audience would say "that is the kind of thing an experienced clinician (or operator, or engineer) would actually write." Authentic does not mean casual. It does not mean personal. It does not mean story-led. Paul's voice is authentic when it reads like a careful scientist. Dani's is authentic when it reads like a commercial operator. Authenticity is the specific voice done well; it is not a separate register.

The single sharpest test of authenticity: "Could this post have been written by anyone at any similar company, or is it recognisably Able Care's leader writing about Able Care's problem?" If it could have been written by anyone, it is not authentic yet.

Five sources of authenticity. Every post must contain at least two. The more the better.

  1. A specific number, with a source. Not "many studies", but "Leong et al., The Lancet, 2015, 139,691 adults".
  2. A named person, place, or institution, used appropriately. Not "a partner hospital", but "the Cypress Home Care team" (when permission has been confirmed).
  3. A first-person observation the leader genuinely made. Not "many clinicians believe", but "I saw this last week in a pilot review".
  4. A concrete design, measurement or operational detail. Not "our platform helps clinicians", but "the assessment runs in five to fifteen minutes, four tests, no specialist licence needed".
  5. A specific uncertainty. Not "we are excited about the future", but "what I do not yet know is whether the pattern holds in shorter protocols".

Posts with zero of these read like a content template. Posts with one read thin. Posts with two or more read like a person thought about this.

2. Hard rules (blocked on every draft)

Every rule below is a bright-line failure. The ghostwriter rewrites and never ships the violating version.

2.1 Prohibited punctuation and typography

  • No em dashes. Use commas, full stops or parentheses.
  • No exclamation marks under any circumstance.
  • No semi-colons on LinkedIn. If a sentence needs a semi-colon, split it in two.
  • No ellipses as a rhetorical device.
  • No ALL CAPS words. Not even for emphasis.
  • No emojis. Zero. Not one.
  • No double quotes for emphasis. Only for actual quotations.

2.2 Prohibited openers

Any post that starts with any of the following is rewritten in full. These are the openers that most reliably trigger AI-detection in a reader's head.

  • "In today's..."
  • "In today's fast-paced..."
  • "Let that sink in."
  • "As a [role]..."
  • "I am thrilled to..."
  • "I am excited to..."
  • "I am pleased to announce..."
  • "We are proud to..."
  • "I am delighted to..."
  • "Today I want to talk about..."
  • "Have you ever wondered..."
  • "Imagine if..."
  • "Here's the thing..."
  • "Let me tell you a story..."
  • "Picture this..."
  • A rhetorical question as the first sentence.

Preferred opener patterns (choose per post): - A specific number or fact. ("A five-kilogram decrease in grip strength was associated with a 17 per cent higher risk of all-cause mortality.") - A named study or source. ("The PURE study followed 139,691 adults across 17 countries.") - A concrete observation. ("A grip measurement that looks average tells a different story once you separate left from right.") - An operator quote the leader heard directly. ("A Commercial VP at a franchised home care group said something on a call last week that has stayed with me.") - A specific design or decision detail. ("The most useful thing we added to Able Assess this quarter is not the faster processing.")

2.3 Prohibited vocabulary

The ghostwriter scans every draft for the following. Any hit, the word is rewritten or removed.

Overused corporate words: delve, synergy, pivotal, tapestry, testament, landscape, ecosystem, journey, empower, unlock, seamless, robust, game-changer, revolutionary, cutting-edge, best-in-class, world-class, transformative, leverage, harness, unleash, elevate, amplify, curate, streamline, align, optimise (as a corporate verb rather than a technical one).

Overused LinkedIn phrases: "at the end of the day", "needless to say", "it is critical that", "it goes without saying", "I am humbled to", "I am honoured to", "without further ado", "buckle up", "strap in", "dive deep", "deep dive", "food for thought", "just my two cents", "that's a wrap", "unpack", "double down".

Hype adjectives: amazing, incredible, awesome, mind-blowing, game-changing, revolutionary, groundbreaking, disruptive.

Where the vocabulary is technically accurate (e.g. "optimise" in a measurement context, or "pivotal" in a clinical trial), it may stay; but when it is decorative, it goes.

2.4 Prohibited structure

  • No three-beat bullet lists with emoji prefixes ("✅ Scale", "🚀 Speed", "💡 Innovation"). Zero tolerance.
  • No alliterative three-beat trios ("People, Process, Platform"). They read as copy, not as thought.
  • No "I did X. Here's what I learned." format unless the leader actually requested it.
  • No "Most [role]s get this wrong. Here's why." format. It is the single most overused LinkedIn opener of 2024-26.
  • No "The secret to [X] is..." format.
  • No "1) ... 2) ... 3) ..." numbered key-points lists unless the post is explicitly a list post (rare; once per month at most per leader).

2.5 Prohibited content patterns

  • No rhetorical questions as hooks. ("What if I told you...", "Ever wondered why...")
  • No sales CTAs at the end of the post. Closing with "DM me if you are interested" is prohibited. Closing with a specific, genuine invitation to reply is welcome.
  • No generalisations about a whole industry. ("Healthcare is broken.", "PE has ruined home care.") Specific beats sweeping.
  • No unsubstantiated claim, ever. Every statistic must be in the Claims Guardrail Library or cited in the source insight file.
  • No comparison to a named competitor, unless specifically pre-approved by Dani and Paul or Mike for that post.
  • No named political content. No election commentary. No regulator criticism by name.
  • No sign-off with a single line of thanks that is generic ("Grateful to be part of this journey"). If thanks are warranted, they must be specific and name a specific contribution.

2.6 Length

  • Paul: 150 to 220 words, target 180.
  • Mike: 150 to 220 words, target 190.
  • Dani: 130 to 200 words, target 170.
  • Corporate news posts: same ranges. Never longer.

LinkedIn truncates at roughly 210 to 250 characters on mobile before a "see more" click. The first two sentences must carry the post even without the click.

2.7 UK English

Every post is written in UK English. - "Behaviour", not "behavior". - "Recognise", not "recognize". - "Programme" (for a plan or campaign), "program" (for software only). - "-ise", not "-ize". - "Towards", not "toward". - "Whilst" is acceptable; prefer "while". - "Honoured", not "honored". - "Practice" (noun), "practise" (verb, UK). - "Colour", "favour", "labour", "organisation", "analyse", "metre" (distance), "centre".

US spellings inside direct quotations are preserved as-is. Article titles and organisation names keep their source spelling ("Center for Medicare Services").

3. Positive rules (what every post must do)

These are the affirmative rules. A draft that fails any of these is rewritten.

3.1 Structure

Every post has four elements in order: 1. A concrete hook (one or two sentences). Not a question, not a cliché. A number, a named study, a specific observation, a design detail, or an operator quote the leader heard directly. 2. The substance (three to five sentences). What the evidence, event or observation actually says. Enough for a thoughtful reader to walk away with one clear idea. 3. The implication (one to two sentences). What this means for the reader's work, spoken to the reader as a peer, not as a prospect. Speak to "we, the field" or "you, the operator" depending on the leader. 4. A closing that invites a specific reply. A single question, a specific invitation, or a clear factual line about what happens next. Never a generic "what do you think?".

3.2 Paragraph rhythm

  • Short paragraphs, two or three sentences each.
  • Variable sentence length. If two sentences in a row are similar length, rewrite one shorter.
  • A paragraph break is a visual cue as much as a semantic one. Use it to let the reader breathe.

3.3 Source and specificity

  • Every claim has a source, in the Claims Guardrail Library or cited in the source insight file.
  • Every specific number has a specific provenance. "A five-kilogram decrease", not "a small decrease".
  • Every named third party has either given permission to be named, or is clearly public domain.

3.4 The "only-the-author-would-know" detail

Every post should contain at least one detail the leader would have to know personally for the sentence to hold up. A date that matters internally, a design choice, a conversation, a hallway observation, a moment in a pilot review. Without this, the post reads like anyone could have written it.

3.5 Honesty about uncertainty

Where the evidence is mixed or the question is genuinely open, say so. "The answer is rarely obvious in advance" reads more credible than "we have clarity on this now". Paul in particular should be comfortable saying "we do not know yet".

3.6 Close with a specific invitation

The close is either: - A specific question the leader genuinely wants an answer to. ("If your care system has a protocol that gets grip strength into a routine visit at scale, I would value the comparison.") - A clear factual line. ("Release notes are on the product page.")

Not a generic "what do you think?", not "comment below", not a sales CTA.

4. How the ghostwriter applies these rules

On every draft, in order:

  1. Read this file in full. Not the summary. The full list.
  2. Draft the post. Apply the voice profile. Apply the post-type rules (industry insight, operator, technical, corporate news).
  3. Run the self-check. Scan the draft for every item in sections 2.1 to 2.7 above. One hit means rewrite. Do this before writing the file.
  4. Run the positive check. Confirm each requirement in section 3 is met. At least two of the five sources of authenticity from section 1 are present.
  5. If either check fails, rewrite once more. If the second pass also fails, write the draft to 05-drafts/ anyway, set authentic_content_passed: false in the frontmatter, and flag the specific failure in "Notes for Dani's review".

5. Editing this file

The system is designed so that changing writing style means changing this file. Three common edit patterns:

Adding a prohibited word. If Dani reads a draft and says "that word reads as AI to me", add the word to section 2.3 with a one-line reason. Ghostwriter picks it up on the next run.

Changing an opener rule. If one of the current prohibited openers starts appearing in strong posts from credible clinicians, it can be relaxed. Move it from section 2.2 to the "Preferred opener patterns" list with a note on why.

Tightening a length limit. If 180 words is consistently too long, change the target in section 2.6. Every future draft adjusts.

No review process, no pull request, no ceremony. One of Paul, Mike or Dani edits this file. The next ghostwriter run picks up the change.

If a rule in this file ever conflicts with a rule in a voice profile, this file wins for universal patterns (em dashes, openers, hype words). The voice profile wins for voice-specific patterns (sentence length ranges, hook preferences, signing-off style).

6. Failure library (before and after)

Each example is a draft the ghostwriter (or a human) produced, followed by the reason it failed, followed by the rewrite.

Example A, hype opener

Before: "I am thrilled to announce that Able Care has partnered with Cypress Home Care to roll out Able Assess across 50 branches. This is a game-changer for falls prevention." Fails: "I am thrilled to announce" opener (2.2), "game-changer" (2.3), no specific detail, no source. After: "Cypress Home Care uses the same falls-screening assessment now across all fifty of its branches. The thing that got us here was not the technology. It was a three-month conversation about the non-clinical staff training protocol. Once that was sound, the roll-out was the easy part. I will post the training outline separately once Cypress has confirmed they are comfortable with it being shared."

Example B, list-post cliché

Before: "Most home care operators get falls prevention wrong. Here are three reasons why: 1) They treat it as a clinical problem, not an operational one. 2) They pick tools that need specialist staff. 3) They measure outcomes at the wrong layer. Agree or disagree?" Fails: "Most [role]s get this wrong" format (2.4), three-beat list (2.4), rhetorical close (2.5). After: "The framing I hear most often from home care operators is that falls are a clinical problem. In practice, the constraint is almost always operational. The tools that work in a clinic require specialist staff. The assessments that fit a home care visit do not. That operational gap is why the clinical evidence and the field adoption diverge by years. If you are running falls screening across a multi-site network, I would value the counter-example."

Example C, generic close

Before: "We are committed to transforming falls prevention in home care. What do you think?" Fails: "Transforming" / corporate register (2.3), generic rhetorical close (3.6). After: "The question I am carrying at the moment is how often care systems are capturing left and right grip readings separately. If yours is not, the richer signal is essentially free to add."

Example D, corporate news in press-release voice

Before: "Today, we are delighted to announce the launch of Able Assess v2.1, featuring faster processing, improved reporting, and a new dashboard experience. Huge thanks to the incredible team that made this happen!" Fails: "We are delighted to announce" opener (2.2, corporate-news rules), "incredible" (2.3), exclamation mark (2.1), no specific detail. After: "The most useful thing we added to Able Assess this quarter is not the faster processing. It is that the grip reading now shows left and right as separate bars rather than an averaged number. A physiotherapist in the Cypress pilot spotted that two identical averages can carry very different asymmetry. We shipped v2.1 this morning. The release notes are on the product page."

7. Editing checklist for Dani (and Paul and Mike)

Dani is the default approver. She (or Paul or Mike) uses this file as the reference during sign-off. When a draft feels off:

  1. Scan section 2.1 to 2.5. Is the draft breaking a hard rule? If yes, that is the edit.
  2. Check section 3. Is a positive rule missing? Most often, the "only-the-author-would-know detail" (3.4) is absent.
  3. Ask: could this post have been written by anyone at any similar company? If yes, the post needs a specific detail added.
  4. If the same issue appears in two or more drafts in the same week, edit this file to codify the rule. Do not re-flag on each individual post.

8. What this skill does not do

  • Does not enforce the voice profiles. That is sections 03, 04 and 05.
  • Does not enforce the claims library. That is Marketing Sign-Off/02 - Claims Guardrail Library v2.md.
  • Does not enforce the contentious-topic list. That is the ghostwriter spec.
  • Does not decide whether a post should be published. That is Dani's sign-off.
  • Does not replace human judgement on tone nuance. For edge cases, Dani asks Paul or Mike directly.

The skill is the universal style floor. The voice profiles are the ceiling. The claims library is the fact-check. Sign-off is the go-live. All four are needed.

Demo pack

07. Sample Posts

Six sample posts, two per leader. Every claim is drawn from the Able Care Claims Guardrail Library v2. Every post has been checked against the voice profile for the named leader, the AI-tell list, and the contentious-topic list.

These are the artefacts to put in front of Dani and the two doctors in the demo. The ask is: does this sound like you?

Post 1: Dr Paul Rinne, Evidence-led educational

Target insight: Grip strength as a settled biomarker in geriatric care Cited claims: 2.1, 2.2 (Claims Guardrail Library v2) Target segment: Clinician and researcher audience (cross-segment) Word count: 186

One of the most useful clinical conversations of the last decade has already happened. It is about grip strength.

The CDC's STEADI guidance lists grip strength decline as a correlate of increased hospitalisation risk, functional decline and premature mortality. Clinical consensus in geriatrics treats it as an independent predictor of outcomes that matter at the system level.

The interesting question is not whether the measurement is worth taking. That debate is over. The interesting question is why it is still a specialist-administered assessment in most settings, when the protocol itself is a two-minute hand-dynamometer measurement.

We have spent the last three years on that specific gap. The measurement is cheap, the clinical value is well documented, and the workflow is the bottleneck. If any care system has worked out a protocol that gets grip strength into a routine visit, at scale, I would value the comparison. We have made progress on the workflow side. I am interested in where the remaining friction sits in other settings.

Suggested first comment: Link to CDC STEADI page. Suggested publish day and time: Tuesday 08:30 UK. Notes for Dani's review: No claim sits close to the guardrail boundary. All references are to CDC STEADI consensus, which is in Claims 2.1 and 2.2.

Post 2: Dr Paul Rinne, Translation-of-research

Target insight: Asymmetrical grip strength as a neuromuscular signal Cited claims: 2.3, 2.4 (Claims Guardrail Library v2) Target segment: Clinicians and home care leadership Word count: 178

A grip measurement that looks average tells a different story once you separate left from right.

Hand dynamometry produces reproducible grip strength readings across repeated measurements. That is the standard clinical result, and it is well established. What a single-value bilateral reading misses is asymmetry. A patient whose dominant-hand reading sits in the expected range but whose non-dominant-hand reading is fifteen per cent weaker is showing a neuromuscular imbalance. In my experience this is a routinely useful signal in home care and community settings, and it often surfaces before a specific clinical concern is documented.

The clinical interpretation sits with the physiotherapist or the physician. The measurement itself does not. That matters operationally, because asymmetry is visible in data long before it becomes visible at the bedside. The question I am carrying at the moment is how often care systems are capturing left and right readings separately. If yours is not, the richer signal is essentially free to add.

Suggested first comment: "Happy to share the observational notes from the Cypress Home Care team if useful." Suggested publish day and time: Thursday 08:30 UK. Notes for Dani's review: Claim 2.4 ("asymmetrical grip strength suggests neuromuscular imbalance and increases fall risk") is sourced to customer evidence. Paul is comfortable referencing this as a clinical observation. If he wants to tighten this to a single-study reference, flag.

Post 3: Dr Mike Mace, Measurement-design

Target insight: The engineering trade-off between peak force and force-curve data Cited claims: 2.3, 3.6 (Claims Guardrail Library v2) Target segment: Engineers, researchers, clinical measurement teams Word count: 204

Hand dynamometry produces reproducible grip strength readings across repeated measurements. That is the reliability story, and it is well earned.

The part that does not get discussed as much is what is in the sampled signal. Older dynamometers capture peak force. You squeeze, the device records the maximum, you stop. One number, one interpretation.

A sensor sampling continuously captures the full force curve. That lets you separate three characteristics that act differently in clinical populations. Peak force maps to muscle capacity. Sustained force tracks neuromuscular control. The rate of force development is what tends to decline first in older adults and what often predicts fall recovery specifically. Each of these is a different physiological signal riding on the same gesture.

The reliability of the measurement does not depend on which of these you look at, provided the protocol is clean. The clinical usefulness does. The devices are portable and reusable, so the real constraint is not hardware cost. It is whether the analysis pipeline is asking questions beyond the peak. If you are designing a clinical workflow and still using peak-only readings, the full curve is where the interesting signal lives.

Suggested first comment: None for this one. Suggested publish day and time: Wednesday 09:00 UK. Notes for Dani's review: This post assumes Mike is comfortable being specific about sampling characteristics. If he is not, substitute a more general "continuous vs peak" framing. The engineering audience will recognise it either way.

Post 4: Dr Mike Mace, Workflow and human factors

Target insight: Non-clinical staff protocol design Cited claims: 3.1, 3.2, 3.3 (Claims Guardrail Library v2) Target segment: Home care and senior living product and operations leaders Word count: 192

The test that works in a clinic does not always survive a home visit.

One of the constraints we designed Able Assess against was the home care aide's visit clock. A typical visit is short. The clinical protocol that requires three trials with two-minute rests between them is sound in a lab and operationally impossible at the bedside. The rest timers alone would eat the available window.

The version that ended up in the field takes five to fifteen minutes from start to finish. It runs as four sequential tests: grip strength, chair stand speed, gait speed and standing balance. A non-clinical staff member can be trained to administer it in under fifteen minutes. There is no specialist licence required.

The reason those numbers matter is that they are what determines whether the assessment is used at all. A protocol that looks like forty-five minutes of unfamiliar clinical work will not survive the first quarter of a rollout. Usability is not a soft constraint in this setting. It is the constraint.

Suggested first comment: "Happy to discuss the reliability study that informed the single-trial protocol." Suggested publish day and time: Friday 09:00 UK. Notes for Dani's review: Claim 3.3 ("non-clinical staff can be trained in under fifteen minutes") is currently flagged in the library as customer-experience evidence needing formal validation. The post uses the phrasing exactly as approved. Safe to publish in current form.

Post 5: Dani, Operator conversation

Target insight: Franchise standardisation as the operator's frame for falls screening Cited claims: 4.5 (Claims Guardrail Library v2) Target segment: PE-backed and franchised home care Word count: 165

A Commercial VP at a franchised home care group said something on a call last week that has stayed with me.

They do not have a falls screening problem. They have a franchise standardisation problem. Each individual branch captures risk differently, if they capture it at all. Corporate needs one defensible outcome story across fifty-plus branches. The branches need a tool that does not require hiring specialist clinical staff to run it.

Her framing made the commercial ask clear. In a market where pricing and caregiving appear similar across agencies, the differentiator is the evidence-based story corporate can show to referral partners and boards. The tool that delivers it has to sit naturally inside a visit that already has no time to spare.

Most multi-site operators I speak to this quarter are landing in the same place. If you see something different in your network, I would value the counter-example.

Suggested first comment: None. Suggested publish day and time: Monday 08:00 UK. Notes for Dani's review: Claim 4.5 is referenced almost verbatim. No competitor or PE firm is named. The conversation is fully anonymised.

Post 6: Dani, Commercial case

Target insight: The economics of losing a high-acuity home care case Cited claims: 1.9, 1.10, 4.6 (Claims Guardrail Library v2) Target segment: PE-backed and independent multi-site home care Word count: 178

The economics of losing one twenty-four-seven home care case are worth putting on paper.

A single twenty-four-seven case generates approximately $350,000 USD in annual revenue. At forty per cent gross margin, which is standard in the segment, that is $140,000 or more in gross profit per case. Losing three high-acuity cases at once affects roughly fifteen caregivers and opens a forty-eight to seventy-two hour window before competitor churn starts.

That is the number behind the falls prevention conversation, and it is why operators in this segment are not asking clinical questions first. They are asking how early risk identification protects the revenue and the roster. Keeping a high-acuity client at home longer, by catching functional decline before the hospital visit, is a retention and revenue argument before it is a clinical one.

I have the full breakdown from the Cypress Home Care case and the Right at Home Tri-County Metro material if anyone running multi-site home care wants to work through the commercial model directly.

Suggested first comment: None. Suggested publish day and time: Wednesday 08:00 UK. Notes for Dani's review: Uses Claims 1.9 and 1.10 at their exact approved wording. Note 1.9 carries the internal flag that the forty per cent margin assumption needs benchmarking. If Dani is not ready to publish that number, we can recast the post around the case-count and caregiver-count impact and drop the revenue figure.

08. Demo Script

Forty-five minutes. Dani hosts. Matt runs the demo. Paul and Mike sit in for the voice-profile sections.

Goals

  1. Get a yes or a no from Paul and Mike on their voice profiles.
  2. Get Dani's approval on the two-agent architecture and the operating cadence.
  3. Agree the pilot plan: two posts per leader per week, four-week pilot, weekly Dani review.
  4. Confirm the vault path, the branch name, and ownership of the weekly research run.

Agenda

00:00 to 03:00 Problem framing

Two sentences from Matt. The market wants to hear from credible clinicians and commercial operators in this space. Paul and Mike are credible but cautious. We need a system that gives them the confidence to post without the friction of writing from scratch.

03:00 to 08:00 The model

Open 00 - Strategy Overview.md. Walk through the architecture diagram. Make three points.

  1. Two agents. One researches, one drafts. They never share a prompt.
  2. Every post passes an evidence gate against the Claims Guardrail Library before it reaches Dani.
  3. Three voice profiles, not one. Paul, Mike, Dani each have their own.

08:00 to 15:00 Sample posts (the main event)

Open 07 - Sample Posts.md. Read each post aloud, one at a time. Ask each named leader the single calibration question: does this sound like you?

Order: Paul first (Post 1 and Post 2), then Mike (Post 3 and Post 4), then Dani (Post 5 and Post 6).

Capture feedback against each post in the margin. The exact wording Paul and Mike use to push back is the single most valuable input of the demo. That wording feeds directly back into the voice profile files.

15:00 to 22:00 Voice profile walk-through

Open 03 - Voice Profile - Paul Rinne.md. Walk the three anchor posts and the "what I never say" list. Paul confirms or edits in real time.

Repeat for Mike with 04 - Voice Profile - Mike Mace.md.

Dani self-confirms 05 - Voice Profile - Danielle Richards.md after the session, no need to walk it live.

22:00 to 28:00 Research agent

Open 01 - Research Agent Spec.md. Show the scope section. Confirm the target market list matches Falls Prevention Commercialization Strategy 2026. Show the runnable prompt.

Do not run it live in the demo. The output already exists in the sample run. Open that file and show it.

28:00 to 34:00 Ghostwriter agent

Open 02 - Ghostwriter Agent Spec.md. Show the three voice-specific prompts, the evidence gate, the AI-tell check, the contentious-topic check. These are the guardrails that make Paul and Mike comfortable.

34:00 to 36:00 Vault structure and knowledge-base link

Open 06 - KB Branch Structure.md. Show the folder tree, including the new 08-news-and-campaigns/ folder. Confirm the vault path (C:\Users\matty\AbleCare\AbleCare-KB) and the proposed branch name (linkedin-agent-system).

Flag the Phase 2 relationship to the able-care-marketing-audit knowledge-base website. Posts that perform well become full KB articles.

36:00 to 38:00 News and campaigns intake, and the style rulebook

Open 09 - News and Campaigns Schedule.md. Show the weekly intake form and the current-quarter schedule. Confirm Dani owns the intake and will log entries weekly.

Open 10 - Authentic Content Skill.md. Show the style rulebook. Make the point clearly: this is the document to edit if the writing style is not quite right. One change here changes every future post. Paul, Mike and Dani can edit it directly.

38:00 to 42:00 Pilot plan and commercials

Propose:

  • Four-week pilot, starting the Monday after sign-off.
  • Two posts per leader per week, six posts total per week.
  • Dani reviews on her standard review day, alongside the blog and KB sign-off lists.
  • Buffer for scheduling. Paul, Mike and Dani each authorise their Buffer connection once.
  • Weekly research run Monday morning, owned by Matt for the pilot.

Ask for sign-off on the plan.

42:00 to 45:00 Decisions and actions

Log three things:

  1. Yes, no or conditional on the architecture.
  2. Voice profile changes flagged in the sample-post review.
  3. Start date.

Pre-demo checklist (Matt, the morning of)

  • All eight files in C:\Users\matty\AbleCare\AI Marketing Action Plan\LinkedIn Agent System\ open in Obsidian or a markdown viewer.
  • The Claims Guardrail Library v2 open in a side pane so any claim can be verified against the exact approved wording during the demo.
  • The six sample posts open in a separate document so Paul and Mike can see them side by side.
  • Buffer or Taplio tab open (scheduler not required for the demo itself, but useful to show the commodity layer).

Post-demo checklist (Matt, same day)

  • Update the three voice-profile files with Paul and Mike's verbatim feedback.
  • Move the sample posts into 05-drafts/ in the vault if the approach is approved.
  • Create the branch linkedin-agent-system in the vault and commit the new folder structure.
  • Diarise the first weekly research run for the following Monday.
  • Add a row to a new LinkedIn Sign-off List.csv at the workspace root for each of the six sample posts, status "pending post-demo".

Failure modes to watch for

Paul says the science is right but the voice is wrong. Expected. The three anchor posts in the voice profile are the lever. Ask him to describe the specific words that are off, then rewrite one post live if time allows.

Mike says the post is too commercial. Also expected. Strip any Able Care brand mentions from Mike's posts beyond the minimum. Mike writes as a scientist first.

Dani says Paul's post is too cautious to get engagement. Real risk. Counter: the goal is not viral engagement. The goal is that senior clinicians and operators reading it respect it. Engagement follows trust in this audience.

Paul or Mike say they will not post this. Not a failure. That is the highest-value signal from the demo. Find out the specific reason. It is almost always one of three things: a claim they do not yet feel confident in, a tone that reads as vendor, or a topic area they would rather stay quiet on. Each of those is fixable in the profile.

Segments and personas

Buyer personas

Buyer Persona Cards — Able Care

Six reusable persona profiles covering every buying committee role across Able Care's priority segments. Each card is grounded in the value framing from the Falls Prevention Commercialization Strategy 2026 and the Claims Library.

Persona 1: PE Operating Partner / Portfolio Manager

Role summary: Oversees a portfolio of healthcare services companies (home care franchises, senior living operators, or SNF chains) on behalf of a private equity fund. Responsible for value creation across the portfolio. Reports to the fund's investment committee. Makes or approves technology decisions that affect multiple portfolio companies simultaneously.

Primary KPIs: - Net Operating Income (NOI) across portfolio companies - EBITDA growth and margin expansion - Portfolio company valuation at exit - Speed of operational standardisation across acquisitions

Top three pains related to falls and staff: 1. Falls create unbudgeted liability exposure across the portfolio. A single lawsuit at one community can wipe out a quarter's margin improvement across the entire holding. 2. Staff turnover in non-clinical home care runs at 60-80% annually. Every new hire costs approximately $10,000 in recruiting, onboarding, and lost productivity. Complex tools make turnover worse because institutional knowledge walks out the door with every departing carer. 3. Technology fragmentation across acquired companies slows integration timelines. Each acquisition brings a different tech stack, and standardising clinical workflows post-acquisition is one of the longest lead-time integration tasks.

Buying criteria: - Demonstrable ROI within 90 days (ideally with a pilot-to-portfolio rollout model) - Minimal IT footprint and fast deployment (no heavy integration project) - Scalable across all portfolio companies without per-site customisation - Defensible clinical basis (regulatory risk is a board-level concern) - Clear data story they can present to the investment committee

Common objections: - "We need to see ROI data before we commit across the portfolio." - "Our portfolio companies each have different workflows. Will this actually work everywhere?" - "We already have clinical programs in place. Why add another tool?"

Proof points they respond to: - One 24-7 home care case generates $350,000/year in revenue at 40% gross margin. Losing one client to a preventable fall costs $140,000+ in gross profit. Preventing a single loss event pays for Able Assess across multiple sites. (Source: Cypress HomeCare Case Study Call) - One in four adults over 65 falls each year. $80 billion in annual US healthcare spend on non-fatal falls. (Source: 5 Pillars Strategy, Website Opportunity Analysis) - Able Assess is administered by non-clinical staff in under 15 minutes with minutes of training. No IT project. No clinical licence required. (Source: 5 Pillars Strategy)

Lead value pillar: Protect the Portfolio — standardise and scale across all communities in days, not months.

Persona 2: Corporate CEO / COO / VP Operations

Role summary: Runs day-to-day operations across a multi-site home care franchise network or senior living brand. Accountable for census, margin, staff retention, and quality ratings. Reports to a PE-backed board or a non-profit board of directors. The person who signs off on technology purchases and sets the operational playbook that individual sites follow.

Primary KPIs: - Census / occupancy rate across all sites - Staff turnover rate and cost per hire - Resident or client retention (length of stay / length of engagement) - Quality ratings (CMS Five-Star for SNFs, state survey results for AL/IL) - Liability claims and insurance costs

Top three pains related to falls and staff: 1. Falls are the single biggest driver of resident turnover in senior living and client loss in home care. Every fall event risks hospitalisation, discharge, and census loss. 2. Non-clinical staff in home care are largely 1099 contractors in a permanent high-turnover loop. Tools that require more than a few minutes of training are abandoned within weeks. 3. Liability from fall-related lawsuits is one of the largest operating cost exposures. Objective documentation of risk assessment is the strongest legal defence, but most operators lack it.

Buying criteria: - Proven impact on the KPIs they own (census, retention, turnover, liability) - Easy rollout that does not burden already-stretched site managers - Fits into existing workflows rather than creating new ones - Credible clinical basis that survives regulatory scrutiny - A vendor that understands operator economics, not just clinical outcomes

Common objections: - "Our staff don't have time for another tool." - "We already do falls screening with paper-based assessments." - "How does this improve our quality ratings specifically?"

Proof points they respond to: - Screening has been shown to reduce falls by 25%. (Source: Falls Prevention Commercialization Strategy, claims awaiting full customer validation) - Non-clinical staff trained in under 15 minutes. No clinical background required. (Source: 5 Pillars Strategy) - Cypress HomeCare uses baseline grip strength to identify weakness, trigger PT referrals, and prevent falls before they happen. Result: client stays in home, avoids hospitalisation, continues on service. (Source: Cypress HomeCare Case Study Call)

Lead value pillar: Protect the P&L — reduce staff burden and turnover with non-clinical screening.

Persona 3: Franchise Owner / Community Executive Director

Role summary: The on-the-ground operator. Runs a single franchise location (home care) or a single community (senior living). Manages the local P&L, hires and retains staff, and is directly accountable for client or resident outcomes. Often the first point of contact in a bottom-up sales motion.

Primary KPIs: - Local census / client count - Staff retention at their site - Client or resident satisfaction scores - Monthly P&L (revenue minus local operating costs) - Referral volume and conversion rate

Top three pains related to falls and staff: 1. Losing a single high-value client (home care) or resident (senior living) to a fall-related event has an outsized impact on their local P&L. They feel each loss personally. 2. Recruiting and retaining carers or activity staff is a daily challenge. Anything that makes the job harder accelerates turnover. Anything that makes staff feel competent and valued improves retention. 3. Competing with neighbouring franchises or communities for the same pool of prospective clients/residents. Differentiation is difficult when the core service (personal care, community living) looks similar across providers.

Buying criteria: - Tangible differentiator they can show to prospective clients and families on the first visit - Easy for their current staff to use without specialist support - Affordable at their individual site budget (they pay franchise fees that reduce margins) - Something that makes their staff feel more professional and reduces day-to-day burden - Quick to show results so they can justify the cost to corporate or their own books

Common objections: - "I can't afford new technology right now." - "My staff are already stretched. I can't add anything to their plate." - "Corporate hasn't approved this yet."

Proof points they respond to: - Right at Home uses Able Assess at community events as a lead generation tool. Hands-on demo creates instant conversation starters and converts interest into assessments. (Source: Right at Home Tri-County Metro Onboarding Call) - "The thing that really stands out about AbleCare is the physical device. There's science behind it, physicality behind it, a measurement behind it. That's a big differentiator for us." (Source: Home Care Growth Brief) - Preventing the loss of one 24-7 home care client pays for Able Assess many times over. (Source: Cypress HomeCare Case Study Call)

Lead value pillar: Protect Growth — tech differentiator that lifts census, attracts residents, accelerates growth.

Persona 4: Head of Clinical / VP Clinical Services / Director of Nursing

Role summary: The clinical champion inside the buying organisation. Responsible for care quality, clinical outcomes, staff clinical competency, and regulatory compliance. They are the person who evaluates whether a tool is clinically sound and recommends it to the CEO or COO. In B2B healthcare, this persona is rarely the budget holder but almost always the internal advocate.

Primary KPIs: - Clinical outcomes (fall rates, hospitalisation rates, readmission rates) - Staff competency and compliance with clinical protocols - Regulatory survey results and quality ratings - Care plan accuracy and documentation quality - Resident or client satisfaction with care

Top three pains related to falls and staff: 1. Subjective clinical judgment misses 30-40% of at-risk individuals. Without objective screening data, care plans are built on incomplete information. 2. Staff burden from falls is enormous. Every fall event triggers incident reports, family notifications, potential regulatory reporting, and increased monitoring. Prevention is dramatically cheaper than response. 3. Maintaining standardised care quality across multiple sites is difficult when each site has different staff capabilities and turnover rates. A tool that works regardless of the user's clinical background solves a real standardisation problem.

Buying criteria: - Clinically defensible methodology (peer-reviewed, guideline-aligned) - CDC STEADI alignment (this is the gold standard they reference) - Produces data they can use in care planning and regulatory reporting - Does not require them to hire additional clinical staff to administer - Integrates with or at least does not conflict with existing clinical workflows

Common objections: - "Is this clinically validated? What guidelines does it follow?" - "Our nurses already handle falls screening. Why change?" - "How does the data integrate with our existing care documentation?"

Proof points they respond to: - Able Assess captures the four measurements recommended by the 2022 World Guidelines for Falls Prevention: grip strength, timed up and go, 30-second chair to stand, and 4-metre gait speed. (Source: Falls Prevention Commercialization Strategy) - CDC STEADI aligned. The screening framework follows the same stepped approach recommended by the CDC's Stopping Elderly Accidents, Deaths and Injuries programme. (Source: Product Overview) - Grip strength is an independent predictor of mortality, morbidity, functional decline, and falls risk. (Source: Grip Strength as Biomarker claim) - "If you do it repetitively, you're going to score about the same. But if you do it over time, you can see there's someone strengthening or someone weakening." (Source: Cypress HomeCare Case Study Call)

Lead value pillar: Protect Residents — fewer falls, fewer hospitalisations, longer independence.

Persona 5: Head of Innovation / IT Director

Role summary: The enabler. Evaluates technology for security, integration feasibility, and deployment complexity. They do not own the budget but they can block a purchase. Their concern is whether the tool fits the existing tech stack, meets security requirements, and can be deployed without creating an IT project that consumes their team's bandwidth.

Primary KPIs: - System uptime and reliability - Security and compliance posture (HIPAA, SOC 2, data handling) - Integration success rate with existing platforms (EMR, care management, billing) - Deployment speed and support ticket volume for new tools - Vendor management overhead

Top three pains related to falls and staff: 1. Every new tool creates integration demands. Home care and senior living operators typically run 5-15 software platforms. Adding another creates data silos unless integration is straightforward. 2. HIPAA compliance is non-negotiable. Any vendor handling patient or resident data must demonstrate adequate security posture. Vendors that cannot answer a security questionnaire quickly are disqualified. 3. Staff turnover means the IT team repeatedly re-onboards users. Tools with complex setup or user management create ongoing support burden that scales with turnover rate.

Buying criteria: - HIPAA compliant with documented security posture - Minimal integration requirements (API-first, or standalone with export capability) - Fast user provisioning and de-provisioning (critical in high-turnover environments) - Mobile-first (staff use phones and tablets, not desktops) - Vendor responsiveness on security questionnaires and procurement documentation

Common objections: - "This needs to go through IT / procurement." - "How does this integrate with our EMR?" - "What's your security posture? Can you complete our vendor assessment?"

Proof points they respond to: - Able Care holds FDA registration, CE marking, ISO 27001, ISO 13485, IEC 60601, HIPAA compliance, and GDPR compliance. (Source: Product Overview, Marketing Audit) - No IT heavy-lift required. Tablet or phone delivery. Data syncs to web portal. EMR integration on the roadmap (AccessCare, Home Care Pro). (Source: AbleAssess Speed and Simplicity claim) - "Our product can be easily integrated to other systems." (Source: Falls Prevention Commercialization Strategy)

Lead value pillar: Protect the Portfolio — standardised, easy-to-deploy tools scale faster than clinical programmes.

Persona 6: CCRC Board Chair / Non-Profit CEO

Role summary: Leads a mission-driven continuing care retirement community. Non-profit CCRC boards are composed of volunteers, often local business leaders, lawyers, and retired residents. They are not optimising for profit. They are optimising for mission alignment, risk mitigation, and long-term financial sustainability of the "life-care fund" that guarantees residents care for life.

Primary KPIs: - Resident wellbeing and satisfaction - Life-care fund actuarial health (can the organisation meet its "stay for life" guarantee?) - Risk mitigation (liability exposure, regulatory compliance) - Staff satisfaction and retention (mission-driven organisations attract values-aligned staff) - Community reputation and census stability

Top three pains related to falls and staff: 1. The "stay for life" guarantee means every resident who progresses to higher-acuity care (from IL to AL to SNF) draws more heavily on the life-care fund. Falls accelerate that progression. Preventing falls directly protects the fund's actuarial health. 2. Non-profit boards are risk-averse by governance obligation. Fall-related lawsuits create reputational damage and financial exposure that is disproportionately threatening to a non-profit compared to a for-profit entity. 3. Mission alignment matters deeply. The organisation exists to maintain resident independence and quality of life. A tool that demonstrably supports that mission has an easier path to approval than one framed purely in commercial terms.

Buying criteria: - Clear alignment with the community's mission (maintaining independence, supporting wellbeing) - Defensible clinical basis (the board includes retired physicians and healthcare executives) - Risk mitigation value (documented screening provides legal defence) - Affordable within a non-profit budget (framing as investment in fund sustainability, not expense) - Resident and family engagement value (something that improves the experience, not just the metrics)

Common objections: - "We're a non-profit. This isn't in our budget." - "Our residents are already well cared for. We don't have a falls problem." - "How does this align with our mission?"

Proof points they respond to: - Falls are the leading cause of resident transition from IL to AL to SNF. Preventing falls extends the time residents spend at lower-acuity (and lower-cost) levels of care. This directly protects the life-care fund. (Source: Falls Prevention Commercialization Strategy, CCRC section) - Able Assess provides objective insights to a resident's healthspan and functional longevity. It helps keep residents engaged in their own wellbeing. (Source: Falls Prevention Commercialization Strategy) - Eskaton (a non-profit CCRC operator) is an existing Able Care customer. (Source: Falls Prevention Commercialization Strategy) - One in four adults over 65 falls each year. Screening reduces falls. This is not speculative. It is the standard of care recommended by the CDC and the 2022 World Guidelines. (Source: Claims Library)

Lead value pillar: Protect Residents — fewer falls, fewer hospitalisations, longer independence. (With secondary emphasis on Protect the Business for the risk mitigation angle.)

How to Use These Cards

  1. Before writing any content: Check which persona the piece targets. Lead with their vocabulary. Reference their KPIs. Use their proof points.
  2. Before any sales outreach: Identify which persona you are contacting. Pull the relevant objections and proof points into your prep brief.
  3. When reviewing drafts: Ask "would this persona care about this sentence?" If the answer is no, rewrite it in their language or remove it.
  4. When building segment pages: Each segment page should address 2-3 personas from this set. Lead with the persona who holds the budget. Support with the persona who champions internally.

Segment one-pagers

Seven clean, standardised HTML one-pagers. Each opens as its own web page with consistent Able Care branding and a 'Save as PDF' button. Share the URL, or print to PDF for a downloadable version. Primary gating asset for each segment's landing page and the lead offer in the BDR sequences.

  • PE-backed home care Opens in a new tab. Save as PDF from inside.
  • Multi-site home care Opens in a new tab. Save as PDF from inside.
  • CCRCs Opens in a new tab. Save as PDF from inside.
  • Corporate assisted living Opens in a new tab. Save as PDF from inside.
  • Independent living and rental retirement Opens in a new tab. Save as PDF from inside.
  • For-profit SNFs Opens in a new tab. Save as PDF from inside.
  • Home health agencies Opens in a new tab. Save as PDF from inside.

Messaging and positioning

Claims Guardrail Library

Able Care Claims Guardrail Library

Master reference document for reviewing and approving any AI-generated content before publication. This library defines approved claims with evidence strength, prohibited language patterns, and step-by-step review protocol.

SECTION 1: APPROVED CLAIMS

All claims listed below are sourced from Able Care's Knowledge Base Evidence & Claims section. Each claim includes exact approved wording, citation, and evidence strength.

Falls Epidemiology
Claim 1.1: One in Four Adults Over 65 Falls Each Year

Approved wording: "One in four adults over 65 falls each year." Evidence strength: Industry estimate (peer-reviewed guideline) Source: CDC STEADI guidelines; AbleAssess for Families Marketing Piece Use in: Operator-facing (position as prevention opportunity); consumer-facing (establish problem urgency) Context: Establishes population prevalence for fall risk; justifies screening need

Claim 1.2: Falls Generate $80 Billion USD Annually in US Healthcare Spend

Approved wording: "$80 billion USD annually in healthcare spend on non-fatal falls." Evidence strength: Industry estimate (peer-reviewed epidemiology) Source: 5 Pillars Strategy; Website Opportunity Analysis Use in: Operator-facing content (emphasise market size and ROI); payer communications Context: Quantifies economic incentive for fall prevention; supports scaling argument

Claim 1.3: 50% of Home Health Beneficiaries Fall Each Year

Approved wording: "50% of home health beneficiaries fall each year." Evidence strength: Industry estimate (healthcare system data) Source: 5 Pillars Strategy Use in: Home health and senior living sales collateral Context: Segment-specific prevalence; higher risk population than general older adult cohort

Claim 1.4: Falls Are Preventable With Early Risk Identification

Approved wording: "Falls are preventable. Warning signs can be identified early through objective screening." Evidence strength: Clinical guidance (CDC STEADI, clinical practice) Source: Clinical guidelines; customer case evidence Use in: All segments Context: Reframes falls from inevitable to addressable; positions early identification as standard of care

Claim 1.5: Falls Trigger Hospitalization, Acuity Progression, and Move-Outs

Approved wording: "A single fall can trigger hospitalization, acuity progression, and loss of independence." Evidence strength: Clinical evidence (outcome data from customer care) Source: Falls Cost and Prevalence claim; Cypress HomeCare Case Study Call Use in: Consumer-facing and operator-facing (retention focus) Context: Quantifies consequence of fall event; justifies prevention investment

Claim 1.6: Falls Are the #2 Reason for Urgent Care / Emergency Department Visits (After Medication Adherence)

Approved wording: "Falls are the second-most common reason people present to urgent care or emergency departments in older adult care, after medication adherence issues." Evidence strength: Customer data [DIRECTIONAL — needs validation] Source: Cypress HomeCare Case Study Call Use in: Operator-facing sales (emphasise operational burden) Context: Links falls prevention to ED utilisation reduction; supports business case

Claim 1.7: Fall-Related Litigation Is a Major Cost Centre in Senior Living and Home Care

Approved wording: "Fall-related legal claims are a significant cost centre in senior living and home care organisations." Evidence strength: Customer anecdote (requires health economics research to validate) Source: Falls Cost and Prevalence KB entry [DIRECTIONAL — awaiting legal/claims analysis] Use in: Risk, compliance, and quality rating discussions Context: Supports documentation and liability defence narrative

Claim 1.8: Falls Drive Staff Burden and Burnout

Approved wording: "Fall events increase staff workload and burnout, as crisis response diverts resources from preventive care." Evidence strength: Customer evidence; operational observation Source: Falls Cost and Prevalence KB entry; customer interviews Use in: Operator-facing (staff retention angle) Context: Links fall prevention to staff morale and retention

Claim 1.9: Losing One 24-7 Care Case Costs $140,000+ in Gross Profit

Approved wording: "A single 24-7 care case generates approximately $350,000 in annual revenue. At 40% gross margin (industry standard), losing one case represents $140,000+ in gross profit loss." Evidence strength: Customer data [proprietary calculation] Source: Cypress HomeCare Case Study Call; Home Care Growth Brief Use in: Home care agency sales and ROI discussions Context: Quantifies revenue protection; supports Able Assess business case Note: 40% margin assumption requires verification against industry benchmarks

Claim 1.10: Losing Three 24-7 Cases Triggers Caregiver Churn

Approved wording: "Losing three high-acuity cases simultaneously affects 15 caregivers and creates a 48-72 hour window for reassignment before competitor churn occurs." Evidence strength: Customer data [proprietary observation] Source: Cypress HomeCare Case Study Call Use in: Home care agency operator discussions Context: Illustrates cascading operational risk; emphasises prevention value Note: Timeframe and caregiver ratio are customer-specific observations; may vary by agency size

Grip Strength as Biomarker
Claim 2.1: Grip Strength Is an Independent Predictor of Mortality and Morbidity

Approved wording: "Grip strength is an independent predictor of mortality, morbidity, hospitalisation risk, and loss of independence in older adults." Evidence strength: Peer-reviewed guidelines (CDC STEADI); emerging research Source: CDC STEADI; Falls Prevention Commercialisation Strategy; clinical practice guidelines Use in: All segments (establish clinical credibility) Context: Positions grip strength as fundamental biomarker; supported by geriatric clinical consensus Note: Seek peer-reviewed publication (JAMA, Lancet) documenting grip strength as independent predictor for full evidence upgrade

Claim 2.2: Grip Strength Correlates With Increased Hospitalisation Risk and Functional Decline

Approved wording: "Grip strength decline correlates with increased hospitalisation risk, functional decline, and premature mortality." Evidence strength: Peer-reviewed guidelines (CDC STEADI); clinical consensus Source: Falls Prevention Commercialisation Strategy; CDC STEADI recommendations Use in: Clinical and operator-facing content Context: Justifies measurement as part of standard assessment protocol

Claim 2.3: Grip Strength Can Be Measured Objectively With High Reproducibility

Approved wording: "Grip strength is measured objectively using hand dynamometry with high reproducibility across repeated measurements." Evidence strength: Clinical validation (laboratory standard) Source: Grip Strength as Biomarker KB; clinical standards for dynamometry Use in: Product marketing (emphasis on precision and reliability) Context: Differentiates from subjective assessment; supports trend analysis capability

Claim 2.4: Asymmetrical Grip Strength (e.g., Left-Side Weakness) Indicates Fall Risk

Approved wording: "Asymmetrical grip strength (weakness on one side) suggests neuromuscular imbalance and increases fall risk." Evidence strength: Customer evidence; clinical observation Source: Cypress HomeCare Case Study Call; physical therapy practice Use in: Operator and clinician-facing sales Context: Provides actionable interpretation for Able Assess results; triggers PT referral

Claim 2.5: Grip Strength Improvement Over Time Indicates Response to Intervention

Approved wording: "Repeated grip strength measurement over time documents response to strength training, nutrition, or rehabilitation interventions." Evidence strength: Customer observation; clinical practice standard Source: Cypress HomeCare Case Study Call; Right at Home Tri-County Metro Onboarding Call Use in: Retention and clinical impact narratives Context: Enables measurement of intervention efficacy; supports ongoing care justification

Claim 2.6: Able Assess Normative Database Based on 1,000+ Healthy Individuals

Approved wording: "Able Assess grip strength norms are benchmarked against more than 1,000 healthy individuals by age and gender." Evidence strength: Product validation (internal database) Source: Right at Home Tri-County Metro Onboarding Call; product documentation Use in: Product credibility discussion Context: Supports interpretation of individual results relative to healthy peers Note: Publish normative data paper (with permission) to validate publicly

Product Speed, Simplicity, and Operability
