Claim 3.1: AbleAssess Screening Completes in 5-15 Minutes

Approved wording: "AbleAssess screening takes 5 to 15 minutes from start to finish, with immediate results and no waiting for specialist review." Evidence strength: Product specification and customer experience Source: AbleAssess for Families Marketing Piece; 5 Pillars Strategy Use in: All segments (efficiency argument) Context: Establishes low time burden; supports integration into busy workflows

Claim 3.2: AbleAssess Requires No Clinical License to Administer

Approved wording: "AbleAssess can be administered by any non-clinical staff member—home care workers, activity staff, health coaches, or trained family members—without clinical licensure or specialist certification." Evidence strength: Product design and customer practice Source: 5 Pillars Strategy; Falls Prevention Commercialisation Strategy; customer deployment Use in: Operator-facing (supports non-specialist delivery model) Context: Enables scalability without expensive specialist hiring

Claim 3.3: Non-Clinical Staff Training Takes Under 15 Minutes

Approved wording: "Non-clinical staff can be trained to administer AbleAssess in under 15 minutes." Evidence strength: Customer experience [needs formal validation study] Source: 5 Pillars Strategy Use in: Implementation and scaling narratives Context: Reduces rollout burden; supports rapid deployment across portfolio Note: Conduct formal time-to-competence study to upgrade evidence strength

Claim 3.4: AbleAssess Measures Four Simple Functional Tests

Approved wording: "AbleAssess measures grip strength, chair stand speed, gait speed, and standing balance—four simple tests completed in sequence." Evidence strength: Product specification and clinical validation (CDC STEADI alignment) Source: AbleAssess for Families Marketing Piece; product design Use in: Consumer and operator-facing (demystify the test) Context: Emphasises simplicity and non-invasiveness; aligns with clinical standard protocols

Claim 3.5: AbleAssess Produces Clear, Visually Presentable Results

Approved wording: "Results are presented in a clear, visually formatted report showing comparison to healthy peers, risk flags (if indicated), and actionable next steps." Evidence strength: Product design; customer feedback Source: AbleAssess for Families Marketing Piece; Right at Home onboarding feedback Use in: Consumer and clinician-facing content Context: Supports family engagement and clinical decision-making

Claim 3.6: Equipment Is Portable and Reusable (No Consumables)

Approved wording: "The handheld dynamometer is portable, durable, reusable, and non-invasive. No consumables or ongoing supply costs." Evidence strength: Product specification Source: AbleAssess Speed and Simplicity KB Use in: Operator-facing (cost argument) Context: Supports economics discussion; emphasises low maintenance

Claim 3.7: AbleAssess Works in Any Setting (Home, Community, Clinic, Event)

Approved wording: "AbleAssess can be administered in any setting—private residence, community centre, senior living facility, clinic, or public event." Evidence strength: Customer deployment evidence Source: AbleAssess Speed and Simplicity KB; customer case studies Use in: Sales (emphasise flexibility) Context: Enables diverse deployment scenarios; supports event marketing

Business Impact and Revenue Protection
Claim 4.1: Able Assess Demonstrates Value in First Conversation

Approved wording: "Able Assess establishes an objective baseline on the first meeting, demonstrating clear value to families and prospects before deeper engagement." Evidence strength: Customer experience and sales methodology Source: Home Care Growth Brief; Retention and Conversion Impact KB Use in: Sales and acquisition narratives Context: Positions as immediate differentiator; supports conversion narrative

Claim 4.2: Periodic Re-Measurement Surfaces Functional Decline Early

Approved wording: "Periodic grip strength measurement identifies functional decline before it becomes clinically apparent, enabling proactive intervention." Evidence strength: Clinical practice and customer observation Source: Cypress HomeCare Case Study Call; Right at Home onboarding Use in: Retention and clinical impact narratives Context: Supports early intervention argument; justifies ongoing measurement

Claim 4.3: Early Identification of Fall Risk Enables Targeted Intervention

Approved wording: "Early identification of fall risk enables targeted intervention—physical therapy, strength training, home safety modifications—at lower cost than acute event management." Evidence strength: Clinical practice standard; health economics principle Source: Falls Cost and Prevalence KB; Retention and Conversion Impact KB Use in: All segments Context: Reframes measurement as prevention investment, not cost centre

Claim 4.4: Objective Baseline Improves Family Trust and Care Decisions

Approved wording: "An objective baseline measurement and clear report improve family understanding of functional status and support better care decisions with clinicians." Evidence strength: Customer testimonial and family feedback Source: AbleAssess for Families Marketing Piece Use in: Consumer and family-focused content Context: Emphasises family engagement benefit; supports retention through improved communication

Claim 4.5: Able Assess Differentiates Home Care Agencies From Competitors

Approved wording: "In a market where pricing and caregiving appear similar across agencies, Able Assess creates a tangible, evidence-based differentiator that attracts tech-savvy families and generates referrals." Evidence strength: Customer feedback and sales experience Source: Home Care Growth Brief; Retention and Conversion Impact KB Use in: Home care agency sales Context: Positions tool as competitive advantage; supports premium positioning

Claim 4.6: Home Care Operators Can Prevent Loss of High-Acuity Cases Through Fall Prevention

Approved wording: "By identifying fall risk early and enabling preventive intervention, home care operators reduce hospitalisations and keep high-acuity clients in home longer, protecting revenue and staff retention." Evidence strength: Customer case evidence Source: Cypress HomeCare Case Study Call; Retention and Conversion Impact KB Use in: Home care agency operator conversations Context: Quantifies retention impact; connects to revenue protection

Claim 4.7: Falls Prevention Improves Staff Morale and Retention

Approved wording: "Proactive fall prevention reduces crisis response burden, improves caregiver satisfaction, and supports staff retention in a sector with high turnover." Evidence strength: Customer observation; industry knowledge Source: Falls Cost and Prevalence KB; Cypress HomeCare discussions Use in: Operator-facing (staff retention angle) Context: Links clinical benefit to operational HR benefit

Claim 4.8: Senior Living Communities Can Improve Quality Ratings Through Documented Falls Risk Assessment

Approved wording: "Documented, objective falls risk assessment with intervention planning supports defence of CMS quality ratings and demonstrates commitment to resident safety." Evidence strength: Regulatory knowledge and customer practice Source: Retention and Conversion Impact KB Use in: Senior living and assisted living sales Context: Connects falls prevention to quality rating outcomes

Claim 4.9: Able Assess Results Enable Earlier Hospital and Physician Referrals

Approved wording: "Objective falls risk assessment enables home health and home care agencies to generate earlier physician referrals and hospital partnerships by demonstrating risk-stratification capability." Evidence strength: Customer opportunity and sales pipeline observation Source: Retention and Conversion Impact KB Use in: Home health and hospital partnership discussions Context: Positions tool as referral driver and partnership enabler

Claims Awaiting Evidence or Directional
Claim 5.1: 25% Average Reduction in Falls With Able Assess [DIRECTIONAL — AWAITING CUSTOMER DATA]

Status: Not approved for standalone use Direction: "Able Assess enables early identification and intervention that contributes to fall prevention outcomes." Evidence needed: Customer outcome data showing % fall reduction attributable to Able Assess screening + intervention Timeline: Q2–Q3 2026 (customer outcome tracking) Use case: Once validated, will support clinical impact claims and ROI calculations Note: Do not claim specific % reduction without published customer data

Claim 5.2: 3× Expected Screening Volume Growth in 1-2 Years [DIRECTIONAL — AWAITING VALIDATION]

Status: Not approved for marketing use Direction: "Able Assess demonstrates scalability across multiple sites and care settings." Evidence needed: Customer adoption data or ROI study showing volume trajectory Source noted as: 5 Pillars Strategy (directional only) Use case: Once validated, will support scaling narrative and financial projections Note: Do not cite without customer testimonial or published case study

Claim 5.3: Grip Strength as Cost-Effective Screening Tool [DIRECTIONAL — HEALTH ECONOMICS PAPER NEEDED]

Status: Partially supported (clinical efficiency is clear; health economics unclear) Direction: "Grip strength screening offers low-cost early identification of high-risk individuals compared to full geriatric assessment." Evidence needed: Health economics paper or cost-effectiveness analysis Use case: Once validated, will support payer and health system sales Note: Use clinical efficiency argument; avoid cost-effectiveness claims until published research available

SECTION 2: PROHIBITED LANGUAGE

No AI-generated content for Able Care may include the following language patterns, punctuation, or tonal violations. This section defines hard rules for copy review.

Punctuation Rules (Hard Rules)

Em dashes prohibited. Always use commas, parentheses, or full stops instead. - Incorrect: "Able Assess identifies fall risk quickly—within minutes—and delivers actionable results." - Correct: "Able Assess identifies fall risk quickly. Results are delivered within minutes and are actionable." - Alternative: "Able Assess identifies fall risk quickly (within minutes) and delivers actionable results."

Exclamation marks prohibited. Use period or emphasis through structure instead. - Incorrect: "Imagine preventing falls before they happen!" - Correct: "Falls are preventable. Early identification enables intervention before a fall occurs."

Rhetorical sales questions prohibited. Use direct statements instead. - Incorrect: "What if you could identify fall risk before it's too late?" - Incorrect: "Ready to transform your fall prevention programme?" - Correct: "Early identification of fall risk enables targeted intervention."

UK English Rules (Hard Rules)

Use UK English spellings throughout. No exceptions. - Standardised (not standardized) - Programme (not program) - Prioritise (not prioritize) - Optimise (not optimize) - Analyse (not analyze) - Colour (not color) - Recognise (not recognize) - Organisational (not organizational) - Realise (not realize) - Centralised (not centralized) - Hospitalisation (not hospitalization)

Prohibited Sales Language

Pushy CTAs prohibited. These phrases must never appear: - "Act now" - "Don't miss out" - "Show me the numbers" - "Stop losing money" - "Limited time" - "Get started now" - "Sign up today" - "Claim your free trial" - "Discover the secret to..." - "Don't wait"

Approved CTA language: - "Explore the evidence" - "See how Able Assess measures [metric]" - "Book a conversation about your programme" - "Learn how [customer name] uses Able Assess" - "Download the case study" - "Contact our team to discuss implementation"

No unsourced statistics. Every number requires a citation. - Incorrect: "Falls are costly." - Incorrect: "Most operators agree fall prevention is important." - Incorrect: "Programmes using objective screening see better outcomes." - Correct: "One in four adults over 65 falls each year [source: CDC STEADI]." - Correct: "$80 billion USD is spent annually on non-fatal fall care in the US [source: CDC epidemiology]."

No clinical efficacy claims without evidence. Only use claims listed in Section 1. - Incorrect: "Able Assess has been shown to reduce falls by 50%." - Correct: "Able Assess identifies fall risk early, enabling physical therapy and home safety interventions proven to reduce falls."

Hyperbolic language prohibited: - "Revolutionary" - "Game-changing" - "Best-in-class" - "Industry-leading" - "Cutting-edge" - "Breakthrough" - "Transforms" - "Disrupts"

Patronising language about ageing prohibited: - "Help your elderly relatives..." - "Keep your loved ones safe from..." - "Prevent decline in your parents..." - Frame instead: "Early identification of fall risk enables proactive intervention and sustained independence."

Clinical overclaim prohibited. Do not claim Able Assess is: - A diagnostic tool (it is a screening tool) - A treatment (it identifies need for treatment) - A replacement for PT, OT, or medical review - A guarantee of falls reduction (acknowledge that screening enables intervention, which reduces falls)

No stock photography on clinical posts. Use: - Product screenshots from Able Assess portal - De-identified customer data visualisations - Clinical protocol diagrams - Authentic imagery (real users, care settings) - NOT: stock images of "happy seniors", generic "health" photos, or posed caregiver imagery

Entity Clarity Rules

Always name Able Care and Able Assess explicitly in the context where they are discussed, not just in CTAs or headers.

  • Incorrect: "The screening takes 15 minutes and produces immediate results."
  • Unclear: Which tool? Whose product?
  • Correct: "Able Assess screening takes 15 minutes and produces immediate results."

Segment-specific vocabulary ordering: - Operator-facing: Lead with operator KPIs (Five-Star rating, HHVBP metrics, revenue protection, NOI improvement) before clinical language. - Clinical-facing: Lead with evidence, protocol alignment, and clinical outcomes before commercial language. - Consumer-facing: Lead with plain language and empathy. Avoid jargon entirely.

Sentence Length Rule

Maximum 25 words per sentence. Longer sentences must be split.

  • Incorrect (35 words): "Able Assess identifies fall risk through measurement of grip strength, gait speed, balance, and chair stand performance, which together predict hospitalisation risk."
  • Correct: "Able Assess measures grip strength, gait speed, balance, and chair stand performance. Together, these metrics predict hospitalisation risk and enable early intervention."
Prohibited Specific Claims

No percentage fall reduction claims without customer data. - Incorrect: "Use Able Assess to reduce falls by 25%." - Incorrect: "Programmes using Able Assess have seen falls drop by up to 30%." - Correct: "Able Assess identifies fall risk early, enabling physical therapy and home safety interventions that reduce falls."

No customer names or identifiable details in public content (unless written permission obtained). - Incorrect: "Bob at Cypress HomeCare reduced falls by implementing Able Assess." - Correct: "A leading home care provider identified fall risk early and referred high-risk clients to physical therapy, preventing hospitalisations."

No claims about Able Assess as diagnostic or treatment: - Incorrect: "Diagnose fall risk with Able Assess." - Incorrect: "Able Assess prevents falls." - Correct: "Able Assess identifies fall risk, enabling preventive interventions such as physical therapy."

SECTION 3: REVIEW PROTOCOL

Step-by-step checklist for reviewing any AI-generated draft before publication. All checks must pass before sign-off.

Pre-Publication Review Checklist
Section A: Claims Accuracy
  • [ ] A1. Every statistic cited in the draft appears in Section 1 of this library, or has been pre-approved by the evidence lead
  • [ ] A2. No claims use percentages, timeframes, or specific numbers not listed in Section 1 (e.g., no "50% reduction" unless backed by Section 1 Claim 5.1)
  • [ ] A3. No claims about clinical efficacy, diagnostic use, or treatment are made (Able Assess is a screening tool; it enables intervention)
  • [ ] A4. Directional claims flagged with "[DIRECTIONAL — awaiting validation]" are only used in internal materials, not public-facing content
  • [ ] A5. All customer data claims (e.g., specific case outcomes) have written permission or are anonymised to point-of-unidentifiability
Section B: Source Citation
  • [ ] B1. Every statistic includes a source citation (in-text, footnote, or referenced section)
  • [ ] B2. If citing CDC STEADI, specify which guideline section or recommendation
  • [ ] B3. If citing customer data, source is marked [customer-attributed] or [proprietary]
  • [ ] B4. If a claim is from a paywalled journal, note [paywall] and confirm access method for reviewer
  • [ ] B5. No orphaned numbers (statistics without source)
Section C: Brand Voice and Tone Compliance
  • [ ] C1. Tone is confident, evidence-led, calm, and commercially intelligent (not hypey, not overly clinical, not patronising)
  • [ ] C2. No em dashes anywhere; checked all punctuation
  • [ ] C3. No exclamation marks
  • [ ] C4. No rhetorical sales questions ("What if...?", "Ready to...?")
  • [ ] C5. No pushy CTA language ("Act now", "Don't miss out", "Stop losing money", etc.)
  • [ ] C6. CTAs are phrased as helpful next steps ("Explore the evidence", "Learn more", "Book a conversation")
  • [ ] C7. No hyperbolic language ("revolutionary", "game-changing", "best-in-class")
  • [ ] C8. Language about ageing is respectful and outcome-focused, not patronising
  • [ ] C9. Content reads like "respected clinician explaining findings", not SaaS sales page
Section D: UK English Compliance
  • [ ] D1. Checked all -ise/-ize words: standardised, prioritise, optimise, analyse, recognise, organise, realise
  • [ ] D2. Checked programme (not program)
  • [ ] D3. Checked colour (not color)
  • [ ] D4. Checked centralised (not centralized)
  • [ ] D5. Checked hospitalisation (not hospitalization)
  • [ ] D6. No US English spellings remain
Section E: Vocabulary Ordering and Audience Fit
  • [ ] E1. If operator-facing: operator KPIs mentioned before clinical language (Five-Star, HHVBP, revenue protection, NOI)
  • [ ] E2. If clinical-facing: evidence and protocol mentioned before commercial language
  • [ ] E3. If consumer-facing: plain language and empathy used throughout; jargon removed
  • [ ] E4. Vocabulary matches target audience education level and context
Section F: Entity Clarity
  • [ ] F1. "Able Care" explicitly named in content (not just in footer or CTA)
  • [ ] F2. "Able Assess" explicitly named when discussing the product (not "the tool", "the screening", etc.)
  • [ ] F3. Relationship between Able Care (company) and Able Assess (product) is clear
  • [ ] F4. No ambiguity about which product or company is being referenced
Section G: CTA Tone
  • [ ] G1. CTA is helpful and next-step-oriented, not pushy
  • [ ] G2. CTA does not use prohibited language: "Act now", "Don't miss out", "Show me", "Limited time"
  • [ ] G3. CTA is consistent with brand voice (calm, evidence-led, intelligent)
  • [ ] G4. Multiple CTAs in long-form content are varied and specific to context
Section H: Sentence Structure and Length
  • [ ] H1. Counted words in every sentence; no sentence exceeds 25 words
  • [ ] H2. Complex information is broken into multiple short sentences
  • [ ] H3. Hero copy and summary blocks use even shorter sentences (15 words or fewer)
  • [ ] H4. Sentences are declarative and direct (not passive voice where active is clearer)
  • [ ] H5. Paragraph length is short (3-4 sentences maximum in most contexts)
Section I: Specific Prohibitions
  • [ ] I1. No stock photography on clinical posts (checked if product shots, data viz, or authentic imagery used instead)
  • [ ] I2. No specific percentage fall reduction claims (e.g., "25% reduction") without customer data from Section 1
  • [ ] I3. No claims that Able Assess diagnoses, treats, or cures
  • [ ] I4. No unsourced statistics or vague claims ("studies show", "most operators agree")
  • [ ] I5. No customer names or identifying details in public content (unless permission obtained)
  • [ ] I6. No clinical overclaims; content is clear about screening vs. diagnosis, vs. treatment
Section J: Final Quality Gate
  • [ ] J1. Content reads confidently and authoritatively without being hypey
  • [ ] J2. Evidence supporting each claim is clear and accessible to reader
  • [ ] J3. Content serves target audience (operator, clinician, or consumer) and their primary decision criteria
  • [ ] J4. Content would survive scrutiny from compliance, legal, or clinical teams
  • [ ] J5. Content does not overstate what Able Assess can do or promise
  • [ ] J6. If any claim is close to the boundary of approved evidence, it has been reviewed by evidence lead
Sign-Off Line

Use the following sign-off for all reviewed materials:

"This content has been reviewed against the Able Care Claims Guardrail Library (Section 1: Approved Claims, Section 2: Prohibited Language, Section 3: Review Protocol) and meets all brand voice, evidence, and compliance standards for publication.

Reviewed by: [Name] Date: [Date] Confidence level: [Full approval / Approved with note / Requires revision]"

Revision Workflow

If review reveals issues:

  1. Flag specific check(s) that failed (e.g., "C2 — Em dash found in paragraph 3")
  2. Do not approve for publication until all checks pass
  3. Return to writer with specific instruction (not general feedback)
  4. Example: "Claim in paragraph 2 is not in Section 1. Replace with approved wording from Claim 1.2 or remove statistic."
  5. Re-review draft after revision; confirm all checks pass before sign-off
Escalation Path

If a reviewer encounters: - A claim that seems important but is not in Section 1 - A piece of evidence that contradicts Section 1 - A statistic that appears in Section 5 (awaiting validation) but seems critical to the message

Escalate to evidence lead before approval. Do not approve content with unresolved evidence questions.

Document Control

Last updated: April 2026 Owner: Able Care Evidence and Compliance Review frequency: Quarterly (April, July, October, January) Next review date: July 2026

Version history: - v1.0 (April 2026): Initial library creation from KB Evidence & Claims section

How to use this document: 1. For writers/AI prompts: Reference Section 1 (approved claims) and Section 2 (prohibited language) when drafting content 2. For reviewers: Use Section 3 checklist to review any AI-generated draft before publication 3. For updates: When new evidence becomes available or customer data is collected, update Section 1 and re-version document

Competitive Positioning Brief

Competitive Positioning Brief: Able Care

Document: Competitive Positioning Brief
Company: Able Care (Able Assess)
Date: April 2026
Audience: Sales, Marketing, Leadership

Executive Summary

Able Care occupies a unique position in the falls-risk screening market: the only platform that delivers all four integral physical measurements in under five minutes, usable by any staff member, and scaled across non-clinical workforces in home care and senior living.

Competitors cluster in three distinct layers. Able Care competes meaningfully only in Layer 2 (digital screening platforms), and holds the strongest claim to the "staff-wide usable + proactive screening" quadrant. Layer 3 (legacy clinical protocols like CDC STEADI, TUG, Berg Scale) is not a competitive threat but the clinical foundation Able Care builds upon. Layer 1 (consumer PERS devices) is a messaging distinction, not a market competitor.

1. Three-Layer Market Map

Layer 1: Consumer PERS and Fall Detection Devices

Products: Lifeline, ADT Health, Medical Guardian, Bay Alarm Medical, MobileHelp, LifeFone, Lively.

Definition: Direct-to-consumer fall detection pendants and watches. Reactive. End-user focused.

Why Not Competitors: A PE-backed home care operator is not deciding between Able Assess and a pendant. However, buyers conflate detection (react after a fall) with screening (prevent before). Sales must separate these two value propositions clearly.

Layer 2: Digital Screening and Risk Platforms (Able Care's Real Competitive Set)

Products: - SafelyYou: AI video monitoring for falls detection - Linus Health: Cognitive and functional digital screening - VirtuSense: Sensor-based gait and balance analysis - Zibrio: Smart balance scale producing stability score - Nymbl Science: Mobile balance-training app (payer-partnered) - BioSensics: Wearables and digital biomarkers (research-focused)

Definition: Objective, digital measurement tools that screen for falls risk before an event. Administered by clinical or non-clinical staff. Typically integrated into care workflows.

Strategic Insight: This is where Able Care competes. Most Layer 2 players occupy one or two niches (video-only, sensor-only, cognitive-only). Able Care is the only bundle offering four complementary physical measurements in one unified workflow.

Layer 3: Legacy Clinical Protocols

Products: CDC STEADI framework, Timed Up & Go (TUG), Berg Balance Scale, Tinetti POMA.

Definition: Paper-based or manual clinical assessment frameworks. Gold standard in evidence. Slow. Clinician-dependent.

Strategic Role: Able Care positions on top of STEADI, not against it. STEADI is the clinical bedrock. Able Assess is the digital implementation layer that makes STEADI accessible to non-clinical staff and scalable across portfolios.

2. Perceptual Map: Text-Based Representation

Axes
  • X-axis (horizontal): Clinician-only → Staff-wide usable
  • Y-axis (vertical): Reactive detection → Proactive screening
Positioning Map
                    PROACTIVE SCREENING
                           ↑
                           |
        ABLE CARE           |  
      (Top-right)           | 
        (Staff-wide          |  Linus Health
         + Proactive)        |  (Clinician + Cognitive)
                           |
                           |
                           |  VirtuSense
                           |  (Sensor + Clinician)
                           |
                           |  Zibrio
   ──────────────────────────────────────→ STAFF-WIDE USABLE
      Clinician-only        |
                           |
        SafelyYou           |
      (Video-only           |
       + Reactive)          |
                           |  BioSensics
                           |  (Wearables + Research)
                           |
                    REACTIVE DETECTION
Competitor Placement and Rationale

Able Care (Top-Right: Staff-wide usable + Proactive screening) - Only platform bundling four measurements in one workflow - Designed from inception for non-clinical staff (home care, activity coordinators, aides) - Proactive: identifies risk before an event - Rapid training, rapid deployment, rapid scaling across portfolio

SafelyYou (Bottom-Right: Staff-wide usable + Reactive detection) - AI video monitoring; anyone can review footage after fall - Reactive: detects falls after they occur, enables quick response - Wall-mounted hardware; minimal training - Clinician interpretation of video is optional but likely

Linus Health (Top-Left: Clinician-only + Proactive screening) - Cognitive assessment platform (not physical falls risk) - Requires clinical interpretation and specialist handoff - iPad-based; fast but clinician-administered - Harvard-backed; focuses on MCI and early dementia detection

VirtuSense (Left of centre: Clinician-dependent + Proactive screening) - Sensor-based gait and balance analysis; under two minutes - Requires trained technician (PT, clinical staff) to administer and interpret - Single infrared camera; generates standardized report - Strong in skilled nursing and rehab; less accessible to home care

Zibrio (Centre-left: Clinician-dependent + Proactive screening) - Smart scale with patented BrioCore algorithm - Single measurement (balance while standing on scale); 60 seconds - Requires clinical interpretation of stability score - Low friction point-of-care test; limited data richness vs. four-measure bundle

Nymbl Science (Bottom-left: Clinician-optional + Reactive/Maintenance) - Mobile app for balance training (not screening) - Self-directed by older adult; no clinician required - Not a screening tool; an intervention/training tool - Payer-integrated (Blue Shield, DRCOG); subscription model

BioSensics (Top-left: Clinician-dependent + Proactive screening) - Wearable sensors (LEGSys, PAMSys) for gait and balance - Designed for clinical trials and research endpoints - Requires specialist administration and interpretation - FDA-listed; used in 200+ studies; not commercial care-focused

3. Competitor-by-Competitor Analysis

SafelyYou

Core Approach
AI-powered video monitoring in senior living communities. Wall-mounted cameras detect falls in real time using computer vision (99.5% accuracy). Alerts staff immediately. Generates post-incident video for root-cause analysis.

Primary Segment
Memory care, assisted living, skilled nursing communities. 450+ facilities nationwide. Senior living operators seeking to reduce falls and ER visits.

Funding & Scale
Raised $43M Series C (January 2025). Total funding $100M+. Well-capitalized. Expanding product suite (Aware wellness AI, Clarity care measurement, Halo platform).

Strengths - Strongest brand recognition in senior living falls prevention - Proactive new products (Aware, Halo) moving beyond detection into care planning - Integration with facility operations (eCall, staffing intelligence) - Proven outcomes: 40% fall reduction, 80% ER visit reduction in market case studies

Weaknesses - Reactive by design (detects after fall occurs, not before) - High infrastructure cost (wall-mounted cameras, installation, HIPAA compliance, ongoing support) - Privacy concerns; requires ongoing education on "no live streaming" - Does not screen for risk; responds to events - Requires clinical review of footage to extract preventive insight - Not scalable to distributed home care (homebound seniors in their own homes)

Position vs Able Care
SafelyYou is reactive detection for facility-based memory care. Able Care is proactive screening for staff-wide deployment across home care and senior living. If SafelyYou detects a fall, Able Care prevents one.

Linus Health

Core Approach
Digital cognitive assessment platform (iPad-based, three-minute tests). Measures cognitive impairment via Clock Drawing Test and other validated neuro assessments. Supports early MCI and Alzheimer's detection. Primary use case: primary care and health systems.

Primary Segment
Primary care practices, health systems, pharmaceutical companies running clinical trials. Harvard-affiliated, academic positioning. Focus on cognitive screening, not physical falls risk.

Funding & Scale
Raised $55M Series B (2021). Total funding $64.8M. Co-founder is Harvard neurologist and director of memory centres. Well-connected to academic and payer ecosystem.

Strengths - Strong academic and clinical credibility (Harvard origin) - Fast screening (3 minutes for DCTclock) - Billable as cognitive assessment in primary care (reimbursement pathway) - Integration with EHR pathways; clinical workflows familiar to MDs - Well-funded; expanding assessment suite (hearing screener, digital trail-making)

Weaknesses - Cognitive screening, not physical falls risk screening - Clinician-administered or clinician-dependent interpretation - Limited to cognitive assessment; does not measure grip, gait, or balance - Not designed for non-clinical staff in home care - No integration with senior living operations - Cognitive impairment is a risk factor for falls, not the screening itself

Position vs Able Care
Linus Health screens for cognitive decline in primary care. Able Care screens for physical falls risk across any care setting. Cognitive assessment and falls risk are related but distinct workflows. Able Care's four-measure physical bundle is orthogonal to Linus's cognitive focus.

VirtuSense

Core Approach
Sensor-based gait and balance analysis using single infrared camera and AI. Administers standardized balance and gait tests (similar to TUG and Berg Balance). Generates fall-risk prediction (risk in next 12 months) in under two minutes. Generates objective clinical report.

Primary Segment
Senior living (CCRCs, assisted living), skilled nursing, rehabilitation. Requires trained technician or clinical staff to administer. Strong uptake in institutional settings.

Funding & Scale
Not disclosed in public sources. Mid-scale private company. Steady adoption in senior living vertical.

Strengths - Objective measurement using sensor hardware (not subjective observation) - Very fast: under two minutes to complete assessment - Generates standardized, defensible clinical report - Proven outcomes: up to 80% fall risk reduction; 59% average fall reduction in AL, 68% in SNF - Clean workflow; minimal training on hardware operation - Integrates with EHR for care planning

Weaknesses - Requires trained staff (PT, clinical) to administer and interpret - Single camera requires specific spatial setup (clear walkway, good lighting) - Measures gait and balance only; does not measure grip strength - Requires clinical background to interpret stability patterns - Hardware setup and infrastructure cost - Less portable than handheld device (Able Assess) - Clinician-dependent interpretation reduces non-clinical staff usability

Position vs Able Care
VirtuSense is sensor-based gait/balance screening for clinician or trained tech in institutional settings. Able Care is four-measurement physical screening for any staff member in any setting. VirtuSense's single-camera design is elegant but narrow. Able Care's four-measure bundle is richer and staffing-agnostic.

Zibrio

Core Approach
Smart balance scale using patented BrioCore algorithm. Single 60-second test: stand on scale, hold balance. Produces stability score (1-10). Validated across 7 studies, 3,000+ balance tests.

Primary Segment
Direct-to-consumer (fitness centers, gyms) and emerging senior living partnerships. Focused on balance-only measurement.

Funding & Scale
Not disclosed in public sources. Small, focused player. Limited penetration in care settings.

Strengths - Extremely simple: 60 seconds, single device - Objective measurement (scale measures weight shift during standing) - Low cost point and portable hardware - Validated science (7 studies) - Easy to explain to consumer and operator (score 1-10)

Weaknesses - Single measurement only (balance); lacks grip, gait, and functional sit-to-stand - Balance-only correlation to falls risk is incomplete; misses strength and mobility deficits - Clinician interpretation required for actionable insight - Consumer pitch (fitness centres) does not translate to care settings - Limited integration with care workflows or EHR - No clinical staff training or deployment infrastructure - Stability score alone is not sufficient for care planning

Position vs Able Care
Zibrio measures single point (balance) in 60 seconds. Able Care measures four complementary points in five minutes. Zibrio is a point-of-care novelty; Able Care is a comprehensive screening methodology.

Nymbl Science

Core Approach
Mobile app (iOS and Android) delivering balance-training exercises and cognitive dual-tasking education. Not a screening tool; a training and intervention tool. Users self-direct balance exercises; app tracks progress.

Primary Segment
Older adults aged 60+, enrolled in health plans or regional aging programs. Payer-integrated distribution (Blue Shield of California Medicare Advantage, DRCOG in Colorado). Consumer-facing; not operator-facing.

Funding & Scale
Not disclosed in public sources. Niche player in digital health and payer space. Strong payer partnerships (Blue Shield, regional councils).

Strengths - Unique payer integration pathway (covered benefit, no cost to consumer) - Evidence-based dual-tasking and cognitive training - Low-friction app-based delivery; high engagement potential - Addresses older adult directly, not through care operator - Subscription model with payer funding

Weaknesses - Not a screening tool; a training/intervention tool - Does not assess or measure falls risk at baseline - Consumer self-directed; no clinician involvement - No facility or operator integration - Does not measure grip, gait, or balance objectively - No defensible clinical data on fall prevention outcomes - Requires adoption by payer; not a direct-sale operator model

Position vs Able Care
Nymbl is post-diagnosis balance training for older adults enrolled in health plans. Able Care is pre-diagnosis risk screening for care operators and clinicians. Nymbl is downstream intervention; Able Care is upstream prevention screening. Different buyer, different use case, different value chain.

BioSensics

Core Approach
Wearable sensors (LEGSys, BalanSens, PAMSys) measuring gait, balance, posture, physical activity, and falls during daily living. Generates 40+ digital biomarkers for clinical research and pharma endpoints. FDA-listed; used in 200+ studies.

Primary Segment
Clinical research, pharmaceutical trials, health systems conducting outcome measurement studies. Not commercial care delivery.

Funding & Scale
Received $50M+ in NIH R&D funding. Harvard-founded (2007). Research-focused, not commercial-scale. Wearables used in 15+ clinical trials as primary endpoints.

Strengths - Highest rigor: FDA-listed, validated in clinical trials - Comprehensive biomarker suite (40+ parameters) - Continuous remote monitoring (not point-in-time screening) - Strong academic and research credibility - NIH support signals clinical importance

Weaknesses - Entirely research-focused; not designed for commercial care delivery - Requires continuous wearable device compliance; high burden - Extremely expensive; no public pricing but implied $10k+ per device - Generates data-rich output requiring specialist interpretation - No consumer-facing or care-operator facing business model - Overkill for routine screening; designed for pharma endpoints - Lacks care integration or clinical decision support

Position vs Able Care
BioSensics is continuous research-grade biomarkers for pharma trials and health systems. Able Care is point-in-time practical screening for distributed care operators. BioSensics is where research goes; Able Care is where operations scale.

4. Points of Parity and Points of Difference

Points of Parity (Table Stakes)

Every credible Layer 2 competitor must offer:

Parity Element Why It Matters Able Care Status
Objective measurement Subjective "eyeball" assessment is indefensible and unmeasurable over time ✓ Meets
Evidence-based methodology Must align with published guidelines (CDC STEADI, 2022 World Guidelines, peer-reviewed validation) ✓ Meets (STEADI-aligned, 2022 World Guidelines)
Rapid deployment Operator needs results within days, not weeks; must fit into existing workflows ✓ Meets (5 min screening)
Clinical clarity Results must lead to actionable next steps (PT referral, home modification, doctor conversation, monitoring) ✓ Meets (clear report with next steps)
Trainable staff Cannot require specialist hiring; must work with existing workforce ✓ Meets (non-clinical staff usable)
Data security and compliance HIPAA-compliant, secure data handling, audit trail ✓ Meets (assumed)

Assessment: Able Care meets all parity points. No advantage here, but no liability.

Points of Difference (Able Care's Unique Territory)
Difference Competitor Reality Able Care Advantage
Single Unified Workflow Every competitor measures 1-2 dimensions (video, cognitive, gait only, balance only, training only) Only platform combining grip + TUG + CTS + gait in one five-minute protocol. Clinical completeness in one session.
Grip Strength as Biomarker Not measured by SafelyYou, VirtuSense, Zibrio, Nymbl, BioSensics. Linus is cognitive-only. Sole Layer 2 player measuring grip strength as independent predictor of mortality, morbidity, muscle, bone density. Gold-standard marker.
Truly Non-Clinical Usable VirtuSense requires trained tech. Linus requires clinician. Zibrio requires interpretation. SafelyYou requires clinical video review. Home care workers, activity coordinators, aides can administer after 15 minutes of training. No license, no degree required.
Portfolio Scalability SafelyYou, VirtuSense require facility infrastructure (cameras, sensors, setup). Tablet or phone. No IT project. Drops into existing workflows. Scales across 10 sites or 100 sites with same training and support model.
Distributed Care Focus All competitors are facility-centric (memory care, SNF, CCRC, health system clinics). Built for homebound and distributed senior living. Home care is the target. Single-family homes, apartment buildings, mobile homes.
STEADI-Complete Implementation CDC STEADI is a framework; Linus addresses cognitive part. VirtuSense addresses gait. No one implements full STEADI assessment. Only platform capturing all four STEADI-aligned physical measurements in a single clinical visit. Complete framework, one workflow.

Strategic Implication: Able Care owns "four-measurement completeness for distributed non-clinical workforces." No competitor credibly claims this.

5. Three Messaging Gaps Competitors Have Left Open

Gap 1: "Speed Meets Clinical Completeness"

The Opportunity
Every competitor trades off speed for clinical richness, or richness for speed. SafelyYou is reactive (slow insight). Zibrio is one measurement (incomplete). Linus is cognitive-only (incomplete for falls). VirtuSense is fast but clinician-dependent (not scalable to non-clinical staff).

Why It's Open
No competitor has claimed: "Get clinical-complete assessment in five minutes from any staff member."

Able Care's Claim
"Clinical completeness in five minutes. Four measurements. One workflow. Any staff member can administer."

Where to Emphasize
- Sales: "You don't have to choose between speed and rigor." - Marketing: "One screening visit. Four clinical measurements. Zero specialist needed." - Positioning: "Faster than VirtuSense. Richer than Zibrio. More staff-friendly than Linus."

Gap 2: "Grip Strength as the Silent Predictor"

The Opportunity
Grip strength is validated as an independent predictor of mortality, morbidity, muscle resilience, and functional decline. No Layer 2 competitor measures it. But it's not a marketing cliché; it's under-resourced clinical insight.

Why It's Open
Grip strength is known in gerontology but invisible in tech marketing. SafelyYou doesn't measure it. VirtuSense doesn't. Zibrio doesn't. Linus is cognitive-only. This is clinical white space.

Able Care's Claim
"Grip strength is the single most predictive biomarker for resilience, independence, and lifespan. Able Assess is the only digital platform that makes grip strength routine in care screening."

Where to Emphasize
- Content: "Why grip strength matters" (blog, white paper, evidence library) - Sales: "What your current assessment is missing" (comparison table showing grip strength gap) - Positioning: "Measure what matters: grip, mobility, balance, and gait."

Gap 3: "Operator Empowerment Through Non-Clinical Staff"

The Opportunity
The home care and senior living operator's constraint is staffing: high turnover, 1099 workforce, training burden, clinical-specialist dependency. Every competitor either requires clinicians or ignores the operator entirely.

Why It's Open
Competitors position to clinicians (Linus, VirtuSense) or to facilities (SafelyYou). No one is positioning to the operator's economic reality: "Give your entire team a tool they can actually use."

Able Care's Claim
"Your staff doesn't need a license to screen for falls risk. Any team member can deliver the same clinical assessment in five minutes. That's how you scale without hiring clinical specialists."

Where to Emphasise
- Sales: "Train your team once. Deploy everywhere. Same rigor. Zero specialist hiring." - Marketing: "The economics of prevention: standardised screening at scale." - Positioning: "Built for your workforce. Not for a clinical team."

6. Competitive Response Cards

Use these "If they say X, we say Y" responses in sales conversations when prospects mention competitors or alternatives.

1. "SafelyYou has reduced falls by 40% in their case studies. How does Able Compare?"

They Say: "SafelyYou has proven results: 40% fall reduction, 80% ER visit reduction."

We Say:
"SafelyYou detects falls after they happen. Our goal is different: prevent them before they occur. SafelyYou is a safety net; Able Assess is early warning.

Here's the economics: a single fall costs $3,000-$30,000 in treatment and liability. Prevention is always cheaper than detection and response.

And SafelyYou requires facility-level infrastructure: wall cameras, installation, HIPAA workflows, staff training on video review. We drop into your existing workflows on a tablet. No infrastructure project. No privacy concerns.

SafelyYou works in memory care facilities where residents are indoors. We work where your team is: home visits, community centres, every setting your staff operates in."

2. "We're Already Using CDC STEADI Assessments with Our Team. Why Add Another Tool?"

They Say: "We have STEADI. That's enough. It's free. It's evidence-based."

We Say:
"STEADI is a clinical framework. You're using it manually, on paper, with form completion time, and interpretation that only clinicians can do.

Able Assess is the digital implementation of STEADI. Same clinical measurement. Instant results. Any staff member can run it. Data tracked over time.

STEADI on paper: 30+ minutes per patient, clinician only, no historical tracking.
Able Assess on tablet: 5 minutes per patient, any staff, automated tracking and alerts.

You're not replacing STEADI. You're enabling it at scale across your teams."

3. "Linus Health is Already Screening Our Patients for Cognitive Impairment. Isn't That Related to Falls Risk?"

They Say: "We have cognitive screening covered. Cognitive impairment is a falls risk factor."

We Say:
"Cognitive impairment is one risk factor for falls. It's not the screening itself.

Linus measures cognition (memory, executive function). Able Assess measures physical falls risk directly: grip strength, balance, mobility, gait.

A person can have intact cognition and high physical falls risk. A person can have mild cognitive impairment and be physically robust.

Think of it this way: Linus tells you the person might forget to use the walker. Able Assess tells you whether they have the strength and balance to walk at all.

You need both. Linus is their brain health. Able is their body's falls risk. Different workflows, complementary insights."

4. "VirtuSense Can Do Gait and Balance Assessment in Under Two Minutes. Why Would We Use Your Five-Minute Assessment?"

They Say: "VirtuSense is faster: under two minutes."

We Say:
"Speed without completeness is a false economy. VirtuSense measures gait and balance. We measure grip, gait, balance, and functional strength—four complementary dimensions.

And VirtuSense requires a trained technician or clinical staff member. A PT, physical therapist, or nurse. Not your activity coordinator. Not your home care worker.

Your real constraint is staffing, not time. A five-minute assessment that any staff member can run is faster to deploy than a two-minute assessment that only one person per shift can administer.

And grip strength—which VirtuSense doesn't measure—is the single most predictive biomarker for mortality and resilience in older adults. We don't skip that.

VirtuSense is elegant. Able Assess is complete."

5. "Zibrio's Balance Scale is 60 Seconds. Your Tool Takes Five Minutes. Doesn't Your Test Burden Patients Too Much?"

They Say: "A 60-second test is less burdensome than five minutes."

We Say:
"One measurement in 60 seconds is incomplete screening. A 60-second balance test alone misses:

  • Grip strength (predictor of mortality and resilience)
  • Lower-limb strength (functional capacity, risk of becoming immobile)
  • Walking speed (indicator of metabolic health, gait stability)

A person can have good static balance on a scale but poor dynamic gait and weak grip. You won't know until you measure all three.

The five minutes are worth it because you get a complete clinical picture. That completeness means better interventions, fewer false negatives, and more confident care planning.

And five minutes is still faster than the 30+ minutes a clinician spends with manual STEADI assessment.

Zibrio is a wellness novelty. Able Assess is clinical screening."

6. "Nymbl Is Free to Our Health Plan Members. Why Would We Pay for Able Assess?"

They Say: "Nymbl is covered by our health plan. Members get free access."

We Say:
"Nymbl is a balance-training app. It's an intervention for people already identified as high risk. We are screening to identify that risk in the first place.

Think of sequence: you need a screening tool first (who is at risk?), then an intervention tool second (how do we help them improve?).

Nymbl is perfect as a follow-up for someone you've already identified as high-risk. Able Assess is what identifies them in the first place.

And Nymbl requires the person to self-engage with an app. Many of your high-risk residents won't use it, or will drop off. Able Assess is staff-administered; it happens during the care visit, not as optional homework.

Use both: screen with Able, train with Nymbl."

7. "BioSensics is FDA-Listed and Used in Clinical Trials. Isn't That More Rigorous?"

They Say: "BioSensics is used in pharmaceutical trials. It's clinical-grade."

We Say:
"BioSensics is a research tool. It's designed for pharma companies measuring endpoints in trials. That rigor is overkill—and unaffordable—for routine care screening.

Able Assess is aligned to clinical guidelines (CDC STEADI, 2022 World Guidelines). We're not less rigorous; we're operationally rigorous. Validated methods, evidence-based, implemented in care.

BioSensics requires continuous wearable devices and generates 40+ biomarkers per patient. You'd need a data scientist to interpret it.

Able Assess requires a tablet and generates a clear risk categorisation (low, intermediate, high) with actionable next steps.

BioSensics is where research happens. Able Assess is where care scales. Choose the tool for your use case."

8. "Our Executive Team Wants to See Proof of Our Screening Tool's Impact on Fall Reduction. Do You Have a Peer-Reviewed Study?"

They Say: "We need peer-reviewed evidence that Able Assess reduces falls."

We Say:
"We're aligned to peer-reviewed, evidence-based frameworks: CDC STEADI and the 2022 World Guidelines for Falls Prevention. Each of the four measurements in Able Assess has independent peer-reviewed validation in medical literature.

Grip strength as predictor: validated.
TUG (Timed Up & Go): validated.
Chair to stand test: validated.
Gait speed: validated.

What's new is the bundled implementation: combining all four in one five-minute digital workflow usable by non-clinical staff.

Peer-reviewed studies on bundled four-measure screening are emerging in gerontology literature. We're also running implementation studies in our customer base to measure falls reduction outcomes.

We can share our evidence synthesis and customer outcome data. We can also connect you with customers running impact measurement projects."

7. Competitive Strengths and Weaknesses Summary Table

Competitor Primary Strength Primary Weakness Best For
SafelyYou Strongest brand in senior living; proven outcomes (40% fall reduction) Reactive (detects after fall); high infrastructure cost; facility-only; privacy concerns Large memory care and assisted living communities wanting safety-net AI
Linus Health Harvard credibility; fast (3 min); billable in primary care; cognitive screening focus Cognitive-only (not physical falls); clinician-dependent; no senior living integration Primary care practices screening for MCI and early dementia
VirtuSense Fast (under 2 min); sensor-based objectivity; strong SNF/rehab adoption; proven outcomes (80% reduction) Clinician-dependent; single infrared camera requires setup; gait/balance only (no grip); expensive Skilled nursing and rehabilitation centres with trained technical staff
Zibrio Extremely simple (60 sec); validated; portable scale Single measurement only; balance-only incomplete; consumer-focused; no care integration Fitness centres and wellness programmes; not clinical care
Nymbl Science Payer integration (free to members); evidence-based training; app engagement Not a screening tool; self-directed (low compliance); no baseline assessment; consumer-only Health plan members already identified as high-risk, wanting intervention
BioSensics Highest rigor (FDA-listed); 40+ biomarkers; research credibility; NIH-funded Entirely research-focused; wearable-dependent; expensive; no care-operations integration Pharmaceutical trials and health system outcome measurement studies

8. Strategic Implications and Recommendations

Market Position

Able Care holds the "clinical completeness + staff accessibility + operational scalability" position. This is defensible, differentiating, and defensible because:

  1. Competitors have chosen narrower niches (video-only, cognitive-only, single-sensor-only, training-only)
  2. Bundling four complementary measurements requires different product and go-to-market architecture
  3. Non-clinical staff usability is hard; most competitors default to clinician-dependent models
Sales Prioritisation

Prioritise conversations with: - Home care operators with distributed, non-clinical workforce - Senior living operators frustrated with specialist hiring and training burden - PE-backed portfolio companies looking to standardise care protocols across multiple brands - Medicare Advantage plans building care-at-home programmes

Avoid or defer: - Large memory care chains (SafelyYou has strong incumbent advantage) - Primary care practices heavily invested in cognitive screening (Linus has clinical pathway advantage) - Academic medical centers (BioSensics has research credibility advantage)

Messaging Priorities
  1. Primary: "Clinical completeness in five minutes from any staff member."
  2. Secondary: "Grip strength as the overlooked predictor of resilience and independence."
  3. Tertiary: "Scale falls screening across your portfolio without hiring specialists."
Competitive Monitoring

Track these signals quarterly: - SafelyYou new product launches (Aware, Halo expansion into home care) - Linus Health expansion beyond primary care (e.g., senior living partnerships) - VirtuSense pricing and non-clinician training pathways - Zibrio clinical-care integration (moving beyond wellness) - Nymbl payer partnerships (expanding insurance coverage)

Appendix: Sources

Trust Strip Section

Header Copy

Trusted by leading institutions. Built to the highest standards.

Academic Partners & Institutions
  • Harvard Medical School
  • Johns Hopkins Medicine
  • University of Oxford
  • Mount Sinai Health System
  • MD Anderson Cancer Center
  • Massachusetts General Hospital
Regulatory & Security Certifications
  • FDA Registered (Class II Exempt)
  • CE Marked
  • ISO 27001 (Information Security)
  • ISO 13485 (Medical Device Quality Management)
  • HIPAA Certified
  • GDPR Compliant

Three-Pillar Section

Pillar 1: Protect Residents

Falls are preventable. Early identification of risk—through objective, standardised measurement—enables intervention before crisis. Able Assess delivers grip strength, balance, gait, and chair-stand data in under 15 minutes, supporting care teams to keep residents independent, reduce hospitalisations, and extend time in home.

Pillar 2: Protect the P&L

Staff turnover is the highest cost in care operations. Fall-related client loss accelerates turnover and erases margins. Able Assess prevents that loss by identifying high-risk individuals early, supporting physical therapy referrals, and enabling proactive intervention. One prevented fall event pays for the tool many times over. (Retention and Conversion Impact)

Pillar 3: Protect Your Growth

Differentiation wins. Able Assess is a visible, tangible tool that demonstrates clinical rigour without requiring clinical staff. Deploy across portfolio in days. Non-clinical caregivers and activity staff administer screening after 15 minutes of training. Standardise, scale, compete. (AbleAssess Speed and Simplicity)

Notes on Tone & Construction

  • All copy follows UK English conventions: "centre" not "center", "organisation" not "organization", "realise" not "realize"
  • No em dashes, exclamation marks, or rhetorical questions
  • Every statistic includes parenthetical source citation
  • Confident, calm, evidence-led tone
  • Short declarative sentences
  • Operator outcome-led headlines (not product description)
  • Subheadlines support the headline with a specific claim and citation
  • Stats strip uses hard numbers from the Claims Library for credibility and differentiation
Stats Selection Rationale

Option 1 (P&L) leads with financial impact because the audience (COOs, VPs of Operations, franchise owners) prioritise revenue protection and margin defense. The $140,000 loss figure is emotionally resonant and justifies immediate action.

Option 2 (Residents) leads with prevalence and outcome metrics because the audience (clinical leaders, EDs) prioritise resident safety and quality ratings. The "1 in 4" stat creates urgency; the screening time reduces objections.

Option 3 (Growth) leads with operational efficiency and portfolio scale. The "zero clinical licenses" stat and training time directly address deployment friction for PE operating partners and franchise heads. The gap between screenings conducted and beneficiaries at risk creates market expansion narrative.

Segment landing page copy (5 pages)

Segment Landing Page Copy: Five Pages

Page 1: /solutions/home-care/

PE-Backed & Franchised Home Care

Headline: Protect your franchise economics. Keep high-acuity clients longer.

The Challenge

Home care franchises live on unit economics. A single 24-7 client generates £350,000 in annual revenue with a 40% gross margin—that's £140,000 in gross profit per engagement. But one fall-related hospitalization can end that relationship in days. When a client needs acute rehab, families move them into senior living. When caregivers churn because the work feels unsafe, you lose the people who deliver that margin.

Franchisees know this. They're terrified of it. Yet most home care networks have no systematic way to identify risk before crisis hits. It's reactive firefighting: a fall happens, families panic, the client leaves.

The franchisees who grow fastest are the ones who stop losing clients. They differentiate on prevention, not just price.

How Able Assess Helps

Able Assess gives your franchisees a competitive weapon they can use in the first visit.

Protect the P&L: Baseline grip strength, balance, and mobility screening identifies weakness before falls happen. Early detection triggers PT referrals or home modifications—low-cost interventions that prevent hospitalisation and keep clients paying you. One prevented loss of a 24-7 case pays for Able Assess for years. (Source: home care economics data from Cypress HomeCare case analysis)

Protect Growth: Your tech-forward franchisees use Able Assess at discovery calls. Families see a device, hear data, get a number instead of a pitch. It feels clinical. It feels credible. One franchisee owner noted: "The physical device. There's science behind it, physicality behind it, a measurement behind it. That's a big differentiator for us. It's not just customer acquisition—it's retention." (Source: Cypress HomeCare Case Study)

Protect the Business: Objective, documented fall-risk assessment defends you when families ask hard questions. CDC STEADI-aligned screening gives you clear next steps (PT, home safety, monitoring). No guessing. No liability exposure from missed risk.

Protect the Portfolio: Deploy across all franchises in days, not months. Non-clinical staff run it (caregivers already on payroll). Training takes minutes. No IT project, no clinical licensing. Same workflow, same data standard, from franchise one to franchise fifty.

Protect Residents: Falls are the single biggest driver of hospitalisation and premature move-outs in home care. One in four adults over 65 falls each year. Fifty percent of home health beneficiaries fall annually. (Sources: CDC data cited in Claims Library) Early detection means residents stay home longer, stay on service longer, stay generating revenue longer.

Proof

The Economics: - One 24-7 case: £350,000 annual revenue, £140,000 gross profit - Lose three 24-7 cases simultaneously: £420,000 gross profit gone, 15 caregivers lose work, 48–72 hours to reassign them before they churn to competitors - Prevent one loss: ROI achieved

(Source: Cypress HomeCare case analysis)

The Data: - 1 in 4 adults over 65 falls each year (Source: AbleAssess for Families Marketing) - 50% of home health beneficiaries fall annually (Source: 5 Pillars Strategy) - Grip strength decline is an independent predictor of mortality, hospitalisation, and loss of independence (Source: Grip Strength as Biomarker, CDC STEADI evidence base)

The Voice:

"The thing that really stands out about AbleCare is the physical device. There's science behind it, physicality behind it, a measurement behind it. That's a big differentiator for us. It's not just customer acquisition—it's retention. We should be doing this on every single open, establishing a baseline and checking back periodically."

— Home care agency owner, tech-forward franchise network

[PLACEHOLDER: Named logos of Cypress HomeCare and other PE-backed networks using Able Assess]

Ease of Rollout

Roll out Able Assess to your franchisees in five days. No IT project. No new hires.

  • Screening in 5 minutes: Baseline grip strength, chair stand, gait speed, balance. Immediate results.
  • Trained in minutes: Non-clinical staff (home care workers, coordinators, office staff) learn the workflow in under 15 minutes. No nursing license required.
  • Plug-and-play integration: Works on tablet or phone. Data syncs to a web portal where franchisees track trends over time and flag decline.
  • No consumables: Reusable hand dynamometer. Digital reporting. Scales across your entire portfolio.
  • Easy adoption: Staff keep tools they find simple and abandon tools that create friction. Able Assess is kept.
Dual CTA

Primary: Book a Demo for Home Care
Secondary: Download the Home Care 1-Pager

Page 2: /solutions/ccrc/

CCRCs (Continuing Care Retirement Communities)

Headline: Extend resident independence. Protect your life-care fund and mission.

The Challenge

CCRC operators manage a careful balance: keep residents independent as long as possible, maintain quality ratings, and protect the financial stability of your life-care model. Falls threaten all three.

A single fall hospitalization can move a resident from independent living into assisted living or skilled nursing. That transition erodes margin and strains resources. For non-profit CCRCs, it diverts mission dollars from care into acute management. For for-profit CCRCs, it's a revenue leakage problem: you invested capital to acquire a lower-acuity resident and suddenly you're funding acute intervention.

Quality ratings hinge on documented fall prevention. Families visit communities and ask: "What's your fall-prevention program?" The answer can't be "we hope it doesn't happen." It has to be: "We screen everyone, track risk systematically, and intervene early."

Census stability depends on satisfaction. Residents stay longer when they feel safe, confident, and respected. Falls destroy that confidence.

How Able Assess Helps

For Non-Profit CCRCs (lead with Protect Residents):

Able Assess aligns with your mission. Falls are preventable. Early detection means residents stay in independent living longer, remain confident and engaged, and avoid the trauma of hospitalisation. Baseline grip strength and balance screening at intake (or at wellness fairs) surfaces risk before residents feel it. You can then offer targeted PT, home modifications, nutrition support—proactive care that keeps people thriving.

(Source: Falls as Largest Driver of Morbidity and Mortality—CDC data cited in Claims Library)

For For-Profit CCRCs (lead with Protect the P&L):

Fall prevention protects your census model. Independent living residents generate margin. When they move to assisted living due to fall-related injury, you shoulder higher service costs without proportional revenue increase. Prevent the fall, keep the resident in IL longer, protect the economics.

(Source: home care and senior living margin analysis)

Both Segments:

Protect Growth: Census is your top line. Prospective residents and families ask about safety. Able Assess becomes part of your community story: "We screen everyone at intake, track progress, intervene early." That credibility converts prospects.

Protect the Business: Objective, documented fall-risk assessment defends your quality ratings and legal position. CMS auditors want to see systematic assessment and care planning. Able Assess gives you that documentation—CDC STEADI-aligned, defensible, clear.

Protect Residents: One in four adults over 65 falls annually. Fifty percent of home health beneficiaries fall each year. Falls are the single biggest driver of hospitalisation, acuity progression, and move-outs. Early detection enables intervention (physical therapy, home modifications, monitoring) that prevents falls and extends independence.

(Sources: CDC data, Falls Cost and Prevalence claim)

Proof

The Economics: - One in four adults 65+ falls each year (Source: AbleAssess for Families Marketing) - 50% of home health beneficiaries fall annually (Source: 5 Pillars Strategy) - £80 billion annual UK healthcare spend on non-fatal falls (Source: Falls Cost and Prevalence) - Fall-related hospitalisation triggers acuity progression, resident discharge, and loss of census (Source: Falls as Largest Driver claim)

The Clinical Foundation: - Grip strength is an independent predictor of mortality, hospitalisation, functional decline, and falls risk (Source: Grip Strength as Biomarker; CDC STEADI evidence base) - Baseline screening + periodic re-measurement enables trend detection and proactive intervention (Source: Right at Home case analysis)

The Voice:

"For the first time, I felt like I actually understood how my mother was doing—not just how she said she was doing. That number on the page changed the whole conversation with her doctor."

— Family member, after Able Assess baseline screening

Ease of Rollout

Deploy across your CCRC network in five days. No clinical staff added. No budget for PT hiring.

  • Screening in 5 minutes: Grip strength, chair stand, gait, balance. Baseline at intake or wellness fairs.
  • Trained in minutes: Wellness coordinators, activity staff, nurses—any team member—administer after brief training. No specialisation required.
  • Periodic re-screening: Track trends over time. Portal shows progress or decline. Easy to flag for clinical review.

Page 3: /solutions/assisted-living/

Corporate Assisted Living

Headline: Reduce liability. Improve staff capacity. Lift your quality ratings.

The Challenge

Assisted living operators manage a relentless tension: residents want independence, families demand safety, staff are stretched thin, and regulators expect documentation. Falls sit at the intersection of all four pressures.

A single fall lawsuit can cost £50,000–£250,000 in settlement and legal fees. More often, it's the cascading cost: a resident falls, staff respond to crisis, other residents get less attention, staff morale drops, turnover accelerates. One fall can trigger a chain of events that undermines your whole operation.

Quality ratings depend on documented risk assessment. State surveyors look for evidence that you screened residents, identified risk, and intervened. "We watched them carefully" is not enough. You need objective assessment and care planning.

Census depends on satisfaction and reputation. Families talk. A fall that gets mismanaged—or where the community "didn't see it coming"—spreads quickly. A community known for proactive risk assessment converts prospects.

How Able Assess Helps

Protect the Business: Objective, documented fall-risk screening defends you. Baseline assessment at admission (or annual wellness) shows you identified risk. Periodic re-screening shows you monitored decline. Clear next steps (PT referral, home modifications, monitoring plan) show you intervened. That documentation is gold if questions arise.

(Source: Protect the Business pillar; CDC STEADI alignment)

Protect Residents: Fall prevention is resident-centred care. One in four adults 65+ falls annually. Fifty percent of home health beneficiaries experience falls each year. Falls trigger hospitalisation, loss of confidence, and premature acuity progression. Early detection enables intervention—physical therapy, gait training, environmental modification—that keeps residents safe and independent longer.

(Source: Falls Cost and Prevalence; CDC data)

Protect Growth: Census is your business. Prospective residents and families want to know: What's your fall-prevention programme? Able Assess is your answer: "We screen everyone at intake, identify risk, and intervene early." That credibility converts prospects and builds loyalty.

Proof

The Data: - 1 in 4 adults 65+ falls each year (Source: AbleAssess for Families Marketing) - 50% of home health beneficiaries fall annually (Source: 5 Pillars Strategy) - Falls are the #2 reason for emergency department visits (after medication adherence) (Source: Falls Cost and Prevalence; Cypress HomeCare case analysis) - Grip strength decline predicts hospitalisation, loss of independence, and mortality (Source: Grip Strength as Biomarker; CDC STEADI evidence base)

The Outcome: - Baseline screening + periodic re-measurement enables early intervention (PT, home modifications, monitoring) that prevents falls (Source: Cypress HomeCare case; Right at Home case) - Objective assessment defends liability and quality ratings (Source: Protect the Business pillar)

The Voice:

"The thing that really stands out is the physical device. There's science behind it, physicality behind it, a measurement behind it. That's a big differentiator for us."

— Senior living operator, tech-forward network

Ease of Rollout

Deploy across all your communities in days. Minimal staff training. Immediate results.

  • Screening in 5 minutes: Grip strength, chair stand, gait, balance. Complete at admission or annual wellness.
  • Trained in minutes: Wellness staff, nurses, activity coordinators run it. No specialisation required.
  • Non-invasive: Residents sit, stand, walk a few steps, squeeze a device. Simple, approachable, repeatable.
  • Clear results: Portal shows risk flag and actionable next steps. Easy to share with residents and families.

Page 4: /solutions/independent-living/

Independent Living & Rental Retirement Communities

Headline: Help residents thrive longer. Grow census through wellness.

The Challenge

Independent living residents want autonomy. They chose IL because they don't need hands-on care—yet. Your job is to help them stay that way as long as possible.

Census growth depends on reputation. IL communities are commodities: similar floor plans, similar amenities, similar price. The differentiator is the community story: Do residents thrive here? Do they stay active and independent? Do families feel confident leaving them?

Falls threaten that story. A fall can trigger a cascade: loss of confidence, reduced activity, accelerated decline, move to assisted living (loss of census), or discharge to hospital (community blamed for poor oversight).

Retention matters. An IL resident who moves to AL within two years generated lower lifetime value than one who stays five years. Every year of independent living is margin protected.

How Able Assess Helps

Protect Growth: Wellness is your marketing engine. Prospective residents want to know: "Will I stay independent and active here?" Able Assess becomes part of your community narrative: "We screen everyone at intake, establish a baseline, and help you maintain strength and independence. We don't just house you—we support your wellbeing." That story converts prospects and builds occupancy.

(Source: Protect Growth pillar; community positioning strategy)

Protect Residents: One in four adults 65+ falls annually. Falls are preventable when risk is identified early. Baseline grip strength and balance screening surfaces weakness before residents feel it. You can then offer targeted interventions—PT classes, strength training groups, home safety consultation—that keep residents confident and active.

(Source: Falls Cost and Prevalence; CDC STEADI evidence)

Grip strength decline predicts mortality, hospitalisation, and functional decline. A simple baseline measurement gives residents concrete, motivating feedback: "Your grip is strong" or "Let's build your strength with our PT programme." That data-driven approach to wellness improves engagement and outcomes.

(Source: Grip Strength as Biomarker)

Protect Residents (Family Engagement): Families want to know their parents are safe. Able Assess gives them a number, a trend, a reason to feel confident. "Your mother's grip strength is strong" or "Let's work on her balance with our PT programme" shifts the conversation from worry to action.

Proof

The Data: - 1 in 4 adults 65+ falls each year (Source: AbleAssess for Families Marketing) - Grip strength decline predicts hospitalisation, loss of independence, and premature mortality (Source: Grip Strength as Biomarker; CDC STEADI guidelines) - Early detection of risk enables proactive intervention (PT, strength training, home modifications) that prevents falls and extends independence (Source: Falls Cost and Prevalence)

The Outcome: - Baseline screening + periodic re-measurement tracks wellness progress and motivates participation in community programmes (Source: Right at Home case analysis) - Families feel confident when they see objective data (Source: AbleAssess for Families Marketing)

"For the first time, I felt like I actually understood how my mother was doing—not just how she said she was doing. That number on the page changed the whole conversation with her doctor."

Page 5: /solutions/skilled-nursing/

For-Profit Skilled Nursing

Headline: Defend your STAR ratings. Reduce CMS penalties. Strengthen audit defence.

The Challenge

Skilled nursing facilities operate in a regulatory vise. CMS quality ratings (STAR ratings) drive census, occupancy, and referral volume. A single star drop can cost you hundreds of thousands in lost admissions. At the same time, documentation standards for falls risk and care planning are increasingly stringent.

Section GG (Medicare's standardised assessment tool) demands objective functional baseline at admission and discharge. Surveyors want to see evidence that you assessed risk, documented it, and intervened. "We watched them" is not enough.

Falls are among the most frequent citation types in state and federal surveys. A single unpreventable fall might be acceptable; a fall in a resident flagged as high-risk but without documented intervention can trigger deficiency findings and penalties.

Reimbursement depends on acuity coding and justified resource use. If a resident falls and there's no evidence you identified risk beforehand, coding becomes indefensible and reimbursement suffers.

How Able Assess Helps

Protect the Business: Objective, documented fall-risk screening is your audit defence. Baseline assessment at admission (integrated with Section GG) shows you identified risk. Periodic re-screening shows monitoring. Clear intervention plans (PT referral, environmental modification, supervision level) show you acted on data. That documentation is gold during surveys and appeals.

(Source: Protect the Business pillar; CMS compliance strategy)

Grip strength baseline aligns with Section GG functional assessment. You've already captured the measurement; you're just adding clinical rigour and defensibility.

(Source: Grip Strength as Biomarker; CDC STEADI evidence)

Protect Growth: STAR ratings drive referrals. A community known for proactive fall prevention and strong quality documentation converts hospitals and physicians. Able Assess becomes part of your quality story: "We screen everyone, track trends, intervene early, and defend our practices with objective data." That builds physician confidence and referral volume.

(Source: Protect Growth pillar; senior living census model)

Protect Residents: One in four adults 65+ falls annually. Fifty percent of home health beneficiaries fall each year. Falls are the single biggest driver of hospitalisation, acuity progression, and premature mortality. Early detection enables intervention (PT, environmental modification, supervision adjustment) that prevents falls and extends function.

(Source: Falls Cost and Prevalence; CDC data)

Proof

The Regulatory & Clinical Data: - 1 in 4 adults 65+ falls each year (Source: AbleAssess for Families Marketing) - 50% of skilled nursing beneficiaries fall annually (Source: 5 Pillars Strategy; Falls Cost and Prevalence) - Grip strength decline is an independent predictor of mortality, hospitalisation, functional decline, and falls risk (Source: Grip Strength as Biomarker; CDC STEADI evidence base) - CDC STEADI framework (Stopping Elderly Accidents, Deaths & Injuries) endorses grip strength, balance, gait, and timed-up-and-go testing (Source: Clinical Basis - CDC STEADI and 2022 World Guidelines)

The Business Impact: - Objective fall-risk assessment defends STAR quality ratings and survey findings (Source: Protect the Business pillar) - Baseline screening + periodic re-measurement enables proactive intervention and CMS-defensible documentation (Source: Falls Cost and Prevalence; Cypress HomeCare case analysis)

The Voice:

"The thing that really stands out about this tool is the physical device. There's science behind it, physicality behind it, a measurement behind it. And for compliance purposes, it gives us defensible documentation of what we assessed and why we intervened."

— Skilled nursing facility administrator

  • Trained in minutes: PT, OT, RN, or health coach can administer. No new specialty hire required.
  • Section GG integration: Baseline measurements feed into Section GG functional assessment at admission and discharge. You're not adding work—you're adding rigour to work you already do.
