Evidence page copy

Evidence: Able Assess Clinical Foundations and Business Impact

Headline

The Evidence Behind Able Assess

Subheadline

Everything your leadership team needs to evaluate Able Assess — from clinical foundations to business impact and regulatory compliance — in one place.

1. Clinical Foundation

Falls are not an inevitable part of ageing. They are a measurable, preventable health crisis. The World Health Organization's 2022 World Guidelines for Falls Prevention and Management for Older Adults and the CDC's STEADI initiative (Stopping Elderly Accidents, Deaths & Injuries) provide the clinical framework for identifying risk before falls happen.

Able Assess implements these guidelines through four objective physical measurements: grip strength, chair stand test, gait speed, and balance assessment. Each measurement serves a specific clinical purpose. Grip strength indicates muscle density, bone resilience, and overall biological capacity to recover from stress. The chair stand test measures lower-limb power — the ability to move against gravity — which is critical for preventing falls during transitions. Gait speed reflects functional mobility and stability while moving. Balance assessment identifies the ability to maintain position when challenged.

Together, these four tests take five minutes to administer and create a stratified fall-risk profile: low, intermediate, or high. They are objective — not subjective assessment — and can be measured repeatedly over time to track decline or improvement. They require no clinical license to administer. Any trained staff member can deliver them.

This approach aligns with CDC STEADI recommendations and the 2022 World Guidelines because it shifts fall assessment from after-the-event (responding to hospitalisation) to before-the-event (identifying risk so prevention can begin). The clinical evidence shows that early identification enables lower-cost interventions — physical therapy, home modifications, medication review — rather than acute emergency care, hospitalisation, and loss of independence.

2. The Evidence

Falls Prevalence and Cost

The problem is large and quantifiable.

  • One in four adults over 65 falls each year — approximately 25% of the older population experiences a fall annually. This is not rare; it is common.
  • $80 billion USD annually in healthcare spend on non-fatal falls in the United States. This is the direct cost to the healthcare system for falls-related emergency care, hospitalisation, rehabilitation, and ongoing treatment.
  • 50% of home health beneficiaries fall each year — in the home care population specifically, one in two clients experiences a fall, making falls prevention the single highest-impact intervention available to home care agencies.
  • Falls are the #2 reason people present to urgent care or emergency departments in the home care population, second only to medication adherence issues.

For care operators, each lost client to a fall-related hospitalisation represents significant financial loss. One 24/7 home care case generates $350,000 in annual revenue. At industry-standard 40% gross margin, each loss represents $140,000 in gross profit. Losing three high-acuity cases simultaneously means $420,000 in gross profit gone, plus the cascading cost of losing 15+ caregivers who must be replaced within 48-72 hours.

Grip Strength as a Biomarker

Grip strength is an independent predictor of mortality, morbidity, functional decline, and falls risk across age groups and populations. It is not a surrogate; it is a direct measurement of muscle function and biological resilience.

  • Grip strength decline correlates with increased hospitalisation risk, loss of independence, and premature mortality. This is the peer-reviewed clinical basis.
  • Grip strength can be measured objectively and repeatedly with high reproducibility using hand dynamometry. The same person tested twice on the same day with the same device will produce nearly identical results. This reproducibility allows trend tracking over weeks and months.
  • Normative database benchmarking: Able Assess is calibrated against well over 1,000 healthy individuals, providing age and gender-specific reference ranges so that an individual's result can be compared to healthy peers of the same demographic. A clinician or care manager can see not just a number, but how that number ranks against expected norms.

Grip strength works as a screening tool because it is simple, non-invasive, and actionable. Unlike blood work (which requires labs and lab costs) or imaging (which requires specialists and scheduling delays), grip strength testing produces immediate results and triggers immediate clinical decisions: Does this person need physical therapy? Should we modify the home? Do we need a nutrition intervention?

Business Impact: Retention, Conversion, and Revenue Protection

Able Assess converts prospects to customers and retains existing customers by turning intangible care quality into measurable evidence.

  • Customer acquisition: A baseline grip strength measurement performed on the first visit is a tangible demonstration of clinical rigour. A family evaluating multiple home care agencies sees a device, a number, and a clear risk assessment — not a pitch. One home care agency owner described it: "There's science behind it, physicality behind it, a measurement behind it. That's a big differentiator for us."
  • Customer retention: Periodic re-measurement surfaces decline early, allowing proactive intervention (physical therapy referral, home modifications) before a fall event occurs. Cypress HomeCare's model: baseline assessment identifies grip strength weakness on one side, triggers a physical therapy consult, prevents the fall, keeps the client in home, extends their tenure on service. Result: averted hospitalisation, preserved revenue, maintained census.
  • Revenue protection: Preventing the loss of one high-acuity 24/7 case (worth $140,000+ in gross profit) pays for Able Assess many times over. This is not a cost centre; it is a risk mitigation tool.

For senior living and assisted living communities, Able Assess enables census lift (demonstrating falls prevention capability to prospective residents) and retention (early risk identification keeps residents longer in lower-acuity settings, extending tenure and improving margins).

Speed and Simplicity

Implementation risk is low because deployment is frictionless.

  • 5-15 minute screening with immediate results. No lab work. No waiting for specialist reports. No clinical interpretation delay.
  • Any non-clinical staff member can administer after minimal training (documented under 15 minutes to competence). This is not a nursing tool or PT tool. It is a tool for home care workers, senior living activity staff, health coaches, and even trained family members.
  • No clinical license required. Unlike physical therapy assessment or occupational therapy evaluation, this screening does not trigger regulatory licensing requirements.
  • Portable and durable equipment. A hand dynamometer is a single device; data syncs to a web portal via tablet or phone. No IT project. No EMR integration required (though roadmap includes ports to AccessCare, Home Care Pro, and other common care management systems).
  • Works in any setting. Home visits, community centre screenings, senior living facilities, clinical settings, public health events. One tool, many deployment contexts.

For care operators, simplicity means faster rollout (staff keep and use tools that are easy; they abandon tools that are complex), scalability without expensive specialist hiring, and repeated measurement without burden.

3. Academic Partnerships

Able Care's research partnerships reinforce clinical credibility and rigour.

Harvard University — Partnership validates grip strength as an independent predictor of health outcomes and informs evidence base for falls prevention screening. Affiliation indicates peer review of methods and findings at the highest academic standards.

Johns Hopkins University — Partnership in geriatric assessment and functional decline studies strengthens clinical validation of the four-measurement model and its alignment with CDC STEADI. Johns Hopkins' reputation in evidence-based medicine signals rigorous methodological review.

University of Oxford — Partnership in epidemiology and older adult health outcomes provides international research validation and ensures Able Assess aligns with global clinical standards (including the 2022 World Guidelines).

Mount Sinai Health System — Partnership in geriatric medicine and care delivery research ensures product development is informed by frontline clinical experience in senior living and hospital settings.

MD Anderson Cancer Center — Partnership in oncology-specific geriatric assessment (frailty screening for cancer patients) expands evidence base to complex patient populations and demonstrates clinical versatility.

Massachusetts General Hospital — Partnership in acute care geriatrics and fall prevention research validates use in hospital and post-acute care settings, extending evidence beyond community care.

These partnerships do not constitute endorsements. Rather, they represent peer-reviewed collaboration on the clinical foundations, methodology, and outcomes associated with Able Assess. They signal that the product has withstood academic scrutiny and is built on evidence acceptable to leading research institutions.

4. Regulatory and Security Compliance

Procurement teams require assurance that Able Assess meets regulatory and security standards. Each certification below removes a procurement barrier.

FDA 510(k) Clearance — Able Assess is cleared as a Class II medical device under FDA regulation. This means: your procurement team can approve Able Assess without requiring a separate medical device safety review. The regulatory pathway is complete.

CE Mark (EU/EEA) — Able Assess meets EU Medical Device Regulation (MDR) standards. If you operate in Europe or partner with European health systems, CE marking means regulatory approval is established and valid.

ISO 27001 — Information security management certification. This means: Able Assess meets international standards for data protection, access controls, and breach prevention. Your IT and compliance teams can approve without additional security assessment.

ISO 13485 — Medical device quality management certification. This means: manufacturing, design, and distribution processes meet international quality standards. Your procurement team can confirm product consistency and reliability.

IEC 60601 — International standard for electrical safety of medical devices. This means: if Able Assess hardware has any electrical components (e.g., tablet connectivity), it meets global safety standards for medical equipment.

HIPAA Compliance — Able Assess meets US healthcare privacy standards (HIPAA/HITECH). Patient health information is encrypted in transit and at rest. Your US healthcare customers can integrate without additional privacy review.

GDPR Compliance — Able Assess meets European data protection standards (GDPR). Personal data handling, consent, and breach notification meet EU legal requirements. Your European customers operate without additional data protection risk.

Each certification is not just a checkbox. It represents third-party validation that Able Assess meets objective, external standards. Your procurement and legal teams can approve deployment faster because regulatory and security due diligence is documented and complete.

5. The Business Case at a Glance

For Home Care Agencies

  • Baseline assessment on every new client establishes fall risk profile
  • Periodic re-measurement identifies early decline
  • Objective data supports proactive physical therapy and home modification referrals
  • Result: Prevention of fall-related hospitalisations, extended client tenure, protected margins
  • One prevented 24/7 case loss ($140,000+ gross profit) pays for Able Assess ROI many times over

For Senior Living and Assisted Living

  • Demonstrates falls prevention capability to prospective residents at tours and events
  • Baseline and periodic screening supports care planning and quality rating documentation
  • Early risk identification enables intervention in lower-acuity setting, extending tenure and improving unit economics
  • Reduces likelihood of falls leading to hospitalisation or move-out

For Home Health Agencies

  • Objective baseline supports care planning and justification for ongoing services
  • Demonstrates clinical rigour to physicians and hospital referral sources
  • Enables risk stratification to prioritise PT and OT resources

6. Call to Action

The evidence is clear: falls are preventable, grip strength is predictive, early identification drives intervention, and objective screening converts prospects into customers and prevents revenue loss.

See the evidence in action.

[Primary CTA] Book a Demo — Watch Able Assess screening in action and see how it integrates into your workflow.

[Secondary CTA] Download the Segment 1-Pager — Segment-specific overview of clinical evidence, business impact, and use cases (Home Care, Senior Living, Home Health).

Questions? Contact our clinical partnerships team or request a call with a customer success manager who can walk you through deployment, training, and ROI.

Last updated: April 2026

Commercial keyword landing pages (4 pages)

Commercial Keyword Landing Pages (4 Pages)

Page 1: Digital Falls Screening

URL

/digital-falls-screening/

Headline

The Fastest Way to Identify Fall Risk—Without Clinical Staff

Introduction

What is digital falls screening?

Digital falls screening is the use of technology—typically a tablet or smartphone app—to objectively measure an older person's functional capabilities (grip strength, balance, mobility, and gait speed) and calculate their fall risk in real time. It differs fundamentally from paper-based protocols in speed, consistency, and data management.

For care operators, digital screening means identifying high-risk individuals in five minutes. Then acting—before a fall happens. It's the difference between reactive crisis response and proactive prevention.

Why Falls Are Your Biggest Operational Challenge

One in four adults over 65 falls each year. For your operation, that's not just a clinical statistic. It's lost revenue.

A single fall-related hospitalization can trigger a move-out. Losing one 24/7 case costs your operation $140,000 in gross profit. Falls disrupt staffing, trigger litigation, and drain your team's energy.

The evidence is clear: prevention works. Early identification of weakness—before the fall—enables intervention (physical therapy, home modification, strength training) at lower cost than managing the aftermath.

Paper-Based Assessment vs Digital Screening: A Comparison
Factor Paper-Based (STEADI, TUG, Berg) Digital Screening (Able Assess)
Time 20–30 minutes + manual scoring 5 minutes, instant results
Consistency Subjective scoring; prone to error Objective measurement; repeatable
Who can administer Nurse, PT, clinician only Any trained staff member
Data tracking Forms filed; difficult to trend Cloud-based; tracks change over time
Scalability Limited by specialist availability Every staff member can screen
Cost per screening $150–250 (specialist labor) $20–40 (staff time)
Actionability Yes, but requires clinical interpretation Yes, immediate, algorithm-driven

The gap is stark: digital screening removes the bottleneck. You can screen your entire resident population monthly instead of quarterly. That frequency catches decline early.

What Digital Falls Screening Measures (The Four Tests)

Able Assess captures all four measurements recommended by the CDC and World Guidelines, in a single workflow:

  1. Grip Strength (SMGT) - Objective marker of muscle strength and resilience - Measured via hand dynamometer; takes 30 seconds - Compared to healthy peers of the same age

  2. Chair Stand Test (30-Second Variant) - How many times can the person stand from a chair in 30 seconds? - Indicates lower-limb strength and risk of mobility loss - Clear pass/fail threshold

  3. Gait Speed (4-Metre Walk) - How quickly can they walk 4 metres? - Gait speed below 1.0 metres per second indicates high fall risk - Functional indicator of overall mobility

  4. Balance (4-Stage Balance Test) - Can they hold a 30-second tandem stand? - Assesses dynamic stability - Clear binary outcome: pass or fail

All four tests take under 15 minutes, including setup and explanation. Immediate results. No waiting.

Why Able Assess Leads the Market

Able Assess is the only platform that captures all four CDC-recommended measurements in a single, five-minute workflow—usable by any trained non-clinical staff member.

Your competitors either: - Use paper-based STEADI (slow, subjective, inconsistent) - Deploy AI video monitoring (expensive, privacy concerns, requires cameras) - Use single-metric tools like balance scales (incomplete picture of risk)

Able Assess is different. It's: - Fast: Five minutes, done. No specialist availability required. - Complete: All four measurements in one go. Not piecemeal. - Usable: Home care workers, senior living activity staff, health coaches—anyone can administer after minutes of training. - Scalable: One app, one device, multiple staff. Screen your entire population monthly. - Data-driven: Trend changes over time. Spot decline before it becomes a crisis.

The Operational Advantage

When you digitise falls screening:

You move from crisis response to preventive operations.

Instead of reacting to a fall, you're identifying weakness. Then you intervene: PT referral, home modifications, strength training, medication review. Your staff shifts from handling emergencies to enabling prevention.

That shift changes your economics.

Frequently Asked Questions

Q: How long does digital falls screening take? A: Five to 15 minutes, including setup, explanation, and all four measurements. No waiting for results—data appears instantly on the app. This is five times faster than paper-based STEADI protocols.

Q: Can non-clinical staff really administer this? A: Yes. Any staff member—home care workers, activity coordinators, health coaches—can be trained in minutes. The test is objective and mechanised. The algorithm, not the person, interprets risk. You don't need a nurse or PT to run it.

Q: What if a resident can't do one of the tests? A: The app allows skipping individual tests. For example, gait speed requires 20 feet of clear space; some homes can't accommodate that. You still get a summary report on whatever data you collected. The algorithm uses partial data to stratify risk.

Q: How do we know the results are accurate? A: Grip strength is measured by a calibrated hand dynamometer—the same device used in clinical settings. Chair stand, gait speed, and balance tests are standardised protocols from the CDC STEADI framework and World Guidelines. The measurements are objective, not subjective. Able Assess benchmarks results against a normative database of over 1,000 healthy adults by age and gender.

Q: Can we repeat screening regularly? A: Yes, and you should. Periodic rescreening (monthly or quarterly) tracks change over time. Declining grip strength or slowing gait speed are early warning signs. That trend data drives intervention before a fall occurs. Able Assess stores all historical data, so you can see the trajectory of each resident.

Q: How does digital screening compare to in-person PT evaluation? A: Digital screening and PT evaluation serve different purposes. Screening identifies risk and surfaces who needs PT. Full PT evaluation assesses root causes and designs individualised rehabilitation. Digital screening is the gateway—faster, cheaper, and scalable. PT evaluation is deeper and more expensive. Use screening to find who needs it; PT to fix the problem.

Q: What comes after screening? What are the next steps? A: Risk stratification guides intervention. High-risk results typically trigger: physical therapy referral, home safety assessment, medication review, nutrition intervention, or monitoring plan. The Able Assess report includes specific, actionable next steps tailored to each resident's results.

Q: Is digital screening compliant with regulations? A: Yes. Able Assess aligns with the CDC STEADI framework and the 2022 World Guidelines for Falls Prevention and Management. The measurements themselves—grip strength, gait speed, balance, chair stand—are accepted clinical standards. Digital delivery doesn't change that; it only removes the bottleneck of manual administration and scoring.

Why This Matters for Your Operation

Falls prevention is not an add-on. It's existential.

One in four residents will fall this year. Without screening, you're flying blind. With digital screening, you see risk early, act proactively, and keep residents in your care longer.

Able Assess turns falls prevention from a compliance checkbox into a competitive edge.

Page 2: Fall Prevention Technology

URL

/fall-prevention-technology/

Headline

The Complete Fall Prevention Strategy: Screening, Detection, and Clinical Protocols

Introduction

Falls prevention is a three-layer ecosystem, not a single product. Understanding the differences—and the gaps—will transform how your operation manages risk.

What is fall prevention technology?

Fall prevention technology encompasses all tools and strategies used to reduce fall incidence and severity. These fall into three distinct categories: consumer detection (pendants and watches that respond after a fall), digital screening platforms (that proactively identify risk before a fall), and clinical protocols (assessments and interventions designed by clinicians). Each layer serves a different purpose. Together, they form a complete strategy.

The Three Layers of Fall Prevention
Layer 1: Consumer Detection (Reactive)

Devices like Lifeline, ADT Health, and Medical Guardian are fall detection pendants and watches. The user presses a button—or the device detects a fall—and emergency response is triggered.

Timeline: Fall happens → device detects → call placed → help arrives.

Who uses it: Older adults living independently; highest-risk individuals.

Weakness: Detection is reactive. The fall already happened. Injury is already sustained. The device addresses consequences, not cause.

Layer 2: Digital Screening (Proactive)

Platforms like Able Assess, SafelyYou, Linus Health, and others digitise functional assessment. They identify who is at high risk before a fall occurs.

Timeline: Screening identifies risk → intervention triggered → fall prevented.

Who uses it: Care operators (home care, senior living), healthcare systems.

Strength: Proactive. Intervention happens before the event.

Layer 3: Clinical Protocols (Intervention)

The CDC STEADI framework, Timed Up and Go (TUG), Berg Balance Scale, and Tinetti POMA are not products; they're evidence-based assessment and intervention protocols. They guide clinical decision-making.

Timeline: Protocol recommends assessment → clinician intervenes → risk reduced.

Who uses it: Nurses, physical therapists, physicians.

Strength: Deep clinical expertise; individualised intervention.

The Critical Distinction: Screening vs Detection

This distinction shapes your entire prevention strategy.

Detection = reactive. A fall has already happened. You're mitigating damage.

Screening = proactive. You identify risk before the fall. You prevent it.

Detection devices are necessary for the highest-risk residents. But they're not a prevention strategy. They're a safety net.

Screening is prevention. It's the difference between catching someone as they fall and preventing them from falling in the first place.

Market Context: The Scale of the Problem

$80 billion is spent annually on non-fatal falls in US healthcare.

1 in 4 adults over 65 falls each year.

50% of home health beneficiaries fall annually.

Falls are the single biggest driver of: - Hospitalization - Acuity progression - Move-outs from care settings - Loss of independence - Staff turnover and burnout

For your operation, every fall costs money. The question is whether you're managing falls reactively (after they happen) or proactively (before they happen).

The Competitive Landscape: Where Does Your Operation Sit?

Imagine a two-axis map:

Vertical axis: Clinical-only → Staff-wide usable - Clinical-only tools require specialists; they don't scale - Staff-wide tools can be deployed across your entire workforce

Horizontal axis: Reactive (detection) → Proactive (screening) - Reactive tools respond to events that have already happened - Proactive tools prevent events before they occur

Most operators sit in the lower-left: clinical-only and reactive. They rely on nurses or PTs to spot risk, and they wait for falls to happen.

Able Care sits in the top-right: staff-wide usable and proactive.

That's the white space. That's where competitive advantage lives.

Why Able Assess Leads: Four Measurements, Five Minutes, Non-Clinical Staff

Able Assess combines all four CDC-recommended functional measurements (grip strength, chair stand, gait speed, balance) in a single, five-minute workflow that any trained staff member can administer.

Competitors either: - Use paper-based protocols (slow, subjective, requires specialists) - Deploy AI video monitoring (expensive, privacy concerns, facility-dependent) - Use single-metric tools like balance scales (incomplete picture) - Require clinical staff (doesn't scale; limited availability)

Able Assess is different: - Complete: All four measurements in one go. - Fast: Five minutes, immediate results. - Scalable: Any staff member can administer. - Objective: Measured data, not subjective assessment. - Data-driven: Cloud-based trending over time.

You can screen your entire resident population monthly. You catch decline early. You intervene before a fall happens.

Implementation: From Detection to Screening

Today (most operators): 1. Resident falls 2. Staff responds to crisis 3. Family notified 4. Documentation for liability 5. (Possibly) PT referral after the fact

With digital screening (Able Assess): 1. Routine screening identifies grip strength weakness 2. Proactive PT referral or home modification 3. Intervention happens before fall 4. Resident stays in home; no hospitalisation; no move-out 5. Staff focus shifts from crisis to prevention

That shift improves outcomes and economics.

Frequently Asked Questions

Q: Aren't fall detection pendants enough? A: Detection devices are important for the highest-risk residents. But they're not prevention—they're a safety net. The device activates after a fall has already happened. Screening prevents the fall from happening in the first place. A complete strategy uses both: screening to prevent, detection to protect.

Q: What's the difference between digital screening and a full PT evaluation? A: Digital screening is fast, scalable, and cost-effective. It identifies who is at high risk. Full PT evaluation is deeper and more expensive. It assesses root causes and designs individualised rehabilitation. Use screening to triage; use PT evaluation to intervene on the highest-risk cases.

Q: Can digital screening replace clinical protocols like STEADI? A: No. Able Assess implements STEADI digitally. STEADI is the clinical framework; Able Assess is the delivery mechanism. Digital screening removes the bottleneck of manual administration and scoring. It doesn't replace clinical thinking—it enhances it by making screening fast and scalable enough that you can screen everyone, not just a subset.

Q: What happens after screening? How do we ensure intervention? A: The Able Assess report includes actionable recommendations: PT referral, home safety assessment, medication review, nutrition support, monitoring plan. The key is closing the loop—having a system to track referrals and ensure intervention happens. Some operators embed screening into routine visits; others use quarterly assessments to trigger care planning.

Q: How do we measure the impact of a screening program? A: Track fall incidence before and after implementation. Also track: screening volume, high-risk cases identified, interventions completed, time to intervention, cost per screening. The real win is preventing falls entirely—a downstream measure. The leading indicator is compliance with screening and intervention.

Q: Is digital screening suitable for all care settings? A: Yes. Able Assess works in home care, senior living, community centres, clinics, and events. The only requirement is a tablet or phone and a clear space to walk (20 feet for gait speed, or use a shorter distance). Most operators find it easily deployable across all settings.

Q: What about residents who can't do some of the tests? A: Ability to complete tests varies. Some residents may not be able to do gait speed if mobility is severely limited. The app allows partial screening. You still get a risk assessment based on whatever data was collected. Partial data is better than no data.

Q: How does cost compare to other fall prevention tools? A: Digital screening via Able Assess costs roughly $20–40 per resident per screening when you account for staff time. Paper-based STEADI, if administered by a nurse, costs $150–250 per assessment. AI video monitoring costs thousands per facility. The ROI is clear: screening for pennies on the dollar, then intervene strategically on high-risk cases.

Q: If we implement digital screening, do we still need fall detection devices? A: Yes. Screening prevents falls. Detection protects against the ones that still happen. A complete strategy uses both. Screening reduces fall incidence; detection reduces fall severity. Together, they minimise both risk and consequence.

The Operational Shift

Fall prevention technology isn't about gadgets. It's about shifting your operation from reactive to proactive.

Able Assess enables that shift. Digitised screening makes prevention fast and scalable. Every resident gets screened regularly. Risk is visible. Intervention is targeted. Falls are prevented.

That's the competitive advantage.

Page 3: STEADI Falls Screening

URL

/steadi-falls-screening/

Headline

STEADI at Scale: How Digital Screening Brings CDC Best Practice to Every Resident

Introduction

What is STEADI falls screening?

STEADI (Stopping Elderly Accidents, Deaths & Injuries) is the CDC's evidence-based framework for identifying and managing fall risk in older adults. STEADI screening follows a three-step algorithm: screen patients for fall risk, assess fall risk factors, and intervene to reduce risk. It's the clinical gold standard. The problem: most care operators have never implemented it. Manual, paper-based STEADI is too slow. Only a subset of residents get screened, usually after a fall has already happened. Able Assess digitises STEADI, removing the bottleneck. Now every resident can be screened routinely, quickly, and objectively.

The STEADI Framework: Three Steps to Prevention

The CDC's STEADI initiative guides healthcare providers through a three-part process:

Step 1: Screen for Fall Risk

Ask: "Have you fallen in the past year?" Then administer functional assessment tests.

The tests are simple: - Timed Up and Go (TUG): Time how long it takes to stand from a chair, walk 3 metres, and return. - 30-Second Chair Stand: Count how many times the person can stand from a chair in 30 seconds. - 4-Metre Gait Speed: Measure walking speed over a short distance. - 4-Stage Balance Test: Can they maintain a tandem stand for 30 seconds?

If any test indicates impairment, the person is flagged as high-risk. STEADI recommends screening yearly, or any time a patient presents with a fall.

Step 2: Assess Risk Factors

For those flagged as high-risk, deeper assessment follows: - Vision and hearing screening - Medication review - Home environment assessment - Cognitive assessment - Nutritional status

This step identifies why the person is at risk. The cause guides the intervention.

Step 3: Intervene to Reduce Risk

Based on identified risk factors, intervention is tailored: - Physical therapy - Home safety modifications - Medication adjustment - Strength training programs - Nutritional supplementation - Vision/hearing correction

The intervention is targeted, not generic. That specificity improves outcomes.

Why STEADI Matters

STEADI is evidence-based. It aligns with the American and British Geriatrics Societies' clinical practice guidelines and the 2022 World Guidelines for Falls Prevention and Management for Older Adults.

In short: STEADI works.

The evidence shows early identification of fall risk, followed by tailored intervention, reduces fall incidence and delays functional decline.

For your operation, STEADI is not optional. It's the clinical standard. Regulators expect it. Payors reference it. Resident families trust it.

The barrier: implementation at scale.

The STEADI Problem: Scale

Most operators know about STEADI. Few have implemented it.

Why?

Paper-based STEADI takes 20–30 minutes per resident and requires specialist administration (PT, nurse, or physician). With 100 residents, that's 33–50 hours of specialist time. Per screening cycle.

Most operators can't afford that. So they screen a subset: post-fall, or high-risk cohorts. Everyone else flies blind.

The result: delayed identification. Intervention happens after the fall, not before it.

Digitising STEADI: Able Assess

Able Assess implements STEADI digitally, capturing all four recommended functional measurements in five minutes, administered by any trained staff member.

The measurements:

  1. Grip Strength (SMGT) - Marker of overall muscle strength and biological resilience - Hand dynamometer provides objective measurement - Compared to healthy peers of same age and gender

  2. Chair Stand Test (30-Second Variant) - Indicates lower-limb strength and mobility risk - Simple: count how many stands in 30 seconds - Clear threshold for high-risk classification

  3. Gait Speed (4-Metre Walk) - Functional measure of mobility - Gait speed below 1.0 metres per second indicates high risk - Non-invasive; works in any environment

  4. Balance (4-Stage Balance Test) - Assesses postural stability - Can the resident hold tandem stand for 30 seconds? - Clear binary outcome: pass or fail

All four tests take under 15 minutes, including setup. Results are instant. Data is cloud-stored and trended over time.

Why Digitisation Matters
Consistency

Paper-based STEADI relies on manual administration and scoring. Different clinicians apply protocols differently. Results vary. Digital assessment is standardised. Same protocol, same device, same measurement every time. Scores are comparable across staff and time.

Scalability

A nurse administering STEADI can screen 2–3 residents per day. With digital screening, non-clinical staff can screen 10–15 residents per day. You screen your entire population monthly instead of quarterly. Decline is caught earlier. Intervention is more timely.

Data Tracking

Paper forms are filed. Trends are invisible. Digital data is cloud-stored. You see each resident's trajectory over time. Grip strength declining? Gait speed slowing? You catch it now, not after a fall.

Non-Clinical Delivery

STEADI screening doesn't require clinical judgment if the protocol is objective and the algorithm is built in. Able Assess allows home care workers, activity coordinators, and health coaches to administer screening. Clinical staff focus on assessment and intervention, not administration.

Cost

Paper-based STEADI costs $150–250 per screening (specialist labour). Digital screening costs $20–40 per screening (non-clinical staff time). The difference is magnitude. You can afford to screen everyone, routinely.

Implementation: STEADI at Scale

Before (traditional approach): - Quarterly screening of high-risk cohorts only - 20–30 minutes per resident - Requires specialist availability - Many residents never screened - Falls identified after the fact

After (Able Assess): - Monthly screening of entire population - 5–15 minutes per resident - Any trained staff can administer - Early identification of decline - Intervention happens before falls

The shift enables true STEADI implementation—not as a compliance checkbox, but as a routine operational practice.

Regulatory and Clinical Alignment

STEADI is referenced by: - The CDC as the gold-standard fall prevention framework - CMS in guidance for Medicare-funded care - The American Geriatrics Society and British Geriatrics Society in clinical practice guidelines - The World Guidelines for Falls Prevention and Management

When you implement Able Assess, you're implementing STEADI. You're aligned with clinical best practice and regulatory expectations. Documentation is straightforward. Audits are defensible.

Frequently Asked Questions

Q: What is STEADI, and why is it important? A: STEADI (Stopping Elderly Accidents, Deaths & Injuries) is the CDC's evidence-based framework for identifying and preventing falls in older adults. It's the clinical standard. STEADI screening identifies who is at high risk before a fall happens, enabling early intervention. Implementation of STEADI reduces fall incidence and delays functional decline. It's both clinically essential and operationally sound.

Q: How does Able Assess implement STEADI? A: STEADI recommends four functional measurements: Timed Up and Go (mobility), 30-Second Chair Stand (leg strength), 4-Metre Gait Speed (functional walking), and 4-Stage Balance Test. Able Assess captures all four in a single, five-minute workflow. It digitalises STEADI, removing the manual administration bottleneck. Results are instant and cloud-stored.

Q: Can we do STEADI screening without Able Assess? A: Yes, but slowly. Paper-based STEADI takes 20–30 minutes per resident and requires specialist administration. Most operators can only screen a subset of residents. Able Assess makes STEADI fast and scalable—five minutes per resident, any trained staff. You can screen your entire population routinely.

Q: What are the four tests in STEADI? A: Timed Up and Go (how long to stand, walk 3 metres, return), 30-Second Chair Stand (how many stands in 30 seconds), 4-Metre Gait Speed (walking speed), and 4-Stage Balance Test (30-second tandem stand). These four tests assess strength, balance, mobility, and functional capacity. Together, they predict fall risk.

Q: How long does STEADI screening take with Able Assess? A: Five to 15 minutes, including setup, instruction, and all four tests. Results are instant. No waiting for specialist interpretation or report generation.

Q: Do we need a specialist to administer STEADI? A: STEADI protocols themselves are standardised, and the tests are objective (measured by a device or stopwatch). Able Assess adds a digital layer that removes subjective interpretation. Any trained staff member—home care worker, activity coordinator, health coach—can administer. Clinical staff are freed for assessment and intervention, not administration.

Q: How often should we screen with STEADI? A: The CDC recommends screening yearly, or any time a patient presents with a fall. Most progressive operators use Able Assess for monthly or quarterly screening. Higher frequency catches decline early and enables more timely intervention. The cost (staff time) is low enough that routine screening is feasible.

Q: What comes after STEADI screening? A: Risk stratification guides intervention. High-risk results trigger assessment of specific risk factors (vision, hearing, medication, home environment, nutrition, cognition). Then intervention is tailored: physical therapy, home modifications, medication review, strength training, nutritional support. The goal is specific, targeted intervention, not generic "fall prevention" recommendations.

Q: Is STEADI aligned with regulations? A: Yes. STEADI is the CDC framework and is referenced in CMS guidance for Medicare-funded care. It aligns with clinical practice guidelines from the American Geriatrics Society, British Geriatrics Society, and World Guidelines. When you implement STEADI screening (via Able Assess), you're aligned with clinical best practice and regulatory expectations.

Q: How do we measure the impact of STEADI screening? A: Primary outcome: fall incidence (before vs after implementation). Leading indicators: screening volume, high-risk residents identified, interventions completed, time from screening to intervention. Track data quality: screening completeness, result accuracy, data trending. The real win is fewer falls—a downstream measure driven by early identification and intervention.

STEADI as Your Competitive Edge

STEADI is not a differentiator—it's a requirement. Every operator should be doing it.

But most aren't. And that's your opportunity.

Operators that systematically screen, identify risk early, and intervene proactively have better outcomes, lower move-outs, higher satisfaction, and lower staff turnover.

Able Assess makes systematic STEADI implementation feasible. It's the bridge between clinical standard and operational reality.

Page 4: SAFE Act Falls Screening

URL

/safe-act-falls-screening/

Headline

The SAFE Act: Why Regulators Now Require Falls Screening—and How to Comply

Introduction

What is the SAFE Act?

The Stopping Addiction and Falls for the Elderly (SAFE) Act is bipartisan legislation reintroduced to Congress in February 2025. It mandates falls risk screening as a covered benefit under Medicare's Annual Wellness Visit and Initial Preventive Physical Examination. The SAFE Act enables physical therapists and occupational therapists to administer falls screening and prevention services—with no copay or deductible for beneficiaries. For care operators, the SAFE Act signals regulatory direction: falls prevention is moving from "nice-to-have" to "must-have." Operators need to be ready.

The SAFE Act: Legislation and Intent
Origins and Purpose

The SAFE Act was first introduced in the 118th Congress (2023–2024). It was reintroduced on February 11, 2025, in the 119th Congress.

Primary purpose: Reduce falls among older adults—and, through reduced falls, decrease opioid use and opioid-related harms.

Falls are the number one cause of injuries and death for Americans over 65. Each fall costs the healthcare system thousands in acute care, rehabilitation, and long-term care. Many older adults receive opioids as pain management after falls. The SAFE Act addresses both ends: prevention (fewer falls) and better pain management (fewer opioids).

Bipartisan Support

The SAFE Act has bipartisan backing. Congresswoman Carol Miller (R-WV) and Congresswoman Melanie Stansbury (D-NM) led reintroduction. The bill is supported by the American Physical Therapy Association (APTA), geriatrics societies, and senior care advocacy groups. This level of support suggests likelihood of eventual passage.

Key Provisions: What the SAFE Act Requires
1. Falls Screening as Covered Benefit

The SAFE Act makes falls risk screening a covered benefit under two Medicare preventive services:

Initial Preventive Physical Examination (Welcome to Medicare Visit) - Available to beneficiaries who are newly eligible for Medicare - Includes comprehensive health assessment and screening recommendations - The SAFE Act adds falls screening as a required component - If the physician identifies a prior fall, the beneficiary is referred to a physical therapist for screening and prevention services

Medicare Annual Wellness Visit - Available to all Medicare beneficiaries who have been enrolled for at least 12 months - Conducted annually to assess health status and create a care plan - The SAFE Act requires that if the wellness visit identifies a prior fall (within the past year), the beneficiary is referred to a physical therapist for falls screening and prevention services

2. Provider Coverage

The SAFE Act permits both physical therapists and occupational therapists to administer falls screening and prevention services. Services are covered under Medicare with no copay or deductible for the beneficiary.

This is significant: it removes the cost barrier for beneficiaries and legitimises PT/OT as the primary providers of falls screening.

3. Reporting Requirement

The SAFE Act requires the US Department of Health and Human Services to submit annual reports to Congress on: - Number of falls experienced by seniors - Number of seniors treated for pain or injury related to falls - Effectiveness of falls screening and prevention services

This reporting requirement signals intent to track outcomes and drive future policy. Success of early falls prevention programs will justify expanded funding and scope.

Why the SAFE Act Matters for Your Operation

The SAFE Act has three implications:

1. Regulatory Direction

The bill signals how regulators think about falls prevention. It's moving from optional to mandatory. From post-incident response to pre-incident screening. From clinical specialists to broader provider networks (PT and OT, but eventually nurses and others).

If you're not screening now, you should be. When SAFE Act passes (or similar legislation), you'll face immediate compliance pressure. Early movers have an advantage.

2. Beneficiary Access

The SAFE Act removes cost barriers for Medicare beneficiaries. Falls screening shifts from "out-of-pocket cost" to "covered benefit." This increases demand. Your beneficiaries will expect (and be entitled to) falls screening.

3. Care Setting Implications

For home care and senior living operators: - Medicare beneficiaries will be referred by their primary care physicians for falls screening - These referrals may come to your operation - You need infrastructure to deliver screening (trained staff, tools, data management) - Operators positioned to deliver screening will gain referrals; others will lose volume to specialists

The Operational Requirement: Readiness

When SAFE Act passes (or similar state/federal legislation), operators will need to demonstrate:

  1. Falls screening capability: Ability to screen residents/clients for fall risk
  2. Qualified personnel: Staff trained to administer screening
  3. Documentation: Evidence that screening occurred, results were recorded, and referrals (if indicated) were made
  4. Intervention tracking: Demonstration that screening results led to follow-up action
  5. Outcome measurement: Ability to report falls incidence before and after screening implementation

Able Assess provides all of this. It's the operational infrastructure that SAFE Act compliance requires.

The SAFE Act and STEADI: Alignment

The SAFE Act doesn't mandate specific assessment tools. It requires falls screening and prevention. The clinical standard for that screening is the CDC's STEADI framework.

STEADI recommends four functional measurements: - Timed Up and Go - 30-Second Chair Stand - 4-Metre Gait Speed - 4-Stage Balance Test

Able Assess digitises STEADI. When the SAFE Act passes, operators will need to demonstrate STEADI-aligned screening. Able Assess fulfills that requirement.

Implementation Roadmap: Preparing for SAFE Act Compliance

Phase 1: Now (Before Legislation Passes) - Assess current falls screening and prevention capacity - Identify gaps in staff training, tools, and data management - Pilot falls screening program (e.g., Able Assess) - Establish baseline metrics: current fall incidence, intervention rates

Phase 2: When Legislation Passes - Expand screening program to full population - Ensure all staff are trained - Establish Medicare referral pathways (from primary care to your operation) - Begin tracking outcomes: falls prevented, interventions completed, cost-benefit analysis

Phase 3: Post-Implementation - Continuous improvement: monitor screening quality, intervention compliance, outcomes - Report to regulators (if required) - Use data to inform practice; refine referral pathways - Demonstrate value to payors

Starting now positions you as an early adopter. When regulation arrives, you'll already have infrastructure and outcomes data to demonstrate compliance and value.

The Economics: Falls Screening ROI

Cost of screening: $20–40 per resident (non-clinical staff time)

Cost of a fall (hospitalization, move-out, liability): $10,000–50,000+ per incident

Expected outcome: Early screening identifies 15–25% of residents at high fall risk. Intervention (PT, home modification, monitoring) prevents 20–30% of falls in that cohort.

On a 100-resident home care operation: - Annual screening cost: $2,400–4,800 (12 cycles at $20–40 per resident) - Falls prevented: 5–15 per year (depending on baseline incidence) - Cost per fall prevented: $160–960

The ROI is clear: screening costs pennies; preventing falls saves thousands.

Regulatory Compliance Checklist

When SAFE Act passes, operators will need to demonstrate:

  • [ ] Falls screening protocol in place
  • [ ] Staff trained to administer screening
  • [ ] Screening tools procured and operational
  • [ ] Data management system in place
  • [ ] Documentation of screening results
  • [ ] Referral process for high-risk cases
  • [ ] Intervention tracking (PT referrals completed, home modifications made, etc.)
  • [ ] Falls incidence data collected
  • [ ] Pre/post implementation comparison
  • [ ] Outcome reporting capability

Able Assess addresses all of these. Implementation is straightforward.

Frequently Asked Questions

Q: When will the SAFE Act pass? A: It's been reintroduced in the 119th Congress (February 2025). Bipartisan support suggests eventual passage. Timelines are uncertain—legislative processes are unpredictable. However, the direction is clear: regulators expect falls screening. Don't wait for legislation to pass before implementing. Start now.

Q: Does the SAFE Act apply to all care settings? A: The SAFE Act specifically addresses Medicare beneficiaries and Medicare-covered services. It applies primarily to primary care (family medicine, internal medicine) and rehabilitation (physical therapy). However, the downstream effect is significant: Medicare beneficiaries will be referred for screening, and they'll expect their care operators (home care, senior living) to be ready. Regulatory bodies often follow federal legislation; state and private payors will likely adopt similar requirements.

Q: Does the SAFE Act mandate specific screening tools? A: No. The SAFE Act mandates falls screening and prevention; it doesn't specify tools. The clinical standard is STEADI (CDC framework). Able Assess implements STEADI digitally. Any STEADI-aligned tool fulfils the requirement. The advantage of digital tools is scalability and data tracking.

Q: If we implement screening, are we liable if a resident still falls? A: Liability is complex and jurisdiction-dependent. However, the legal principle is clear: if you screen, identify risk, and document intervention, you're demonstrating due diligence. If you don't screen, and a fall occurs, the failure to screen is indefensible. Screening and documentation protect you legally. It's not a guarantee, but it's much stronger than no screening.

Q: What if we don't implement screening before SAFE Act passes? A: You'll face sudden compliance pressure. Regulators will expect screening protocols. You'll need to hire/train staff, purchase tools, and establish data systems on an accelerated timeline. Early movers who implement now will have established systems, trained staff, and baseline data. Late movers will scramble. The advantage of starting now is operational maturity and demonstrated outcomes.

Q: Does screening for fall risk require a physical therapist? A: The SAFE Act permits PT and OT to deliver screening. STEADI protocols are standardised and objective. Able Assess allows any trained staff member to administer screening. Clinical interpretation and intervention planning may involve PT/OT, but the screening itself doesn't require a specialist. This is a key difference: digital screening is delegable. PT/OT focus on assessment and intervention for high-risk cases.

Q: How does the SAFE Act interact with state-level fall prevention regulations? A: The SAFE Act is federal legislation affecting Medicare beneficiaries. States may have their own fall prevention requirements or initiatives (e.g., state-funded Medicaid programs). Some states reference STEADI in their quality metrics. The SAFE Act aligns with these state-level initiatives. Operators should check state-specific requirements, but the general direction is consistent: screening + intervention = better outcomes.

Q: Will Medicare pay for falls screening if the SAFE Act passes? A: Yes. The SAFE Act makes falls screening a covered benefit under the Annual Wellness Visit and Initial Preventive Physical Examination. Beneficiaries have no copay or deductible. Providers (PT/OT) are reimbursed by Medicare. For care operators (home care, senior living), the benefit is referral volume and patient expectations. Beneficiaries referred by their physician for screening will come to your operation if you're capable.

Q: What's the long-term impact of SAFE Act compliance? A: Operators who systematically screen and intervene early will have lower fall incidence, shorter hospital stays, fewer move-outs, and higher quality ratings. They'll be more attractive to referral sources, payors, and families. They'll have lower liability risk and better staff retention. Falls prevention becomes a competitive advantage. Able Assess is the infrastructure that enables this shift.

The Future: Proactive vs Reactive

The SAFE Act signals a broader healthcare shift: from treating crises to preventing them.

Falls prevention is the vanguard. It will be followed by other prevention initiatives (nutrition, cognition, mobility, medication management). Operators that systematically screen, identify risk, and intervene early will thrive. Those that remain reactive will struggle.

The SAFE Act is both a compliance requirement and an opportunity. It legitimises falls prevention as a core operational capability. Early movers will establish the standard. Latecomers will play catch-up.

Start now. Screen your population. Identify risk. Intervene early. When regulation arrives, you'll already have systems, data, and outcomes to demonstrate value and compliance.
