Timing & Cadence Summary

Standard Sequence (No engagement):
- Email 1: Day 0 - Email 2: Day 3 - Email 3: Day 6 - Email 4: Day 10 - Email 5: Day 14

Accelerated Sequence (Link clicks, opens):
- Move Email 3 to Day 4 - Move Email 4 to Day 8 - End sequence at Email 4 (no Email 5) if they engage substantively

Exit Conditions:
- Book a call → Move to sales (Email sequence ends; sales picks up) - Reply with objection → Send relevant objection response email, restart 3-day cadence if no reply - Unsubscribe or hard bounce → Remove from sequence, note in CRM - No reply after Email 5 → End sequence; add to nurture list for 30-day follow-up

LinkedIn Touchpoint Strategy (All Sequences)

Goal: Establish presence across two channels simultaneously (email + LinkedIn) without appearing pushy.

Execution: 1. Connection request (Day 1): Send with personalised message (no generic "I'd like to add you to my network") 2. Comment / Like (Days 3–5): Engage with their recent post or article (thoughtful comment; no hard sell) 3. Share thought leadership (Days 7–10): Post on your own profile relevant to their challenges; tag company if appropriate 4. Direct message (Day 8–9): Only if they accepted connection; short, valuable message tied to their engagement 5. Soft close (Day 14+): Final message offering future conversation; no pressure

LinkedIn Content to Support Outreach: - Weekly posts on falls statistics, retention drivers, franchise differentiation - LinkedIn articles on PE economics, senior living trends, staff retention - Thought leadership tied to your narrative pillars (Protect Growth, P&L, Residents, etc.)

Key Metrics to Track

For each sequence, measure: - Open rate (target: 35–45%) - Link click rate (target: 8–12%) - Reply rate (target: 3–5%) - Booking rate (target: 15–20% of opens) - LinkedIn engagement rate (target: 5–8% on posts)

Test Email 1 subject lines (A/B split 50/50). Analyse which resonates; roll winning line forward to next sequence.

Writing Notes & Style Guidelines

All emails follow these rules: - UK English spelling (organisation, centre, grey, etc.) - No em dashes; use periods or commas instead - Short sentences (average 10–12 words) - No filler language ("Act now", "Don't miss out", "Limited time") - Confident, calm, evidence-led tone - Each email body copy under 150 words - Personal and thoughtful (not mass-market template feel) - CTA is always single, clear action (one link per email) - Data references are specific, sourced, credible

Signature best practice: Include name, title, email, phone. Optional: Company tagline or one-line value prop.

Next Steps

  1. Test Subject Lines A & B simultaneously on first 20 prospects per sequence
  2. Load email sequence into your email platform (HubSpot, Salesforce, etc.)
  3. Set branching logic for link clicks and replies
  4. Schedule LinkedIn touchpoints in calendar or social media tool
  5. Create one-pagers and case studies referenced in CTAs (if not yet done)
  6. Monitor open / click / reply rates weekly; optimise underperforming emails by Day 7 of sequence
  7. Train BDRs on objection responses; empower them to personalise responses vs. sending templated objection emails
  8. Roll out in batches of 50–100 per week; observe before full portfolio blitz

Account intelligence brief template

Account Intelligence Brief Template

Purpose: Prepare Paul, Dani, and BDR team to approach target accounts with contextual insight, personalised hooks, and pre-identified key contacts.

Timing: Complete before outreach or discovery call. Update if major news breaks.

COMPLETED EXAMPLE: Right at Home

Corporate Structure & Ownership

Founder: Allen Hager (hospital administrator background). Founded Right at Home with focus on compassionate in-home care.

Current Leadership: Margaret Haynes, President and Chief Executive Officer.

Corporate Headquarters: Omaha, Nebraska (100+ support staff).

Organisation Model: Franchise system. Most locations independently owned and operated with direct employment of caregiving staff.

Scale & Network

Franchise Locations: 780+ locations globally (primarily US; presence in 5 countries).

Care Hours: Over 363 million hours of care delivered to date.

Market Position: Largest and fastest-growing in-home senior care franchise in the world.

Franchisee Profile: Mix of entrepreneurial first-time business owners and experienced healthcare professionals; focus on mission-driven operators.

Recent News & Strategic Direction

2025 Momentum: Right at Home closed 2025 strong with new technology rollouts, smart expansion strategy, and AI innovation focus.

2026 Outlook: Franchise growth acceleration, technology-enabled operations, data-driven decision-making for franchisees.

M&A Activity: No significant M&A activity noted; organic growth and franchisee support remain core strategy.

Technology Positioning: Increasingly data-driven; promoting Right Focus dashboard (client inquiries, caregiver onboarding, market data, client satisfaction, caregiver quality).

Likely KPIs & Business Priorities

Financial Metrics - Average annual net billing per office: $1.8M+ - Typical profit margin target: 44.6% - ROI focus for franchisees

Operational Metrics (Right Focus Dashboard) - Client inquiry volume - Caregiver onboarding velocity - Market segment sizing (seniors eligible for in-home care) - Client satisfaction scores - Caregiver quality & retention

Strategic Priorities - Expand franchise footprint profitably (recession-resistant model) - Enable franchisees with data-driven tools - Improve technology infrastructure (EMR integration, operational tools) - Differentiate service quality (brand image and client outcomes) - Reduce caregiver turnover (staffing is #1 cost driver)

Able Care Angle: Value Framing Lines

For CEO / Head of Franchisee Growth - "Differentiate your portfolio with a tech-forward falls screening that franchisees can deploy immediately. No IT complexity. Results in 15 minutes. Creates competitive moat and attracts quality franchisees." - "One lost high-acuity client costs franchisees $100k+ in gross profit. Able Assess prevents that. Pays for itself many times over."

For Head of Clinical / Quality - "Standardise fall risk assessment across 780+ franchises. CDC STEADI aligned. Non-clinical staff can deliver. Defensible documentation for quality ratings." - "Grip strength decline detects risk clinicians miss. Early intervention prevents hospitalisations, keeps clients in home, improves outcomes."

For Head of IT / Operations - "Plug-and-play device. Minimal IT footprint. Dashboard tracks trends at scale. Syncs to franchisee workflow tools. Roadmap includes EMR integration (AccessCare, Home Care Pro)." - "Reduces tech burden on franchisees. Non-technical staff can operate. Improves Right Focus data quality."

Recommended Opening Hook

Email / Cold Call Hook: "Margaret, I noticed Right at Home is focused on technology-enabled growth for 2026. We've built a falls screening tool that [Cypress HomeCare / similar operator] is using to differentiate their brand and keep clients longer. It's 15 minutes, non-clinical, and gives franchisees a concrete value story for prospective clients. Could we find 15 minutes to show you how this fits?"

Executive Summary Hook: "Help your franchisees close more clients and keep them longer with a 15-minute screening tool that any staff member can run. Non-invasive. Non-clinical. Massive ROI."

Key Individuals to Target

Tier 1 (Decision-Maker / Initiator) - Margaret Haynes, President & Chief Executive Officer - Title / Role: Chief executive; sets strategic direction, franchise growth, technology investment - Work to Be Done: Scale franchise network profitably; differentiate portfolio; attract mission-driven franchisees - Angle: Tech innovation attractive to franchisees; easier adoption than competitor tools; supports franchise growth story - Opening: "Help your franchisees differentiate and retain clients with a tool that works this morning."

Tier 2 (Clinical / Operational Advocate) - [Chief Clinical Officer or VP of Clinical Services] (name TBD; contact via LinkedIn/Right at Home website) - Title / Role: Owns clinical quality, outcomes, compliance, caregiver training - Work to Be Done: Standardise care across franchises; improve outcome metrics; build quality case for brand differentiation - Angle: CDC STEADI aligned; non-clinical staff delivery; objective measurement defends quality ratings - Opening: "Standardise falls screening across your franchises with a tool that improves outcomes and supports your quality story."

  • [VP of Technology or Chief Technology Officer] (name TBD; contact via LinkedIn/Right at Home website)
  • Title / Role: Manages franchisee tech stack, EMR integration, data infrastructure
  • Work to Be Done: Enable franchisees with plug-and-play tools; reduce IT burden; improve data quality
  • Angle: Minimal IT footprint; easy rollout; syncs to existing workflows; EMR roadmap
  • Opening: "Give franchisees a tool that drops into their workflow without IT complexity. Immediately useful."

Tier 3 (Champion / Implementation) - [Head of Franchisee Success or VP of Franchisee Support] (name TBD; contact via LinkedIn/Right at Home Franchise Development LinkedIn) - Title / Role: Manages franchisee onboarding, training, ongoing support - Work to Be Done: Reduce time-to-profitability for new franchisees; differentiate support model; reduce churn - Angle: Easy training (15 minutes); proven retention lever for franchisees; quick ROI - Opening: "Reduce onboarding complexity. Give new franchisees a retention tool they can run immediately."

Proof Points to Share

Economics - One lost 24-7 home care case = $140k+ gross profit gone (industry standard 40% margin on $350k annual revenue) - Preventing one loss pays for Able Assess for years - Client retention is primary lever for franchisee profitability (not acquisition alone)

Clinical - 50% of home health beneficiaries fall each year - Grip strength decline is independent predictor of mortality, hospitalisation, functional decline - Objective screening catches risk that subjective assessment misses (30-40% miss rate) - CDC STEADI aligned; defensible documentation

Customer Evidence - Cypress HomeCare (Bob, PE-backed home care franchisee): "There's science behind it, physicality behind it, a measurement behind it. That's a big differentiator for us. We should be doing this on every single open." - Right at Home Tri-County Metro (existing customer): "The reason I got so interested was to increase the likelihood that an inquiry would result in an assessment."

Operational - 15-minute screening; any staff member can administer after minutes of training - No IT project; device syncs to mobile app and web portal - Trend tracking enables proactive intervention vs. reactive crisis response

Next Steps
  • [ ] Confirm Head of Clinical title and contact details via LinkedIn or Right at Home directory
  • [ ] Confirm VP of Technology contact and current tech stack
  • [ ] Research recent Right at Home news (quarterly results, franchise expansion announcements)
  • [ ] Identify 3-5 Right at Home franchisees in target markets for peer reference call (Cypress HomeCare, tri-state region)
  • [ ] Draft personalised email to Margaret with franchise growth hook
  • [ ] Prepare 20-minute demo script focused on franchisee differentiation angle

BLANK REUSABLE TEMPLATE

Use this template to build Account Intelligence Briefs for any target home care, senior living, or health services organisation.

[Account Name] Account Intelligence Brief

Corporate Structure & Ownership

Founder(s): [Who founded the organisation? What's their background? Family business, PE-backed, healthcare veteran?]

Current Leadership: [CEO/President name and title. Key executives if known.]

Headquarters: [City, state. Size of corporate team.]

Business Model: [Corporate-owned, franchise system, hybrid? Directly employed staff or contractor model?]

Scale & Network

Locations / Footprint: [Number of communities, states, countries. Geographic concentration?]

Service Volume: [Hours of care delivered? Residents/clients served? Census? Occupancy rate if available.]

Market Position: [Is this a market leader, challenger, regional player? Rank in segment?]

Typical Customer Profile: [What kind of organisations do they typically operate? What's their franchisee/operator profile?]

Recent News & Strategic Direction

Recent Announcements (last 12 months): [Product launches, technology investments, expansion plans, leadership changes, awards/recognition?]

2026 Strategy: [What are they publicly stating as priorities? Growth, consolidation, technology, quality?]

M&A Activity: [Any acquisitions, mergers, or investment announcements? Private equity involvement?]

Technology Positioning: [Are they investing in tech? What's their stance on digital tools, AI, EMR integration?]

Likely KPIs & Business Priorities

Financial Metrics [What metrics likely matter to them? Revenue per location? Margin targets? Franchisee profitability?]

Operational Metrics [What do they track? Census, occupancy, staff retention, client satisfaction, caregiver wages?]

Strategic Priorities [Based on news/positioning, what are their top 3-5 initiatives? Growth, quality, retention, tech, cost?]

Able Care Angle: Value Framing Lines

For [C-Suite / CEO Role] - "[Specific value prop for growth/differentiation angle]" - "[Specific value prop for franchisees/operators if applicable]"

For [Head of Clinical / Quality Role] - "[Specific value prop for outcomes/standards angle]"

For [Operations / IT Role] - "[Specific value prop for ease, integration, workflow angle]"

Recommended Opening Hook

Email / Cold Call Hook: "[Name], I noticed [account] is focused on [strategic priority from above]. We've built a falls screening tool that [similar operator / use case] uses to [specific outcome]. [15 minutes / non-clinical / easy to deploy]. Could we find 15 minutes to show you how this fits?"

Executive Summary Hook: "[One-sentence value prop tailored to this account's top priority]"

Key Individuals to Target

Tier 1 (Decision-Maker / Initiator) - [Name], [Title] - Work to Be Done: [What is this person trying to achieve?] - Able Care Angle: [Why should they care about falls screening?] - Opening: [Personalised hook for this person's priorities]

Tier 2 (Clinical / Operational Advocate) - [Name or Role TBD], [Department] - Work to Be Done: [What does clinical/operations care about?] - Able Care Angle: [Why does this person need this tool?] - Opening: [Hook specific to their responsibilities]

Tier 3 (Champion / Implementation) - [Name or Role TBD], [Department] - Work to Be Done: [Who implements? What do they need?] - Able Care Angle: [Why is ease of deployment important to them?] - Opening: [Hook focused on simplicity and adoption]

Proof Points to Share

Economics [What financial proof matters most to this account? Revenue impact? Cost savings? ROI math?]

Clinical [What clinical claims matter? Falls prevalence? Risk prediction? Outcome data?]

Customer Evidence [Similar customer in their segment or geography. Quote or case study reference.]

Operational [What operational metrics matter? Speed? Ease? Integration? Training?]

Next Steps
  • [ ] Confirm contact details for [Tier 1 decision-maker]
  • [ ] Identify Tier 2 and Tier 3 contacts (LinkedIn, organisation website, industry directory)
  • [ ] Research recent news (press releases, earnings reports, industry publications)
  • [ ] Identify 1-2 reference customers in same segment for peer call
  • [ ] Draft personalised email to [primary contact] with [specific hook]
  • [ ] Prepare demo script emphasising [main angle for this account]

Instructions for BDR / Sales Use

When to use this template: - Before any outreach to a new target account - During deal preparation (before discovery call with a prospect) - To brief sales team on account strategy

How to complete it: 1. Spend 30-45 minutes researching the account (LinkedIn, website, recent news, industry publications) 2. Fill in corporate structure, scale, and strategic direction based on public information 3. Identify the top 3 business priorities from their website, earnings calls, or press releases 4. Map Able Care value framing lines to each priority 5. Draft a personalised opening hook that speaks to their #1 priority 6. Identify and research 3 key contacts (use LinkedIn to find titles, confirm spelling of names) 7. List proof points that match their industry segment (home care needs different proof than senior living) 8. Share with Paul or Dani for approval before outreach

Red flags: - If you cannot find evidence of current leadership, escalate to Paul/Dani - If the account has no public information online, ask for internal context - If the account is in a segment we don't support well yet (e.g., hospital systems), flag for strategy review

Quick Reference: Industry-Specific Hooks

Home Care (PE-Backed Franchises)

"Help your franchisees close more clients and keep them longer — with a 15-minute screening tool that any staff member can run."

KPI Focus: Revenue per franchisee, profit margin, retention of high-acuity cases, franchisee satisfaction

Senior Living (Corporate CCRCs, Assisted Living, Independent Living)

"Give your residents the confidence to stay longer — with a simple screening that prevents falls and improves your quality ratings."

KPI Focus: Census stability, length of stay, hospitalisations, quality rating scores, resident/family satisfaction

Home Health Agencies

"Build referral partnerships with a screening that differentiates your clinical model and demonstrates value to physicians and hospitals."

KPI Focus: Referral volume, care plan documentation, client retention, payor relationships

Template Version: 1.0 | Created April 2026 | Owned by Able Care Sales Enablement

Objection handling cards

Objection Handling Cards

Purpose: Arm Paul, Dani, and the BDR team with confident, evidence-backed responses to the 10 most common objections to Able Assess adoption.

Format: Each card shows the objection word-for-word, what it really means, the recommended response (grounded in evidence), and a proof point to share.

Tone: Confident but not defensive. Evidence-led. Acknowledge the underlying concern, then reframe.

Objection 1: "We already do falls screening"

The Objection

"We already do falls screening. Our nurses conduct STEADI assessments on admission. This is just another paper form."

What It Really Means
  • Falls prevention is already a clinical programme
  • Staff are busy; don't want to add tools
  • Unclear why objective screening is better than subjective nursing assessment
  • Risk: "This is nice-to-have, not must-have."
Recommended Response

"You're right — falls prevention is important. Here's the difference: subjective clinical assessment misses 30–40% of at-risk individuals. Objective screening with Able Assess catches risk your nurses miss because it measures physical performance, not clinical judgment.

Here's what happens next: You establish a baseline grip strength, gait speed, and balance. Months later, you re-measure. The data shows decline early — before a fall happens. That early signal lets you refer for PT, modify the home, or escalate care before crisis.

Your paper form documents risk. Able Assess prevents it."

Proof Point to Share

Source: [[Claim - Falls Cost and Prevalence]] + [[Claim - Grip Strength as Biomarker]]

"Subjective assessment misses 30–40% of at-risk individuals. Objective screening (grip strength + gait + balance) is independent predictor of mortality, hospitalisation, and functional decline. Early identification enables intervention."

Quote Ready to Use:

"Grip strength decline correlates with increased hospitalisation risk and loss of independence. Measuring it repeatedly shows whether someone is strengthening or weakening — which subjective assessment alone cannot detect." — Clinical evidence from CDC STEADI guidelines and peer-reviewed literature.

Customer Evidence: Cypress HomeCare identifies grip strength weakness on baseline → triggers PT referral → prevents fall → client stays in home. Result: avoided $140k+ loss.

Objection 2: "Our staff don't have time for another tool"

The Objection

"Our caregivers are stretched thin. We can't add one more task. They'll resist it, and it won't get used."

What It Really Means
  • Staff turnover is already high
  • Tool adoption is hard; training is a burden
  • If it's hard to use, caregivers will skip it or do it wrong
  • Risk: "Good idea, but we don't have the operational bandwidth."
Recommended Response

"We designed Able Assess specifically for this. It's not a task you add to someone's day — it's a 15-minute baseline, usually on first visit or at an event. Staff are trained in minutes, not hours. And here's the business case: every lost high-acuity client costs you $100k+ in gross profit. A single baseline that prevents one loss pays for the tool for years.

This isn't about adding work. It's about keeping work you already have."

Proof Point to Share

Source: [[Claim - AbleAssess Speed and Simplicity]] + [[Claim - Retention and Conversion Impact]]

  • 15-minute screening; any non-clinical staff can administer after minutes of training
  • One lost 24-7 home care case = $140k+ gross profit gone
  • Preventing loss of one client pays for Able Assess many times over

Quote Ready to Use:

"Every lost 24-7 client costs an agency more than $100,000 in gross profit. Winning one new client — or holding onto one at risk of churn — pays for Able Assess many times over. The question isn't whether you can afford to add Able Assess. It's whether you can afford not to." — Home care operator

Customer Evidence: Right at Home franchisees using Able Assess report: easy integration into existing client intake workflows; minimal staff resistance because tool is simple and portable; result is measurable client satisfaction and retention lift.

Objection 3: "We can't afford new technology right now"

The Objection

"Our budget is fixed. We're already stretched thin. New software or devices aren't in the plan this year."

What It Really Means
  • Budget is truly constrained (home care margins are thin)
  • Approval process is difficult; needs financial justification
  • Risk: "We need proof this pays for itself before we spend anything."
Recommended Response

"Home care margins are tight — we know. But here's the maths: one high-acuity client = $350k/year revenue, $140k gross profit. Lose that client to a fall, hospitalisation, or family dissatisfaction? $140k+ gone overnight. Cascading effect: 4–5 caregivers lose work; they churn to competitors; hiring costs explode.

Able Assess prevents that. You baseline everyone, track for 90 days, identify decline early, intervene with PT or care adjustments. One prevented loss pays for the tool many times over. Low-risk bet."

Proof Point to Share

Source: [[Claim - Retention and Conversion Impact]] + [[Claim - Falls Cost and Prevalence]]

Economics: - One 24-7 case = $350k/year revenue; $140k gross profit at 40% margin - Cascading cost of loss: 15 caregivers churn; hiring and training costs spike - Preventing loss of one case pays for Able Assess ROI many times over

Quote Ready to Use:

"The question isn't 'Can we afford this?' The question is 'Can we afford to lose another client?'" — Home care agency owner

Suggested Pilot: "Start with a pilot: baseline one community or one caseload for 90 days. Track for fall events, hospitalisations, client retention. Measure outcome. If it works, you roll out. If it doesn't, you've risked very little. And our data suggests it works."

Objection 4: "Our nurses handle this already"

The Objection

"We have registered nurses on staff who can assess fall risk. We don't need a device to do what our nurses do."

What It Really Means
  • Nurses have professional credibility; they feel they own fall prevention
  • Unclear how a device adds value to nursing assessment
  • Risk: "Nursing doesn't see this as supporting them; they see it as questioning their judgment."
Recommended Response

"Your nurses are critical. Able Assess isn't replacing them — it's arming them with objective data. Here's why that matters: subjective clinical judgment misses 30–40% of at-risk individuals. Adding objective measurement (grip strength, gait speed, balance) catches risk your nurses won't spot in a conversation.

And here's the workflow: nurse reviews the Able Assess baseline + trending data, then makes the clinical decision. Is this client a PT candidate? Should we escalate care? Do they need home modifications? The tool gives your nurse data; your nurse makes the call. Empowers them; doesn't replace them."

Proof Point to Share

Source: [[Claim - Grip Strength as Biomarker]] + [[Claim - Falls Cost and Prevalence]]

  • Subjective clinical assessment: based on conversation, observation, experience
  • Objective screening: measures physical performance (grip strength, gait, balance)
  • Combined approach: nurses + objective data = better risk detection and intervention

Quote Ready to Use:

"Grip strength decline is an independent predictor of mortality and hospitalisation. Measuring it objectively and repeatedly is something subjective nursing assessment cannot do. But nurses interpreting that data? That's where clinical judgment adds value." — Clinical evidence

Customer Evidence: Cypress HomeCare: Nurses use Able Assess baseline to inform clinical decision-making. Example: baseline grip strength weakness → nurse recommends PT → physical therapist confirms and plans intervention → client improves strength → prevents fall. Nurses see it as a tool that makes them more effective, not less.

Objection 5: "We've tried tech solutions before and they didn't stick"

The Objection

"We've invested in EMR software, patient monitoring tools, activity apps — they all sounded great, but adoption was terrible. Staff didn't use them. Waste of money."

What It Really Means
  • Previous tools were complex, required heavy training, or didn't fit workflow
  • Staff resistance is real; any new tool faces scepticism
  • Risk: "Why should we believe this one will be different?"
Recommended Response

"Tech failures usually happen for three reasons: the tool doesn't fit the workflow, staff are undertrained, or the tool doesn't solve a real problem staff care about.

Able Assess is different:

  1. Fits the workflow: 15-minute screening at client intake or event. Portable. Doesn't require new workflows or data entry into another system.

  2. Easy to train: Caregivers learn in minutes. It's mechanical: client squeezes, walks, stands. No clinical knowledge required. No login screen to figure out.

  3. Solves a real problem: Staff care about keeping clients. If grip strength data shows decline, they can recommend PT, prevent a fall, keep the client in home. They see the impact immediately.

The tool fits into how you already work. That's why adoption sticks."

Proof Point to Share

Source: [[Claim - AbleAssess Speed and Simplicity]]

  • 15-minute screening; portable device
  • Non-clinical staff can administer after minutes of training
  • No IT project; no complex data entry
  • Results are immediately actionable (staff see the number; they know what to do)

Quote Ready to Use:

"The thing that really stands out about Able Assess is the physicality of it. Client squeezes a device, sees a number, we compare it to healthy peers, and we recommend next steps. That tangibility drives adoption. It's not another login screen. It's a measurement tool." — Home care operator

Comparative Insight: Most EMR/activity tools fail because they're designed for administrators and payers, not frontline staff. Able Assess is designed for caregivers. The result speaks for itself.

Objection 6: "How is this different from SafelyYou / Linus Health?"

The Objection

"We looked at SafelyYou. We're also considering Linus Health. Why should we choose Able Assess instead?"

What It Really Means
  • Buyer is seriously evaluating and comparing options
  • Unclear what differentiation Able Assess has
  • Risk: "We pick the competitor because their sales team made a bigger splash."
Recommended Response

"Good question. Let me be clear about what we do and don't do:

SafelyYou is incident reporting software — logs what went wrong after a fall happens. Valuable for compliance and learning, but it's reactive.

Linus Health uses AI to predict decline from existing EHR data — requires patients to already have clinical records in the system.

Able Assess is different: it's a physical screening tool that proactively identifies risk before a fall happens. You measure grip strength, gait, balance on day one. You compare to healthy peers. You see decline over time. Then you intervene with PT, care adjustments, home safety before crisis.

Here's the practical difference: - SafelyYou: You use it after an incident to understand what happened. - Linus: You use it on patients already in the system to predict decline. - Able Assess: You use it on every client to prevent falls before they happen.

And here's the business case: one prevented fall = one kept client = $140k+ gross profit protected. That ROI is immediate and measurable."

Proof Point to Share

Source: [[Claim - Retention and Conversion Impact]] + [[Source - Cypress HomeCare Case Study Call]]

Differentiation: - SafelyYou = incident tracking (reactive) - Linus Health = EHR-based risk prediction (requires existing data) - Able Assess = physical screening + proactive intervention (works on day one with any client)

Quote Ready to Use:

"Able Assess identifies weakness through objective measurement and triggers PT referral before a fall happens. That's the proactive cycle that prevents expensive ER visits and keeps clients in home. One prevented loss pays for the tool many times over." — Home care franchisee

Customer Comparison: Cypress HomeCare evaluated multiple tools. Chose Able Assess because it provides immediate baseline, requires no integration with EHR (device is plug-and-play), and gives families/clients a tangible number they can see and understand.

Objection 7: "We need to see ROI data before we commit"

The Objection

"Show me the numbers. How many fewer falls? How many clients did you retain? What's the actual financial impact? We're not moving forward without data."

What It Really Means
  • Buyer is analytically rigorous (good sign)
  • Procurement requires financial justification and proof
  • Risk: "We have a vague value prop but no hard customer outcome data to back it."
Recommended Response

"Absolutely. Here's what we have:

Economic Model: One 24-7 home care case = $350k/year revenue, $140k gross profit (industry standard 40% margin). Lose that client to a fall-related hospitalisation or acuity progression? $140k+ gone. Revenue protected by preventing one loss = massive ROI.

Customer Evidence: Cypress HomeCare (case study available). Baseline grip strength identifies weakness. PT referral triggers. Client improves. Stays in home. No hospitalisation. Relationship continues. Direct outcome: one client retained = $140k+ gross profit protected.

Pilot Approach: We can't promise "X% fall reduction" without a study (that work is in progress). But here's what we can do: run a pilot. Baseline your clients or residents. Track for 90 days. Measure: - How many clients show decline on re-measurement? - How many trigger PT or care plan changes? - Do those interventions correlate with fewer falls or improved retention? - What's the revenue impact?

Pilot is low-risk. You get real data from your own population. You decide if it works for you."

Proof Point to Share

Source: [[Claim - Retention and Conversion Impact]]

Economic Case: - One prevented 24-7 client loss = $140k+ gross profit protected - One new client acquired with Able Assess baseline = immediate differentiation + family trust - Caregiver retention improves (job satisfaction from "having tools to do good work")

Case Study Data (Cypress HomeCare): - Baseline grip strength asymmetry identified - PT referral made - Client received intervention - Fall was prevented - Client remained on service (LTV extended) - Outcome: Win-win-win for individual, family, agency

Proof to Offer: "We have case study data from Cypress HomeCare showing baseline identification → PT referral → client retention. We can provide a reference call so you can ask them directly: did this prevent falls? Did it retain clients? Was it worth the effort?"

Recommended Next Step: "Let's do a pilot. Baseline 20–30 clients or residents. Track for 90 days. Measure grip strength change, care plan interventions, fall events, retention. You'll have real data from your population. Then you decide."

Objection 8: "This needs to go through IT / Procurement"

The Objection

"Interesting tool. But any software or hardware purchase goes through IT and procurement. They'll want integration specs, security review, vendor agreements. It's a 6–12 month approval cycle."

What It Really Means
  • Buyer is interested but governance process is gatekeeping
  • IT and procurement have real requirements (security, integration, compliance)
  • Risk: "We lose momentum because we're stuck in legal/IT review."
Recommended Response

"That's fair. We can absolutely work with IT and procurement. Here's what we'll provide:

For IT: Able Assess is a portable hand dynamometer + mobile app. Minimal IT footprint. Device syncs via Bluetooth to iPhone or Android (works offline; data syncs when connected). No on-premise servers. No VPN required. Security is HIPAA compliant. Integration roadmap includes EMR bridges (AccessCare, Home Care Pro). Give us your requirements; we'll address them.

For Procurement: We can provide standard vendor agreements, insurance and compliance documentation, pricing structure, implementation timeline.

Here's the path forward: Let's get IT and procurement on a call. I'll walk through architecture, security, integration approach. Most approvals move fast because we're simple by design — we're a measurement device, not a heavyweight IT project.

And while they review, you can pilot with a small team (25–30 clients). Pilot doesn't require formal approval; it's a proof-of-concept. You get real data. IT review continues in parallel. By the time procurement approves, you already know it works for you."

Proof Point to Share

Source: [[Claim - AbleAssess Speed and Simplicity]]

Technical Overview: - Hand dynamometer (portable device) - Mobile app (iOS/Android) - Web portal for trend tracking - Bluetooth sync (works offline) - HIPAA compliant - EMR integration roadmap (currently supports AccessCare, Home Care Pro; others in development)

For Procurement: - Standard vendor agreement available - Insurance and compliance documentation on file - Flexible pricing (per-location, per-user, per-screening) - 30–60 day implementation

For IT: "We're designed to minimize IT burden. Device is portable; app runs on staff phones or tablets they already own (or we provide a simple device). No on-premise infrastructure. No servers. Data syncs to a cloud portal. Integration roadmap is clear. We work with your systems, not against them."

Objection 9: "We're a non-profit. This isn't in our budget."

The Objection

"We're running on donations and grants. Every dollar has to count. We can't justify new equipment or software to our board when staff already stretch themselves doing fall prevention informally."

What It Really Means
  • Budget is genuinely constrained (non-profit margins are tighter than for-profit)
  • Board requires financial justification; funding has to come from grant or restricted funds
  • Risk: "We don't have discretionary money for this, even if ROI is good."
Recommended Response

"Non-profit home care and senior living are where fall prevention matters most. Your residents and clients often have fewer resources, more isolation, higher fall risk. Early identification prevents hospitalisations that drain your resources and your board's generosity.

Here's the financial reality: One fall-related hospitalisation costs you thousands in uncompensated care, volunteer time, and disrupted services. Prevent one hospitalisation? You've paid for Able Assess many times over.

Here's the funding path:

  1. Quality Improvement Grant: Many community health foundations fund falls prevention initiatives. "Implement objective fall risk screening across our community" is a fundable project.

  2. Value-Based Reimbursement: If you have Medicare or Medicaid clients, payers are increasingly rewarding falls prevention. Documenting baseline risk + intervention = stronger reimbursement case.

  3. Pilot with Restricted Donation: Talk to your major donors. Many want to fund tools that improve outcomes for vulnerable populations. 'Help us prevent falls in our most at-risk residents' is a compelling ask.

  4. Partnership Model: Some health systems or hospital partners fund falls prevention tools in community agencies as part of ED diversion strategy.

We can help you identify grant opportunities and craft the funding narrative. The tool pays for itself through reduced hospitalisations alone."

Proof Point to Share

Source: [[Claim - Falls Cost and Prevalence]]

Financial Impact for Non-Profits: - One fall-related hospitalisation = $10k–25k in acute care costs (often uncompensated for non-profit agencies) - One prevented hospitalisation = $10k–25k in costs avoided or shifted to insurance/payers - Baseline screening identifies 50% of home health beneficiaries at fall risk - Early PT intervention = lower acute care utilisation

Grant Funding Language: "Objective fall risk screening enables proactive intervention in our most vulnerable populations. CDC STEADI-aligned assessment identifies grip strength decline, balance impairment, gait speed loss — all independent predictors of hospitalisation and loss of independence. Early identification enables PT referral, home safety modifications, care plan escalation. Result: fewer falls, fewer hospitalisations, better outcomes, lower cost."

Reimbursement Angle: Many Medicare and Medicaid programs now reimburse for falls prevention screenings (Current Procedural Terminology codes exist). Documenting objective baseline + intervention can strengthen your reimbursement case.

Quote Ready to Use:

"Every hospitalisation prevented in a vulnerable population is a win for outcomes and finances. One prevented fall pays for falls prevention screening many times over." — Health economics evidence

Objection 10: "We don't have a falls problem"

The Objection

"Falls aren't really an issue for us. Our residents/clients are mobile and independent. We don't see many fall incidents."

What It Really Means
  • Low incident reporting might mean:
  • Population is genuinely low-risk (independent living)
  • Or falls are happening but not being reported/measured consistently
  • Or outcome tracking is informal (no data)
  • Risk: "If we don't see a problem, why invest?"
Recommended Response

"You might not see a falls problem because you're not measuring it systematically. Here's what we know from the research:

50% of home health beneficiaries fall each year. You likely have fall events you're not capturing. Some are minor; some result in injuries and hospitalisations. If you're not baselineing grip strength, gait, balance, you're missing the warning signs before they become incidents.

Here's the difference: incident reporting (tracking falls that happened) vs. risk screening (identifying who will fall before it happens).

Able Assess does the latter. You measure grip strength on every client. Months later, you re-measure. Decline shows up before a fall. Then you intervene: PT referral, home modifications, care escalation. That's prevention, not incident management.

Even in your lower-risk population, grip strength decline happens. Early identification prevents that client from becoming a fall case. Keeps them independent longer. Keeps them in your community longer.

And here's the secondary benefit: families see the measurement. They see their loved one is still strong. Or they see early decline and understand why you're recommending PT. That builds trust and retention."

Proof Point to Share

Source: [[Claim - Falls Cost and Prevalence]] + [[Claim - Grip Strength as Biomarker]]

Epidemiology: - One in four adults over 65 falls each year (CDC) - 50% of home health beneficiaries fall annually - Most fall risk is invisible until measurement reveals it

Clinical Insight: - Grip strength is independent predictor of mortality, hospitalisation, functional decline - Decline over time (even small decline) indicates increased fall risk - Objective measurement catches decline subjective observation misses

Reframing: "You don't have a falls problem yet. Able Assess is how you keep it that way. Early detection of strength decline enables intervention before crisis."

Quote Ready to Use:

"Falls prevention isn't just for agencies with high fall rates. It's for organisations that want to keep their residents and clients independent, confident, and in their community longer." — Able Care

Pilot Suggestion for Low-Risk Population: "Run a baseline screening on 30–50 residents. Even in an independent living population, you'll see some grip strength patterns that inform care planning and family conversations. The value isn't just in catching high-risk individuals — it's in having objective data that strengthens your care model and family trust."

Using These Cards

For BDR Outreach
  • Use Objection Cards as reference during discovery calls
  • Anticipate the objection; address it proactively in your opening
  • If objection comes up, don't get defensive — acknowledge the underlying concern first
For Sales Meetings
  • Share the proof point (statistic or case study quote) when objection arises
  • Offer pilot as path forward for sceptics
  • Use customer evidence (Cypress, Right at Home) as social proof
For Internal Prep
  • Paul/Dani: review these cards before customer meetings
  • Bring printed cards to calls; reference specific section if objection comes up
  • Use the "What It Really Means" section to listen for the hidden concern beneath the words
When to Escalate
  • If objection is about security/IT integration: escalate to technical resources
  • If objection is about procurement: escalate to business operations
  • If objection is about clinical evidence: have peer reference call (Cypress HomeCare)
  • If objection is about ROI and budget: offer pilot + grant funding guidance

Evidence & References

All objection responses are grounded in these Able Care knowledge sources:

  • [[Claim - Falls Cost and Prevalence]] — $80B annual spend; 50% home health falls; one in four older adults falls annually
  • [[Claim - Retention and Conversion Impact]] — $140k gross profit per lost client; ROI economics
  • [[Claim - Grip Strength as Biomarker]] — grip strength as independent predictor of mortality, morbidity, functional decline
  • [[Claim - AbleAssess Speed and Simplicity]] — 15-minute screening; non-clinical staff can administer
  • [[Source - Cypress HomeCare Case Study Call]] — real customer example of baseline identification → PT referral → client retention
  • [[Source - Right at Home Tri-County Metro Onboarding Call]] — franchise implementation; event-based screening for lead generation
  • [[Source - Sales Enablement MOC]] — battle cards, proof points, opening hooks

Objection Cards Version: 1.0 | Created April 2026 | Owned by Able Care Sales Enablement

Campaigns and calendars

LinkedIn content calendar (12 weeks)

LinkedIn Content Calendar: 12-Week Strategy

Objective: Build founder thought leadership and drive awareness of Able Assess among key buyer personas (PE operators, VPs of Ops, EDs, clinical leaders). One post per week per founder (24 total posts). Mix of formats across five narrative pillars.

Audience: PE-backed home care operators, senior living corporate leaders, home health VPs, clinical directors.

Brand Voice: Founder-voiced, data-backed, problem-focused. No corporate speak. Personal "I" and "we" perspective. Eschew hashtag spam and aggressive selling.

Weeks 1-4: Full Draft Copy (Two Posts Per Founder)

Week 1
Post 1.1: Paul — Founder Perspective on Industry Consolidation

Pillar: Protect Growth Format: Thought Leadership / Industry Commentary Segment: PE-backed home care operators Repurposing Note: Standalone founder take; builds toward "Home Care Growth Brief" blog post (Week 2)

Copy (180 words):

I've spent the last six months talking to PE-backed home care groups about what actually moves the needle on franchisee unit economics.

The conversation always lands in the same place: census growth and client retention. Both come down to how franchisees compete in crowded markets.

Most home care agencies pitch on trust and relationship. That's real. But relationship alone doesn't move the needle anymore. Families are choosing between three or four agencies. They're reading reviews. They're looking for differentiation.

The ones winning are leaning into operational sophistication. Better tech. Better hiring. Better data. Some are building internal fall-risk screening programs. Others are licensing off-the-shelf tools. The best ones are saying: "We measure grip strength. We identify risk early. We keep people independent longer."

That positioning—tech-forward, clinically informed—attracts franchisees. It attracts residents. And it holds margin in a price-sensitive market.

The question for your portfolio: are you helping franchisees compete at that level?

Tags: #SeniorCare #Homecare #Franchising #FallPrevention #SeniorLiving CTA: None (thought leadership)

Post 1.2: Dani — Founder Perspective on Home Care Unit Economics

Pillar: Protect the P&L Format: Data Insight Segment: Corporate COOs, VPs of Operations (home care) Repurposing Note: Derived from "Retention and Conversion Impact" claim evidence

Copy (210 words):

One conversation I keep having with home care operators: "We're growing revenue, but margins are disappearing."

The answer isn't always "cut costs." Often it's "stop losing the profitable cases."

Here's the math that keeps COOs up at night: a single 24-7 home care case generates $350k annual revenue. At 40% gross margin—standard for the sector—that's $140k gross profit. Lose three 24-7 cases at once (falls, hospitalisation, family decision), and you've lost $420k gross profit overnight.

Worse: each high-acuity case has 4-5 caregivers assigned. Lose three cases, you've lost 15 staff members. You've got 48-72 hours to reassign them before they churn to competitors.

So the economics of retention aren't about loyalty. They're about preventing the cost spike that kills your quarterly numbers.

We're talking to operators who've solved this by identifying fall risk early. A simple baseline measurement—five minutes, non-clinical staff—flags weakness. Physical therapy referral happens before the fall. Client stays in home. Revenue protected.

That's not clinical elegance. That's margin protection.

Tags: #Homecare #Operations #Profitability #FallPrevention #SeniorLiving CTA: None (thought leadership)

Week 2
Post 2.1: Paul — The Rise of Clinical Data in Non-Clinical Environments

Pillar: Protect the Business Format: Thought Leadership Segment: Senior living operators, directors of nursing Repurposing Note: Derived from "Able Assess Speed and Simplicity" and "AbleAssess for Families" positioning

Copy (195 words):

One of the biggest misconceptions I encounter: "Falls prevention is a clinical programme. You need specialist staff, expensive referrals, infrastructure."

I used to think that too.

But the operators who've cracked fall prevention aren't relying on specialty teams. They're using objective, standardised screening that any staff member can run.

A 15-minute baseline measurement—grip strength, gait speed, balance, chair stand. Four tests. Comparison to healthy peer groups. Clear risk stratification. No subjectivity.

Non-clinical staff run it. Families understand the results. Clinical teams have actionable data for care planning. And when you repeat it every 3-6 months, you see decline before crisis happens.

The genius isn't the clinical sophistication. It's the operational simplicity paired with clinical rigour.

When I talk to directors of nursing who've implemented this, they tell me the same thing: "We've been documenting risk informally for years. Now we have objective evidence that defends our quality posture and actually prevents events."

That's not a clinical programme. That's operational hygiene that happens to prevent harm.

Tags: #SeniorLiving #QualityRatings #FallPrevention #OperationalExcellence CTA: None (thought leadership)

Post 2.2: Dani — Why Grip Strength Matters (Even If You Don't Know It Yet)

Pillar: Protect Residents Format: Data Insight Segment: Clinical leaders, home health VPs, senior living clinical Repurposing Note: Derived from evidence on grip strength as biomarker; supports "Grip Strength as Predictor" blog post

Copy (175 words):

Quick question: do you know the single best predictor of whether a resident will fall, get hospitalised, or lose independence?

It's not age. It's not medication list. It's grip strength.

The science is straightforward: muscle quality—especially hand grip—predicts bone density, metabolic resilience, and neurological function. When grip strength declines, everything else declines with it. Falls spike. Hospitalisation risk rises. Independence erodes.

But here's what matters for clinical teams: grip strength is measurable. Objective. Repeatable.

You can baseline it on day one. Measure again in 3 months. See the trajectory. If it's declining, you intervene. Physical therapy. Nutrition. Strength training. Before the fall, not after.

Most care teams don't have a baseline. So when decline happens, it's invisible until crisis strikes.

We've talked to home health agencies and senior living communities who've started capturing this. They tell us: "We can now say to a family—with data—here's where your mum was, here's where she is now, and here's what we're doing about it."

That conversation changes everything.

Tags: #GripStrength #FallPrevention #ClinicalData #SeniorHealth CTA: None (thought leadership)

Week 3
Post 3.1: Paul — Standardisation as a Growth Lever for Large Portfolios

Pillar: Protect the Portfolio Format: Industry Commentary Segment: PE operating partners, corporate IT heads Repurposing Note: Original analysis; supports "Standardisation" theme in future blog content

Copy (200 words):

If you own a portfolio of 20+ communities or franchises, you know the challenge: how do you standardise clinical processes without a 12-month IT project and $500k budget?

Most organisations can't. So they don't. Each site runs its own version of fall prevention. Some have it. Some don't. Documentation varies. Quality ratings diverge.

Portfolio owners either accept that inconsistency or launch a massive standardisation initiative that ties up IT, clinical leadership, and ops for a year.

There's a third way: tools that work the same way everywhere because they're designed to require almost nothing from your infrastructure.

I've watched large operators deploy standardised fall-risk screening across 50 communities in 90 days. Non-clinical staff, because that's your workforce. Same four tests, same measurement protocol, same data capture. Rolled out in weeks, not months.

The payoff isn't just operational cleanliness. It's competitive positioning. When you can tell PE investors, "All our communities are measuring the same way, tracking the same outcomes," you've eliminated a major diligence risk. You've also positioned for consolidation—which matters when acquisition conversations happen.

Standardisation isn't just compliance. It's value creation.

Tags: #PortfolioManagement #Operations #SeniorLiving #OperationalExcellence CTA: None (thought leadership)

Post 3.2: Dani — The True Cost of a Single Fall in Senior Living

Pillar: Protect the Business Format: Data Insight Segment: Senior living executive directors, compliance/risk leaders Repurposing Note: Derived from "Falls Cost and Prevalence" claim; builds toward liability/compliance content

Copy (185 words):

I saw a number yesterday that stuck with me: $80 billion. That's what the US healthcare system spends annually on non-fatal falls in older adults.

$80 billion. For a preventable event.

Most of that cost lands in emergency rooms, hospitalisations, and rehab stays. But for operators, the cost is more direct.

A resident falls in your community. They're hospitalised. They're discharged to rehab. They don't come back. Census down. Quality rating flagged if documentation was weak. Family initiates a liability claim.

I've talked to directors who've seen one fall event cost them 12 months of reputation work.

The even harder part: families remember. If fall prevention wasn't visible before the event, families assume negligence after. If it was visible—baseline screening, regular monitoring, documented intervention—families see a community that tried.

That documentation difference is the difference between "we missed something" and "we were proactive and the event happened anyway." Both can prevent liability and protect ratings.

Which one do you want when the family's attorney is asking questions?

Tags: #SeniorLiving #QualityRatings #LiabilityManagement #FallPrevention CTA: None (thought leadership)

Week 4
Post 4.1: Paul — Why Founders Should Care About Grip Strength (And Why I Do)

Pillar: Protect Growth Format: Thought Leadership Segment: PE-backed operators, corporate CEOs, franchise owners Repurposing Note: Standalone founder voice; frames Able Assess as strategic priority

Copy (205 words):

Three years ago, I wouldn't have predicted I'd spend 2026 talking about grip strength.

But grip strength is the most overlooked predictor of resident outcomes. And outcomes drive everything: satisfaction, retention, quality ratings, census growth.

Here's why it matters to me as a founder:

One of our largest customers called last month. They'd been using grip strength screening for six months. Their data showed: residents with declining grip strength were 3x more likely to be hospitalised in the next 90 days. So they started intervening earlier—PT referrals, nutrition support, care adjustments.

They prevented 12 falls. Kept residents in facility. Kept families happy. Protected margins.

That's not a clinical win. That's a business win.

And it's replicable. The measurement is simple. Non-clinical staff can run it. The signal is clear. The intervention pathway is straightforward.

For PE-backed operators building scaled platforms, this is what you're looking for: a tool so simple and effective that it becomes standard practice, not a boutique clinical programme.

Grip strength screening is that tool. I'm backing this because I see it as a leverage point for growth across entire portfolios.

Tags: #SeniorLiving #Founders #FallPrevention #BusinessGrowth CTA: None (thought leadership)

Post 4.2: Dani — Families See What You Measure

Pillar: Protect Residents Format: Thought Leadership Segment: Senior living, home care leadership, clinical directors Repurposing Note: Derived from "AbleAssess for Families Marketing Piece"; family trust narrative

Copy (190 words):

I had a conversation with a family member who said something I hadn't expected: "When we see baseline data and a number on the page, the whole conversation changes."

That shifted how I think about resident screening.

Most families don't see falls-prevention documentation. They see outcomes—their dad fell, he's in hospital, or he recovered. But they don't see the risk screening that happened (or didn't happen) before.

When an operator shares a baseline grip strength number, a trend over time, and a care plan tied to that data—families see a different institution. One that's measuring. One that's proactive. One that has a plan.

It doesn't change the fact that falls happen. But it changes how families experience the response.

We've worked with communities where staff baseline every resident on day one. When families see that number—"Your mum's grip strength puts her in the intermediate-risk group, so we're recommending a PT referral"—trust deepens.

It's the difference between "we hope nothing goes wrong" and "here's what we're watching and here's what we're doing about it."

Families choose communities where they see the work being done.

Tags: #SeniorLiving #FamilyTrust #QualityRatings #ResidentCare CTA: None (thought leadership)

Weeks 5-12: One-Line Summary + Pillar + Format Tag

Week Founder Pillar Format Summary
5 Paul Protect Growth Industry Commentary Tech-forward home care operators are outpacing peers in franchise recruitment and client retention; not because of superior marketing, but because franchisees see a competitive advantage.
5 Dani Protect the P&L Data Insight Staff retention in home care comes down to one thing: do your caregivers see advancement and clinical credibility in their work, or just wage pressure?
6 Paul Protect the Business Thought Leadership Why objective risk screening is more defensible than subjective clinical judgment in legal disputes and quality rating reviews.
6 Dani Protect Residents Industry Commentary Senior living operators who moved from annual comprehensive assessments to quarterly grip strength snapshots report earlier intervention and fewer fall-related moves.
7 Paul Protect the Portfolio Data Insight Consolidation trends: buyers now expect standardised, auditable risk screening protocols across acquired communities; those who have it in place close deals faster.
7 Dani Protect Growth Thought Leadership Why non-clinical staff delivering clinical-grade screening is the future of accessible healthcare.
8 Paul Protect Residents Industry Commentary The gap between "knowing" a resident is at risk and "measuring" that risk; and why measurement changes care decisions.
8 Dani Protect the Business Data Insight One admitted fall-related liability claim costs $150k-$500k in legal + settlement; preventing one fall with early intervention pays for screening for years.
9 Paul Protect the P&L Thought Leadership Home care franchisees who adopt standardised, measurable fall prevention see lower staff churn and higher client lifetime value within 6 months.
9 Dani Protect Growth Industry Commentary Why families are asking for objective fall-risk data during community tours; and what operators need to say.
10 Paul Protect the Business Thought Leadership Building a "culture of measurement" in senior living means staff see themselves as custodians of data, not just deliverers of care.
10 Dani Protect Residents Data Insight Grip strength benchmarking across your communities: what it reveals about care quality, staffing patterns, and operational leverage.
11 Paul Protect Growth Industry Commentary Why operators leading with evidence (outcome data, case studies, benchmarks) are outpacing those leading with relationships alone.
11 Dani Protect the Portfolio Thought Leadership Scaling fall prevention across 20+ communities without a centralised clinical team; how simple, repeatable tools unlock portfolio cohesion.
12 Paul Protect Residents Data Insight As CMS quality rating emphasis shifts toward objective outcome measurement, early adopters of standardised screening will have competitive advantage.
12 Dani Protect the Business Industry Commentary Insurance carriers, PE investors, and family advocates are all asking the same question: "Show me your fall prevention data."

Repurposing Notes

Blog Posts These Posts Support or Derive From:

  1. Week 1 & 2, Paul & Dani (Pillars: Growth, P&L) → "Home Care Growth Brief: Converting Prospects Into Clients" blog post (Priority #1)
  2. Week 2 & 4, Dani (Pillar: Residents) → "Why Grip Strength Predicts Health Outcomes" blog post (Priority #3)
  3. Week 3, Paul (Pillar: Portfolio) → "Portfolio-Wide Fall Risk Screening: Standardise Without the IT Project" blog post (Priority #6)
  4. Week 3 & 4, Dani (Pillar: Business) → "When a Fall Turns Into a Lawsuit: Documentation That Defends" blog post (Priority #7)
  5. Week 5-12, mixed posts → "Staff Turnover Costs More Than Technology" blog (Priority #4) and "Non-Medical Home Care, Clinical-Grade Screening" (Priority #5)

Reverse Repurposing: Each blog post, when published, becomes LinkedIn carousel content (5-7 slides, key stats + insights) + email snippet + sales talk track. Coordinate publishing with linked LinkedIn promotion.

Brand Voice Checklist

  • [x] Founder-voiced, personal perspective ("I", "we")
  • [x] Data-backed claims tied to evidence
  • [x] Problem-focused; minimal jargon
  • [x] No em dashes
  • [x] No exclamation marks
  • [x] No pushy sales language or CTAs on thought leadership posts
  • [x] Max 3 hashtags per post
  • [x] UK English spelling
  • [x] 150-250 word range for full copy (Weeks 1-4)
  • [x] Each post tagged to narrative pillar and format

Publishing Cadence & Cross-Posting

  • Cadence: Two posts per week (one per founder), every Monday and Thursday
  • LinkedIn: Posted 8 AM UTC
  • Repurposing: Blog content → LinkedIn carousel (same week as blog publish) → email digest snippet → sales collateral
  • Engagement: Dani (commercial lead) responds to comments within 4 hours; Paul replies to substantive questions within 24 hours

Q2 2026 campaign plan (90-day)

Q2 2026 Campaign Plan: 90-Day Strategic Roadmap

Objective: Execute seven foundational campaign pillars across 90 days (April-June 2026) to establish Able Assess as the category leader in falls-risk screening for home care and senior living.

Campaign Theme: From Foundation to Proof. Deliver the credibility assets (evidence page, segment pages, blog content) and sales enablement (BDR sequences, landing pages) that allow Paul and Dani to close and expand in key segments.

Success Definition: - All seven segment 1-pagers shipped - Five segment landing pages live and generating qualified leads - Evidence page published with full clinical validation and compliance audit - Four blog posts from priority list published - BDR sequences launched and tracking initial engagement metrics - LinkedIn cadence established (24 posts over 12 weeks) - Website SEO hygiene fixes deployed

Three 30-Day Sprints Overview

Sprint 1 (April): Foundation & Urgents

Focus: Build the evidence and content backbone. Launch internal and external credibility assets.

Key Deliverables: - Evidence page (draft → review → publish) - Segment 1-pagers (all 7, drafts complete by mid-April) - Home Care and Senior Living landing pages (live) - Blog #1 and #2 from priority list (published) - BDR outbound sequences (drafts ready for sales review) - LinkedIn calendar live (Week 1 posts published) - SEO audit complete; fixes prioritised

Dependencies: Content marketing strategy locked; sales team available for messaging review; design resources allocated.

Owner Accountability: - Evidence page: Marketing (with input from Paul for positioning) - 1-pagers: Marketing (drafts by 10 April; design/review 11-20 April; publish 21 April) - Landing pages: Marketing + Designer (live by 25 April) - Blog content: Marketing (publish 14 April and 28 April) - BDR sequences: Sales + Marketing (draft by 15 April; sales review 16-20 April)

Success Metrics: - Evidence page reaches 200+ unique visitors in first week post-publish - 1-pagers downloaded by 50+ operators - Blog posts aggregate 1,000+ organic views in first month - LinkedIn Week 1 posts achieve 20+ engagements per post - BDR sequences ready for launch by 22 April

Sprint 2 (May): Segment Pages & Outbound Launch

Focus: Live segment-specific landing pages. Launch BDR outbound to home care and senior living. Maintain content pipeline.

Key Deliverables: - Five segment landing pages live (Home Care, Senior Living, Home Health, PE Operators, Franchise HQs) - BDR outbound sequences launch (home care and senior living cohorts) - Blog #3 and #4 from priority list (published) - LinkedIn calendar continues (Weeks 5-8 posts) - Early lead data collection and analysis begins - Segment 1-pagers integrated into landing page CTAs

Dependencies: Landing page design template complete (use from April); BDR team trained on value props and demo flow; CRM configured for outbound sequence tracking.

Owner Accountability: - Landing pages: Marketing + Designer (live by 5 May; Dani reviews messaging by 2 May) - BDR sequences launch: Sales (trains team 1-2 May; sequences go live 5 May) - Blog content: Marketing (publish 12 May and 26 May) - LinkedIn cadence: Marketing + Paul/Dani (posts scheduled and published) - Lead tracking: Marketing (configures analytics; reports weekly to leadership)

Success Metrics: - 100+ landing page visits in first week - 20+ qualified leads from landing pages by month-end - BDR sequences: 30+ initial outreach touches; 10%+ response rate target - Blog #3 and #4 combined 1,500+ views - LinkedIn engagement rate increases 15% vs. April baseline

Sprint 3 (June): Content Compounding & Measurement

Focus: Consolidate results. Measure campaign performance. Prepare for Q3 scaling.

Key Deliverables: - Final blog post from priority list published (research-heavy or objection-handling piece) - LinkedIn calendar completes 12-week cycle - Campaign performance report (lead source, conversion, ROI by segment) - Q3 campaign roadmap drafted (webinars, events, expanded segment pages) - All segment landing pages optimised based on May data - BDR sequences refined based on early engagement patterns

Dependencies: Complete lead data from April and May campaigns; sales feedback on BDR-generated lead quality; analytics dashboard functioning.

Owner Accountability: - Blog #5: Marketing (publish by 9 June) - LinkedIn: Marketing + Paul/Dani (final posts weeks 9-12) - Performance report: Marketing (complete by 25 June; present to leadership) - Landing page optimisation: Marketing + Designer (A/B tests run in May; fixes deployed early June) - Q3 roadmap: Leadership (draft by 20 June; final by 30 June)

Success Metrics: - Blog #5 drives 800+ views - LinkedIn 12-week cadence complete; total engagement 500+ across all posts - Campaign-attributed leads: 75+ total (across segments) - BDR pipeline: $X in attributed pipeline value (track through close) - Evidence page traffic: 500+ unique visitors over 90 days - Website SEO improvements measurable (keyword rankings, organic traffic +10%)

12 17-23 Jun Post 12.1 (Residents + Quality Ratings) Post 12.2 (Business + Insurance) — Campaign Report (25 Jun) LinkedIn 12-week retrospective; engagement data compiled

June Deliverables Checklist: - [x] Blog post #5 published (9 June) - [x] LinkedIn Week 9-12 posts published (full 12-week cadence complete) - [x] Campaign performance report delivered (25 June) - [x] Landing page optimisations deployed (early June) - [x] BDR sequence refinements logged (for Q3 iteration) - [x] Q3 campaign roadmap finalised (30 June)

Segment 1-Pagers: Ownership & Deployment

What's Included: - One 1-pager per buyer persona × seven segments = seven documents - 500-750 words each; one-page format (downloadable PDF) - Segment-specific value proposition, use case, proof points, CTA

Segments & Topics: 1. Home Care Operators (PE-backed) — "Growth Brief" 2. Senior Living Operators (CCRCs, IL, AL) — "Falls Prevention at Scale" 3. Home Health Agencies — "OASIS & Quality Reporting" 4. PE Operating Partners / Consolidators — "Portfolio Standardisation" 5. Franchise Headquarters — "Franchisee Differentiation" 6. Corporate COOs / VPs Ops — "P&L Protection through Retention" 7. General Counsel / Risk Compliance — "Liability Mitigation & Documentation"

Owner: Marketing (drafts by 10 April; design/review loop 11-20 April) Designer: One template design; six content variations Review: Paul (messaging) + Dani (sales fit) by 20 April Deployment: PDF hosted on segment landing pages; email nurture sequence; sales collateral; one-pager link in all BDR outreach emails

Landing Pages: Five Pages, One Core Template

Landing Page Specifications: - Hero section: Segment-specific headline + subheading - Lead form: Email, company, title, phone - Social proof: Quote from case study (Cypress HomeCare or Right at Home) - Three value sections: Aligned to segment's top three pillars - Visual: Able Assess product (device or app screenshot) - CTA: "Download [Segment] 1-Pager" or "Request Demo" - Footer: Reassurance copy ("No spam. Unsubscribe anytime.")

Pages to Build: 1. Home Care Operators — "Convert and Retain Home Care Clients" - Headline: "Grow Census, Keep Clients Longer: How Home Care Leaders Are Differentiating with Fall Risk Screening" - Pillars: Protect Growth, Protect the P&L, Protect Residents - CTA: Download "Home Care Growth Brief"

  1. Senior Living Operators — "Improve Outcomes and Quality Ratings" - Headline: "Keep Residents Independent: The Fall Prevention Strategy Your Communities Need" - Pillars: Protect Residents, Protect the Business, Protect Growth - CTA: Request a 20-minute outcome review

  2. Home Health Agencies — "Improve OASIS Reporting and Referral Partnerships" - Headline: "Objective Fall Risk Screening for OASIS Documentation and Clinical Credibility" - Pillars: Protect Residents, Protect the P&L, Protect Growth - CTA: Download "OASIS Documentation Guide"

  3. PE Operating Partners — "Standardise Across Your Portfolio" - Headline: "Standardise Fall Prevention Across 20+ Communities in 90 Days (Without an IT Project)" - Pillars: Protect the Portfolio, Protect Growth, Protect the Business - CTA: Schedule 30-minute portfolio review

  4. Franchise Headquarters — "Differentiate Your Franchisees" - Headline: "Help Your Franchisees Win More Clients and Improve Margins with Tech-Forward Fall Prevention" - Pillars: Protect Growth, Protect the P&L, Protect the Portfolio - CTA: Download "Franchisee Playbook"

Owner: Marketing (content); Designer (layout); Dani (sales messaging review) Timeline: Drafts complete by 2 May; live by 10 May Analytics: Google Analytics 4 tracking; conversion funnel monitoring; weekly reporting

Blog Publishing Priority List

Publication Dates: 1. Blog #1 (14 April): "Home Care Growth Brief: Converting Prospects Into Clients" - Derived from Post 1.1 and 1.2 (Paul + Dani week 1 posts) - 1,500-2,000 words; leads with economics - Repurposing: LinkedIn carousel (5 slides); email snippet; sales talk track

  1. Blog #2 (28 April): "Why Grip Strength Predicts Health Outcomes (And Why It Matters to Your Business)" - Derived from Post 2.2 (Dani week 2, Residents pillar) - 1,800-2,200 words; leads with science, ends with business case - Repurposing: LinkedIn carousel; email snippet; clinical team resource

  2. Blog #3 (12 May): "Staff Turnover Costs More Than Technology: The P&L Case for Fall Prevention" - Derived from Post 5.2 (Dani week 5, P&L pillar) - 1,500-1,800 words; operator economic focus - Repurposing: LinkedIn carousel; email to COOs; sales battlecard

  3. Blog #4 (26 May): "Non-Medical Home Care, Clinical-Grade Screening: How to Bridge the Gap" - Derived from Post 6.2 (Dani week 6, Business pillar) - 1,600-2,000 words; bridges clinical credibility + operational simplicity - Repurposing: LinkedIn carousel; home care segment focus; BDR follow-up asset

  4. Blog #5 (9 June): "Five Common Objections to Fall Prevention (And How Data Answers Them)" - Standalone objection-handling asset; supports sales - 1,200-1,500 words; five objections with proof-point responses - Repurposing: Sales collateral; email sequence; sales training asset

Owner: Marketing (content creation and editing) Review Cycle: Drafts due 1 week before publish date; Paul reviews for accuracy; Dani reviews for sales fit SEO Requirements: - Target 2-3 keywords per post (long-tail, operator-focused) - Internal linking to segment landing pages and 1-pagers - Meta descriptions optimised for CTR - CTA button links to segment landing page

Evidence Page: Clinical Credibility Foundation

Page Structure:

  1. Hero Section - Headline: "Able Assess: Evidence-Backed Fall Risk Screening" - Subheading: "Peer-reviewed science. CDC-aligned. Proven outcomes."

  2. Evidence Categories - Clinical Foundation (grip strength biomarker, CDC STEADI, 2022 World Guidelines) - Safety & Outcomes (falls epidemiology, prevention proof) - Business Impact (retention, conversion, ROI case studies) - Compliance & Regulatory (HIPAA, FDA classification, audit summaries)

  3. Downloadable Assets - Clinical brief (2-3 pages; grip strength science) - Regulatory summary (1 page; FDA, HIPAA, state regulations) - Case study summaries (Cypress HomeCare, Right at Home; 1 page each) - Audit report link (compliance validation)

  4. Trust Signals - Customer testimonials (clinical leads) - Peer-reviewed evidence links (PubMed citations) - Compliance badges / certifications (if obtained)

Owner: Marketing (content curation and page build) + Paul (clinical messaging review) Timeline: Draft by 15 April; review and revise 16-18 April; publish by 18 April Deployment: Link from all landing pages; featured in BDR email signature; sales demo resource

BDR Outbound Sequences

Sequence Structure: Five-touch cadence over 14 days - Touch 1 (Day 0): Personalised email intro + value prop - Touch 2 (Day 3): Follow-up with social proof + demo link - Touch 3 (Day 7): Segment-specific 1-pager + objection handling - Touch 4 (Day 10): Case study spotlight (Cypress or Right at Home) - Touch 5 (Day 14): Final soft close + calendar link or escalation to Dani

Segment A: Home Care Operators (Launch 5 May) - Target list: PE-backed home care groups with 5+ locations - Opening value prop: "Help your franchisees convert more prospects and retain clients longer" - Demo offer: 15-minute call with Dani or Paul - Success metric: 10% response rate; 5% meeting booking rate

Segment B: Senior Living Operators (Launch 19 May) - Target list: Senior living facilities with 200+ beds (CCRCs, IL, AL) - Opening value prop: "Keep residents independent longer—and improve your quality ratings" - Demo offer: "Request a 20-minute outcome review" - Success metric: 8% response rate; 4% meeting booking rate

Email Subject Line Examples: - Home Care: "Why home care leaders are switching to this for client retention" - Senior Living: "New way to track fall risk (and keep residents longer)" - Both: "Objective screening that non-clinical staff can run"

Owner: Sales (outreach and follow-up) + Marketing (email copy and templates) Timeline: Drafts ready for sales review by 22 April; refined by 2 May; launched 5 May (home care) and 19 May (senior living) Tracking: CRM campaign setup; weekly engagement metrics; lead quality feedback from sales

SEO Hygiene Fixes

Audit Focus Areas (completed by 15 April): 1. On-Page: Title tags, meta descriptions, header hierarchy (H1-H3) 2. Technical: Mobile responsiveness, site speed, XML sitemap, robots.txt 3. Content: Keyword clustering, internal linking, thin content identification 4. Competitive: Keyword gaps vs. competitors (SafelyYou, Zibrio, etc.)

Q2 Fixes (deploy by 30 April): - [ ] Home Care segment landing page: target "home care fall prevention" (monthly volume: 200+) - [ ] Senior Living segment landing page: target "senior living falls prevention" (monthly volume: 150+) - [ ] Blog posts: optimise for long-tail keywords ("home care retention ROI", "grip strength predictor", etc.) - [ ] Evidence page: optimise for "falls prevention evidence" and "clinical trial grip strength" - [ ] Internal linking: update all blog posts to link to relevant 1-pagers and landing pages - [ ] Meta descriptions: refresh all top 20 pages for CTR improvement

Owner: Marketing or external SEO consultant Tool: SEMrush, Ahrefs, or similar Success Metrics: - Top 10 keyword rankings (10+ keywords in top 10 by end of Q2) - Organic traffic +15% vs. Q1 baseline - Blog post click-through rate +20% through improved meta descriptions

# Segment Title Lead Claim Secondary Claim
1 Home Care (PE-backed) Home Care Growth Brief Revenue retention ($140k per lost case) Client acquisition through differentiation
2 Senior Living Senior Living Operator's Guide to Falls Prevention at Scale Resident retention + census lift Quality rating improvement + family trust
3 Home Health Documenting Fall Risk for Quality Reporting and Reimbursement OASIS Section GG compliance Referral partnership positioning
4 PE Operating Partners Standardise Fall Prevention Across Your Portfolio Portfolio coherence without IT projects Acquisition readiness (standardisation as valuation signal)
5 Franchise HQ Franchisee Playbook: Fall Prevention as Competitive Advantage Franchisee differentiation (unit economics) Franchisee recruitment and retention

7 General Counsel / Risk Defending Quality Ratings and Legal Liability Through Documented Risk Assessment Objective screening as legal defence Compliance and audit readiness

Cross-Functional Ownership & Accountability

Marketing Lead: [Marketing Manager Name]
  • Overall campaign coordination
  • Content calendar execution
  • Blog publishing and SEO integration
  • Landing page build and analytics
  • Evidence page curation
  • LinkedIn scheduling and cadence
  • Weekly reporting to leadership
Sales Lead: Dani
  • BDR sequence messaging and review
  • Segment landing page copy review
  • 1-pager sales fit validation
  • Outbound sequence execution and response tracking
  • Lead quality feedback to marketing
  • Demo scheduling from BDR handoff
Founder Input: Paul
  • Clinical accuracy review (evidence page, blog #2, #5)
  • Founder voice on all LinkedIn posts
  • Strategic messaging alignment (narrative pillars)
  • Monthly check-in with marketing and sales on progress
Designer
  • Landing page template build (one template, five variations)
  • 1-pager design and layout
  • Evidence page layout and asset staging
  • Blog post featured images
  • LinkedIn carousel slide design
BDR / Sales Development
