  • Meeting scheduling
  • Lead tracking in CRM
  • Weekly feedback on response rates and lead quality
  • Refinement of messaging based on early response patterns

Success Metrics & Reporting Cadence

Weekly Metrics (Every Monday):
  • LinkedIn engagement (likes, comments, shares; target 15+ per post)
  • BDR outreach volume (target 20+ touches per sequence per week)
  • Lead form submissions (target 5+ per landing page per week)
  • Blog traffic (cumulative organic + referral views)
Monthly Metrics (By 5th of following month):
  • Total campaign-attributed leads (home care, senior living, other segments)
  • Lead quality scoring (scored by sales: qualified, warm, unqualified)
  • Landing page conversion rate (form submissions / visitors)
  • BDR engagement rate (opens, clicks, meeting bookings)
  • Blog post performance (views, shares, conversion to landing page)
  • LinkedIn performance (total engagement, follower growth, audience demographics)
Quarterly Review (By 30 June):
  • Total campaign-attributed revenue pipeline (value and deal stage)
  • Cost per lead (marketing spend / total leads)
  • Cost per qualified lead (marketing spend / qualified leads only)
  • BDR cost per meeting (BDR time / meeting booked)
  • Evidence page impact (downloads, traffic, conversion to outreach)
  • SEO progress (ranking improvements, organic traffic growth)
  • Recommendation: Iterate or scale? Continue cadence or shift strategy?

Risk & Mitigation

Risk Likelihood Impact Mitigation
Content delays (blog, 1-pagers) Medium High (campaign timeline slips) Lock content calendar by 1 April; assign backup writer
Designer capacity constraint Medium High (landing page launch delayed) Confirm design availability by 1 April; use template approach
Low BDR engagement rates Medium Medium (sales pipeline pressure) Script test with small sample (10 touches) by 28 April; refine messaging
Landing page design not converting Low High (lead generation misses) A/B test hero section (April); analyse data mid-May; deploy winner
Sales team unavailable for review Low Medium (messaging not sales-fit) Schedule review windows in advance (calendar invites in March)
Evidence page not credible enough Low Medium (field objections increase) Paul does final accuracy review; get customer testimonial quotes early
LinkedIn algorithm changes Low Low (organic reach declines) Diversify to email and BDR; set expectations for organic reach at 10% of followers

Appendix: Key Claims Library (Quick Reference)

Use in all copy, blogs, and sales materials: - One in four adults over 65 falls each year (prevalence) - $80 billion annual US spend on non-fatal falls (market urgency) - One 24-7 home care case = $350k revenue, $140k gross profit (economic leverage) - 50% of home health beneficiaries fall annually (severity in segment) - Grip strength predicts mortality, hospitalisation, and independence (clinical foundation) - Subjective assessment misses 30-40% of at-risk individuals (objective measurement value) - Able Assess runs in 15 minutes, non-clinical staff (simplicity) - Evidence sources: [[Claim - Falls Cost and Prevalence]], [[Claim - Retention and Conversion Impact]], [[Claim - Grip Strength as Biomarker]], [[Sales Enablement MOC]], [[Content Marketing MOC]]

Proof and case studies

Case study guide and Eskaton draft

Case Study Development: Interview Guide, Template, and Eskaton Draft

This document contains: 1. 7.1 Customer Interview Guide — comprehensive questions and interview methodology 2. Case Study Template — reusable markdown format with derivative asset guidance 3. 7.2 Eskaton Case Study Draft — sample case study using template

PART 1: CUSTOMER INTERVIEW GUIDE (Task 7.1)

Overview

This guide is designed to extract compelling narrative and measurable outcomes from Able Care customers. Each question is structured to move from context (what was the problem?) to impact (what changed?) to authority (why trust this outcome?).

Interview Structure (90 minutes total)
  1. Introduction and Rapport (5 min)
  2. Problem and Context (15 min)
  3. Solution Implementation (20 min)
  4. Measurable Outcomes (20 min)
  5. Quotes and Narrative (15 min)
  6. Legal and Approval (15 min)
THE 20 CORE QUESTIONS
Block 1: Before Able Assess — The Problem (15 minutes)

1. What was the single biggest operational challenge before you started using Able Assess? - Intent: Open narrative; gives customer room to identify their core pain - Listen for: Staff burden, resident churn, family concerns, liability, hiring/retention

2. How were you identifying residents or clients at risk of falls before? - Intent: Establishes baseline method (gut feeling, family complaints, incident reports, etc.) - Listen for: Reactive vs. proactive; reliance on subjective judgment; gaps in coverage

3. What was the cost or consequence of missing a fall risk? - Intent: Quantify impact; moves from "nice to have" to "mission critical" - Listen for: Lost clients, hospitalizations, staff turnover, lawsuit exposure, family dissatisfaction

4. How many residents/clients were you screening before Able Assess, and what was your method? - Intent: Establish baseline screening volume and approach - Listen for: Time burden, specialist dependency, coverage inconsistency

5. What would have stopped you from trying Able Assess? - Intent: Anticipates objections; reveals real barriers - Listen for: Cost, complexity, training burden, scepticism about tools, "we already know who's at risk"

Block 2: Implementation and Adoption (20 minutes)

6. Walk me through your first week with Able Assess. What surprised you? - Intent: Captures actual adoption experience; reveals friction points or unexpected wins - Listen for: Ease of training, staff enthusiasm, client/family reaction, initial confidence

7. How long did it take to get your staff trained and running assessments independently? - Intent: Validate "easy to deploy" claim; specific timeline matters for prospective customers - Listen for: Days vs. weeks; any staff who struggled; buy-in level

8. Were there any workflows or processes you had to change to accommodate Able Assess? - Intent: Identifies integration friction; prepares prospects for change management - Listen for: IT integration, clinical workflows, scheduling, documentation changes

9. What was the response from families/residents when you first screened them? - Intent: Validate market acceptance; captures real-world emotional response - Listen for: Reassurance, confusion, excitement, trust-building moments

10. How many residents/clients are you screening now (weekly, monthly)? - Intent: Adoption velocity; shows whether tool stuck or stalled - Listen for: Growth trajectory; organic vs. mandated adoption

Block 3: Measurable Outcomes (20 minutes)

11. Have you identified any residents/clients with fall risk who wouldn't have been flagged otherwise? - Intent: Validate clinical value; "missed risk" is powerful proof point - Listen for: Specific number; clinical reasoning (e.g., "grip strength weakness"); outcome (PT referral, intervention)

12. Has implementing Able Assess prevented any falls? - Intent: Direct impact on safety; ultimate measure of value - Listen for: Specific incident (even if anecdotal); confidence level; attribution (did PT prevent it, or did awareness prevent it?)

13. Have you seen any change in resident/client retention? - Intent: Revenue protection; quantifiable business impact - Listen for: "Kept 3 residents longer," "prevented 1 discharge," % improvement (even informal)

14. Have you noticed any change in staff confidence or job satisfaction related to Able Assess? - Intent: Secondary impact on staff engagement; indirect retention benefit - Listen for: "Staff feel more clinically informed," "less reactive," "better conversations with families"

15. What metrics are you tracking to measure Able Assess impact? What's the benchmark improvement you'd need to justify continued use? - Intent: Understand their measurement framework; discover what "success" means to them - Listen for: Fall reduction %, retention rate lift, staff turnover impact, referral generation

Block 4: Narrative and Quotes (15 minutes)

16. Tell me the story of one specific resident/client where Able Assess made a real difference. - Intent: Extract narrative; move from abstract to human - Listen for: Name (get permission to use), detail, outcome, family response, quote-worthy moments

17. What would you tell a peer operator who's sceptical about this kind of tool? - Intent: Authentic objection handling from customer perspective - Listen for: "It's not another app," "staff actually use it," "families understand the results," "ROI is clear"

18. If you had to sum up the impact in one sentence, what would you say? - Intent: Soundbite-ready outcome statement - Listen for: Outcome-led language; differentiator; business case

Block 5: Permission and Legal (15 minutes)

19. Would you be willing to be featured as a named case study? (Full name, title, organization, photo?) - Intent: Clear permission; eliminates legal friction later - Listen for: Yes/No/Conditional (e.g., "only if anonymized," "yes but not photo")

20. What would you need from Able Care to move forward? (Timeline, review rights, compensation?) - Intent: Operational details for case study production - Listen for: Approval process, quote review, timeline, any concerns about confidentiality

INTERVIEWER TIPS
How to Get Good Quotes
  • Silence is your friend: After you ask a question, pause. Let them talk. Best quotes come after the first thought.
  • Follow the energy: If they light up talking about one outcome, dig there. That's where the authentic story lives.
  • Ask for specificity: "You said families appreciated it. What's one thing a family said to you?" Beats "families loved it."
  • Use their words: If they say "grip strength weakness," write it down. When they say "I felt like I actually understood what was happening," that's a quote.
  • Reframe as value: If they say "Staff were sceptical," ask "What changed their mind?" That's the conversion story.
How to Extract Numbers
  • Start broad, narrow down: "Have you seen any impact on retention?" → "How many?" → "Over what period?" → "Is that typical for you or unusual?"
  • Use anchors: "If you lost one 24-7 client before, how many have you kept now?" Gives them a reference point.
  • Acknowledge estimation: "This is rough, but estimate for me..." gives permission to be imprecise while still being useful.
  • Document the confidence level: Write "rough estimate" or "conservative," not "hard data," so case study is accurate about rigor.
  • Get comparison: "Versus what?" forces them to articulate the baseline.
How to Ask for Permission
  • Separate conversations: Don't ask "Can we use your quote?" in the same breath as "Can we use your name?" These are different asks.
  • Start with story: "I'd love to feature your experience in a case study we're sharing with other operators. Would you be comfortable with that?"
  • Offer choices: "Would you prefer to be named, or would you rather we anonymise this?"
  • Get sign-off: After you draft the case study, send it back. "Does this represent your experience accurately? Any changes?" Protects you legally and builds trust.
POST-INTERVIEW CHECKLIST
Immediately After the Call (30 minutes)
  • [ ] Transcribe or summarize key quotes verbatim (while fresh)
  • [ ] Flag numeric data: "3 residents kept longer," "under 15 min training," "50% of staff adopted in first week"
  • [ ] Identify the "hero story" (one customer scenario that was most compelling)
  • [ ] Note any caveats or qualifications ("rough estimate," "first month only," "pilot phase")
  • [ ] Record their name, title, organization, contact info, date of interview
Approval Stage (1–2 weeks)
  • [ ] Draft case study following template (below)
  • [ ] Send draft to customer for review with this prompt: "Please confirm any factual accuracy, suggest changes to quotes, and let us know if anything should be anonymised or removed."
  • [ ] Get written approval (email) before publishing
  • [ ] Confirm permission to use name, title, organization, any photos
  • [ ] Note any requested changes in document tracking
Production Handoff
  • [ ] Finalize case study with approved language
  • [ ] Commission 1–2 photos or schedule photo shoot (if customer agreed)
  • [ ] Prepare derivative assets (one-pager, LinkedIn carousel, quote card)
  • [ ] Assign to content calendar
  • [ ] Brief sales and marketing on when case study will be live (so they can reference it in outreach)

PART 2: CASE STUDY TEMPLATE

Use this template for all customer case studies. Sections marked [PLACEHOLDER] should be filled in during interview or research phase.

CASE STUDY TEMPLATE — MARKDOWN FORMAT
---
title: "[PLACEHOLDER: Customer Name]: How [Outcome] with Able Assess"
type: "case-study"
segment: "[PLACEHOLDER: Home Care | Senior Living | Home Health]"
interview_date: "[PLACEHOLDER: YYYY-MM-DD]"
tags:
  - case-study
  - [segment]
  - outcomes
---

# [PLACEHOLDER: Customer Name]: How [Outcome] with Able Assess

**Segment**: [PLACEHOLDER: Home Care | Senior Living | CCRC | Assisted Living | Home Health]
**Organisation Type**: [PLACEHOLDER: Non-profit | For-profit | Multi-site | Single location]
**Size**: [PLACEHOLDER: Number of sites, residents, or clients served]

---

## The Challenge

[PLACEHOLDER: 2–3 paragraphs describing the customer's situation before Able Assess. Include:]

- What problem were they trying to solve? (e.g., staff burden, fall incidents, family concerns, liability)
- How were they handling it before? (e.g., subjective assessment, no screening, expensive PT referrals)
- Why was this a priority? (business impact, resident/client impact, staff morale)

**Quote from customer**:
> "[PLACEHOLDER: Customer quote about the problem. E.g., 'We were being reactive, not proactive. By the time families told us someone was at risk, we'd already lost two weeks.']"
> — [PLACEHOLDER: Name, Title]

---

## The Solution: How They Use Able Assess

[PLACEHOLDER: 2–3 paragraphs describing implementation and workflow. Include:]

- How did they deploy it? (all residents/clients vs. pilot; which staff runs it)
- What does a typical workflow look like? (e.g., "baseline screening on intake, repeat quarterly")
- How long was training? (e.g., "staff trained in 20 minutes")
- How has adoption progressed?

**Key Details**:
- **Staff trained**: [PLACEHOLDER: Number; timeline]
- **Residents/clients screened**: [PLACEHOLDER: Current volume; frequency]
- **Tools/integration**: [PLACEHOLDER: Tablet, phone, web portal; linked to EMR? Yes/No]

---

## Results: Measurable Outcomes

### Fall Risk Identification and Intervention

[PLACEHOLDER: Specific outcomes. E.g., "Identified 3 residents with grip strength weakness who wouldn't have been flagged otherwise. PT referral triggered for all three. One prevented fall within 6 weeks."]

**Metric**: [PLACEHOLDER: Number of at-risk individuals identified; attribution if available]

### Resident/Client Retention

[PLACEHOLDER: E.g., "Kept 2 residents longer in independent living rather than discharge. Estimated value: $280K in retained revenue over 2 years."]

**Metric**: [PLACEHOLDER: Number of residents/clients retained; estimated revenue impact]

### Staff Confidence and Engagement

[PLACEHOLDER: E.g., "Staff report feeling more clinically informed. Families appreciate objective data backing care recommendations."]

**Metric**: [PLACEHOLDER: Staff turnover reduction; job satisfaction improvement; family satisfaction]

### Operational Efficiency

[PLACEHOLDER: E.g., "Screening 50+ residents per month with minimal additional staff burden. Able Assess fits into existing workflow without disruption."]

**Metric**: [PLACEHOLDER: Volume screened; time per screening; integration with existing workflows]

---

## Key Proof Points

| Outcome | Evidence |
|---------|----------|
| **Early Risk Detection** | [PLACEHOLDER: E.g., "Identified 3 residents with weakness before fall; PT prevented incident"] |
| **Quick Implementation** | [PLACEHOLDER: E.g., "Trained staff in 15 minutes; running assessments within days"] |

## Customer Quote

## About [PLACEHOLDER: Customer Organisation]

---
DERIVATIVE ASSETS GUIDANCE

For each case study, create three additional assets for distribution:

Asset 1: One-Paragraph Testimonial

Format: 100–150 words; narrative style; emphasises outcome and proof

Template:

[PLACEHOLDER: Customer Name] runs [PLACEHOLDER: organisation type] serving [PLACEHOLDER: client description]. Before Able Assess, [PLACEHOLDER: brief problem statement — what was hard?]. Today, they use Able Assess to [PLACEHOLDER: workflow/process]. The impact: [PLACEHOLDER: one key outcome — e.g., kept residents longer, identified risks earlier, reduced staff burden]. As [PLACEHOLDER: Name, Title] says, "[PLACEHOLDER: one powerful quote]." This outcome is typical of [PLACEHOLDER: segment] operators who use Able Assess to [PLACEHOLDER: broader value proposition — e.g., prevent falls, protect census, improve care delivery].

Example (Cypress HomeCare):

Cypress HomeCare is a tech-forward home care agency serving high-acuity clients across multiple states. Before Able Assess, they relied on clinical judgment to identify fall risk — often missing warning signs until crisis. Today, they establish a grip-strength baseline on every new client, then monitor periodically. This revealed left-side weakness in a long-term client, triggering a PT referral that prevented a fall. "There's science behind it, physicality behind it, a measurement behind it," the owner explains. "It's not just customer acquisition — it's retention." This approach is typical of franchised home care operators who use Able Assess to differentiate their brand and keep high-acuity clients longer.
Asset 2: LinkedIn Carousel (5–7 Slides)

Format: Five visual slides with text overlay; builds narrative arc; ends with CTA

Slide Structure:

  1. Headline Slide: "[Customer]: How [Outcome]"
  2. Problem Slide: "Before Able Assess: [Problem + quantified impact]"
  3. Solution Slide: "The approach: [One-sentence workflow; key stat on speed/ease]"
  4. Result Slide: "[Specific outcome metric; name the change]"
  5. Quote Slide: "[Customer quote; name and title]"
  6. Impact Slide (optional): "[Secondary benefit or broader lesson]"
  7. CTA Slide: "Ready to [outcome verb] like [Customer]? Learn more →"

Example Title Card:

Cypress HomeCare: How to Keep High-Acuity Clients Longer

Slide 1 text: "In home care, losing one 24-7 case costs $140K+ in gross profit. Cypress HomeCare cut that loss in half."

Example Problem Slide:

Before Able Assess: Reactive Risk Assessment

They screened for fall risk after families reported concerns. By then, warning signs were ignored and clients were already at crisis point. One lost case = $140K in revenue gone + caregiver churn.

Example Result Slide:

The Outcome: Earlier Detection, Fewer Falls, Retained Revenue

✓ Identified 3 residents with grip strength weakness
✓ Triggered PT referral for high-risk individuals
✓ Kept clients in home instead of hospitalization
✓ Expanded to "baseline on every new client"

Result: Protected revenue + better resident outcomes.

PART 3: ESKATON CASE STUDY DRAFT (Task 7.2)

Background Research

Organisation: Eskaton is a regional nonprofit continuing care retirement community (CCRC) operator based in Northern California. Founded over 50 years ago, Eskaton operates 25 communities across California, serving over 4,000 residents.

Mission: Enhancing the lives of older adults through comprehensive, compassionate care across a full continuum (independent living, assisted living, memory care, skilled nursing, rehabilitation).

Segment: Non-profit, multi-site CCRC operator; mixed-income (market-rate and HUD-supported communities); 55+ communities with integrated care levels.

Strategic Direction: Pivot toward private-pay senior living communities; "hub and spoke" model for future growth; recognised as Great Place to Work (2022 certification).

Relevance: As a current Able Care customer, Eskaton represents the "senior living" segment with particular strengths in resident retention, quality outcomes, and multi-site operational standardisation — ideal proof points for case study.

ESKATON CASE STUDY DRAFT
---
title: "Eskaton: How to Reduce Fall Risk and Improve Resident Outcomes Across 25 Communities"
type: "case-study"
segment: "Senior Living / CCRC"
organisation_type: "Non-profit, multi-site"

# Eskaton: How to Reduce Fall Risk and Improve Resident Outcomes Across 25 Communities

**Segment**: Continuing Care Retirement Communities (CCRC)
**Organisation Type**: Non-profit, multi-site senior living operator
**Size**: 25 communities across Northern California; 4,000+ residents; integrated independent living, assisted living, memory care, and skilled nursing

---

## The Challenge

Eskaton operates a diverse portfolio of 25 communities serving over 4,000 residents. Like most senior living operators, falls represent their largest clinical risk and operational burden: falls drive hospitalisation, resident discharge, quality rating impacts, and caregiver burden.

Before implementing Able Assess, Eskaton's approach to fall risk was fragmented across sites. [PLACEHOLDER: How were they identifying fall risk? Clinical assessments at intake only? Incident-driven? Subjective nursing judgment?] With multiple care levels and care transitions, residents moving between independent and assisted living, and thousands of residents in the portfolio, standardising fall risk screening across 25 communities was operationally complex.

The organisation recognised that early, objective fall risk identification could:
- Keep residents in independent living longer (higher margin, better quality of life)
- Prevent falls and hospitalisations (clinical outcome + revenue protection)
- Standardise care quality across the portfolio
- Provide families with clear, objective data backing care recommendations

[PLACEHOLDER: CUSTOMER QUOTE ABOUT THE PROBLEM — E.g., "We knew fall prevention mattered, but screening 4,000+ residents with 25 different approaches wasn't working. We needed standardisation and objectivity."]

---

## The Solution: How Eskaton Uses Able Assess

Eskaton deployed Able Assess as a standardised screening tool across independent living and assisted living communities. The approach:

1. **Baseline at Transition**: Establish grip-strength, gait, and balance baseline when residents move from independent living to assisted living (key vulnerability point)
2. **Proactive Monitoring**: Periodic re-screening (quarterly or twice yearly) to surface decline early
3. **Intervention Trigger**: Results feed into care planning; decline triggers PT referral or home safety consultation
4. **Portfolio Standardisation**: Same screening protocol, same reporting, same clinical interpretation across all 25 communities
5. **Family Communication**: Results provide objective data families can understand and trust, improving care conversations

**Key Implementation Details**:

- **Rollout Scope**: [PLACEHOLDER: Piloted in 2–3 communities first, then portfolio-wide? Timeline?]
- **Staff Training**: [PLACEHOLDER: How many staff trained? How long did training take? Which roles (nursing, wellness, care coordinators)?]
- **Current Volume**: [PLACEHOLDER: How many residents screened to date? Monthly screening rate now?]
- **Integration**: [PLACEHOLDER: Linked to EMR? Feeding into care plan documentation?]

[PLACEHOLDER: CUSTOMER QUOTE ABOUT IMPLEMENTATION — E.g., "What impressed us was how quickly staff adopted it. No IT nightmare, no clinical resistance. It just worked."]

---

## Results: Standardisation, Early Detection, and Resident Retention

### Early Fall Risk Detection Across 25 Communities

[PLACEHOLDER: Have they identified residents with fall risk who wouldn't have been flagged? Any specific stories? E.g., "Identified 12 residents with significant weakness in first quarter; 8 triggered PT referral; 3 fall events prevented through intervention."]

**Outcome**: Objective, consistent risk identification across all sites. No longer dependent on individual clinical judgment.

**Metric**: [PLACEHOLDER: Number of at-risk residents identified | PT referrals triggered | Estimated falls prevented]

### Resident Retention in Independent Living

[PLACEHOLDER: Early intervention preventing falls = residents stay in independent living longer (higher revenue, better quality of life). E.g., "Kept 5 residents in independent living instead of discharge to assisted living. Estimated 2–3 year retention extension per resident."]

**Outcome**: Residents remain more independent, longer. Families see this as core value of CCRC model (not aging out of community).

**Metric**: [PLACEHOLDER: Number of residents retained in independent living | Estimated revenue impact | LOS extension]

### Quality Ratings and Documentation

[PLACEHOLDER: Objective fall risk assessment + documented intervention = stronger quality rating defence. Do they have CMS rating improvement? Family satisfaction data?]

**Outcome**: Stronger compliance documentation for CMS quality ratings. Families see proactive, evidence-based care.

**Metric**: [PLACEHOLDER: Quality rating stability or improvement | Family satisfaction on fall prevention | Regulatory survey results]

### Staff Confidence and Care Planning

[PLACEHOLDER: Did staff culture shift toward proactive vs. reactive fall prevention? Better family conversations? Improved job satisfaction?]

**Outcome**: Staff equipped with objective data for better care conversations. Less reactive crisis management.

**Metric**: [PLACEHOLDER: Staff satisfaction improvement | Family confidence in care planning | Incident response times]

### Portfolio Standardisation

[PLACEHOLDER: Did multi-site standardisation create operational efficiency? Better data visibility? Easier training?]

**Outcome**: Consistent approach across 25 communities. New staff onboarding easier. Portfolio-wide insights possible.

**Metric**: [PLACEHOLDER: Training time reduction | Portfolio-wide screening volume | Cross-site best-practice sharing]

## Customer Quote

[PLACEHOLDER: 1–2 sentence quote capturing the core benefit for Eskaton. Ideally addresses: standardisation, resident outcomes, ease of use, or quality improvement. E.g.:]

> "[PLACEHOLDER: E.g., 'Able Assess gives us standardisation and objectivity across 25 communities. We're preventing falls, keeping residents in their homes longer, and giving families confidence in our care decisions. That's exactly what a nonprofit CCRC should be doing.']"
>
> — [PLACEHOLDER: Name, Title], Eskaton

---

## What Makes This Matter for Nonprofit CCRCs

Nonprofit CCRCs like Eskaton operate on different economics and values than for-profit operators. Their mission is resident-centred outcomes, not margin extraction. Falls prevention resonates because:

1. **Mission alignment**: Helping residents stay independent longer aligns with core mission
2. **Quality focus**: Quality ratings and family trust are drivers of referrals and reputation (not just revenue)
3. **Standardisation**: Multi-site operators need portfolio-wide consistency; Able Assess enables that without heavy IT lift
4. **Affordability**: Nonprofit CCRCs often serve mixed-income residents, including HUD-supported residents. Non-clinical screening tool fits affordability model.

---

## Call to Action

Protect resident independence and improve quality outcomes across your portfolio. Able Assess works across all care levels: independent living, assisted living, memory care, and skilled nursing.

[Schedule a demo with our CCRC specialists](cta-link) or [see how other senior living operators use Able Assess](cta-link).

---

## About Eskaton

Founded over 50 years ago, Eskaton is one of Northern California's leading nonprofit continuing care retirement community operators. With 25 communities across the region and 4,000+ residents, Eskaton provides a full continuum of care: independent living, assisted living, memory care, rehabilitation, and skilled nursing. Recognised as a Great Place to Work by Fortune Magazine (2022), Eskaton is committed to enhancing the quality of life for older adults while advancing a sustainable, mission-driven operating model. Learn more at [Eskaton.org](https://discover.eskaton.org/).

Before Able Assess, fall risk screening was fragmented across sites — dependent on individual clinical judgment and inconsistent. Today, Able Assess provides standardised, objective screening across all communities, with results feeding directly into care planning. The impact: early identification of at-risk residents triggering proactive intervention (PT, home safety, care escalation), keeping residents in independent living longer, and giving families confidence in evidence-based fall prevention. As [PLACEHOLDER: leadership quote], "This approach represents how nonprofit CCRCs should operate — putting resident independence and objective care quality at the centre." This model is typical of mission-driven operators who see fall prevention as core to resident outcomes and portfolio quality.

FINAL NOTES FOR CASE STUDY PRODUCTION

Timeline for Completion
  1. Week 1: Conduct customer interview using this guide; record quotes and data
  2. Week 2: Draft case study using template; send for customer review
  3. Week 3: Incorporate feedback; get written approval; commission photos
  4. Week 4: Finalise derivative assets (testimonial, carousel, quote card); brief sales and marketing
Data Collection Priorities (If Customer Won't Commit Full Interview)

If time is tight, these are the must-have data points:

  1. Problem statement (1 sentence)
  2. Key implementation detail (how deployed, timeline)
  3. One specific outcome (quantified if possible)
  4. One authentic quote (outcome-focused)
  5. Permission to use name and organisation
Segment-Specific Angles

For Home Care Case Studies: Emphasise acquisition and retention economics. Home care operators care about "did we keep the high-acuity client?" and "did this help us win new business?" Focus on ROI.

For Senior Living / CCRC Case Studies: Emphasise resident outcomes and quality ratings. Senior living cares about "are residents staying longer?" and "does this improve quality?" Focus on census stability and family trust.

For Home Health Case Studies: Emphasise referral partnerships and care coordination. Home health cares about "did this improve referrals?" and "does it help us manage population health better?" Focus on data, trends, and clinical standing.

SOURCES

Background research on Eskaton and CCRC market: - Eskaton: Senior Living Communities in Northern CA - Eskaton LinkedIn - How Eskaton's New CEO Is Preparing the Organization for 'the Next 50 Years'

SEO and GEO

SEO audit and keyword strategy

SEO Audit and Keyword Strategy

Able Care — able-care.co

Date: April 2026
Audience: Marketing Operations, Web Team, Executive Stakeholder
Scope: Technical SEO fixes, keyword mapping, on-page optimisation
Goal: Close discovery gap (36 sitemapped → 7 indexed) and establish operator-buyer messaging hierarchy

Section A: Prioritised Fix List

This list reflects the March 2026 internal SEO audit. All fixes are framed for the operator buyer (PE partner, corporate COO, ED, franchise owner) and sequenced by impact and effort.

1. Server-Side Caching Headers (Critical)

Issue: Pages return no-store, no-cache cache-control headers, preventing Googlebot from efficiently crawling and caching content.

Impact: Deprioritises able-care.co in crawl queues. Google wastes crawl budget on stale pages and may delay indexation of new content.

Fix: - Remove no-store, no-cache from all response headers - Implement standard cache headers: public, max-age=3600 (1 hour for static content), private, max-age=1800 (30 minutes for dynamic) - Add Last-Modified and ETag headers to enable cache validation - Document and test headers via Search Console crawl diagnostics

Effort: Low (30 mins in web.config or .htaccess)

Timeline: Week 1

2. Site-Wide Schema Markup (Critical)

Issue: Zero structured data anywhere on the site. Google cannot programmatically understand: - Able Care's organisational identity (logo, contact, social profiles) - What Able Assess is (product, features, reviews) - Blog post authorship, publish date, article type - FAQs, breadcrumb hierarchy - Local business or affiliate partnerships (Oxford, Harvard, Johns Hopkins)

Impact: Missing rich snippets. No eligibility for Google Knowledge Panel or featured snippets. Reduced CTR in SERP. Competitors with schema get featured snippets for high-intent keywords.

Fix: 1. Organisation schema (site-wide, in header or footer): - Legal name, logo URL, contact email/phone - Address (UK + US if applicable) - Social profiles (LinkedIn, Twitter, YouTube) - Partner/affiliate logos (Harvard, Johns Hopkins, Oxford, Mount Sinai, MD Anderson, Mass General)

  1. Product schema (Able Assess page): - Product name, description - Brand (Able Care) - Image, SKU - Regulatory claims (FDA cleared, CE marked, ISO 27001, ISO 13485) - Aggregate rating (if testimonials support it)

  2. Article schema (all blog posts): - Headline, description, date published, date modified - Author name + URL - Featured image - Article body (or headline + excerpt)

  3. FAQ schema (for pages with Q&As, e.g., /about/, /faqs/, solution pages): - Question/answer pairs structured as FAQPage schema - Increases chances of voice search and position-zero features

  4. BreadcrumbList schema (site-wide): - Establish clear hierarchy: Home → Solutions → [Solution Type] → [Page] - Improves SERP display and helps Google understand IA

  5. LocalBusiness schema (if Able Care has a physical office): - Address, phone, geo coordinates (for local SEO)

Template approach: - Use JSON-LD format in <head> - Validate all markup via schema.org validator and Google Rich Results Test - Add to master template to avoid duplication

Effort: Medium (4–6 hours implementation + QA across 20+ page types)

Timeline: Week 2–3

3. Missing H1 Tags (Medium–High)

Issue: About page, Grip Strength page, and Able Assess page lack H1 tags. Google struggles to identify page primary topic.

Impact: Weak topical relevance signals. Reduced click-through from SERPs. Poor accessibility.

Fix: - /about/: Add H1 "About Able Care" or "Meet the Clinical Team Behind Falls Prevention" - /solutions/grip-strength-screening/: Add H1 "Grip Strength Screening for Senior Living Operators" - /able-assess/ (or product page): Add H1 "Able Assess: Digital Falls Risk Screening in 5 Minutes" - Ensure one H1 per page, use as page title, place before fold

Effort: Low (15 mins)

Timeline: Week 1

4. Alt Text for 60+ Images (Medium)

Issue: Homepage and solution pages display 60+ images (logos, product screenshots, team photos) with empty alt="" attributes. Misses both accessibility and SEO opportunity.

Impact: Fails WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility standard. No crawl signal for logo, certification, and product imagery. Missed opportunity for image search traffic.

Fix: See Section D for detailed alt text recommendations. Implement across: - Trust logos (Harvard, Johns Hopkins, Oxford, Mount Sinai, MD Anderson, Mass General) - Certification badges (FDA, CE, ISO 27001, ISO 13485, HIPAA, GDPR) - Product screenshots and UI previews - Hero and feature images - Team member photos

Effort: Medium (2–3 hours to write and implement all alt text)

Timeline: Week 2

5. Homepage Indexation Gap (36 sitemapped → 7 indexed) (Critical)

Issue: Sitemap includes ~36 URLs; Google Search Console shows only ~7 indexed. ~29 URLs are sitemapped but not discovered or crawled.

Impact: Severe. 81% of sitemapped pages are invisible to Google. Likely causes: low crawl budget (tied to caching headers), low-quality content, internal link structure, or soft 404s.

Fix: 1. Diagnose via Search Console: - Pull coverage report: check for "Excluded by robots.txt", "Soft 404", "Crawled but not indexed" - Pull crawl statistics: compare to 90 days prior (is crawl rate declining?) - Pull site:able-care.co manual search to verify indexation

  1. Internal audit: - Check robots.txt for over-broad blocks - Verify no noindex meta tag on strategic pages - Check for duplicate content or thin pages - Audit internal link structure (crawlability)

  2. Remediation: - Remove caching headers (see fix #1) - Consolidate or remove thin/duplicate pages - Strengthen internal linking to undiscovered pages - Request recrawl in Search Console for strategic URLs - Add high-authority internal links from homepage/main sections

  3. Success metric: 80%+ of sitemapped URLs indexed within 4 weeks

Effort: High (depends on diagnosis; expect 8–12 hours)

Timeline: Week 1 (diagnosis) → Weeks 2–4 (remediation)

6. Subdomain Authority Leakage (resources.able-care.co) (Medium)

Issue: Blog and resource content lives at resources.able-care.co instead of /resources/ or /blog/. Subdomains are treated as separate sites by Google; authority (backlinks, content signals) doesn't consolidate with main domain.

Impact: Blog posts rank individually but can't leverage main domain authority. Cross-domain internal linking is weaker. Fragmented user journey.

Fix: 1. Strategy: Move all resources to /resources/ or /blog/ subdirectory 2. Technical migration: - Create 301 redirects: resources.able-care.co/* → able-care.co/resources/* - Update all internal links to point to new domain - Submit new URLs in Search Console (able-care.co) - Monitor crawl and indexation for 4 weeks - Remove old subdomain from Search Console once 100% migrated

  1. CMS: If using a CMS, consolidate into single domain in settings

Effort: Medium (4–6 hours including redirect setup, testing, monitoring)

Timeline: Week 3–4

7. TUG Test URL Cannibalisation (Low–Medium)

Issue: Multiple URLs may target the same keyword cluster (e.g., "Timed Up and Go test"): - Potential URLs: /solutions/timed-up-and-go/, /ability-assess/ (if it mentions TUG), blog post on TUG - Google must pick which to rank; authority dilutes

Impact: Weak keyword targeting. Pages compete internally instead of supporting each other.

Fix: 1. Audit: Map all URLs currently targeting "Timed Up and Go", "TUG", "mobility assessment", "dynamic balance" keywords 2. Consolidate: - Designate one primary page as the canonical target (e.g., /solutions/timed-up-and-go/) - Add <link rel="canonical" href="/solutions/timed-up-and-go/" /> to all other pages - Modify secondary pages to support (link to, not compete with) the primary - Consider merging thin secondary pages into primary

  1. Repeat for: - Grip strength keyword cluster - 30-second chair stand cluster - 4-metre gait speed cluster - Falls risk assessment (general) - STEADI-specific keyword cluster

Effort: Low–Medium (2–3 hours to identify + implement)

Timeline: Week 2

8. Meta Descriptions and Canonical Tags (Low)

Issue: 4 pages missing meta descriptions. Some pages lack canonical tags or have incorrect ones.

Impact: In SERPs, Google may auto-generate descriptions from page text (often poor). Duplicate content risk if canonicals are wrong.

Fix: 1. Audit: Identify all pages with missing/thin meta descriptions or incorrect canonicals 2. Rewrite meta descriptions: - All descriptions 155–160 characters - Frame for operator buyer (ROI, turnover reduction, compliance, liability) - Include primary keyword (naturally) - No keyword stuffing; natural language only - See Section C for priority page rewrites

  1. Add canonicals: - Self-referential canonicals on all pages - Ensure trailing slashes are consistent (choose one: /solutions/ or /solutions) - Test with URL inspection in Search Console

Effort: Low (1–2 hours)

Timeline: Week 2

9. Missing hreflang Tags for US/UK Content (Medium)

Issue: If Able Care operates in both US and UK markets, no hreflang tags clarify regional versions to Google.

Impact: Google may rank wrong regional variant in wrong market. Duplicate content penalties possible.

Fix: 1. Determine scope: Does able-care.co serve US and UK audiences with different content? - If yes, create parallel URL structures or language paths - If mostly UK with some US content, use hreflang sparingly

  1. Implementation options: - Option A (subdomain): us.able-care.co, uk.able-care.co with hreflang linking to each other - Option B (path): /us/, /uk/ with hreflang - Option C (parameter): ?region=us, ?region=uk (not recommended; less clean)

  2. Tag syntax in <head>: html <link rel="alternate" hreflang="en-US" href="https://us.able-care.co/solutions/" /> <link rel="alternate" hreflang="en-GB" href="https://able-care.co/solutions/" /> <link rel="alternate" hreflang="x-default" href="https://able-care.co/" />

  3. Sitemap: Include hreflang in XML sitemap for easier Google parsing

Effort: Medium (3–4 hours if regional structure needed)

Timeline: Week 3–4 (defer if US expansion is future; not critical for UK-first strategy)

10. No Pillar/Hub Content Structure (High—Strategic Priority)

Issue: Able Care has no thematic pillar pages or topic clusters that establish topical authority. Blog posts exist but aren't linked into a coherent hub strategy. Google struggles to identify areas of topical expertise.

Impact: Weak topical authority signals. Missed opportunity to rank for broad, high-intent keywords in falls prevention and senior living operations.

Fix: 1. Establish pillar pages (4–5 per solution segment): - Pillar 1: "Falls Prevention Strategy for Home Care Operators" (/solutions/home-care/) - Pillar 2: "Falls Risk Screening in Assisted Living Communities" (/solutions/assisted-living/) - Pillar 3: "Falls Assessment & Prevention in Skilled Nursing Facilities" (/solutions/skilled-nursing/) - Pillar 4: "Digital Falls Screening for Continuum Care" (/solutions/continuum-care/)

  1. For each pillar, create or repurpose 8–12 cluster content pieces: - Blog posts addressing specific pain points, workflows, regulations - Internal links from pillar page to cluster articles, cluster articles back to pillar - Use pillar as canonical for broad topic; let cluster articles own long-tail variations

  2. Example cluster for "Home Care Falls Prevention" pillar: - "Non-Clinical Staff Falls Screening: Onboarding 1099 Aides" (blog) - "Falls Prevention Compliance: Home Care Edition" (guide/blog) - "Grip Strength as a Predictor of Falls Risk in Home Care" (clinical white paper) - "STEADI-Based Falls Assessment in Home Care: Workflow Integration" (case study) - Links back to: /solutions/home-care/

  3. Technical implementation: - Each pillar page includes a "Related Articles" or "Learn More" section - Cluster articles link to pillar with anchor text containing primary keyword - Use category taxonomy in CMS to automate linking if possible

Effort: High (initial: 8–12 hours to plan structure; ongoing: content creation)

Timeline: Month 2 onwards (strategic long-term build)

Summary: Prioritised Fix Timeline

Week Fix Effort Impact
Week 1 Caching headers, H1 tags, robots.txt audit Low–Medium Critical indexation recovery
Week 2 Schema markup (site-wide + article), meta descriptions, alt text (phase 1) Medium–High Rich snippets, crawlability
Week 3–4 Subdomain migration, hreflang (if needed), canonicals, alt text (phase 2) Medium Domain consolidation, regional clarity
Month 2+ Pillar/hub content strategy, new segment pages High Long-term topical authority

Section B: Keyword Targeting Plan

This section maps target keywords to recommended URLs and provides intent, competition, and content guidance for the operator buyer.

Keyword Clusters by Intent
Primary (High Intent, Operator-Focused)
Keyword Recommended URL Intent Competition Page Type Content Brief
digital falls screening /solutions/digital-falls-screening/ Solution awareness Medium Pillar page What digital falls screening is; comparison to manual; workflow integration; ROI for operators. Operator frame: "Standardise screening across all staff roles, eliminate subjectivity."
fall prevention technology /solutions/fall-prevention-technology/ Solution awareness High Pillar page Overview of fall-prevention tech stack; sensor vs software vs hybrid; regulatory landscape. Frame: "Technology as an operational control, not just clinical tool."
STEADI falls screening /solutions/steadi-falls-screening/ Tactical/compliance Medium Hub/cluster page CDC STEADI protocol overview; how Able Assess implements STEADI; compliance tick-box. Frame: "Use CDC-backed protocols without clinical overhead."
SAFE Act falls screening /solutions/safe-act-falls-screening/ Compliance/regulatory Low–Medium Blog/resource Regulatory context (if SAFE Act applies to operator's jurisdiction); falls screening as regulatory control; audit readiness. Frame: "Meet regulatory expectations with standardised, documented screening."
falls prevention for home care /solutions/home-care/ Segment-specific Medium Pillar page Falls prevention tailored to home care ops: non-clinical staff, 1099 workforce, infection control, turnover. Frame: "Reduce falls-related turnover and rehospitalisations in home care."
falls screening for assisted living /solutions/assisted-living/ Segment-specific Medium Pillar page Falls screening in AL context: memory care, resident autonomy, family communication, state regulations. Frame: "Proactive falls prevention in memory care: early intervention, liability reduction."
falls risk assessment home health /solutions/home-health/ Segment-specific Medium–High Pillar page Falls assessment in skilled home health: interRAI/OASIS alignment, billing/outcomes documentation, RUG-IIIm compliance. Frame: "Falls assessment that improves outcomes and billing accuracy."
Timed Up and Go test /solutions/timed-up-and-go/ Tactical/education Low–Medium Cluster page What TUG is, why it matters, how to administer, interpretation. Positioning: "TUG as a standalone tool and one component of Able Assess's four-point screening."
grip strength screening /solutions/grip-strength-screening/ Tactical/education Low–Medium Cluster page Why grip strength is prognostic; population norms; how it predicts falls risk; how it measures frailty. Frame: "Grip strength: the five-minute marker of falls risk and biological resilience."
30-second chair stand test /solutions/30-second-chair-stand/ Tactical/education Low Cluster page Protocol, interpretation, use cases. Positioning: "Chair stand as lower-limb strength proxy."
4-metre gait speed /solutions/4-metre-gait-speed/ Tactical/education Low Cluster page Why gait speed matters clinically; interpretation; use in screening bundles.
falls risk factors assessment /solutions/falls-risk-factors/ Educational Low–Medium Blog/hub Multifactorial falls risk: intrinsic (strength, balance, cognition, medications) and extrinsic (environment, staffing). Frame: "Understand which factors your ops can control."
Secondary (Medium Intent, Awareness/Differentiation)
Keyword Recommended URL Intent Competition Page Type Content Brief
non-clinical falls assessment /solutions/ or /able-assess/ Differentiation Low Product page or blog Positioning: "Falls screening that non-clinical staff can deliver with fidelity." Addresses home care/franchise pain point.
objective falls risk screening /able-assess/ Differentiation Low Product page "Objective + objective = standardised: Able Assess vs subjective eyeball assessment." Contrasts with legacy practice.
fall detection vs fall prevention /solutions/ Educational Low Blog Clarify distinction: detection (react after fall) vs screening (prevent before). Contextualise Able Care in prevention layer.
senior living falls technology /solutions/senior-living/ (if pillar created) Awareness Medium–High Pillar page Broad positioning for senior living operators. Can slot multiple sub-segments (AL, IL, CCRC).
frailty screening /ability-assess/ or /blog/ Educational Medium Blog/product Frailty as falls risk driver; grip strength + TUG as frailty markers; Able Assess as frailty proxy.
Section GG falls screening /solutions/home-health/ or /blog/ Compliance Low–Medium Blog Medicare Section GG compliance: falls as quality outcome; reporting requirements; Able Assess as audit trail.
Tertiary (Low Intent, Brand/Support)
Keyword Recommended URL Intent Competition Page Type Content Brief
CDC STEADI certification /able-assess/ Verification Low Product or FAQ Does Able Assess have CDC endorsement? (Answer: No formal endorsement, but STEADI-aligned.) Manage expectations.
falls screening best practice /solutions/ or /blog/ Educational Medium Blog hub Industry consensus on best-practice screening protocols. Able Assess as implementation vehicle.
elderly fall prevention /solutions/ Awareness High Pillar or blog Broad, high-competition keyword. Target only if brand authority is strong enough; otherwise deprioritise.
home care compliance requirements /solutions/home-care/ Compliance Medium Blog/guide State regulations, CMS requirements, infection control, staffing models. Able Assess as compliance tool.
Keyword Research Findings

From web search, high-intent keywords with moderate-to-low competition opportunities:

  • "Digital falls screening" (medium volume, growing trend) — Able Care is positioned as the sole unified four-measurement platform; strong differentiation
  • "STEADI falls screening" + "falls risk assessment home health" — Moderate volume, regulatory-driven intent; Able Care aligns directly with CMS/Medicare workflows
  • "Fall prevention technology senior living" — Moderate–high volume; Able Care competes in dense space (SafelyYou, Linus Health, Zibrio); requires strong differentiation messaging
  • "Non-clinical falls assessment" — Low volume but high strategic relevance for home care/franchise segments; unique positioning for Able Care
  • "Grip strength screening" + "Timed Up and Go test" — Low–medium volume; educational; long-tail opportunity for thought leadership
Implementation Roadmap

Phase 1 (Weeks 1–4): - Create/optimise pillar pages for three core segments: Home Care, Assisted Living, Home Health - Map 12–15 high-intent keywords to pillar + cluster URLs - Publish 3–4 cluster blog posts to support pillars

Phase 2 (Month 2): - Expand to 4th pillar (Continuum Care or CCRC) - Conduct competitive keyword gap analysis: which keywords do SafelyYou, Linus Health, Zibrio own? Where can Able Care differentiate? - Add hreflang for US vs UK variants (if applicable)

Phase 3 (Month 3+): - Build out secondary/tertiary keyword content (FAQ, support, thought leadership) - Establish monthly content cadence to target 3–4 new long-tail keywords

Section C: Title Tag and Meta Description Rewrites

All rewrites below frame the operator buyer (PE partner, corporate COO, ED, franchise owner) and follow the template: Buyer Benefit | Category | Able Care

Priority Pages for Rewrite
1. Homepage

Current: (to be verified)
Recommended Title: Reduce Falls, Reduce Turnover | Falls Screening Platform | Able Care (60 chars)
Meta Description: Standardise falls screening across all staff roles. Able Assess screens five-minute in 5 minutes, aligns to CDC STEADI, and reduces falls-related turnover in home care and senior living. (156 chars)

Rationale: Lead with operator ROI (turnover), not clinical features. Mention STEADI for compliance credibility. "All staff roles" addresses non-clinical workforce pain point.

2. Able Assess Product Page (/able-assess/)

Recommended Title: Objective Falls Screening for Non-Clinical Staff | Able Assess (61 chars)
Meta Description: No clinical degree required. Able Assess delivers objective, STEADI-aligned falls risk stratification in five minutes with any staff member. FDA cleared, ISO 27001. (155 chars)

Rationale: "No clinical degree required" is operator gold (solves 1099 workforce problem). Emphasise objective + speed. Add regulatory credibility (FDA, ISO).

3. About Page (/about/)

Current: (unknown)
Recommended Title: Trusted by Leading Healthcare Systems | About Able Care (54 chars)
Meta Description: Trusted by Harvard, Johns Hopkins, Oxford, and Mount Sinai. Able Care's founding team created the Grip Strength Test and leads digital falls prevention in senior living. (157 chars)

Rationale: Lead with trust (logos), not company history. Anchor credibility to named institutions. "Digital falls prevention in senior living" positions against competitors.

4. Solutions Hub Page (/solutions/)

Recommended Title: Falls Prevention Platform for Home Care & Senior Living | Able Care (65 chars)
Meta Description: Customised falls screening for home care, assisted living, skilled nursing, and CCRC operators. Reduce census loss, turnover, and liability with objective, staff-wide screening. (161 chars)

Rationale: Segment diversity messaging. Operator metrics (census, turnover, liability). "Staff-wide" emphasises non-clinical capability.

5. Home Care Solution Page (/solutions/home-care/)

Recommended Title: Reduce Falls Turnover in Home Care | Non-Clinical Staff Screening (64 chars)
Meta Description: Standardise falls screening across 1099 aides with no clinical training required. Reduce falls-related turnover, improve compliance, cut rehospitalisation rates. (157 chars)

Rationale: "1099 aides" signals exact persona. "No clinical training required" solves home care's staffing crisis. Outcomes: turnover, compliance, rehospitalisation.

6. Assisted Living Solution Page (/solutions/assisted-living/)

Recommended Title: Falls Prevention Strategy for Memory Care & AL Communities (59 chars)
Meta Description: Standardised falls screening for AL and memory care. Reduce liability, improve family communication, keep residents independent longer with early intervention. (157 chars)

Rationale: "Memory care" signals AL's dominant segment. Outcomes: liability (operator pain), family satisfaction (market differentiator), resident autonomy (marketing angle).

7. Home Health Solution Page (/solutions/home-health/)

Recommended Title: Falls Risk Assessment Aligned to Section GG & OASIS | Home Health (65 chars)
Meta Description: Medicare-compliant falls assessment that improves Section GG outcomes and RUG-IIIm reimbursement. Five-minute screening, objective documentation, audit-ready. (158 chars)

Rationale: "Section GG" and "OASIS" are compliance keywords. "RUG-IIIm" signals reimbursement (home health CFO cares deeply). Emphasise audit trail.

8. Blog/Resources Hub (/resources/ or /blog/)

Recommended Title: Falls Prevention Guides & Operator Resources | Able Care (54 chars)
Meta Description: Downloadable guides, case studies, and evidence for falls prevention in home care, assisted living, and senior living. STEADI, regulatory compliance, staffing strategies. (160 chars)

Rationale: Frame as resource centre for operators. Keywords (STEADI, compliance, staffing) signal expertise. Mention downloadables to increase CTR.

9. Pricing or Demo Page (/pricing/ or /demo/)

Recommended Title: See Able Assess in Action | 15-Minute Demo (41 chars)
Meta Description: See how home care and AL operators standardise falls screening in under five minutes. Book a guided demo with our team. FDA cleared, staff-friendly, STEADI-aligned. (160 chars)

Rationale: Clear CTA. Emphasise speed and staff-friendliness. Add regulatory credibility.

10. Evidence/Research Page (/evidence/ or /research/)

Recommended Title: Clinical Evidence & Peer-Reviewed Studies | Able Care (54 chars)
Meta Description: Access peer-reviewed publications, clinical validation studies, and regulatory documentation. Grip strength, TUG, falls risk stratification, outcomes data. (157 chars)

Rationale: Frame for both clinical (champions) and operator (procurement) audiences. Lead with "peer-reviewed" for credibility. Signal data-driven approach.

General Title + Meta Description Guidelines

  1. Operator buyer frame always: - Lead with ROI/outcome: "Reduce turnover", "Improve compliance", "Cut liability" - Avoid clinical jargon unless absolutely necessary - Name segments: "Home care", "Assisted living", "Senior living" - Quantify when possible: "In 5 minutes", "No clinical degree", "FDA cleared"

  2. Keyword placement: - Primary keyword in title (left-weighted for CTR) - Secondary keyword in meta description (natural, not forced) - Both title and description should be readable aloud

  3. Technical: - Titles: 50–60 characters (Google shows 50–65 on desktop, 40–45 on mobile) - Meta descriptions: 155–160 characters (Google shows 155–165 on desktop, 120–130 on mobile) - Use pipes (|) or dashes (–) to separate components for readability

  4. Testing: - Before/after A/B test in Search Console after 4 weeks - Monitor CTR changes via Search Console - Rewrite if CTR drops >5% without ranking change

Section D: Alt Text Recommendations

All alt text is written for accessibility (screen readers) and SEO. Keep descriptions concise (80–125 characters), descriptive, and operator-relevant.

Trust Logos (Affiliation Badges)
Logo Alt Text Rationale
Harvard Harvard Medical School logo – trusted partner in digital health Identifies institution, establishes credibility. Screen reader users need to know what the logo represents.
Johns Hopkins Johns Hopkins University logo – clinical validation partner Signals validation/research partnership.
Oxford University of Oxford logo – international academic partnership Global credibility marker.
Mount Sinai Mount Sinai Health System logo – US healthcare leader Establishes US market credibility.
MD Anderson MD Anderson Cancer Center logo – oncology falls prevention research Signals specialist clinical focus.
Mass General Massachusetts General Hospital logo – founding institution partner Establishes flagship hospital association.
Certification Badges
Badge Alt Text Rationale
FDA Cleared FDA cleared medical device mark – regulatory compliance Users need to know this is a regulatory approval, not just a logo.
CE Marked CE mark – European regulatory compliance for Able Assess Signals EU market approval.
ISO 27001 ISO 27001 security certification – HIPAA-compliant data handling Operator cares about data security; this signals compliance.
ISO 13485 ISO 13485 medical device quality certification Quality assurance signal for regulated device.
HIPAA HIPAA privacy and security compliance – protected health information Operator needs to know patient data is protected.
GDPR GDPR compliance badge – European privacy regulation For UK/EU operators, signals GDPR adherence.
Product Screenshots and UI Elements
Element Alt Text Rationale
Homepage hero video placeholder Able Assess interface showing four-step screening workflow: grip strength, Timed Up and Go, chair stand, and gait speed measurements Describes the primary user journey. Screen reader users should understand the product's core value (four-step screening).
Dashboard / Results screen Able Assess risk stratification dashboard displaying low, intermediate, and high falls-risk tiers with colour coding and staff action recommendations Explains what the operator sees after screening. Emphasises the actionable output.
Mobile app screenshot Able Assess mobile app on iPad showing non-clinical staff conducting a Timed Up and Go test with a resident Real-world use case. Shows non-clinical usability (operator benefit).
Setup/onboarding screen Able Assess setup guide for training non-clinical staff on five-minute screening protocol Emphasises ease of staff adoption.
Compliance report / Audit trail Ability Assess audit report showing standardised screening data, resident risk tier, intervention history, and timestamps for regulatory compliance Operator cares about documentation and audit readiness.
Hero and Feature Images
Image Type Alt Text Rationale
Homepage hero (home care aide with elderly resident) Home care aide performing a standardised falls risk screening with an elderly resident using Able Assess on a mobile device Contextualises the product in a realistic scenario. Screen reader users understand the setting and use case.
Assisted living memory care image Assisted living community staff member administering a Timed Up and Go balance test to measure falls risk in a memory care resident Specific segment visual. Emphasises staff (not clinician) administering test.
Home health nurse with patient Home health nurse documenting falls risk assessment data during in-home visit, improving Section GG compliance Operator-relevant: emphasises documentation/compliance benefit.
Senior living operator at desk reviewing dashboard Senior living operator viewing falls screening analytics on dashboard to identify high-risk residents and improve care planning Operator use case. Emphasises the decision-making benefit of having screening data.
Team photo (Able Care founders) Able Care founding team: [names, titles if visible]. Clinical and engineering backgrounds in falls prevention and digital health Adds credibility and human context. Screen reader users should know who they are.
General Alt Text Rules for Able Care
  1. Be specific: "Able Assess interface" not "screenshot"
  2. Operator frame: Emphasise outcomes (compliance, staff ease, data) not just features
  3. Keep under 125 characters: Screen reader users don't want essays
  4. Avoid: "Image of", "Picture of", "Click here", "Learn more" (redundant with context)
  5. For decorative images: Use empty alt="" (screen readers skip) — reserve descriptive alt for meaningful images
  6. Audit: Use axe DevTools or WAVE browser extension to find missing alt text after implementation

Appendix: Web Search Insights & Competitive Context

Search Landscape Summary

Research identified these keyword opportunities and competitive dynamics:

  1. "Digital falls screening" — Moderate search volume, growing trend as senior living digitises. Able Care is the only bundled four-measurement platform; strong differentiation if marketed to operators.

  2. "STEADI falls screening" + "falls risk assessment home health" — Regulatory-driven keywords. CMS/Medicare compliance is primary intent. Able Care's alignment with CDC STEADI protocol is valuable SEO asset.

  3. "Fall prevention technology senior living" — High volume, dense competition (SafelyYou, Linus Health, Zibrio mentioned). Requires strong differentiation on operator ROI (turnover, liability, staffing) not just clinical efficacy.

  4. "Non-clinical falls assessment" — Low volume, high strategic relevance. Able Care's positioning as the non-clinical platform (unique in market) should own this keyword cluster.

  5. Wearables + AI detection — Competitive threats focus on fall detection (after fall occurs) vs. Able Care's screening (prevents fall). Messaging must clearly separate "prevention" from "detection" to avoid conflation with Lifeline/ADT/PERS products.

Competitive Context (Layer 2 Players)
  • SafelyYou: Dominates "fall detection" via AI cameras. Able Care competes on "screening before fall", not "detecting after fall". Positioning is complementary, not directly competitive.
  • Linus Health: Harvard-origin, well-funded cognitive + functional platform. Broader scope (cognition + mobility). Able Care's narrow focus on physical falls screening is defensible for home care/AL segment.
  • Zibrio, VirtuSense, Nymbl Science: Sensor-heavy or app-based. Able Care's human-centric (non-sensor, any staff usable) approach differentiates.
Sources Consulted
  • CDC STEADI Algorithm and Toolkit: https://www.cdc.gov/steadi
  • Fall Prevention Technology Research: Nature Digital Health, PMC/NIH Articles
  • Home Care Best Practices: Missouri Alliance for Home Care MAHC-10 Tool
  • Senior Living Fall Prevention: ComForCare, Chateau Retirement, US News Health

Implementation Checklist

Tier 1: Quick Wins (Week 1–2)
  • [ ] Remove no-store, no-cache cache headers
  • [ ] Add H1 tags to About, Grip Strength, Able Assess pages
  • [ ] Submit robots.txt audit report
  • [ ] Request Search Console recrawl for strategic URLs
Tier 2: On-Page Foundation (Week 2–3)
  • [ ] Implement site-wide Organisation + Product schema
  • [ ] Rewrite homepage, product, solution titles and meta descriptions (Section C)
  • [ ] Add alt text to all trust logos, certifications, product screenshots (Section D)
  • [ ] Map keywords to URLs (Section B)
Tier 3: Domain & Structure (Week 3–4)
  • [ ] Plan and execute subdomain migration (resources → /resources/)
  • [ ] Add 301 redirects; monitor indexation
  • [ ] Implement pillar/hub site structure (if pursuing topical authority strategy)
Tier 4: Content & Authority (Month 2+)
  • [ ] Publish 3–4 cluster blog articles supporting primary pillars
  • [ ] Create /evidence/ page with research, white papers, case studies
  • [ ] Establish monthly keyword content calendar
  • [ ] Monitor Search Console for ranking changes; A/B test titles if needed

Document Owner: Marketing Operations
Last Updated: April 2026
Next Review: May 2026 (post-implementation assessment)

GEO baseline audit

GEO Baseline Audit: Able Care

Date: April 2026
Scope: Generative Engine Optimisation baseline assessment for Able Assess positioning and content strategy.

Executive Summary

Web search analysis across 15 buyer-language queries reveals significant GEO gaps for Able Care. Whilst traditional falls risk tools (Hendrich II, Morse Scale) and clinical frameworks (CDC STEADI) dominate generalist searches, no competitor strongly owns the emerging non-clinical, staff-usable, five-minute-completion space that Able Assess occupies.

Able Care appears in only 2 of 15 target searches, both within blog posts about specific assessment components rather than homepage authority. The biggest opportunities lie in queries combining speed + non-clinical usability + digital standardisation, where high buyer intent meets low competitive density.

Query-by-Query Analysis

1. Best falls risk assessment for assisted living

Top brands/sources: - Joerns Healthcare (equipment provider with content hub) - Relias (online training platform) - The Hendrich II Fall Risk Model (tool, not product) - AHRQ (government resource) - Higher Point Residence / McKendree (facility operator blogs)

Able Care appears: No

Content structure: Mixed – combination of vendor how-to guides, educational content about assessment tools, and facility operator case studies. Listicle structure popular.

Competitive density: Moderate-to-high. Fragmented across equipment vendors (Joerns) and training platforms (Relias), but no single product-company dominates the assisted-living-specific angle.

2. Digital falls screening platform

Top brands/sources: - Able Care (appears prominently with three blog posts: gait speed test, chair-to-stand test, TUG test) - OneStep - OAK Device (Khymeia) - Clovi (blog on paper vs. digital) - Screeners.com - Academic research (PMC, PubMed on wearable sensors)

Able Care appears: Yes – but only in blog content, not product/homepage authority

Content structure: Mix of academic research, vendor comparison blogs, and product pages. Strong presence from blog comparisons.

Competitive density: Lower than expected. OneStep and OAK underrepresented in SERPs; Able Care's blog content is among the few direct product examples cited.

3. Fall prevention technology for home care

Top brands/sources: - ComForCare (home care franchise blog) - Leading Age (industry publication) - US News Health (consumer buying guide) - Amazon (product listing aggregator) - Amara Home Care (competitor blog) - Academic research (PMC articles on detection/monitoring systems)

Able Care appears: No

Content structure: Listicles (top X technologies), buying guides, vendor comparison blogs, academic overviews, Amazon product pages. Guides and blogs rank higher than vendor homepages.

Competitive density: Moderate. Broadly competitive category; many blog-based resources without clear product winner.

4. How do PE-backed home care operators reduce liability

Top brands/sources: - Trillian Health (market research) - PE industry publications (PE Stakeholder, Modern Healthcare) - Home Health Care News (industry journalism) - Home Care Magazine (trade publication) - CEPR, academic research on PE impact - Insurance guides (7 Must-Have Coverages article)

Able Care appears: No

Content structure: Editorial journalism, market research reports, industry white papers, insurance provider guidance. Very little product vendor presence.

Competitive density: Low product competition, but high editorial/research presence. This is a business operations query, not a product query.

Key insight: Buyers here are researching PE strategies, not software solutions. Able Care could position as a liability reduction tool (falls prevention = reduced risk, reduced litigation exposure).

5. STEADI falls screening software

Top brands/sources: - StopFalls.app (explicitly CDC STEADI-based; ranks first) - CDC official STEADI resources - NC Falls Prevention Coalition - Network Health (explainer on STEADI scoring) - Fall Risk Streamlit App (academic implementation) - Safe Communities Madison (public health)

Able Care appears: No

Content structure: Official CDC toolkit pages, vendor product pages (StopFalls), educational explainers, academic implementations. CDC authority dominates.

Competitive density: Moderate. StopFalls.app has clear ownership of "STEADI software" position, but CDC presents STEADI as a framework, not a vendor product. Opportunity to position Able Assess as "The digital implementation layer for STEADI."

6. Grip strength assessment for senior care

Top brands/sources: - Physiopedia (clinical knowledge base) - BTE Technologies (hand strengthening devices) - Academic research (BMC Geriatrics, PubMed, Nature journals) - CGA Toolkit Plus (comprehensive geriatric assessment) - ScienceDirect (research aggregator)

Able Care appears: No

Content structure: Academic papers, clinical education resources, device vendor pages, clinical knowledge bases. Very little consumer or operators-focused content.

Competitive density: Very low product competition. Most results are academic or clinical training resources. Gap opportunity: "grip strength assessment for non-clinical staff in care homes."

7. Falls prevention for CCRCs

Top brands/sources: - VirtuSense (explicitly positions for CCRC market) - NCOA (evidence-based programmes guide) - CDC (general community prevention) - Harborview Injury Prevention Center (LTC toolkit) - Falcon's Landing (facility operator blog)

Able Care appears: No

Content structure: Research guides, facility operator blogs, vendor product pages (VirtuSense). Clinical evidence frameworks common.

Competitive density: Lower-to-moderate. VirtuSense has explicit CCRC positioning; no Able Care equivalent.

8. Non-clinical falls screening tool

Top brands/sources: - Physiopedia (FRAT tool documentation) - Relias (standardised tools overview) - Academic research (systematic reviews, PMC) - CDC Foundation (Falls Free CheckUp tool) - Exer.ai (health outcomes blog) - MedlinePlus (government health info)

Able Care appears: No

Content structure: Academic systematic reviews, clinical knowledge bases, government resource pages, blog explainers. NICE guidance mentioned (2025).

Competitive density: Moderate. Non-clinical focus is explicit in several results, but most centre on clinical tools being adapted. Gap: a product explicitly designed from inception for non-clinical staff.

9. SAFE Act falls screening compliance

Top brands/sources: - APTA (physical therapy professional association) - Federal Reserve (mortgage SAFE Act) - NCUA, CFPB, FDIC (other mortgage SAFE Act resources) - Able Care blog post on Able Assess

Able Care appears: Yes – but in a tangential way (product mention in blog, not the primary SERP result)

Content structure: Legislative documents, regulatory guidance, professional association news. Very little vendor content.

Competitive density: Very low product competition. Query is legislative/regulatory, not product-focused. But the legislation creates a tailwind for falls screening adoption.

Key insight: SAFE Act (Stopping Addiction and Falls for the Elderly Act) is still being legislated and isn't yet a compliance requirement. Early mover advantage for products that can help operators meet this future requirement.

10. Falls risk assessment for skilled nursing facilities

Top brands/sources: - Joerns Healthcare (evidence-based practices guide) - AHRQ (official nursing home safety programme) - Cedar Hill Care (facility blog) - Texas HHS (state guidance) - Academic research (PMC, MDPI, ScienceDirect) - Morse Fall Scale and Hendrich II (tools, not products)

Able Care appears: No

Content structure: Institutional guidance, facility operator blogs, academic evidence, clinical guidelines. Vendor presence limited to equipment providers (Joerns).

Competitive density: Moderate. No software vendor has clear ownership; tools (Morse, Hendrich) are the main references.

11. Five minute falls screening

Top brands/sources: - CDC Foundation (Falls Free CheckUp) - CDC STEADI resources - Relias (tools overview) - Academic research (five-minute cognitive tests, not falls-specific) - MDinteractive (MIPS quality measure)

Able Care appears: No

Content structure: Official CDC toolkit, regulatory quality measure documents, academic research. Limited vendor product pages.

Competitive density: Low. "Five-minute" is underexploited as a positioning angle. CDC resources mention various assessment times but no single product owns the "five-minute speed" positioning.

Key insight: Able Assess's five-minute promise is a major differentiator that is not yet represented in the top SERPs. This is a significant opportunity.

12. CDC aligned falls prevention platform

Top brands/sources: - CDC STEADI official resources - CDC Foundation (Falls Partners programme, LIFE tool) - APTQI (PT-focused news) - Academic research (PMC articles on STEADI adoption) - NCOA (NCOA resources)

Able Care appears: No

Content structure: Official government toolkit pages, programme pages, educational content, academic research. Very few vendor products mentioned.

Competitive density: Low product competition. CDC STEADI is positioned as a framework, not a vendor product. Opportunity to position Able Assess as "the digital platform built on CDC STEADI."

13. Home care technology for franchise operators

Top brands/sources: - CareSmartz360 (franchise management software) - AxisCare (franchise operations) - Home Helpers (franchise brand blog) - BrightStar Care (franchise brand) - Axxess (home care software) - Tendio (franchise tech platform) - International Franchise Association (industry research)

Able Care appears: No

Content structure: Vendor product pages, franchise operator blogs, franchise comparison content, industry research. Heavy vendor presence.

Competitive density: High. Multiple established competitors in franchise-focused home care tech. But most focus on scheduling, billing, and care coordination – not falls risk or liability reduction.

Key insight: Able Care could differentiate by positioning Able Assess as the "non-clinical staff enablement tool" that franchise operators need to reduce liability and standardise care across multi-unit networks.

14. Reduce falls in assisted living

Top brands/sources: - ECRI Institute (best practices guide) - Pegasus Senior Living (facility operator blog) - AHCA (American Health Care Association – industry body) - Various facility operator blogs (Oaks at Inglewood, HPU Online) - Academic research (PMC, HCQIS) - Texas HHS (state guidance)

Able Care appears: No

Content structure: Best practices guides, facility operator blogs, institutional guidance, academic evidence. Equipment vendors (grab bars, etc.) mentioned but no software vendor dominates.

Competitive density: Moderate. Content-heavy but fragmented. No product company has claimed the "assisted living falls reduction" space.

15. Falls prevention ROI calculator

Top brands/sources: - Fall Protect (workplace fall protection ROI calculator) - Hillrom (hospital falls burden calculator) - NCOA (evidence-based programme ROI report) - American Data Network (Morse Fall Scale calculator) - Academic research on ROI (PubMed, UK government)

Able Care appears: No

Content structure: Interactive calculators (vendor tools), research reports, academic ROI studies. Strong interest in quantifying cost-benefit.

Competitive density: Low but growing. Hillrom's calculator exists for hospitals; no residential/assisted-living ROI calculator found. Major gap.

Gap Analysis

Biggest Opportunity Gaps (High Buyer Intent + Low Competition + Able Care Absent)

Tier 1 – Critical Gaps:

  1. "Five-minute falls screening" (Query #11) - High intent: Buyers want speed; this is explicitly what they're searching for - Very low competition: No product company has claimed this angle - Able Care invisible but perfectly positioned (Able Assess takes exactly five minutes) - Why it matters: Speed is a core buying signal in busy, under-staffed home care and assisted living

  2. "CDC aligned falls prevention platform" (Query #12) - High intent: Regulatory/clinical alignment is essential for adoption - Low competition: CDC STEADI treated as a framework, not a product category - Able Care invisible despite being STEADI-aligned - Why it matters: Regulatory confidence unlocks enterprise sales to operators and PE-backed groups

  3. "Non-clinical falls screening tool" (Query #8) - High intent: Operators explicitly want non-clinical usability; signals the PE/franchise segment - Low-to-moderate competition: Confusion between clinical tools and non-clinical adaptation - Able Care invisible despite this being Able Assess's core differentiator - Why it matters: Non-clinical usability is not yet a crowded positioning, but it's what PE-backed operators need most

Tier 2 – High-Potential Gaps:

  1. "Falls prevention ROI calculator" (Query #15) - High intent: Decision-makers calculating cost-benefit; direct purchase signal - Very low competition: Only Hillrom (hospitals) and workplace safety firms have tools - Able Care invisible; no ROI calculator published - Why it matters: PE-backed and franchised operators make budget decisions on ROI; a public calculator is a lead magnet

  2. "Grip strength assessment for senior care" (Query #6) - High intent: Buyers want to understand one of Able Assess's four core measurements - Very low competition: Mostly academic/clinical content; no commercial product owns this angle - Able Care invisible - Why it matters: Educational content here becomes a "trojan horse" for Able Assess product discovery

  3. "How do PE-backed home care operators reduce liability" (Query #4) - High intent: Target buyer segment explicitly searching for risk mitigation - Low product competition: Editorial and research content dominate; no vendor has claimed this angle - Able Care invisible but could position falls screening as a liability reduction tool - Why it matters: PE operators care about litigation risk; Able Assess prevents falls, reduces liability claims

Tier 3 – Building Blocks:

  1. "Best falls risk assessment for assisted living" (Query #1) - Moderate-to-high intent; moderate competition (Joerns, Relias, tools) - Able Care missing; opportunity to contest the assisted-living segment

  2. "Home care technology for franchise operators" (Query #13) - High intent; high competition but mostly non-falls-related tech - Able Care missing; opportunity to be the falls/liability solution in the franchise tech stack

Three Priority "Definitive Answer" Pages

Priority 1: "Five-Minute Falls Screening: Why Speed Matters in Assisted Living"

Target query: "Five minute falls screening"
Intent level: High (buyers searching explicitly for speed)
Current SERP state: Dominated by CDC generic resources; no product addresses speed directly

Recommended page structure: 1.
