Hero statement (200 words): "Why five-minute screenings are changing assisted living" – opens with the problem (staff time scarcity), introduces speed as a solution, positions Able Assess implicitly 2. Four-part assessment breakdown (800 words): Deep dive into each of Able Assess's four measurements (grip, TUG, chair-to-stand, gait) with: - Clinical definition (20 words max) - What it measures (e.g., "lower-limb strength predicts fall risk") - Time required (all fit within 5 minutes combined) - Why non-clinical staff can administer it - Visual flow diagram showing total time breakdown 3. Comparison table (200 words): Traditional assessments (Hendrich II, Morse, STEADI tools) vs. five-minute digital screening; emphasise CDC alignment 4. Implementation guide (600 words): How to roll out in assisted living; addresses non-clinical staff training, integration with existing workflows 5. ROI section (300 words): Cost per screen, time saved per staff member per month, liability reduction angle 6. FAQ (300 words): Regulatory questions, staff training, clinical validity

Key content elements: - "Five-minute, four-measurement standard" as the lead differentiator - CDC STEADI alignment stated explicitly - Non-clinical usability emphasis throughout - Visual timeline showing how Able Assess fits the busy schedule - Staff-testimonial section (future: case study quotes from franchise operators)

Which action plan tasks address this gap: - Task [falls risk content series]: Directly addresses this - Task [competitor comparative content]: Enables the Hendrich II vs. Able Assess angle - Task [video content]: Five-minute demo video would support this page

Priority 2: "How Non-Clinical Staff Can Screen for Fall Risk: A PE-Backed Operator's Guide"

Target queries: "Non-clinical falls screening tool," "How do PE-backed home care operators reduce liability"
Intent level: High (PE/franchise operators searching for operational solutions and liability reduction)
Current SERP state: Editorial content about PE liability strategies; no product addresses the staff-enablement angle

Recommended page structure: 1. Hero statement (250 words): "Why PE-backed operators need non-clinical fall screening" – opens with the problem (clinical staff scarcity, multi-unit training burden, liability exposure), positions non-clinical enablement as the solution 2. The PE-backed operator's dilemma (400 words): - 1099 and part-time staff can't be trained like clinical nurses - Liability exposure increases with scale (multi-unit operators) - Consistency challenge across franchised locations - Cost per screening must be near zero 3. Non-clinical screening methodology (600 words): - Why traditional tools (Morse, Hendrich II) require clinical interpretation - Able Assess's non-clinical design: objective measurements, no subjective judgment, staff training time measured in minutes - Walkthrough of the four measurements with non-clinical staff in mind 4. Implementation playbook for PE operators (800 words): - On-site staff training (time required, no clinical prerequisites) - Integration with shift handovers - Data collection and trending - Escalation to clinical review (when needed) - Multi-unit reporting and standardisation 5. Liability reduction evidence (400 words): - Falls screening as a documented standard of care - Reduction in incident claims (cite any case studies available) - Insurance premium angle (if any) - Regulatory compliance (SAFE Act positioning) 6. FAQ (300 words): Staff training burden, integration with EHRs, compliance questions

Key content elements: - PE and franchise operator persona explicitly throughout - Liability/risk mitigation as the primary value driver - Non-clinical usability as the enabler - Standardisation across multi-unit networks emphasis - Walkthrough of the four measurements with minimal training - Data consistency and reporting (PE operators care about aggregated metrics)

Which action plan tasks address this gap: - Task [PE-backed operator segment content]: Directly - Task [liability messaging]: Directly - Task [multi-unit network case study]: Supports this with evidence

Priority 3: "Falls Prevention ROI: What a Screening Program Really Costs (and Saves)"

Target query: "Falls prevention ROI calculator"
Intent level: Very high (ROI queries are late-stage purchase signals; buyers are calculating cost-benefit)
Current SERP state: Hillrom has a calculator for hospitals; no residential/assisted living ROI calculator exists

Recommended page structure: 1. Interactive ROI calculator (tool): - Input: Facility size, staff size, number of residents screened per month, average insurance claim cost (pre-filled industry data) - Outputs: Total cost of screening programme per year, estimated fall reductions (cite clinical data), estimated liability cost savings, break-even timeline - Embeddable on website; downloadable as PDF report 2. The full cost of a fall (educational section, 400 words): - Direct costs: Hospital, treatment, rehabilitation - Indirect costs: Staff time, litigation, insurance claims - Emotional and reputational costs - Cite specific costs (e.g., average hip fracture treatment cost) 3. ROI of falls screening (educational section, 600 words): - Cost per screening with Able Assess (time-based, staff cost) - Screening frequency per resident per year - Estimated fall reduction (cite clinical data on four-measurement effectiveness) - Calculation: Cost of programme vs. avoided falls * cost per fall - Timeline to positive ROI (typically 6–12 months for medium-sized facilities) 4. Three case study scenarios (600 words): - Small assisted living (50 residents, mostly non-clinical staff) - Medium franchise group (5 locations, mixed staff) - Large CCRC (200+ residents, clinical on staff) - For each: cost breakdown, estimated falls reduction, net savings 5. Regulatory angle (300 words): - Falls screening as a standard of care (reduces liability exposure) - SAFE Act positioning (future compliance) - Insurance premium reductions (cite any data available) 6. Implementation cost section (400 words): - Licensing cost (if any) - Staff training (time required, cost) - Technology integration (EHR, if applicable) - Ongoing support and data management

Key content elements: - Transparent, easy-to-understand calculator (not hidden behind a form; public access) - Downloadable ROI report for each scenario - Industry-standard cost figures (cited, sourced) - Conservative estimate assumptions (underpromise, overdeliver) - Visual charts showing break-even timeline - Comparison with traditional assessment methods (cost per Morse screening vs. Able Assess)

Which action plan tasks address this gap: - Task [ROI case studies]: Directly supports content development - Task [PE-backed operator segment content]: Data feeds the calculator - Task [sales enablement materials]: ROI calculator becomes a top sales asset

GEO Optimisation Recommendations

Generative engines (Google's SGE, Claude, Perplexity, ChatGPT) prioritise sources differently from traditional search engines. They reward structured authority, cited definitions, and comprehensive coverage of a topic's known universe. Below are evidence-backed optimisations for Able Care content.

1. Schema Markup and Structured Data

Why it matters: Generative engines extract factual claims from structured data (Schema.org) more reliably than from prose. Schema signals authoritative information to AI models.

Implementations for Able Care:

  • FAQPage schema on all educational content
  • Format: Question → Answer pairs addressing "how do I administer grip strength assessment?" "What makes Able Assess non-clinical?" "How long does screening take?"
  • Generative engines surface FAQ schema answers directly in responses

  • Example: <script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https://schema.org","@type":"FAQPage","mainEntity":[{"@type":"Question","name":"How long does an Able Assess screening take?","acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Five minutes, covering four measurements: grip strength, timed up and go, 30-second chair stand, and 4-metre gait speed."}}]}</script>

  • ScholarlyArticle schema on clinical/evidence-based content

  • Mark educational guides and whitepapers as scholastic content
  • Include author (clinical or editorial authority), publication date, and citations

  • Signals to generative engines that the content is peer-vetted or evidence-based

  • Product schema on Able Assess landing page

  • Name, description, brand (Able Care)
  • AggregateRating (once customer testimonials/case studies exist)

  • Offers (pricing, if public)

  • LocalBusiness schema if Able Care launches UK office

  • Signals location-specific service availability
2. FAQ Structure (Format-Level Optimisation)

Generative engines prefer structured Q&A over narrative prose for factual claims.

Implementation:

  • Add a "Frequently Asked Questions" section to every major page
  • Structure as 5–10 questions that directly mirror buyer searches
  • Examples for Able Care:
  • "What is grip strength assessment?" (mirrors query #6)
  • "Can non-clinical staff administer falls screening?" (mirrors query #8)
  • "How does Able Assess align with CDC STEADI?" (mirrors query #12)
  • "What is the five-minute falls screening methodology?" (mirrors query #11)

  • "What does the four-measurement approach measure?" (foundational)

  • Keep answers concise (100 words max)

  • Link to longer content where needed; don't bury the answer
3. Clear Definitions and Entity Naming

Generative engines resolve ambiguous terms by extracting definitions. Able Care should own explicit, consistent definitions.

Implementation:

  • Define the four measurements early on every relevant page:

Use exact consistent phrasing each time: - "Single Maximum Grip Strength Test (SMGT) – peak grip force as a marker of muscle strength and biological resilience" - "Timed Up and Go (TUG) – mobility and dynamic balance assessment" - "30-Second Chair to Stand (CTS) – lower-limb strength measure" - "4-Metre Gait Speed – functional locomotion assessment"

Consistency aids AI model training on Able Care terminology.

  • Define Able Care's positioning clearly:

"Able Assess is the first falls-risk screening platform that captures all four integral physical measurements in a single five-minute workflow, delivered by any clinical or non-clinical staff member."

Repeat this exact phrasing (or minor variants) across all key pages. Consistency helps generative engines internalise your claim.

  • Distinguish Able Assess from other categories:

Use explicit language: - "Not a fall detection system (which reacts after a fall occurs); a fall prevention system (which identifies risk before a fall)" - "Not a clinical-only tool; designed for non-clinical staff usability" - "Not a traditional risk prediction scale; an objective, data-driven physical measurement platform"

4. Comprehensive Coverage of Topic Universe

Generative engines reward sources that cover a known topic comprehensively. Able Care should map the "falls screening universe" and ensure coverage of all relevant elements.

Implementation:

  • Create a hub page or section titled "Complete Guide to Falls Risk Assessment" that covers: 1. What falls risk screening is 2. Why it matters (clinical evidence) 3. The four key measurements Able Assess uses 4. Comparison with traditional tools (Hendrich II, Morse, STEADI) 5. Regulatory and compliance context (SAFE Act, CDC STEADI) 6. Implementation in different settings (assisted living, CCRCs, home care, franchises) 7. ROI and cost justification 8. Staff training and non-clinical usability 9. Data privacy and integration 10. Future of digital screening

  • Link all related content (blog posts, product pages, case studies) back to this hub

  • Use consistent terminology throughout

  • This "comprehensive coverage" approach signals to generative engines that Able Care is a serious authority on falls screening

5. Structured Data for Medical/Clinical Content

If Able Care publishes clinical evidence, follow HealthKnowledgeGraph standards.

Implementation:

  • For clinical evidence pages (clinical trials, efficacy data):
  • Use MedicalWebPage schema
  • Include "Medical Audience" declaration (healthcare professionals, operators, decision-makers)
  • Cite sources (PubMed IDs, URLs) using standard markup
  • Example: <script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https://schema.org","@type":"MedicalWebPage","about":{"@type":"MedicalCondition","name":"Fall Risk"},"audience":{"@type":"MedicalAudience","healthsafetyConsideration":"Falls Prevention"}}</script>
6. Author Attribution and Expertise Signals

Generative engines weight claims from attributed experts more heavily. Able Care should publish author bios.

Implementation:

  • Publish author bylines on all editorial content
  • Name, credentials (e.g., "Clinical Lead, Able Care"; "CEO, Able Care"; "Operations Consultant")
  • Link to LinkedIn or professional biography

  • Signals that content is from a human expert, not generic copy

  • Cite expert interviews in longer-form content

  • "According to [operator name], Operations Director at [PE-backed home care group]…"

  • Real-world validation strengthens claims for generative engines

  • Publish short author bios in an "About the Author" footer section

  • Generative engines extract this to assess credibility
7. Citation and Source Linking

Generative engines verify claims by checking cited sources. Able Care should cite liberally and link to original sources.

Implementation:

  • When citing clinical evidence (CDC STEADI, clinical trials, etc.), link directly to the source
  • E.g., "The CDC STEADI framework recommends four key functional assessments (link to CDC STEADI toolkit)"

  • Improves credibility; signals to AI models that you've verified the claim

  • When referencing competitor products or tools, link to their pages

  • Shows you've done your homework; improves SEO authority by signalling related links

  • Create a bibliography or sources page for major guides

  • Centralized, citable source list improves trust
8. FAQ Content from Search Query Data

Generative engines improve answers when they can find FAQ sections addressing real search queries.

Implementation:

  • Mine search logs (if available) for questions Able Care website visitors are asking

  • E.g., "How is grip strength measured?" "Can a caregiver administer this?" "Is Able Assess HIPAA compliant?"

  • Create FAQ pages specifically addressing these questions

  • Target long-tail, conversational queries

  • Generative engines will surface these FAQs as direct answers

  • Publish content answering search-adjacent questions

  • If people search "non-clinical falls screening," create a page explicitly addressing "why non-clinical staff need falls screening"
9. Glossary and Terminology Page

Generative engines extract definitions from dedicated glossary pages.

Implementation:

  • Create a Falls Screening Glossary page covering:
  • TUG (Timed Up and Go)
  • STEADI (CDC framework)
  • Gait speed
  • Grip strength / SMGT
  • Risk stratification
  • Four-measurement approach
  • Non-clinical staff
  • Five-minute screening
  • Falls detection vs. falls prevention

  • Liability and standard of care

  • Use consistent formatting: Term – one-sentence definition – link to full explanation

  • Generative engines use glossaries to disambiguate terminology in their responses

10. Competitor Comparison Content (Structured)

Generative engines reward transparent comparison content. Able Care should publish honest comparisons.

Implementation:

  • Create a "Able Assess vs. [Competitor/Tool]" page for each relevant comparison
  • Able Assess vs. Hendrich II Fall Risk Model
  • Able Assess vs. STEADI tools
  • Able Assess vs. VirtuSense

  • Able Assess vs. SafelyYou (for memory care angle)

  • Structure each as a neutral comparison table:

  • Feature (e.g., "Staff training required," "Measurement time," "Non-clinical usable")
  • Able Assess
  • Competitor

  • Explanation (why this matters)

  • Use side-by-side comparison schema markup

  • Link to competitor pages (signals fairness and research integrity)
11. Interactive Tools and Calculators

Generative engines reward pages with interactive content, which they cite as sources for specific calculations.

Implementation (Priority 3 page addresses this):

  • Publish the ROI Calculator on its own page with:
  • Inputs: facility size, staff count, current fall rate
  • Outputs: cost of programme, estimated fall reduction, savings, break-even timeline

  • Embeddable code so partners can integrate it

  • Publish a "Four-Measurement Walkthrough" interactive guide:

  • Illustrate how each of the four measurements works
  • Time estimate for each
  • Why each matters

  • Animated or video walkthrough

  • Generative engines cite these tools directly: "According to Able Care's ROI calculator, a 50-resident assisted living facility would break even in approximately 8 months."

12. Link Authority and Internal Linking Strategy

Generative engines reward topically related, well-interlinked content clusters.

Implementation:

  • Create content clusters around each core topic:

  • Cluster 1: Falls Risk Screening Basics

    • Hub page: "What is falls risk screening?"
    • Spoke 1: Blog post on each of the four measurements
    • Spoke 2: Regulatory and compliance overview
    • Spoke 3: Evidence and clinical background

  • Cluster 2: Able Assess Product & Implementation

    • Hub page: Able Assess product overview
    • Spoke 1: Case studies and operator testimonials
    • Spoke 2: Implementation guide
    • Spoke 3: Training and onboarding

  • Cluster 3: Operator Personas

    • Hub page: PE-backed and franchised operators
    • Spoke 1: Liability and risk management
    • Spoke 2: Multi-unit standardisation
    • Spoke 3: ROI and cost justification

  • Internal linking: Link related spoke pages to their hub and to each other

  • Generative engines reward topical clusters (signals comprehensive coverage)
13. Freshness and Update Signals

Generative engines favour recently updated content.

Implementation:

  • Publish regular updates to major pages (even minor tweaks)
  • Add new case study
  • Update statistics
  • Refresh clinical references

  • Add Q&A based on customer feedback

  • Update publication date when material changes occur

  • Create a "Latest Research" or "Updates" section on the homepage
  • Publish monthly or quarterly blog posts summarising new evidence or market developments

Monitoring and Next Steps

GEO Ranking Benchmarks (4–6 weeks post-publication)

Track appearance in generative engine responses for the 15 target queries:

Query Current Able Care Visibility Target (6 months) Tracking Method
Best falls risk assessment for assisted living 0 Top 3 citation ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude responses
Digital falls screening platform Blog only Homepage + blog
Fall prevention technology for home care 0 Top 5 citation
PE-backed operators reduce liability 0 Top 3 citation
STEADI falls screening software 0 Top 5 citation
Grip strength assessment for senior care 0 Educational blog cited
Falls prevention for CCRCs 0 Top 5 citation
Non-clinical falls screening tool 0 Top 3 citation
SAFE Act falls screening compliance Tangential Direct citation
Falls risk assessment for skilled nursing facilities 0 Top 5 citation
Five minute falls screening 0 #1 citation (ownership)
CDC aligned falls prevention platform 0 Top 3 citation
Home care technology for franchise operators 0 Top 5 citation (niche)
Reduce falls in assisted living 0 Top 5 citation
Falls prevention ROI calculator 0 Primary source (tool cited)
Publishing Timeline (8–12 weeks)
  • Weeks 1–2: Develop Priority 1 page ("Five-Minute Falls Screening")
  • Weeks 3–4: Develop Priority 2 page ("PE-Backed Operators Guide")
  • Weeks 5–6: Develop Priority 3 page + ROI calculator tool
  • Week 7: Schema markup and meta optimisation across all three + supporting blog posts
  • Weeks 8–12: Supporting content (glossary, FAQ expansions, competitor comparisons, blog series)
Measurement
  • Monitor generative engine citations (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude, Google's SGE)
  • Track inbound traffic from generative search (distinct from traditional Google Search)
  • Measure engagement metrics on priority pages (time on page, FAQ click-through, calculator usage)
  • Quarterly review of SERP changes for the 15 target queries

Summary of Key Findings

  1. Able Care is nearly invisible in GEO searches – appearing in only 2 of 15 buyer-language queries, and only in blog content, not product/homepage authority.

  2. Biggest gaps are high-intent, low-competition queries – particularly "five-minute falls screening," "non-clinical screening tools," and "CDC-aligned platforms," where buyer intent is clear but no vendor dominates.

  3. Speed and non-clinical usability are underexploited positioning angles – despite being Able Assess's core differentiators, they are not yet represented in search results as distinct product categories.

  4. PE-backed and franchised operators are explicitly searching for liability reduction and standardisation tools – but no product company has claimed this positioning; content opportunity is high.

  5. Generative engines reward structured, cited, comprehensive content – Able Care's blog posts on individual measurements are a good start, but hub pages, glossaries, FAQs, schema markup, and interactive tools are needed to establish topical authority.

  6. Interactive calculators (ROI, assessment walkthroughs) are a high-leverage tactic – they become directly citable sources in generative engine responses, driving both authority and lead capture.

The three priority pages recommended above, combined with structured data optimisation and a supporting content cluster, will establish Able Care as a visible, citable authority on falls screening for operators and non-clinical staff – the exact positioning that drives PE-backed and franchised operator adoption.

Document prepared: April 2026
Audit scope: 15 buyer-language queries, top-10 SERP analysis, competitive landscape mapping
Methodology: Web search analysis (Google), intent classification, content structure review, schema and structural data recommendations

Events and video

Events, video briefs, onboarding

9.1-9.3 Events Calendar, Video Production Briefs, and Onboarding Content

PART 1: EVENTS CALENDAR AND GO-TO-MARKET PLAN (Task 9.1)

Events Calendar 2026

Able Care's target events span five audience segments: home care franchise operators, PE-backed home care networks, senior living operators (CCRCs, IL, AL), home health agencies, and quality/regulatory focus. The calendar below identifies 11 high-priority events for 2026.

# Event Dates Location Primary Segment Est. Attendance Why It Matters for Able Care
1 2026 NIC Spring Conference 30 Mar – 1 Apr Nashville, TN Senior Living, PE Operating Partners 1,800+ Premier investor + operator gathering; executive directors, EDs, heads of clinical attend; strong alignment with Protect Residents and Protect Growth pillars
2 2026 LeadingAge Leadership Summit 20–22 Apr Washington, D.C. Senior Living, Non-Profit Operators 2,000+ 6,000+ attendees across LeadingAge events; board leaders and C-suite from largest senior living networks; receptive to standardised clinical tools
3 LeadingAge PA Annual Conference & Expo 11–13 May Hershey, PA Senior Living, Regional Operators 800+ State-level regional operators; hands-on workshop format; smaller venues allow intimate booth conversations
4 Home Care Leadership Summit 19–21 May Orlando, FL Home Care, Home Health Agencies 1,200+ C-level home care leaders; focuses on operations and retention economics; audiences align with Protect the P&L messaging
5 Senior Living Executive Conference (Argentum) 18–20 May Nashville, TN Senior Living (IL, AL, CCRC, SNF) 3,500+ Official Argentum meeting; largest assembly of senior living operators; covers clinical, ops, nursing leadership across brands
6 McKnight's Women of Distinction Awards + Forum 4 May Chicago, IL Senior Living, Home Care, Home Health 400+ High-influence female leaders across all segments; strong brand positioning opportunity; awards ceremony + educational sessions
7 Home Care 100 Winter Conference 25–28 Jan Scottsdale, AZ PE-Backed Home Care, Multi-Unit Franchisees 200+ Ultra-premium; CEOs and C-suite from top 100 home care agencies; highest decision-making power; PE backing indicators
8 Home Care 100 Summer Conference 31 May – 3 Jun Dana Point, CA PE-Backed Home Care, Multi-Unit Franchisees 200+ Second cohort of top home care leaders; executive education + strategy focus; post-spring sales cycle follow-up
9 IFA Annual Convention 23–25 Feb Las Vegas, NV Franchise Owners, Multi-Unit Developers 4,000+ Largest franchise event in North America; home care franchise networks attend in force; differentiation messaging resonates with growth-focused owners
10 CMS Quality Conference 2026 16–18 Mar Baltimore, MD (hybrid) Compliance Officers, Quality Directors, PE Operating Partners 1,500+ Free attendance; focuses on quality metrics and regulatory alignment; strong fit for Protect the Business messaging (documentation, liability, CDC alignment)
11 2026 National Home Care Conference (HCAOA) 28–29 Sep Orlando, FL Home Care Operators, Franchise HQs, Home Health 1,200+ Fall season; captures full home care market; strong showcase for scaling and portfolio-wide rollout stories
Go-to-Market Playbook by Event
Pre-Event Outreach (14–21 Days Before)

Objectives: - Build awareness of Able Care booth/presence - Schedule pre-event calls or meetings - Identify attendees from current customer base (advocates, references) - Create sense of anticipation

LinkedIn Strategy: - Post event-specific content: "See us at [Event Name]" with booth number, booth theme/giveaway - Tag speakers or session leaders from customer base - Share "5 things senior living leaders ask about fall prevention at [Event Name]" carousel - Estimated reach: 500–2,000 impressions per post

Email to Known Attendees: - Segment by company (franchisee, corporate operator, health system) - Subject line: "See us at [Event] – booth [X] – schedule a demo" - Include booth number, daily themes (e.g., Monday: Home Care, Tuesday: Senior Living), one QR code to book meeting - Personalised versions: one for PE operating partners (Protect Growth, Protect the P&L), one for clinical leaders (Protect Residents, Protect the Business) - Send Day 15 before event

LinkedIn Outreach to High-Value Prospects: - Target operators within 50-mile radius of event - Message: "I'll be at [Event] showcasing how [segment] leaders are using Able Assess to [outcome]. Would love to connect for 15 minutes?" - Personalise by segment and pain point - Expect 8–12% response rate from cold outreach

Customer Advocate Recruitment: - Call existing customers 14 days before - Invite them to "ride the booth" during peak hours or co-host a 30-minute booth session - Offer: travel/booth entertainment budget, speaking slot, co-branded 1-pager - Objective: 2–3 customers per event as validators

Booth Strategy and Presence

Booth Setup and Visual Design: - A frame or pull-up banner with tagline aligned to event theme: - "Prevent falls. Improve outcomes. Scale fast." (General) - "Protect your residents. Protect your ratings." (Senior living focus) - "Grow your agency. Keep your clients longer." (Home care focus) - Tablet demo station: pre-loaded app showing the four tests (grip, chair stand, gait, balance) - Physical hand dynamometer on table for attendees to squeeze and see measurement - Sample report printout (1-page, visually clear) showing healthy peer comparison and risk flag - QR code linked to: event-specific landing page, demo video (60 sec), calendar booking link - Colours and fonts consistent with Able Care brand

What to Demo: - Full 5-minute baseline screening using tablet (if attendee willing) OR quick 2-minute grip strength demo - Show results in real time: score, comparison to peers, risk status - Emphasise: speed (5–15 min), simplicity (any staff can run), clarity (one page, clear next steps) - Key talking point per persona:

PE Operating Partners / Corporate COOs: "This is standardised fall-risk screening that rolls out across 50 communities in days, not months. One baseline identifies who's at risk. Periodic checks catch decline early. That prevents hospitalisations, improves retention, and protects margins."

Senior Living EDs / Clinical Leaders: "Your residents sit, stand, walk, and squeeze a device. 5–15 minutes. Non-clinical staff runs it. Results are defensible, CDC-aligned documentation that improves your quality ratings and defends liability."

Home Care Franchisees / Agencies: "Every lost high-acuity client costs you $100k+ in gross profit. This tool identifies decline early, triggers PT referral, keeps clients home, and differentiates your brand. Your staff runs it today."

Procurement / Finance: "One prevented fall-related hospitalization or client churn pays for Able Assess. ROI model available. Start with a pilot: 30 days, baseline everyone, track outcomes."

Key Materials at Booth: - 200× "Falls Prevention 101" 1-pager (tri-fold, evidence-led, segment-agnostic) - 100× "Able Assess Product Sheet" (spec sheet, 4 tests, timeline, training) - 50× Segment-specific case studies (Cypress HomeCare for home care; Right at Home for senior living) - 30× Objection cards (5 common objections + evidence-led responses, laminated, pocket-sized) - Giveaway: custom grip-strength stress ball with Able Care logo + event date

On-Site Talking Points Mapped to Five Value Pillars

Pillar 1: Protect Growth - Segment: PE operating partners, corporate COOs, franchise CEOs - Messaging: Tech-forward differentiation attracts residents and franchisees; standardisation accelerates portfolio growth - Talk track: "In a crowded market, tech differentiation wins. Home care franchisees see Able Assess as a competitive advantage. They can tell prospects: 'We do objective fall-risk screening.' That differentiates them, attracts price-insensitive clients, improves conversion. For senior living, objective baseline data improves reputation and attracts families. And across portfolio, standardised screening enables you to scale from 10 to 50 communities without building clinical teams." - Proof point: Cypress HomeCare quote: "There's science behind it, physicality behind it, a measurement behind it. That's a big differentiator." - Elevator pitch: "Able Assess is the tech-forward differentiator that helps you grow your census and your portfolio in days, not months."

Pillar 2: Protect the P&L - Segment: Corporate COOs, VPs Operations, Franchise owners - Messaging: Staff turnover is the #1 cost; simple tools reduce burden, improve retention, save margin - Talk track: "Staff turnover in home care averages 40–50% annually. Every lost client cascades into losing caregivers. Able Assess is a 5–15 minute non-clinical tool that any staff member can run. It's kept because it's simple. It improves job satisfaction (staff feel they're doing clinical-grade screening). It identifies decline early, which triggers PT referral and prevents crises. One lost high-acuity client costs $100k+ gross profit; one prevented loss or kept client pays for Able Assess for years. That's pure P&L impact." - Proof point: "One 24-7 case = $350k revenue, $140k gross profit. Losing one case = $140k gone + cascading staff churn." - Elevator pitch: "Reduce staff burden, improve retention, protect gross profit. Able Assess is the simple tool that saves your P&L."

Pillar 3: Protect Residents - Segment: EDs, heads of clinical, directors of nursing, wellness directors - Messaging: Fewer falls, fewer hospitalisations, longer independence, better quality ratings - Talk track: "One in four adults over 65 falls each year. But falls are preventable. Able Assess establishes a baseline of grip strength, balance, and mobility. Early decline triggers intervention: PT referral, home modifications, care plan adjustment. That prevents the fall that would hospitalise your resident, damage quality ratings, and move them out. Better outcomes mean longer tenure, higher satisfaction, better census, and defensible documentation for regulators." - Proof point: "$80 billion annual US healthcare spend on non-fatal falls. Grip strength is the single best predictor of decline and mortality. One baseline + periodic checks = proactive cycle." - Elevator pitch: "Prevent falls. Keep residents independent longer. Improve your ratings and census."

Pillar 4: Protect the Business - Segment: General counsel, risk/compliance leaders, corporate COOs - Messaging: Objective, CDC-aligned falls data mitigates liability and defends quality ratings - Talk track: "Subjective 'we think this resident is at risk' doesn't defend a quality rating or a lawsuit. Objective measurement does. Able Assess gives you CDC STEADI–aligned baseline data showing you screened everyone, identified risk, and documented interventions. That defensible documentation protects your quality rating and your legal posture. And when a fall does occur, documented baseline + trend data shows you followed best practice, which limits liability." - Proof point: "CDC STEADI guidelines recommend standardised fall-risk screening. Able Assess is designed to that standard. One baseline = documented due diligence." - Elevator pitch: "Objective documentation that defends your quality ratings and protects your business."

Pillar 5: Protect the Portfolio - Segment: PE operating partners, corporate CEOs, heads of IT - Messaging: Standardise and scale across all communities in days, not months - Talk track: "Scaling a clinical program across 50 communities takes months: hiring specialists, building training, managing variance. Able Assess scales in days. Minimal IT footprint (tablet or phone). Non-clinical staff trained in minutes. Same workflow across all communities. EMR integration roadmap means data ports to your existing systems. One rollout playbook. Standardised results. Zero clinical variance. That's why PE-backed operators love it." - Proof point: "Right at Home deployed Able Assess across multiple settings (IL, AL, home health) in weeks, not months." - Elevator pitch: "Standardise falls screening across your entire portfolio in days. Scale without the clinical project."

Post-Event Follow-Up Sequence

Within 48 Hours: - Email all booth visitors: "Great to meet you at [Event]. Here's a 60-second demo video. [Link] Any questions?" - Include link to "Able Assess 101" one-pager tailored to their segment - Include calendar link (Calendly or similar) to book 15-minute follow-up call - Personalised subject: "Quick demo from [Booth #] at [Event Name]" - Expected response rate: 15–20%

Within 1 Week (Day 5–7): - Phone call or LinkedIn message to high-value prospects (PE-backed operators, franchisees, ED-level contacts) - "I wanted to follow up on the conversation at [Event]. You mentioned [specific pain point]. Able Assess addresses that. Would Tuesday or Wednesday work for a brief call?" - Prepare 3–5 minute pitch tailored to their stated pain - Expected conversion to call: 40–60% of contacts

Within 2 Weeks (Day 12–14): - Email sequence: "If you didn't get a chance to chat with us, no worries. Here's why [segment] leaders are adopting Able Assess." - Share 1-2 case studies or objection responses - Re-offer calendar link and note booth giveaway will be sent if they book a call - Expected response rate: 10–15% of non-responders

30-Day Post-Event: - Segment contacts into four buckets: (1) Hot (has problem, has budget, has timeline), (2) Warm (interested, not yet urgent), (3) Cold (visited booth, no clear fit), (4) Unqualified - Schedule demos/pilots with Hot leads (target: 2–4 pilots per event) - Send case studies and content to Warm leads monthly - Move Cold and Unqualified to nurture sequence - Target: 5–10 qualified pipeline additions per event

Per-Event Content Kit Checklist

Each event should have pre-prepared, printed, and digital assets. Customise by segment focus.

Standard Kit (All Events): - [ ] 200× "Falls Prevention 101" 1-pager (generic) - [ ] 100× Able Assess Product Sheet (specs, timeline, training) - [ ] 30× Laminated objection cards (5 objections, evidence-led responses) - [ ] 50× Copy of latest case study (generic, not segment-specific) - [ ] Tablet with pre-loaded Able Assess app (demo-ready) - [ ] Physical hand dynamometer (for attendee grip test) - [ ] Sample 1-page report (printed, showing healthy peer comparison and risk flag) - [ ] QR code signage (links to event landing page, demo video, calendar booking) - [ ] Able Care branded giveaway (e.g., grip-strength stress ball, branded notebook) - [ ] Booth staffing schedule (2 people, staggered breaks)

Home Care / Franchise Focus Add-Ons: - [ ] 50× "Home Care Growth Brief" 1-pager (growth, retention, P&L focus) - [ ] 30× Cypress HomeCare case study (home care operator proof point) - [ ] 10× ROI one-pager ("Every lost 24-7 case costs $100k+") - [ ] Sales objection card: "It's just another app" response

Senior Living / CCRC Focus Add-Ons: - [ ] 50× "Senior Living Operator's Guide to Falls Prevention at Scale" 1-pager - [ ] 30× Right at Home case study (senior living + events proof point) - [ ] 10× Quality Ratings 1-pager (documentation, compliance, defensibility) - [ ] Sales objection card: "Isn't fall prevention just a clinical program?" response

Quality / Compliance / CMS-Focused Events Add-Ons: - [ ] 50× "CDC STEADI Alignment" 1-pager (evidence, standardisation, defensibility) - [ ] 30× Regulatory Compliance Fact Sheet (FDA status, ISO, HIPAA, etc.) - [ ] 10× Liability Mitigation 1-pager (documentation, legal defence)

Post-Event Followup Materials (Digital): - [ ] Segment-specific demo video (60–90 sec, tailored to home care OR senior living) - [ ] Personalised thank-you email template (segment-customised) - [ ] 15-minute discovery call guide (questions for PE ops, EDs, franchise owners) - [ ] Pilot programme guide (scope, timeline, success metrics for 30-day pilot)

PART 2: VIDEO PRODUCTION BRIEFS (Task 9.2)

Video 1: Product Demo (60–90 Seconds)

Objective: Introduce Able Assess in action. Show speed, simplicity, and clarity of results. Drive secondary CTA on homepage.

Format: Voiceover narration over on-screen demonstrations. No actors required.

Distribution Channels: - Homepage (secondary CTA below fold) - YouTube (Able Care channel) - LinkedIn (feed + carousel conversion) - Sales email (demo link in follow-up)

Video 1 Script: Voiceover Narration

[00:00–00:10] Scene 1: Introduction (Screen: Clean white background with Able Care logo; then transition to tablet interface)

Voiceover: "Able Assess is a five-minute falls-risk screening for any care setting. Here's how it works."

[00:10–00:25] Scene 2: Grip Strength Test (Screen: Tablet showing app interface. Hand enters frame holding grip dynamometer. Client squeezes. Device registers measurement. Number appears on screen: "32 kg")

Voiceover: "First: Grip strength. Your client squeezes a small hand dynamometer. We measure peak grip force. It's a biomarker of muscle strength, bone density, and resilience. One squeeze. Instant result."

[00:25–00:35] Scene 3: Chair Stand Test (Screen: Split view. Left: client video standing from chair, timed. Right: tablet showing timer counting seconds. Final count: "10 seconds.")

Voiceover: "Next: Chair stand. How many times can they stand from a chair in 30 seconds? Lower-limb strength matters. We time it. Objective measurement."

[00:35–00:45] Scene 4: Gait Speed (Screen: Top-down view of client walking 4 metres. Tablet shows distance and time overlay. Result: "1.2 metres per second.")

Voiceover: "Then: Gait speed. Walking speed over a short distance. A predictor of independence and fall risk. We measure it objectively."

[00:45–00:55] Scene 5: Balance Test (Screen: Client standing on one leg. Tablet shows timer. Client holds position. Timer stops at 35 seconds. Checkmark appears: "Normal balance.")

Voiceover: "Finally: Balance. Can they stand for 30 seconds? A simple test of stability. We record whether they pass."

[00:55–01:10] Scene 6: Results Report (Screen: Single-page report appears on tablet. Large header: "Assessment Complete." Below: Risk stratification (low, intermediate, high). Visual comparison chart showing client's scores vs. healthy peers. Green, yellow, and red zones clearly marked.)

Voiceover: "Fifteen minutes later, you have a clear, visually simple report. Risk flag, if indicated. Practical next steps. And a comparison to healthy adults their age. That's what changes the conversation."

[01:10–01:20] Scene 7: Impact Statement (Screen: Text overlay on clean background)

Voiceover: "One baseline prevents fall-related hospitalisation, loss of independence, and staff burden. Early identification enables intervention."

(Text on screen: "One baseline. Four measurements. Fifteen minutes. Actionable insight.")

[01:20–01:30] Scene 8: Call to Action (Screen: Able Care logo, tagline, and CTA button)

Text on screen: "See how Able Assess works in your setting. Book a demo."

Voiceover: "Able Assess. Falls prevention that scales."

Video 1 On-Screen Statistics (Lower Thirds)

Integrate these statistics as text overlays during voiceover narration:

  • Scene 2 (Grip): "Grip strength predicts mortality, morbidity, independence"
  • Scene 3 (Chair Stand): "Lower-limb weakness is the #1 preventable fall risk factor"
  • Scene 4 (Gait): "Gait speed decline signals functional loss 3–5 years before fall"
  • Scene 5 (Balance): "Balance failure = 3x higher fall risk in the next 12 months"
  • Scene 6 (Results): "50% of home health beneficiaries fall each year. Early detection works."

Video 2: Home Care Segment (90–120 Seconds)

Objective: Show Able Assess as a growth and retention tool for home care franchises and agencies. Emphasise P&L impact, ease of adoption, and brand differentiation.

Format: Interview-style (franchise owner or agency CEO) plus B-roll footage. Scripted interview questions below.

Distribution Channels: - /solutions/home-care/ landing page (hero video) - LinkedIn (primary feed content + conversation starter) - Email campaigns (franchise owner segment) - Sales presentations (B2B deck)

Video 2 Interview Questions (5 Questions for Franchise Owner or Agency CEO)

Question 1: What was the biggest operational pain before Able Assess? Expected response arc: High client turnover due to falls/decline they didn't see coming. Staff burden. Reactive crisis management. Lost clients = lost caregiver assignments.

Question 2: Why did you choose to implement Able Assess? Expected response arc: Saw it as non-clinical, easy for staff to run, fast. Could differentiate brand. Aligned with growth strategy. Wanted to move from reactive to proactive.

Question 3: How did your team respond to the new screening? Expected response arc: Surprised by speed. Staff adoption was immediate (simplicity). Clients/families responded well (tangibility of measurement). Felt like they were doing clinical-grade work without clinical licensing.

Question 4: What's one client success story you've seen? Expected response arc: Baseline identified grip weakness, triggered PT referral, prevented fall, kept client in home. Client stayed longer. Revenue protected. Family grateful.

Question 5: How has Able Assess changed your conversation with prospects? Expected response arc: Can now say: "We do objective fall-risk screening." Differentiates from competitors. Helps convert price-sensitive prospects into quality-conscious ones. Improves retention narrative.

  6. Two caregivers discussing screening results (office/staff meeting setting, positive conversation)
  7. Client doing PT exercise at home based on Able Assess recommendation (motion shot, proof of intervention)
  8. Caregiver on phone (presumably calling PT or doctor) (establishing "next step" action)

Easy tools are kept. Hard tools are abandoned." - "Your caregivers run screening in 5–15 minutes. No specialist needed. No extra labour cost."

Supporting Statistics (On-Screen Text): - "One 24-7 client = $350k revenue, $140k gross profit" - "Staff turnover costs 40–50% of salary replacement in home care" - "Early identification of weakness triggers PT referral, prevents fall, keeps client in home"

Video 3: Senior Living Segment (90–120 Seconds)

Objective: Position Able Assess as resident safety and operational excellence tool. Focus on outcomes, quality ratings, and family satisfaction.

Format: Interview-style (ED or Wellness Director) plus B-roll. Scripted interview questions below.

Distribution Channels: - /solutions/ccrc/ and /solutions/assisted-living/ landing pages - LinkedIn (targeted at senior living operators) - Email campaigns (ED and clinical leadership segment) - Sales presentations

Video 3 Interview Questions (5 Questions for ED or Wellness Director)

Question 1: What does fall prevention mean to your community? Expected response arc: Resident independence, quality of life, avoiding hospitalisations. Reduces liability. Improves quality ratings. Core to mission.

Question 2: Before Able Assess, how did you identify fall risk? Expected response arc: Mostly subjective assessment. Nursing observation. Reactive response after a fall. Inconsistent documentation.

Question 3: How has objective baseline screening changed your approach? Expected response arc: Now proactive, not reactive. Can track decline over time. Better defensible documentation. Staff feel more confident.

Question 4: What impact have you seen on resident outcomes? Expected response arc: Fewer falls, fewer hospitalisations, residents stay longer, families more satisfied, quality rating improved. Feel like you're truly preventing decline.

Question 5: How do families respond when you share the results? Expected response arc: Families appreciate tangible number (not vague worry). Changes conversation with doctor. Families feel informed and involved. Trust improves.

Video 3 Key Messages Mapped to Value Pillars

Protect Residents: - "Objective baseline data identifies decline early. We can intervene with PT, home modifications, care adjustments." - "That prevents the fall that would hospitalise your resident, move them out, and damage their independence."

Protect the Business: - "Documented baseline + periodic reassessment = defensible evidence you screened everyone and followed best practice." - "That documentation protects your quality rating and your legal posture."
