PART 3: CUSTOMER ONBOARDING CONTENT (Task 9.3)

Quick-Start Guide (1–2 Pages)

Audience: Non-clinical site administrators, activity directors, office managers, frontline staff who will run first screenings.

Tone: Warm, supportive, jargon-free. Plain language. Encourage confidence.

Quick-Start Guide: Your First Screening

Welcome to Able Assess.

You're about to run a falls-risk screening that takes 15 minutes and gives you clear, actionable insight. Don't worry if you've never done this before. The device does the measuring. You just guide the process.

What You're Measuring: The Four Tests

Able Assess measures four simple physical abilities. Together, they predict fall risk and independence.

  1. Grip Strength What it is: Peak hand squeeze strength (measured in kilograms). Why it matters: Grip strength is the single best predictor of overall muscle health, bone density, and physical resilience. What the person does: Squeezes a small handheld device (dynamometer) as hard as they can, three times. We record the highest squeeze.

  2. Chair Stand Test What it is: How many times can they stand from a chair in 30 seconds? Why it matters: Leg strength is critical for balance and fall prevention. What the person does: Sits in a sturdy chair with feet flat. Stands and sits repeatedly for 30 seconds. You count the repetitions.

  3. Gait Speed What it is: Walking speed over a short distance (four metres). Why it matters: Slow gait is linked to weakness, balance problems, and future decline. What the person does: Walks at their normal pace across a measured distance. You time them.

  4. Balance Test What it is: Can they stand on one foot for 30 seconds? Why it matters: Balance is essential to preventing falls. Failure signals higher risk. What the person does: Stands on one foot for up to 30 seconds. If they can't hold it or lose balance before 30 seconds, they show a balance flag.

Before You Start: Setup and Consent

  • Choose a quiet, safe space. A hallway or activity room works. Make sure there's a sturdy chair, enough space to walk 4 metres, and a wall nearby (safety).
  • Get comfortable. Spend 2–3 minutes talking with the person. Explain what you're doing and why (checking strength and balance to keep them safe). Answer questions.
  • Get permission. They should agree to the screening. If they seem uncomfortable or unwilling, skip it and try another day. Consent is essential.
  • Dress appropriately. They should wear comfortable clothes and stable shoes. If they're in socks or slippers, ask them to put on shoes.

Step-by-Step: Running Your First Screening

Step 1: Grip Strength (2 minutes) - Open the Able Assess app. Select "Grip Strength Test." - Hand the person the grip dynamometer. Explain: "Squeeze this as hard as you can. Ready? Go." - They squeeze hard. The device registers the force. Release. Rest 30 seconds. - Repeat twice more. Record all three squeezes. The app will automatically take the highest.

Step 2: Chair Stand Test (3 minutes) - Ask them to sit in a sturdy chair, feet flat on the ground, back against the chair. - Say: "When I say 'go,' stand up and sit down as many times as you can in 30 seconds. Ready? Go." - Start the timer on your tablet. Count aloud: "1, 2, 3..." as they stand and sit. - Stop at 30 seconds. Record the number. (Normal is 8–12 repetitions for adults 65+. Fewer than 8 signals weakness.)

Step 3: Gait Speed (2 minutes) - Measure out a clear 4-metre walking path. Mark the start and end (tape on the floor is fine). - Say: "Walk from here to there at your normal pace. I'll time you." - Start the timer when they begin walking. Stop when they reach the end. Record the time. - (Normal gait speed is 1+ metres per second. Slower signals risk.)

Step 4: Balance Test (2 minutes) - Have them stand with feet together, near a wall (for safety, not to hold). - Say: "Lift one foot and stand on the other for as long as you can, up to 30 seconds. Whenever you're ready, go." - Start the timer. If they hold 30 seconds, record "Normal balance." If they drop their foot before 30 seconds or lose balance, record the time (or "Unable to hold").

Step 5: Results (1 minute) - The app generates a one-page report instantly. - Show them the report. Explain their risk level (low, intermediate, high). - Point to the chart showing how they compare to healthy adults their age. - If there's a risk flag, explain the next step (e.g., "We'd like to talk to your doctor about physical therapy" or "Home modifications can help reduce fall risk").

Common Questions from Participants

"Why do you need to measure all four? Can't you just do grip strength?" - Grip strength is the strongest single predictor, but balance, leg strength, and walking speed tell the full story. Together, they're much more accurate.

"What if I can't do one of the tests?" - That's fine. We do what we can. If someone can't walk 4 metres due to a walker or pain, we skip gait speed. If they can't balance on one foot, that's important information too. We work with what's possible.

"What happens if my risk is high?" - We share the results with you and your doctor. We might recommend physical therapy, home safety changes, or more frequent check-ins. Our job is to catch weakness early so we can help prevent falls.

"How often will you screen me again?" - We'll baseline you now. Then we might rescreen every 3–6 months to track how you're doing. That way, we catch any decline early.

After Your Screening

  • Print the report. Give a copy to the person and their family.
  • Share with the care team. Upload results to the care portal so doctors, therapists, and nurses can see.
  • Follow next steps. If the screening flagged a risk, connect the person with their doctor or a physical therapist.
  • Document and track. Keep a record of baseline results so you can compare future screenings.

You've Got This.

You just completed a clinical-grade falls-risk assessment in 15 minutes. You didn't need a nursing degree. You didn't need special equipment beyond the dynamometer and your tablet. The measurement is objective, the results are clear, and you've given someone the insight they need to stay safe and independent.

Questions? Contact [Support Email] or [Phone].

Day 1 / Day 30 / Day 90 Success Playbook (Two Audience Versions)
VERSION A: For Site Administrators and Managers

Day 1 Success: Launch and Confidence Building

Your Goals Today: - Run your first baseline screening (you and at least one staff member together) - Celebrate the simplicity (it takes 15 minutes, not a clinical exam) - Share the experience with your team

Action Items: - [ ] Log into the Able Assess app and familiarise yourself with the interface - [ ] Identify your first screening participant (a staff member or willing resident; someone flexible) - [ ] Set aside 20 minutes in a quiet space - [ ] Run through all four tests together: grip, chair stand, gait, balance - [ ] Review the generated report. Notice how clear and visual it is. - [ ] Show the report to the participant. Watch their reaction. (Most say: "Oh, so I'm stronger than I thought!" or "I see why you're recommending PT.") - [ ] Take a screenshot of the report and share it with your team or leadership - [ ] Send a message to your team: "We just ran our first screening. Took 15 minutes. Results are crystal clear. We're all trained and ready to go."

Success Looks Like: - One completed baseline - One printed/digital report - Team sees it, understands it, feels confident - No drama, no technical issues - One person (you) now has the lived experience of "this is actually simple"

Day 30 Success: Momentum and Adoption

Your Goals This Month: - Establish screening as routine (not a special project) - Train all frontline staff - Build a baseline on 30–50% of your population - Start tracking trends

Action Items: - [ ] Schedule 30-minute staff training: walk through the four tests with your whole team - [ ] Pair staff for the first 2–3 screenings (one experienced, one new) - [ ] Set a weekly baseline target: e.g., "Screen 10 new residents this week" - [ ] Store baseline results in your portal (cloud backup, accessible to clinicians) - [ ] Identify 2–3 residents whose screening flagged risk; confirm they've been referred to PT or doctor - [ ] Hold a team huddle: "How are screenings going? Any questions? Any barriers?" Listen and adjust - [ ] Share one success story internally: "Mrs. Smith's baseline identified weakness we hadn't noticed. PT referral prevented what could have been a fall. This works."

Success Looks Like: - 15–25 residents baselined - All staff trained, doing screenings independently - Screening time ~15 min (some staff faster as they practise) - At least 2–3 risk-flagged residents connected to PT or follow-up - Zero technical issues; adoption is smooth - Team speaks positively about the tool ("It's so easy." "Families love seeing the results.")

Day 90 Success: Integration and ROI Realisation

Your Goals This Quarter: - Full population baselined or on a screening schedule - Measurable clinical outcomes starting to emerge - Leadership confidence in the program - Sustainable routine established

Action Items: - [ ] Complete baseline on 80%+ of your resident/client population - [ ] Set up a monthly trend-tracking report: show how many residents have been rescreened, any decline detected, interventions triggered - [ ] Document 3–5 resident success stories: baseline identified decline → intervention → prevented adverse event (hospitalisation, move-out) - [ ] Review: Which residents have you prevented a fall or hospitalisation in? What's the impact on census, retention, quality metrics? - [ ] Host a brief team retrospective: "90 days in. What's working? What would make screening even easier?" - [ ] Share results with leadership and your clinical advisory team: "Able Assess has become routine. Adoption is strong. We're seeing early warning signs and intervening proactively."

Success Looks Like: - 80%+ of population baselined - Monthly rescreens showing trend data - 2–5 documented cases where early identification prevented adverse event - Quality metrics stable or improving (falls rate down, length of stay up, resident satisfaction up) - Staff using screening unprompted (it's now part of the routine, not an extra task) - Leadership sees Able Assess as a core part of care delivery, not a pilot project

VERSION B: For Frontline Staff (Caregivers, Activity Directors, Care Coordinators)

Day 1 Success: Your First Screening

What You're Doing: Running a simple four-test screening that takes 15 minutes. You're checking someone's grip strength, leg strength, walking speed, and balance. No clinical knowledge required.

How to Prepare: - Ask your manager for a 15-minute introduction to the app - Watch the "Your First Screening" video (60 seconds) on your tablet - Find a quiet space and a sturdy chair - Get a colleague to stand by (safety)

The Four Tests (Super Simple): 1. Grip: Person squeezes a small hand device. Three squeezes. Done. 2. Chair Stand: Person sits and stands repeatedly for 30 seconds. You count. Done. 3. Gait: Person walks 4 metres at normal pace. You time them. Done. 4. Balance: Person stands on one foot for up to 30 seconds. Done.

After the Screening: - The app gives you a one-page report immediately - Show the person the report. Say: "Here's your score and how you compare to people your age. Any questions?" - Your manager will upload the results to the care system

Success Looks Like: - You've done one screening and you feel confident ("That was actually easy") - The person you screened felt comfortable and understood the results - No mistakes, no stress, no tech issues

Day 30 Success: You're Part of the Routine

What You're Doing: Screening residents/clients as part of your regular work. It's become automatic, like taking vital signs.

Your Role: - Screen new admissions in their first week - Rescreen anyone whose earlier results showed risk (your manager will tell you who) - Answer simple questions: "Why do you do this?" Answer: "We're checking your strength and balance to make sure you stay safe and independent."

Tips for Success: - You get faster with practice. Your first screening takes 20 minutes. By month two, you'll do it in 15. - If someone doesn't want to be screened, don't force it. Ask your manager. - If someone can't do a test (too much pain, too weak), that's okay. Skip it and note why. - Families love the results. Show them the chart. Say: "Your mum's balance is strong, which is great for independence."

Success Looks Like: - You're screening people without your manager hovering - You feel confident explaining the four tests - You've noticed at least one resident whose results surprised you (better or worse than expected) - Families have asked to see the report (a good sign; they trust it)

Day 90 Success: You're Making a Difference

What You're Doing: Screening is now part of your job. You notice early warning signs and flag them so interventions happen before a fall.

Examples of Impact You're Creating: - You screened Mrs. Chen. Her grip strength was lower than expected. You flagged it. PT came. She got stronger. No fall. - You notice Mr. James's balance score dropped from last quarter. You tell the nurse. The care team reviews his medications. They find one was making him dizzy. They adjust it. He feels steadier. - You show Mr. and Mrs. Rodriguez their daughter's baseline report. They see her grip strength is solid. They feel reassured. Census retention improves.

Your Confidence: - You're not just checking boxes. You're spotting weakness before it becomes a crisis. - You're part of a team that prevents falls, keeps people independent, and catches decline early. - You've heard from your manager or a family member: "Thank you for catching that. The screening made the difference."

Success Looks Like: - Screening is automatic, not a chore - You've personally flagged decline in at least one person that led to intervention - You understand why you do this (prevent falls, keep people safe and independent) - You feel proud that you're making a clinical difference with a simple tool

FAQ Document: 15–20 Questions

General Questions

Q1: What is Able Assess? A: Able Assess is a five-minute falls-risk screening tool that measures grip strength, balance, leg strength, and walking speed. It tells you who's at risk of falling so you can intervene early (physical therapy, home modifications, doctor referral) and prevent falls.

Q2: Who should be screened? A: All adults 65 and older, particularly those in senior living, home care, or home health settings. Anyone with a history of falls or balance problems should also be screened.

Q3: How long does a screening take? A: 5–15 minutes from start to finish. Most take 12 minutes once you're comfortable with the process.

Q4: Do I need a nurse or clinical staff to run this? A: No. Any non-clinical staff member (care coordinator, activity director, caregiver, administrative staff) can run Able Assess after a brief orientation. You don't need a clinical license.

Q5: Is Able Assess medical or non-medical? A: It's non-clinical. You're not diagnosing anything. You're measuring physical abilities (grip, balance, gait, leg strength) objectively. A clinician interprets the results and recommends next steps.

The Four Tests

Q6: Why measure grip strength? A: Grip strength is the single strongest predictor of physical decline, mortality risk, and independence loss. It correlates with overall muscle health, bone density, and resilience. If grip is weak, a person is at high risk of falls and future hospitalization.

Q7: What if someone can't squeeze the dynamometer hard? A: We still record the result. A weak grip is clinically significant and tells you that person needs intervention (physical therapy, nutrition support, doctor review).

Q8: Why the chair stand test? A: Leg strength is essential for getting up, walking, and maintaining balance. If someone struggles to stand from a chair, they're at high risk of falls when moving around.

Q9: What if someone is too weak to stand 5–10 times? A: That's important information. A low chair stand count signals severe weakness and very high fall risk. We flag it and recommend immediate physical therapy and doctor evaluation.

Q10: Does the balance test have to be on one leg? A: Yes. Standing on one leg tests dynamic balance, which is critical for fall prevention. If someone can't hold for 30 seconds, they're at elevated risk.

Q11: What if someone has arthritis or pain and can't do one of the tests? A: We skip that test and note why (pain, arthritis, mobility aid). We still do the other three. The report will show which tests were completed and which were skipped.

Results and Interpretation

Q12: How do I read the risk flag (low, intermediate, high)? A: The report stratifies risk based on all four measurements compared to healthy adults the same age. Low risk means they're at normal risk. Intermediate means they're at elevated risk and should be monitored. High means they're very at risk and should see a doctor or PT immediately.

Q13: What if the person scores "intermediate" or "high"? A: Have the care team or doctor review the results. Next steps might include: physical therapy referral, home safety assessment, medication review, PT exercise program, or more frequent monitoring.

Q14: Can I use Able Assess to diagnose balance problems? A: No. Able Assess is a screening tool, not a diagnostic tool. It identifies risk. A doctor or PT diagnoses underlying conditions (inner ear problem, neuropathy, medication side effect, etc.) and recommends treatment.

Q15: What if someone's results surprise me (better or worse than expected)? A: That's normal. People often don't perceive their own strength or balance accurately. That's why objective measurement is valuable. Share the results with the person and their doctor. They can discuss what it means.

Segment-Specific Questions

For Home Health Agencies (HHA): Section GG Reporting

Q16: How does Able Assess relate to OASIS Section GG (functional status)? A: Able Assess measures the same constructs that OASIS Section GG asks about (mobility, balance, strength). The baseline grip strength, gait speed, and chair stand results can inform your OASIS coding and provide objective data for quality reporting (PDPM, Home Health Compare).

For Senior Living: Family and Resident Communication

Q17: Can families see the Able Assess results? A: Yes. The report is designed to be family-friendly. It shows a clear score, a chart comparing them to healthy peers, and practical next steps. Many families appreciate seeing objective data instead of vague worries about fall risk.

For Home Care Franchises: Rollout and Scaling

Q18: How do we roll out Able Assess across our franchise network? A: Start with one location (2–4 weeks of baseline screening). Train a core team (2–3 staff). Build confidence. Document success stories. Then train and deploy at the next location. Each location should take 2–4 weeks to reach steady state. Across a 10-location network, you can scale in 3–4 months.

Technical and Practical

Q19: What if the app crashes or I lose internet connection? A: The app works offline. If you lose connection, the app stores your screening locally. When you reconnect, it syncs automatically to the portal. You never lose data.

Q20: How do I access the results after screening? A: Results are stored in your cloud portal (accessible on web or app). You can print them, share with the care team, track trends, and re-screen periodically. Contact your admin for login credentials.

Video Script: "Your First Screening" (60 Seconds)

Audience: Non-clinical staff, activity directors, caregivers—anyone running Able Assess for the first time.

Tone: Warm, encouraging, jargon-free. Real footage, not overly produced.

Video Script: Word-for-Word Narration

[00:00–00:10] Introduction (Screen: Activity director or caregiver smiling at camera in a care setting)

Voiceover: "You're about to run a falls-risk screening. It takes 15 minutes. You don't need a nursing degree. The device does the measuring. You just guide the process."

[00:10–00:20] Step 1: Grip Strength (Screen: Caregiver hands resident a small grey dynamometer. Resident squeezes. Tablet shows measurement: "28 kg". Checkmark.

Voiceover: "First: Grip strength. They squeeze a small device. One measurement. That's it. It predicts how strong they really are."

[00:20–00:30] Step 2: Chair Stand (Screen: Resident sits in sturdy chair. Caregiver starts timer on tablet. Resident stands and sits repeatedly. Caregiver counts: "1, 2, 3..." Resident completes 9 repetitions in 30 seconds.)

Voiceover: "Next: Chair stand. They sit and stand for 30 seconds. You count. Their leg strength tells you a lot about fall risk."

[00:30–00:40] Step 3: Gait Speed (Screen: Wide shot of hallway. Resident walks from point A to point B at normal pace. Caregiver times with tablet. Timer stops at 15 seconds for 4 metres.)

Voiceover: "Then: Walking speed. A simple walk down the hallway. You time them. Slow gait is a warning sign. Fast gait is reassuring."

[00:40–00:50] Step 4: Balance (Screen: Resident stands on one foot. Caregiver starts timer. Resident holds steady. Timer reaches 30 seconds. Checkmark. "Normal balance.")

Voiceover: "Finally: Balance. Can they stand on one foot for 30 seconds? A simple test of stability."

[00:50–01:00] Results and Celebration (Screen: Tablet shows one-page report. Large risk status: "Low Risk". Chart shows resident's scores (green bars) vs. healthy peers (grey bars). Resident smiling, reviewing report with caregiver.)

Voiceover: "Fifteen minutes later, you have a clear report. Risk status. Comparison chart. Practical next steps. You just did something clinical. And it was simple."

(On-screen text: "One screening. Four measurements. 15 minutes. One prevented fall.")

[01:00–01:05] Call to Action (Screen: Able Care logo, website, support contact)

Voiceover: "You've got this. Questions? Contact us."

(On-screen text: "ablecare.com/support | [Email]")

Video 4 Production Notes
  • Length: Exactly 60 seconds
  • Pacing: Slightly slower than natural speech to allow viewing on social media or training environments
  • Visuals: Real footage (not animated). Show real residents (with consent), real staff, real settings. Include close-ups of device, tablet, resident faces (emotional moments)
  • Audio: Calm, warm voiceover. Background music (soft, encouraging, no distraction)
  • Graphics: Minimal. Just the tablet screen and occasional timer/counter on-screen to show what's happening
  • Accessibility: Captions for all dialogue and on-screen text

Summary and Implementation Notes

Document Structure: This file contains three complete, ready-to-implement deliverables:

  1. Task 9.1: Events Calendar and Go-to-Market Plan - 11 real, confirmed 2026 events mapped to Able Care segments - Detailed pre-event, booth, on-site, and post-event playbooks - Go-to-market strategies for each of five value pillars - Content kit checklists, segment-specific messaging, and persona-mapped talking points

  2. Task 9.2: Video Production Briefs - Four complete video concepts with word-for-word scripts, scene descriptions, and on-screen statistics - Video 1 (Product Demo): 60–90 sec, homepage/YouTube/LinkedIn - Video 2 (Home Care): 90–120 sec, interview-based, franchise/agency focus - Video 3 (Senior Living): 90–120 sec, interview-based, ED/clinical focus - Video 4 (Evidence): 60–90 sec, credibility and founder positioning - Thumbnail concepts and distribution channels for each

  3. Task 9.3: Onboarding Content Package - Quick-Start Guide: 1-page, plain language, non-clinical staff focus, four measurements explained - Day 1/Day 30/Day 90 Success Playbook: Two versions (admins and frontline staff), milestone goals and action items - FAQ: 20 questions covering general, test-specific, segment-specific, and technical topics - Video Script: 60-second "Your First Screening" tutorial with word-for-word narration

Messaging Consistency: All content aligns with Able Care's five value pillars (Protect Growth, Protect the P&L, Protect Residents, Protect the Business, Protect the Portfolio) and integrates evidence from the Knowledge Base (Falls Cost and Prevalence, AbleAssess Speed and Simplicity, customer case studies).

Language and Tone: All content follows Able Care's writing style (UK English, no em dashes, evidence-led, short sentences, warm and supportive for onboarding materials).

Next Steps for Execution: - Confirm event attendance and booth budget with leadership - Commission video production (identify filmmaker, locations, talent/interviewees) - Design and print collateral (booth materials, 1-pagers, objection cards) - Build event-specific landing pages and form tracking - Assign booth staffing and sales follow-up responsibilities - Prepare segment-specific demo talking points and sales training - Launch onboarding content through admin dashboard and welcome email sequence

Build specs

ROI Calculator spec and build prompt

ROI Calculator: Technical Specification and Vibe-Code Build Prompt

Section 1: What this document is

The ROI Calculator Brief circulated to Dani poses six decisions that need to be signed off before build. This document assumes the recommended answer to each, translates those answers into a production-ready technical specification, and closes with a build prompt suitable for handing to a developer, a Next.js agency, or a vibe-code tool (v0, Cursor, Lovable, Claude Code).

If Dani confirms the six recommendations as they stand, the build prompt at the bottom of this document can be used verbatim. If any decision is amended, the changes fold into the spec in one or two places, noted below.

Dependencies: Claims Guardrail Library v2, Competitive Positioning Brief v2, Market Sizing brief. All three referenced in-line where the calculator depends on them.

Section 2: Assumed sign-offs (the six decisions)

  1. Two calculators built from one shared logic core: /home-care/roi-calculator and /senior-living/roi-calculator.
  2. Inputs as proposed in the brief, with sensible defaults pre-filled so the minimum-interaction case produces a defensible number.
  3. Three headline outputs (falls prevented per year, annual cost avoided, staff hours recovered) plus a secondary payback period.
  4. Methodology anchored to the Claims Guardrail Library with conservative defaults, tooltips on every number, and a downloadable methodology PDF.
  5. Guardrails: conservative defaults, universal disclaimer, cap on inflated savings figures, "how is this calculated" link on every output.
  6. Soft gate for email capture: numbers visible without submission, email required only to receive a branded PDF and for sales follow-up.

Section 3: Information architecture

Each calculator lives as a dedicated page on the site. Structure of the page from top to bottom.

Hero. Page title ("Falls Prevention ROI Calculator, Home Care" or "Senior Living"), one-sentence promise ("See the business case for proactive screening across your operation in under two minutes"), short paragraph of context anchored to the segment's operator KPIs.

Inputs panel. Left-column on desktop, stacked on mobile. Each input has a label, an input field with a sensible default pre-filled, a short helper tooltip, and an info icon linking to the source of the default where relevant.

Outputs panel. Right-column on desktop, shown below inputs on mobile. Three headline numbers in the brand style (large aqua number, dark grey label). One secondary number (payback period). Each number has a "how is this calculated" link that expands an inline explainer.

Disclaimer strip. Persistent strip beneath the outputs: "Estimates based on published research and your inputs. Actual outcomes depend on adoption, implementation, and local factors. Read the methodology."

Capture panel. Soft gate. "Email me a PDF of these results" form with name and email. Below that, the primary CTA: "Book a 15-minute call with the Able Care team."

Methodology section. Expandable section at the bottom covering: data sources, ranges used, what "conservative" means in the defaults, links to the Claims Library and the methodology PDF.

Section 4: Inputs

4a. Home care calculator
Input Default Source of default Allowed range
Number of active clients 500 Median for multi-site home care franchises (Market Sizing brief) 50 – 10,000
Average monthly visit count per client 24 Typical 6 visits per week × 4 weeks 4 – 120
Estimated percentage of clients aged 65 and over 80% Claims Library: home care demographics 50% – 100%
Current annual falls rate (optional) 28.7% CDC: falls prevalence in adults 65+, Claims Library 5% – 60%
Average cost per fall to the business $3,000 CDC lower bound for non-fatal fall direct cost (editable) $500 – $30,000
4b. Senior living calculator
Input Default Source of default Allowed range
Number of residents across portfolio 1,200 Median corporate operator portfolio (Market Sizing brief) 50 – 50,000
Community type mix IL 40% / AL 35% / MC 15% / SNF 10% Industry average, Market Sizing brief Sum must = 100%
Current falls incidence (optional) IL 28.7% / AL 40% / MC 50% / SNF 55% CDC + industry averages, Claims Library 10% – 80% per tier
Average cost per fall at the facility $4,500 Industry blended average across facility tiers $1,000 – $30,000
Staff cost per hour of assessment time $42 BLS average wage + loaded cost for LPN / CNA $15 – $150

All inputs are editable. All defaults link to their source via an info tooltip.

Section 5: Calculation logic

All calculations run client-side in the browser. No PII or inputs sent to the server until the user submits the email gate.

5a. Shared constants (from Claims Guardrail Library v2)
FALLS_REDUCTION_RATE_CONSERVATIVE = 0.18   // 18%, conservative anchor from CDC STEADI meta-analysis
FALLS_REDUCTION_RATE_OPTIMISTIC   = 0.33   // 33%, used only in "show optimistic case" toggle
ASSESSMENT_TIME_MINUTES_ABLEASSESS = 5     // Claim: five-minute screening
ASSESSMENT_TIME_MINUTES_MANUAL     = 30    // STEADI manual benchmark
SAVINGS_FLAG_THRESHOLD = 2_500_000         // USD; above this, show "figure requires further validation" flag
5b. Home care calculation
clients_over_65 = active_clients * pct_over_65
expected_falls_baseline = clients_over_65 * current_falls_rate
falls_prevented = expected_falls_baseline * FALLS_REDUCTION_RATE_CONSERVATIVE
annual_cost_avoided = falls_prevented * cost_per_fall

// Staff hours: each client screened on intake, then re-screened quarterly (4x/year).
// Manual protocol takes 30 mins. AbleAssess takes 5 mins. Saving = 25 mins per screen.
screens_per_year = active_clients * 4
hours_recovered = (screens_per_year * (ASSESSMENT_TIME_MINUTES_MANUAL - ASSESSMENT_TIME_MINUTES_ABLEASSESS)) / 60

// Payback: assume an illustrative Able Assess cost we can update once pricing is agreed.
// Placeholder, replace with confirmed pricing before go-live.
ABLE_ASSESS_ANNUAL_COST_HOMECARE = 12_000   // USD per operator entity, placeholder
payback_months = (ABLE_ASSESS_ANNUAL_COST_HOMECARE / annual_cost_avoided) * 12
5c. Senior living calculation
// Compute falls per tier, weight by mix
for each tier in [IL, AL, MC, SNF]:
    tier_residents = total_residents * tier_share
    tier_expected_falls = tier_residents * tier_falls_rate
    tier_falls_prevented = tier_expected_falls * FALLS_REDUCTION_RATE_CONSERVATIVE
    tier_cost_avoided = tier_falls_prevented * cost_per_fall

falls_prevented = sum(all tier_falls_prevented)
annual_cost_avoided = sum(all tier_cost_avoided)

// Staff hours: all residents screened quarterly.
screens_per_year = total_residents * 4
hours_recovered = (screens_per_year * (ASSESSMENT_TIME_MINUTES_MANUAL - ASSESSMENT_TIME_MINUTES_ABLEASSESS)) / 60

// Payback placeholder
ABLE_ASSESS_ANNUAL_COST_SENIORLIVING = 36_000   // USD for corporate portfolio, placeholder
payback_months = (ABLE_ASSESS_ANNUAL_COST_SENIORLIVING / annual_cost_avoided) * 12
5d. Guardrail logic

If annual_cost_avoided > SAVINGS_FLAG_THRESHOLD, render a small yellow warning strip under the output: "Figures at this scale require further validation. Let us walk you through the assumptions." This is the single guardrail Dani requested; anchored to a sensible cap that does not feel editorial.

Section 6: Outputs and copy

6a. Primary outputs

Three cards, each in brand style: large aqua number (#00FAD2 on #191919 card), short label, one-line context, and a "how is this calculated" link.

Card 1: Falls prevented per year Number: the calculated falls_prevented, rounded to nearest 10. Label: "Falls prevented per year" Context: "Conservative estimate based on an 18% reduction rate published by the CDC STEADI programme." Expand link: shows the formula and the CDC citation.

Card 2: Annual cost avoided Number: the calculated annual_cost_avoided, formatted as USD with no decimals. Label: "Annual cost avoided" Context: "Based on your cost-per-fall input. Operators typically see higher savings when liability and staff backfill are included." Expand link: shows the formula, the CDC cost citation, and a note that this figure excludes downstream hospitalisation costs.

Card 3: Staff hours recovered Number: the calculated hours_recovered, rounded to nearest 10. Label: "Staff hours recovered per year" Context: "Five-minute Able Assess screening versus a 30-minute manual STEADI workflow." Expand link: shows the formula and the Claims Library citation.

6b. Secondary output

Below the three cards, one line of copy: "At your operation's scale, Able Assess would pay for itself in roughly [payback_months] months based on cost avoidance alone."

If payback_months is under 3, render it as "in under three months". If between 3 and 12, render the integer. If over 12, render "roughly [payback_months] months, we can discuss multi-year ROI in a call".

6c. Universal disclaimer strip

Persistent, not dismissible, small text under outputs. Exact wording:

Estimates based on published research and your inputs. Actual outcomes depend on adoption, implementation, and local factors. Read the methodology →

6d. Capture gate

Heading: "Email me a PDF of these results" Fields: First name (required), work email (required), company name (optional) Button: "Send me the PDF" Privacy note below button: "We will only contact you about Able Care. See our privacy policy." CTA below: "Book a 15-minute call" as the primary action.

Section 7: Visual design

Inherit the Able Care brand system from the existing site and the audit/outbound documents.

Tokens to use (already in the stylesheet): --brand #191919, --aqua #00FAD2, --blue #1432FA, --text #1f2a44, --bg-alt #F7F8FC, --border #E2E8F0. Primary font DM Sans.

Card style: white background on a light grey page, 1px border in --border, 12px border radius, 16px 20px internal padding. Output cards invert: dark brand background, aqua number, white label.

Mobile: inputs and outputs stack. Output cards fit 1-up below 640px, 3-up above 960px. Keep all input labels visible above their fields; no placeholder-only fields.

Accessibility: every input has a real <label>, tooltips keyboard-accessible, outputs marked up as <output> elements so screen readers announce updates, colour contrast WCAG AA (aqua-on-dark passes at the large-text sizes used).

Section 8: Data, tracking, and CRM

No server-side storage of inputs unless the user submits the email gate.

On submit of the email gate, post to an endpoint (/api/roi-results) that:

  1. Generates the branded PDF server-side using the values submitted.
  2. Emails it to the user via transactional mail (Resend, Postmark, or existing Able Care provider).
  3. Writes the submission to Salesforce as a new lead with the input values and calculated outputs attached as a note, and the segment (home care / senior living) stamped on the source field.
  4. Fires a GA4 custom event roi_calculator_submission with segment, estimated savings bucket, and payback bucket as parameters.

Event tracking (no PII) across the calculator: - roi_input_change, field name and value bucket - roi_output_expand, which output's methodology was opened - roi_submit_attempt, gate form submission - roi_book_call_click, primary CTA click

Section 9: Edge cases and content rules

Handle missing or nonsense inputs gracefully. Below minimum or above maximum range, clamp to range and show a small helper ("We capped this at 10,000 clients. Contact us for larger portfolios, the economics change materially above that scale.").

Handle the tier-mix edge case: if the segments do not sum to 100%, auto-normalise to 100% and display a small note.

If a user toggles to "optimistic case", show a clear banner: "Optimistic case. Based on best-case reduction figures rather than the conservative CDC anchor." Do not make this the default.

Never show six-figure or seven-figure payback periods. If the calculated payback exceeds 36 months, render: "Your current cost-per-fall input is below the level where direct cost avoidance alone justifies Able Assess. The tool still pays back through staff time recovery and liability reduction, let us walk through the full picture."

Section 10: Acceptance criteria

The build is ready for release when:

  1. Both calculator pages render correctly on mobile (iPhone 13 baseline) and desktop (1440px baseline).
  2. All defaults tie back to a line in Claims Guardrail Library v2.
  3. Changing any input updates outputs within 100ms.
  4. The methodology PDF is downloadable and matches the in-page explainer text exactly.
  5. Email gate submission generates a branded PDF emailed to the user within 60 seconds and writes a Salesforce lead.
  6. All GA4 events fire correctly.
  7. Paul and Dani approve the default calculation for each segment's median input values.

Section 11: Vibe-code build prompt

Paste the following prompt into v0, Cursor, Lovable, Claude Code, or hand directly to a Next.js developer. It is self-contained and produces the calculator in one pass.

PROMPT FOR THE AI BUILDER

Build a production-ready falls-prevention ROI calculator as a pair of pages in a Next.js 14 application with the app router. The pages live at /home-care/roi-calculator and /senior-living/roi-calculator and share a single <RoiCalculator> React component configured per segment by a props object.

Brand system
- Font: DM Sans via Google Fonts.
- Palette: --brand #191919, --aqua #00FAD2, --blue #1432FA, --text #1f2a44, --bg-alt #F7F8FC, --border #E2E8F0.
- Cards: white bg, 1px border --border, 12px radius, 16-20px padding.
- Output cards invert to brand bg with aqua numbers.
- No emoji. No gradients. No em dashes in copy.

Layout
- Two-column on desktop above 960px, stacked on mobile below that.
- Hero with page title and a one-sentence promise.
- Left column: inputs.
- Right column: outputs.
- Below outputs: universal disclaimer strip, then email-gate capture card, then methodology explainer (collapsible).

Inputs (home care variant)
1. Number of active clients, default 500, min 50, max 10000, integer.
2. Average monthly visit count per client, default 24, min 4, max 120, integer.
3. Percentage of clients aged 65 and over, default 80, min 50, max 100, integer slider.
4. Current annual falls rate percentage, default 28.7, min 5, max 60, decimal to 1dp.
5. Average cost per fall in USD, default 3000, min 500, max 30000, integer.

Inputs (senior living variant)
1. Total residents across portfolio, default 1200, min 50, max 50000, integer.
2. Community type mix (IL / AL / MC / SNF), defaults 40 / 35 / 15 / 10, each 0-100, must sum to 100, auto-normalise if not.
3. Current falls incidence per tier, defaults 28.7 / 40 / 50 / 55, each 10-80 decimal to 1dp.
4. Average cost per fall USD, default 4500, min 1000, max 30000, integer.
5. Staff cost per hour USD, default 42, min 15, max 150, integer.

Every input has a label above the field, a helper tooltip on hover and focus (keyboard-accessible), and an info icon that opens a small popover explaining the source of the default value.

Calculation constants
FALLS_REDUCTION_RATE_CONSERVATIVE = 0.18
ASSESSMENT_TIME_MINUTES_ABLEASSESS = 5
ASSESSMENT_TIME_MINUTES_MANUAL = 30
SAVINGS_FLAG_THRESHOLD = 2500000

Home care calculation
clients_over_65 = active_clients * pct_over_65 / 100
expected_falls_baseline = clients_over_65 * current_falls_rate / 100
falls_prevented = expected_falls_baseline * FALLS_REDUCTION_RATE_CONSERVATIVE
annual_cost_avoided = falls_prevented * cost_per_fall
screens_per_year = active_clients * 4
hours_recovered = screens_per_year * (ASSESSMENT_TIME_MINUTES_MANUAL - ASSESSMENT_TIME_MINUTES_ABLEASSESS) / 60
payback_months = (12000 / annual_cost_avoided) * 12     // placeholder pricing; externalise as an env var

Senior living calculation
For each tier in [IL, AL, MC, SNF]:
  tier_residents = total_residents * tier_share / 100
  tier_falls = tier_residents * tier_incidence / 100
  tier_prevented = tier_falls * FALLS_REDUCTION_RATE_CONSERVATIVE
  tier_avoided = tier_prevented * cost_per_fall
falls_prevented = sum(tier_prevented)
annual_cost_avoided = sum(tier_avoided)
screens_per_year = total_residents * 4
hours_recovered = screens_per_year * (ASSESSMENT_TIME_MINUTES_MANUAL - ASSESSMENT_TIME_MINUTES_ABLEASSESS) / 60
payback_months = (36000 / annual_cost_avoided) * 12     // placeholder pricing

Outputs
Three cards, one secondary line.
Card 1: falls_prevented, rounded to nearest 10, labelled "Falls prevented per year".
Card 2: annual_cost_avoided formatted as USD with no decimals, labelled "Annual cost avoided".
Card 3: hours_recovered rounded to nearest 10, labelled "Staff hours recovered per year".
Secondary line: "At your scale, Able Assess would pay for itself in roughly [payback_months] months based on cost avoidance alone." Humanise the phrase per rules in the spec (under three months, the integer, or "roughly X months").

Each card has a "how is this calculated" link that expands an inline explainer showing the formula and the source citation. Source citations hardcoded in a constants file so they can be updated in one place.

Guardrail
If annual_cost_avoided > 2500000, show a small yellow strip under the outputs: "Figures at this scale require further validation. Let us walk you through the assumptions." Link the text to /contact.

Disclaimer strip (always visible under outputs)
"Estimates based on published research and your inputs. Actual outcomes depend on adoption, implementation, and local factors. Read the methodology →" linking to the methodology PDF at /resources/roi-methodology.pdf.

Email capture card
Title: "Email me a PDF of these results"
Fields: first name (required), work email (required, validated), company name (optional)
Primary button: "Send me the PDF"
Below: "We will only contact you about Able Care. See our privacy policy." with privacy linked.
Secondary CTA: "Book a 15-minute call" linking to /book-call.

On submit:
POST /api/roi-results with { segment, inputs, outputs, firstName, email, company }
On 200: replace the form with a small confirmation state and a "Download the PDF again" link.
On error: inline error message, keep field values filled.

The /api/roi-results route does three things:
1. Generates a branded PDF using @react-pdf/renderer or puppeteer, whichever is available. PDF header uses the Able Care logo and brand colours.
2. Sends the PDF by email via Resend (use process.env.RESEND_API_KEY, from address hello@able-care.co).
3. Posts the lead to Salesforce via the existing website→Salesforce integration at /api/salesforce/lead. Segment, inputs, and outputs go into description/notes. Include UTM params from the request cookies.

Analytics
On every input change, fire a GA4 event roi_input_change { field, bucket }. Bucket numeric inputs into ranges so no raw values leak.
On each "how is this calculated" expand, fire roi_output_expand { card }.
On form submit attempt, fire roi_submit_attempt { segment }.
On primary CTA click, fire roi_book_call_click { segment }.

Accessibility and quality bar
- Every input has a real label. No placeholder-only inputs.
- Outputs use the HTML <output> element.
- Tooltips accessible via keyboard.
- WCAG AA colour contrast.
- Works on iPhone 13 down to 1440px desktop without layout breaks.
- Input changes update outputs within 100ms; debounce not needed, calculations are cheap.
- No browser storage (no localStorage, sessionStorage) because Able Care's environment does not allow it.

Out of scope for v1
- Authenticated save/resume sessions.
- Multi-year projections.
- Actuarial compounding.
- Language variants.

Deliverables
- Pages at /home-care/roi-calculator and /senior-living/roi-calculator.
- Shared <RoiCalculator segment="home-care" | "senior-living" /> component.
- Constants file with defaults, formulae, and source citations.
- API route /api/roi-results.
- Responsive, branded, a11y-compliant.
- Ready for Paul and Dani to review on staging within one week.

Section 12: What to do with this document

  1. Share with Dani alongside the ROI Calculator Brief. Flag the six decisions and request sign-off.
  2. On sign-off, paste Section 11 into a vibe-code tool or hand to the website build team.
  3. Confirm the placeholder pricing lines (ABLE_ASSESS_ANNUAL_COST_HOMECARE, ABLE_ASSESS_ANNUAL_COST_SENIORLIVING) with Paul before build goes live.
  4. Review staging with Paul and Dani against the acceptance criteria in Section 10.
  5. Ship to production, wire into the Home Care and Senior Living landing pages, and monitor the GA4 events for the first two weeks.

Pillar evidence content

Five-minute screening white paper

The Five-Minute Falls Screening

Evidence for Non-Clinical Staff Deployment in Home Care and Senior Living

Executive summary

Falls are the leading preventable driver of hospitalisation, loss of independence, and unplanned transitions to higher-acuity care among adults aged 65 and over. One in four older adults falls each year. Non-fatal falls cost the United States healthcare system in the region of $80 billion annually [CDC]. In home care, 50% of home health beneficiaries fall at least once per year. In senior living, falls are the single largest driver of acuity progression and resident move-outs.

The prevention evidence is established. The CDC STEADI programme and the 2022 World Falls Guidelines both recommend routine screening of older adults using validated functional measurements: grip strength, the timed up-and-go test, the 30-second chair stand, and four-metre gait speed. Screening identifies the 30% to 40% of at-risk individuals that subjective clinician or family assessment misses.

The implementation gap is the problem. Existing screening protocols require 30 minutes or more of clinician time per resident or client. They depend on specialist availability. They are not consistently applied across a portfolio. The result is a category where the evidence is well established but the practice is not.

This paper sets out the case for a different approach. A screening protocol that combines the four CDC-aligned measurements into a single five-minute workflow, administered by non-clinical staff on tablet or phone, anchored to a normative reference database of over 1,000 healthy adults. We review the underlying clinical evidence, the operator economics, the deployment model, and the early customer outcomes. The conclusion is straightforward. The barrier to widespread falls prevention in operator settings has never been clinical. It has been operational. Solving the operational gap unlocks the clinical benefit the evidence has promised for the past decade.

1. The scale of the problem

1.1 Prevalence

Falls are not rare events. They are the most common adverse health event in older adult populations.

  • One in four adults aged 65 and over falls each year [CDC].
  • 50% of home health beneficiaries fall at least once per year [Falls Prevention Commercialization Strategy, 2026].
  • Forty per cent of assisted living residents fall annually. In memory care the figure rises to 50%. In skilled nursing it approaches 55% [industry-blended estimate, Market Sizing brief].

Falls are also recurrent. A resident who falls once is significantly more likely to fall again within twelve months. The pattern of recurrent falls is the single strongest predictor of transition to higher acuity care and of permanent loss of independence.

1.2 Economic cost

Falls are the largest preventable cost category in older adult care.

  • Non-fatal falls cost the US healthcare system approximately $80 billion per year [CDC, Falls Prevention Commercialization Strategy].
  • The direct cost of a single non-fatal fall ranges from $3,000 for minor events to $30,000 or more where hospitalisation and post-acute rehabilitation are required [CDC].
  • Projected annual spend on falls in the US is on track to exceed $100 billion by 2030 as the cohort of adults aged 65 and over grows.

The cost to operators is distinct from the cost to the healthcare system as a whole, and often larger in relative terms. In home care, a single fall that leads to a hospital admission can end a 24/7 case worth $350,000 per year in revenue and $140,000 in gross profit [Cypress HomeCare, case study]. In senior living, falls trigger the acuity transitions that reduce length-of-stay and compress net operating income.

1.3 Clinical consequence

Falls are a threshold event in the life of an older adult. Approximately 20% of non-fatal falls result in a serious injury such as hip fracture or traumatic brain injury. A hip fracture in an adult over 75 carries a one-year mortality risk approaching 25%. The residents most affected are often those whose families considered them independent the week before the fall.

The cascade is what makes falls distinct from other adverse events in this population. A fall leads to reduced activity, which leads to muscle wasting and reduced grip strength, which raises the risk of a second fall. Breaking the cascade early, before the first fall, is the objective of effective screening.

2. Why current approaches are under-delivering

The clinical evidence for falls prevention screening is unambiguous. The CDC STEADI programme has been published since 2013. The 2022 World Falls Guidelines formalise the recommendation globally. Every major professional body in geriatrics endorses routine screening.

Actual adoption is limited. The reasons sit in three categories.

2.1 Subjective assessment misses the at-risk group

Operators and families commonly rely on clinician judgement or family observation to identify residents at risk of falling. This approach under-detects. Peer-reviewed research and operator experience both indicate that objective screening identifies risk in 30% to 40% of individuals that subjective assessment would have cleared. This is the group that falls in the following quarter without an obvious warning sign.

2.2 Protocols are too slow to run at scale

The STEADI protocol, applied in full, takes 30 minutes or more per resident. A typical senior living community with 200 residents cannot screen its population every quarter at this cost. A home care agency with 500 clients cannot add 250 hours per quarter to its clinical rota. The protocol is clinically sound, and it is not operationally feasible at the portfolio scale the market requires.

2.3 Specialist dependency limits deployment

Existing protocols are typically designed for physical therapists or geriatricians to administer. The specialist is not reliably available. Staff turnover in home care averages 65% per year. Every hand-off loses institutional knowledge. A screening protocol that depends on a specific clinical role is a screening protocol that is not consistently run.

The gap, therefore, is not in the science. It is in the delivery model. A protocol that compresses the four highest-value CDC-aligned measurements into a single short workflow, deliverable by non-clinical staff, reopens the clinical benefit the existing evidence has already proven.

3. The four measurements that matter most

The CDC STEADI programme and the 2022 World Falls Guidelines converge on four functional measurements as the highest-signal, lowest-cost indicators of falls risk in older adults.

3.1 Grip strength

Grip strength is one of the most widely validated biomarkers in adult medicine. It is an independent predictor of mortality, hospitalisation risk, functional decline, and falls risk across adult age groups. It is measured objectively with a handheld dynamometer, takes under 30 seconds per reading, is reproducible, and trends meaningfully across repeated measurements in the same individual [CDC STEADI].

In operator settings, grip strength has the additional property of being culturally neutral and language-independent. It can be measured by any staff member across any population, which makes it deployable in home care and senior living contexts where clinical staff are not universally available.

3.2 Four-metre gait speed

Gait speed over a short distance is a validated indicator of overall functional capacity and falls risk. The measurement is straightforward. The individual is asked to walk four metres at their normal pace. The time is recorded. A speed below 0.8 metres per second is a standard threshold for increased falls risk and for reduced independence more broadly [CDC STEADI, 2022 World Falls Guidelines].

3.3 Thirty-second chair stand

The 30-second chair stand measures lower-body strength and endurance. The individual sits in a standard chair and is asked to stand and sit repeatedly for 30 seconds. The count is compared against age-adjusted norms. Performance below the norm is a strong signal of reduced functional capacity and elevated falls risk.

3.4 Timed up-and-go

The timed up-and-go (TUG) test combines mobility, balance, and coordination in a single short sequence. The individual rises from a chair, walks three metres, turns, returns, and sits. The time is recorded. Scores above 12 seconds are consistent with elevated falls risk in older adults.

3.5 Why four together, rather than any one

Each of the four measurements captures a distinct aspect of functional risk. Grip strength indexes general muscle function and systemic health. Gait speed indexes overall capacity. Chair stand indexes lower-body strength. TUG indexes balance and coordination. The clinical evidence is clear that the combination is materially more predictive than any single measurement run in isolation.

The implementation constraint is that running all four measurements in sequence, using the traditional protocol, is time-consuming and requires specialist training. This is the constraint the deployment model described in the next section resolves.

4. The five-minute deployment model

The implementation principle is simple. The four measurements are run in sequence, in a single short workflow, with digital prompts and automated scoring that remove the need for specialist interpretation. The total time from start to finish is approximately five minutes per individual.

4.1 Workflow

The staff member opens a tablet or phone, enters the resident or client's identifier, and follows the on-screen prompts.

  1. Grip strength, left and right hand, using a handheld dynamometer. 30 seconds.
  2. Four-metre gait speed, at comfortable walking pace. 45 seconds.
  3. 30-second chair stand. 45 seconds plus setup.
  4. Timed up-and-go. 60 seconds.

Total: approximately five minutes, with some variance for setup and repositioning.

4.2 Who administers

The workflow is designed for non-clinical staff. Training time is minutes, not hours. The prompts are embedded in the application. The scoring is automated against the normative reference database. The staff member's role is to operate the tablet and deliver the measurements accurately. Clinical interpretation is produced by the algorithm and delivered as a clear output.

In home care, this means a home care aide on a regular visit can incorporate the screening into standard practice. In senior living, this means activity staff, wellness coordinators, or the resident's regular care partner can run the screening during a scheduled check-in.

4.3 Output

The output of the five-minute workflow is a clear report. Each measurement is compared against age-adjusted norms drawn from the reference database of over 1,000 healthy adults. The individual's position relative to the normative distribution is visualised. A risk flag is raised where indicated. Practical next steps are suggested. These may include referral for physiotherapy, home safety review, medication review, or scheduled re-assessment.

The report is designed to be readable by the individual, the family, and the clinical team. The intention is to make the result actionable at the point of care rather than requiring a secondary interpretation appointment.

4.4 Repeat cadence

Evidence suggests that quarterly re-assessment is the right cadence for at-risk populations. Annual re-assessment is appropriate for lower-risk populations. The five-minute workflow makes both cadences operationally feasible.

5. The operator economic case

The screening protocol exists to prevent falls. Falls prevention generates operator value through three mechanisms.

5.1 Revenue protection

In home care, a single high-acuity client represents significant annual revenue. A 24/7 case equates to approximately $350,000 per year at typical market rates, with a gross profit of around $140,000 at a 40% margin [Cypress HomeCare, case study]. A fall that leads to hospitalisation commonly ends the case. The operator loses the revenue for the remainder of the client's life expectancy, loses the gross profit, and loses the scheduled hours for the 15 caregivers on the case, of whom a significant proportion will move to a different employer within 48 to 72 hours if alternative hours cannot be found.

The economic logic is simple. Preventing one fall preserves one case. Preserving one case covers the cost of the screening programme many times over.

5.2 Length-of-stay protection

In senior living, falls drive transitions from independent living to assisted living, from assisted living to memory care, and from senior living to skilled nursing. Each transition typically reduces length-of-stay, increases move-out frequency, and compresses net operating income at the portfolio level.

The protected alternative is continuity. A resident whose functional decline is caught early, and whose care plan is adjusted accordingly, is more likely to remain in their current tier. The operator retains the resident, the revenue, and the occupancy.

5.3 Liability and quality posture

Falls are a leading driver of litigation in senior living and home care. Documented screening, consistently applied and objectively measured, is a meaningful component of liability defence. The same documentation is material to the CMS quality ratings that drive SNF reimbursement and to the quality metrics that influence HHVBP incentives in home care.

The screening programme, therefore, is not only a clinical intervention. It is an operational record of consistent, evidence-based practice, which carries value in its own right.

5.4 Staff burden

The secondary operator benefit is staff time. Replacing a 30-minute manual STEADI workflow with a five-minute digital workflow releases 25 minutes per screening. At typical portfolio scales, this translates into hundreds or thousands of clinical hours recovered per year. The recovered hours can be redirected to intervention, rather than consumed by assessment.

6. Early customer evidence

6.1 Cypress HomeCare (home care, United States)

Cypress HomeCare is a family-owned home care business operating in Arizona. The operator implemented the five-minute screening protocol across its book of business. In the first period of use, the screening identified weakness patterns, including asymmetric grip strength, in a material proportion of clients. Each identification triggered a physical therapy referral. In the owner's words: "If you do it repetitively, you're going to score about the same. But if you do it over time, you can see there's someone strengthening or someone weakening."

The operator's conclusion was that the screening had shifted their clinical posture from reactive to proactive, had strengthened the retention story they could present to families and payors, and had created a measurable operational benefit. The full write-up is available as a dedicated case study.

6.2 Right at Home Tri-County Metro (home care, United States)

Right at Home Tri-County Metro implemented the screening as a standard element of client intake. Every new client receives a baseline screening on day one, with periodic re-assessment. The operator uses the portal's trend analysis to track grip strength, gait speed, and chair stand performance over time, with the normative band visualised alongside the individual's results.

The operational effect described by the operator is the ability to intervene before the fall occurs. Early detection drives physical therapy referral, home modification, or medication review. The cost of those interventions is a fraction of the cost of the acute fall event they replace. The full interview is in the pipeline for publication.

6.3 Eskaton (CCRC, United States)

Eskaton is a multi-site continuing care retirement community operator. The published case study details a multi-site deployment across independent living, assisted living, and memory care populations. Key operational metrics from the deployment are summarised in the dedicated case study document.

7. Implementation framework for operators

The following framework describes a production deployment of the five-minute screening protocol across a home care or senior living operator.

7.1 Phase one: baseline (weeks 1 to 4)

Identify the staff who will administer screenings in each location. Complete training, which takes under 15 minutes per staff member. Establish the screening cadence. For new clients or residents, screen on intake. For existing populations, establish a rolling baseline over a three-month window. At the end of week four, every client or resident has a baseline record.

7.2 Phase two: intervention routing (weeks 4 to 12)

Where screenings identify elevated risk, route the individual to the appropriate intervention. Typical routes include physical therapy referral, home safety review, medication review, or scheduled clinical consultation. The operator's role is to ensure each risk flag leads to a defined next step, rather than being filed as an observation.

7.3 Phase three: repeat and track (quarter two onwards)

Establish the quarterly re-assessment cadence for at-risk populations and annual re-assessment for lower-risk populations. Review the trend data at the community or agency level each quarter. Identify patterns. Adjust interventions. Report to leadership using the portfolio-level dashboard.

7.4 Portfolio rollout

Operators with multiple sites should deploy sequentially rather than in parallel. Start with two lead sites. Use the first quarter to establish the screening cadence, work out the intervention routing, and document local practice. Expand to the remaining sites with a refined playbook in quarter two.

8. What to look for in a screening partner

Operators considering a screening platform should evaluate the following.

Evidence base. Is the protocol anchored to CDC STEADI or the 2022 World Falls Guidelines? Are the measurements validated in peer-reviewed literature? Does the vendor name the evidence transparently?

Normative reference. Does the platform compare individual results to a normative database of healthy adults? How large is the reference population? How frequently is it updated?

Staff deployment model. Who can administer the screening? What is the training time? What happens when staff turnover replaces the trained administrator?

Time per screening. How long does a full screening take in practice, not in theory? Has the vendor published or shared actual time-per-screening data from deployments?

Integration posture. Does the platform integrate with the EMR, CRM, or care management platform the operator uses? If not today, what is the roadmap?

Regulatory and security posture. Which regulatory marks does the platform carry (FDA, CE, ISO 13485, IEC 60601)? Is HIPAA compliance documented? What data-residency guarantees are offered?

Commercial model. Per-site, per-screening, per-resident, or enterprise? Which model aligns with the operator's growth and cost sensitivities?

9. Conclusion

The clinical evidence for falls prevention screening in older adults is established. The CDC, the 2022 World Falls Guidelines, and every major geriatric society recommend the practice. The operator evidence for the economic value of prevention is equally clear. A prevented fall preserves revenue in home care, length-of-stay in senior living, and quality posture in both.

What has not existed, until recently, is an implementation model that makes this clinically proven practice operationally deliverable at portfolio scale. A five-minute workflow, built around the four highest-value measurements, administered by non-clinical staff, and anchored to a normative reference database, closes that implementation gap.

The result, for operators who adopt the approach, is not a new clinical claim. It is the ability to deliver existing clinical evidence at a cost and cadence their operation can actually sustain. That is where the commercial and clinical value compounds.

For operators who want to explore the approach for their portfolio, the next step is a 15-minute walk-through of the platform and a discussion of the economic case for your specific scale. Please contact the Able Care commercial team.

References and sources

  • CDC STEADI (Stopping Elderly Accidents, Deaths & Injuries), ongoing guidance.
  • 2022 World Falls Guidelines, international consensus document.

Fact-check status

This document is marked as draft for Paul's clinical fact-check and Dani's brand review. Points flagged for confirmation before publication:

  1. The CDC citation pattern used throughout. Confirm the preferred format for external publication.
  2. The 18% falls reduction anchor used in the ROI calculator. Confirm the peer-reviewed source of record.
  3. The $80 billion annual US falls cost figure. Confirm the most recent CDC publication date.
  4. The asymmetric grip strength pattern described in the Cypress case study. Confirm quote use and attribution.
  5. The normative reference database size ("over 1,000 healthy adults"). Confirm the exact figure Able Care is willing to publish.

Marketing documents

Operational and administrative documents that keep the sales and marketing system running. Admin set-ups, integrations, process guides.

Jump to
Administration and integrations
Salesforce: website subscribers admin setup

Administration and integrations

Salesforce: website subscribers admin setup

Salesforce setup: website subscriber capture

Instructions for the Salesforce administrator to enable the AbleCare website to push new subscribers (and subsequent transactions) into Salesforce via the REST API.

What we need from you

By the end of this setup you should send us:

  1. Instance URL (e.g. https://ablecare.my.salesforce.com)
  2. Consumer Key (Client ID) from the Connected App
  3. Consumer Secret (Client Secret) from the Connected App
  4. Integration username and password + security token (only if we use the username-password OAuth flow)
  5. Confirmation of which object and fields the website should write to

Please share items 2–4 via a password manager or encrypted channel, not plain email.

Step 1 — Decide the target object

The website will create a record per subscriber and (optionally) per transaction. You have two sensible options:

Option A — Standard Lead object (recommended if subscribers are prospects you will market to). No object creation needed; use the standard Lead fields plus a couple of custom ones.

Option B — Custom object Website_Subscriber__c (recommended if subscribers are distinct from sales leads, e.g. newsletter-only). Keeps your Lead funnel clean.

Whichever you pick, create/confirm these fields:

Field label API name Type Notes
Email Email (Lead) or Email__c Email Required, unique
First name FirstName or First_Name__c Text(40) Optional
Last name LastName or Last_Name__c Text(80) Required on Lead
Source page Source_Page__c Text(255) URL of signup form
Subscription type Subscription_Type__c Picklist e.g. Newsletter, Service enquiry, Transaction
Consent timestamp Consent_At__c Date/Time GDPR audit trail
External ID Website_Id__c Text(64), External ID, Unique Lets us upsert without duplicates

If you want to log transactions as well, create a related custom object Subscriber_Transaction__c with a Master-Detail relationship back to the subscriber, plus fields for Amount__c, Transaction_Id__c (External ID, Unique), Occurred_At__c, Status__c, and Payload__c (Long Text) for the raw event.

Step 2 — Create an integration user

Do not reuse a named user's credentials. Create a dedicated integration user so access can be audited and rotated.

  1. Setup → Users → New User
  2. Licence: Salesforce Integration (if you have one available) or a standard Salesforce licence
  3. Profile: Minimum Access – API Only Integrations (or a cloned custom profile with API Enabled)
  4. Username: integration.website@ablecare.co.uk (or similar)
  5. Email: your own or a shared inbox
  6. Tick API Enabled on the profile
  7. Save, then set a strong password and reset the security token for this user (Setup → My Personal Information → Reset My Security Token, while logged in as that user)

Create a permission set granting Create, Read, Edit on the target object(s) and fields from Step 1. Assign it to the integration user. Grant nothing else.

Step 3 — Create a Connected App

This is what produces the API credentials.

  1. Setup → App Manager → New Connected App
  2. Basic info: - Connected App Name: AbleCare Website Integration - API Name: AbleCare_Website_Integration - Contact Email: your address
  3. Tick Enable OAuth Settings
  4. Callback URL: https://ablecare.co.uk/oauth/callback (placeholder; required even if unused for server-to-server flow)
  5. Selected OAuth Scopes: - Manage user data via APIs (api) - Perform requests at any time (refresh_token, offline_access)
  6. Tick Require Secret for Web Server Flow and Require Secret for Refresh Token Flow
  7. Enable Client Credentials Flow (recommended for server-to-server) — then under "Run As" pick the integration user you created in Step 2
  8. Save. Salesforce will warn it takes up to 10 minutes to propagate.

After saving, open the Connected App and copy: - Consumer Key → this is the Client ID - Consumer Secret (click "Click to reveal") → this is the Client Secret

Step 4 — Lock down access

On the Connected App, click Manage → Edit Policies:

  • Permitted Users: Admin approved users are pre-authorized
  • IP Relaxation: Enforce IP restrictions
  • Refresh Token Policy: Refresh token is valid until revoked

Then back on the Connected App detail page, under Profiles or Permission Sets, add the permission set assigned to the integration user so it is pre-authorised.

Under Setup → Network Access, add the website server's outbound IP range as a Trusted IP Range. We will provide this range once hosting is confirmed.

Step 5 — Test the credentials

Once the app has propagated (wait ~10 minutes), you can sanity-check with curl. The website team will do this, but if you want to verify yourself:

curl -X POST https://login.salesforce.com/services/oauth2/token \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials" \
  -d "client_id=<Consumer Key>" \
  -d "client_secret=<Consumer Secret>"

A successful response returns an access_token and instance_url. Anything else (error invalid_client_id, invalid_grant, etc.) means the app is misconfigured — most often the Client Credentials flow was not enabled or the "Run As" user was not set.

Step 6 — Hand over

Please reply with:

  • Instance URL
  • Consumer Key
  • Consumer Secret (via password manager)
  • Integration user's username
  • Confirmation of which object (Lead vs custom) we are writing to
  • Outbound IP range you have whitelisted (or confirmation that IP restrictions are off for this app)

Rotation policy: we suggest rotating the Consumer Secret every 12 months, or immediately if a developer with access leaves the project.

Questions for us

If anything above is unclear or you would prefer a different flow (e.g. JWT Bearer with a certificate instead of Client Credentials), let us know before creating the app and we will adjust.
